## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define a microsensor. List any four types of microsensors based on the energy domain they transduce."** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **microsensor** is a micro-scale device that detects a physical, chemical, biological, or optical stimulus from the environment and converts it into a measurable electrical signal (typically voltage, current, resistance, capacitance, or frequency). It is fabricated using MEMS-compatible batch-fabrication processes on a silicon substrate.

The four types of microsensors based on the energy domain are:
1. **Mechanical sensors** — Piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric (sense pressure, force, acceleration, strain).
2. **Thermal sensors** — Thermocouples, thermistors, bolometers (sense temperature, heat flux, IR radiation).
3. **Optical sensors** — Photodiodes, CCD/CMOS image sensors, DMDs (sense light intensity, wavelength, phase).
4. **Chemical / Biochemical sensors** — ISFET, gas sensors, glucose sensors, DNA microarrays (sense chemical or biological analytes).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is a microactuator? With the help of neat diagrams, explain the working of an electrostatic comb-drive actuator."** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **microactuator** is a micro-scale device that converts an input electrical signal (or other form of energy) into a **mechanical motion, force, or displacement** at the micro-scale. Typical output displacements range from **0.1 µm to 100 µm** and forces from **µN to mN**.

**Working of an Electrostatic Comb-Drive Actuator:**

```
  Stator Comb (Fixed)         Rotor Comb (Movable)
  | | | | | | | |            | | | | | | | |
  | | | | | | | | <-- gap --> | | | | | | | |
  | | | | | | | |            | | | | | | | |
       (a) At Rest                (b) At Rest
```

A comb-drive consists of two sets of interdigitated "fingers" (electrodes): one set is **fixed** (stator) and the other is attached to a **movable** structure (rotor). When a voltage $V$ is applied between the stator and rotor, an **electrostatic attractive force** develops between the overlapping fingers. Because the fingers are interdigitated in the lateral direction, the force is purely in-plane and pulls the rotor towards the stator. The force is given by:

$$
F = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r N t V^2}{g}
$$

where $N$ is the number of finger pairs, $t$ is the finger thickness (out-of-plane), $V$ is the applied voltage, and $g$ is the gap between fingers.

**Key features:** (i) Produces **large in-plane forces**, (ii) **linear force-displacement** characteristic (unlike parallel-plate actuators, which suffer from pull-in instability), and (iii) **low power consumption** with fast response.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question 3 (A) — Comprehensive Long-Answer

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the various types of microactuators based on the energy conversion principle. Compare their displacement, force, and response time in a tabular form.

**(b) (7 Marks)** With neat sketches, explain the operation of a **piezoresistive pressure sensor**. Derive the expression for the fractional change in resistance of a piezoresistor under longitudinal and transverse stress.

---

### Question 3 (A) — Model Solution

#### Part (a): Types of Microactuators (7 Marks)

Microactuators can be classified based on the **form of energy** used to produce mechanical output.

**1. Electrostatic Actuators**
- **Principle:** Coulombic attraction between oppositely charged conductors.
- **Types:** Parallel plate, comb drive.
- **Force equation:** $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A V^2 / d^2$ (parallel plate)
- **Displacement:** 1 – 50 µm
- **Response time:** µs (very fast)
- **Limitation:** Pull-in instability limits stable travel to ~33% of initial gap.

**2. Piezoelectric Actuators**
- **Principle:** Converse piezoelectric effect — applied electric field causes strain in crystal.
- **Common materials:** PZT (lead zirconate titanate), AlN, ZnO, PVDF.
- **Strain equation:** $S = d_{ij} E$
- **Displacement:** 0.1 – 10 µm
- **Response time:** µs
- **Feature:** Highest force density of all microactuators.

**3. Thermal Actuators**
- **Principle:** Differential thermal expansion of two bonded materials (bimorph principle).
- **Types:** Bimorph cantilever, chevron (V-beam).
- **Displacement:** 10 – 100 µm (largest of all)
- **Response time:** ms to s (slow, limited by thermal time constant)
- **Limitation:** High power consumption; requires continuous current.

**4. Magnetic Actuators**
- **Principle:** Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor in B-field, or shape memory effect.
- **Force equation:** $F = I L \times B$ (Lorentz)
- **Displacement:** 10 – 500 µm
- **Response time:** ms
- **Advantage:** Compatible with biological fluids (no electrolysis).

**5. Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuators**
- **Principle:** Martensite-to-austenite phase transition in Ni-Ti (Nitinol) alloys upon heating.
- **Displacement:** 50 – 500 µm
- **Strain:** Up to 8% recoverable
- **Response time:** 100 ms – 1 s
- **Use:** Micro-grippers, biomedical stents.

**Comparison Table:**

| Actuator Type | Displacement (µm) | Force (relative) | Response Time | Power (relative) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Electrostatic (comb) | 1 – 50 | Low – Medium | µs (Fast) | Very Low |
| Piezoelectric | 0.1 – 10 | High | µs (Fast) | Low |
| Thermal (bimorph) | 10 – 100 | Medium | ms – s (Slow) | High |
| Magnetic (Lorentz) | 10 – 500 | High | ms | Medium – High |
| SMA | 50 – 500 | Very High | 100 ms – 1 s | High |

#### Part (b): Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor (7 Marks)

**Construction (Neat Sketch):**

```
      Top View                        Cross-Section
   +-------------+                ___________
   |  Si Diaphragm|               |  SiO2    |  ← passivation
   |   _______   |               |  ________|
   |  |   R1  |  |               | |        |  ← Wheatstone bridge
   |  |  ---  |  |               | | R1 R2  |     on diaphragm
   |  |  R3 R4|  |               | | R3 R4  |
   |  |  ---  |  |               | |________|
   |  |_______|  |               |__________|
   |     ___     |               |          |
   |    | P |    |   P→          |  Si      |  ← bulk Si substrate
   +-----| |-----+   pressure    |__________|
        +---+                   ___________
        cavity                  | cavity   |  ← sealed reference
                               |__________|
```

**Operation:**

1. A thin silicon diaphragm is etched from the back side of a silicon wafer, leaving a flexible membrane.
2. Four piezoresistors (R₁, R₂, R₃, R₄) are **diffused** or **ion-implanted** near the edges of the diaphragm, where the **maximum stress** occurs (two at the center, two at the edge, oriented along the (110) direction).
3. The four resistors are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.
4. When a pressure $P$ is applied to the diaphragm, it deflects, generating **tensile stress** in two resistors and **compressive stress** in the other two. This changes their resistances and unbalances the bridge, producing a measurable output voltage.

**Derivation of the Piezoresistive Equation:**

Consider a rectangular resistor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A_c = w \cdot t$, and resistivity $\rho$ aligned along the $x$-axis. The resistance is:

$$
R = \frac{\rho L}{A_c}
$$

Taking the differential change:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A_c}{A_c}
$$

For a semiconductor piezoresistor, the dominant contribution is the **change in resistivity** due to stress, while the geometric terms ($\Delta L / L$ and $\Delta A_c / A_c$) are negligible (about 1% of the total in silicon). The fractional resistivity change is governed by the **piezoresistive coefficients**:

$$
\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t
$$

where:
- $\pi_l$ = **longitudinal** piezoresistive coefficient (stress parallel to current flow), units: **Pa⁻¹**
- $\pi_t$ = **transverse** piezoresistive coefficient (stress perpendicular to current flow)
- $\sigma_l$, $\sigma_t$ = corresponding **stress** components, units: **Pa**

Therefore, the complete fractional change in resistance is:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t}
$$

In silicon, the dominant piezoresistive coefficients are $\pi_{44} \approx 138 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ for p-type and a similar value for n-type — roughly **two orders of magnitude** higher than in metals.

The bridge output voltage is then:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

where $V_{ex}$ is the bridge excitation voltage.

---

### Question 3 (B) — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Deal-Grove model** of thermal oxidation. Derive the linear-parabolic equation and discuss the two limiting regimes.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the **photolithography process** in detail with all its steps. Discuss the Rayleigh criterion for resolution and its practical implications.

---

### Question 3 (B) — Model Solution Outline

#### Part (a): Deal-Grove Model of Thermal Oxidation (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Thermal oxidation is the process of growing a thin layer of **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** on a silicon wafer by exposing it to an oxidizing ambient (dry O₂ or wet H₂O) at high temperature (800 – 1200 °C). The reactions are:

$$
Si_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow SiO_{2(s)} \quad \text{(dry)}
$$

$$
Si_{(s)} + 2 H_2O_{(g)} \rightarrow SiO_{2(s)} + 2 H_{2(g)} \quad \text{(wet)}
$$

**Derivation Steps (summary, full derivation in Section 3.1):**
1. Identify the three sequential processes: gas-phase transport → diffusion through oxide → surface reaction at interface.
2. Write the flux for each: $F_1 = h_g(C_g - C_s)$, $F_2 = D(C_s - C_i)/x_0$, $F_3 = k_s C_i$.
3. Apply steady-state $F_1 = F_2 = F_3 = F$.
4. Combine to eliminate $C_s$ and $C_i$.
5. Define the Deal-Grove constants: $B = 2 D C_g / N_1$ and $B/A = k_s C_g / (N_1(1 + k_s/h_g))$.
6. Integrate to obtain:

$$
\boxed{x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)}
$$

**Two Limiting Regimes:**
- **Thin-oxide / Parabolic regime** ($x_0 \ll A$): $x_0 \approx \sqrt{B t}$ — diffusion is rate-limiting, oxide grows as $\sqrt{t}$.
- **Thick-oxide / Linear regime** ($x_0 \gg A$): $x_0 \approx (B/A)(t + \tau)$ — surface reaction is rate-limiting, oxide grows linearly with $t$.

> **Important Note:** For very thin oxides (< 10 nm), the Deal-Grove model breaks down and an **enhanced growth regime** (rapid initial growth) is observed, attributed to the presence of a native oxide and stress effects.

#### Part (b): Photolithography Process (7 Marks)

Photolithography is the process of transferring a geometric pattern from a **photomask** onto the wafer surface using light and a photosensitive polymer called **photoresist**.

**Seven Steps:**
1. **Vapor Priming (HMDS):** Hexamethyldisilazane is applied to improve photoresist adhesion to the wafer.
2. **Spin Coating:** Photoresist is dispensed onto the wafer, which is spun at 1000 – 5000 rpm to produce a uniform film (typically 0.5 – 2 µm thick).
3. **Soft Bake:** Wafer is heated to ~90 °C for 60 s to evaporate the solvent.
4. **Exposure:** UV light (365 nm i-line, 248 nm DUV, or 193 nm ArF) is projected through the photomask onto the resist. In **positive** resist, exposed regions become soluble; in **negative** resist, exposed regions crosslink and become insoluble.
5. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB):** Brief bake (~110 °C) to reduce standing-wave effects caused by thin-film interference.
6. **Development:** Wafer is immersed in a developer solution (TMAH for positive resist), dissolving the exposed (or unexposed) regions and revealing the underlying substrate.
7. **Hard Bake:** Wafer is baked at ~120 °C for 2 min to harden the remaining resist and improve etch resistance.

**Rayleigh Criterion for Resolution:**

The minimum resolvable feature size is:

$$
R = k_1 \frac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the exposure wavelength, $NA$ is the numerical aperture of the projection lens, and $k_1$ is a process-dependent factor (typically 0.4 – 0.8 for production processes, 0.25 in research).

**Practical Implications:**
- **Shorter wavelength** → finer features (driving the industry to **EUV lithography at 13.5 nm**).
- **Larger numerical aperture** → finer features (modern immersion lithography uses $NA = 1.35$ with water as the immersion medium).
- **Depth of Focus (DOF):** $DOF = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$ — a trade-off against resolution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in MEMS Module 1 (KTU Board Exam):**
>
> 1. **Confusing transduction directions:** Students often write "piezoresistive sensor" when they mean "piezoelectric sensor" (or vice versa). Remember: **piezoresistive = change in resistance under stress**; **piezoelectric = generation of charge under stress**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constant $\tau$ in the Deal-Grove equation:** A bare answer of $x_0^2 = Bt$ (without the $A x_0$ term and the time shift $\tau$) will lose 1 – 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the order of photolithography steps:** The correct sequence is **HMDS → Spin Coat → Soft Bake → Expose → PEB → Develop → Hard Bake**. Out-of-order answers lose marks.
>
> 4. **Drawing unimpressive diagrams:** A diagram with the **layer thicknesses, labels, and arrows** explicitly drawn fetches higher marks than a vague sketch. Always label the silicon substrate, SiO₂ layer, photoresist, and mask.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** A typical KTU 14-mark question has a 2-mark sub-part for the numerical answer — **always state the units** (µm, nm, mN, etc.).
>
> 6. **Citing "Comb-Drive" without explanation:** Just naming an actuator is not enough. You must state the **principle, the governing equation, and the typical application**.
>
> 7. **Skipping the diagram in the long-answer question:** KTU examiners award **1 – 2 marks** for neat, well-labeled diagrams in the 7-mark sub-parts. Never skip them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, PEPET522 MEMS**

### A. Foundational Definitions
- [ ] **MEMS** = Micro Electro Mechanical Systems; integrates mechanical + electrical on silicon.
- [ ] **Microsensor** = converts a non-electrical input into an electrical output.
- [ ] **Microactuator** = converts an electrical input into a mechanical output.
- [ ] **Transduction** = the process of energy conversion between domains.

### B. Microsensors — Must Remember
- [ ] **Piezoresistive**: $\Delta R / R = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ — dominant in silicon.
- [ ] **Capacitive**: $C = \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A / d$ — used in accelerometers.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric**: $Q = d_{ij} F$ — materials: quartz, PZT, AlN, ZnO.
- [ ] **Thermal**: based on Seebeck effect or RTD principle.
- [ ] **Optical (MOEMS)**: DMD in projectors.
- [ ] **Chemical/Bio**: ISFET, glucose sensor, DNA arrays.

### C. Microactuators — Must Remember
- [ ] **Electrostatic**: $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A V^2 / d^2$ — fast, low power, pull-in limit.
- [ ] **Comb-drive**: in-plane, linear force-displacement.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric**: $S = d_{ij} E$ — high force, small displacement.
- [ ] **Thermal (bimorph)**: large displacement, slow, high power.
- [ ] **Magnetic (Lorentz)**: $F = I L \times B$ — large force, hard to integrate.
- [ ] **SMA (Nitinol)**: 8% recoverable strain, biomedical use.

### D. Fabrication Process — Must Remember
- [ ] **Silicon wafer** — substrate of choice; orientations (100), (111), (110).
- [ ] **Oxidation** — Deal-Grove model: $x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$; dry vs. wet.
- [ ] **Diffusion** — Fick's laws; high temperature; not very precise.
- [ ] **Ion Implantation** — precise dose and depth; requires annealing.
- [ ] **Thin-Film Deposition** — CVD (LPCVD, PECVD), PVD (evaporation, sputtering).
- [ ] **Lithography** — 7 steps: HMDS → Spin → Soft Bake → Expose → PEB → Develop → Hard Bake.
- [ ] **Resolution** — Rayleigh: $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$.
- [ ] **Etching** — Wet (isotropic/anisotropic), Dry (RIE, DRIE — Bosch process).
- [ ] **Metallization** — Al, Cu, Au; PVD or CVD.
- [ ] **Packaging** — cavity, hermetic sealing, electrical feedthroughs.

### E. High-Yield Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] Young's modulus of silicon: **≈ 130 GPa**
- [ ] Piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_{44}$ of p-type Si: **≈ 138 × 10⁻¹¹ Pa⁻¹**
- [ ] Young's modulus of silicon is **~two orders of magnitude** higher than in metals for piezoresistivity.
- [ ] Typical pull-in gap for electrostatic actuators: **~ 1/3 of initial gap**.
- [ ] Standard wafer sizes: **100 mm, 150 mm, 200 mm, 300 mm**.
- [ ] Oxidation temperatures: **800 – 1200 °C**.
- [ ] DRIE aspect ratios: **up to 50:1**.

### F. Industrial Applications to Remember
- [ ] **Automotive**: airbag accelerometer, tire pressure sensor.
- [ ] **Consumer electronics**: smartphone accelerometer, gyroscope, microphone.
- [ ] **Medical**: blood pressure sensor, insulin pump, lab-on-chip.
- [ ] **Industrial**: pressure transmitter, flow meter, gas sensor.
- [ ] **Optical**: DLP projector (Texas Instruments DMD).
- [ ] **RF**: MEMS switch, FBAR filter.

### G. KTU Exam Quick Wins
- [ ] Always **draw neat, labeled diagrams** for 7-mark questions.
- [ ] Always **state the governing equation** for any named transducer.
- [ ] Always **state units** in numerical answers.
- [ ] Always **list two applications** of any MEMS device you describe.
- [ ] Always **compare two methods in a table** when asked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — PEPET522 MEMS.**
> These notes are aligned to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus and use the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze) as specified in the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework. All questions are mapped to **Course Outcomes CO1 and CO2** of PEPET522.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials for MEMS: Silicon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Materials for MEMS: Silicon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Silicon (Si)** is a Group IV (Group 14) elemental semiconductor with atomic number **14** and atomic mass **28.0855 g/mol**. In the context of Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS), silicon is the foundational structural, electronic, and mechanical material due to its exceptional combination of mechanical strength, electronic tunability, and well-established integrated circuit (IC) compatible microfabrication processes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, silicon is treated as a **multi-functional engineering material** — simultaneously serving as:
- A **structural layer** (mechanical beams, membranes, cantilevers)
- An **active electronic layer** (piezoresistive sensing elements)
- A **piezoelectric-like material** (through engineered doping and stress)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Silicon's dominance in MEMS stems from three pillars — (1) Excellent mechanical properties approaching those of steel, (2) Semiconducting behaviour enabling integration of sensors and actuators on-chip, (3) Mature, batch-fabrication processes borrowed directly from the CMOS industry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Steel of the Micro-World" Analogy**
>
> Imagine building a city. In the macro-world, **steel** is the dominant material because it is strong, abundant, and its properties are well understood. In the micro-world (devices on a chip), **silicon** plays exactly the same role. Just as skyscrapers are built with steel I-beams, micro-sensors are built with silicon micro-beams. The remarkable fact is that a silicon micro-cantilever (a tiny diving-board like structure) can be **stronger than steel per unit weight**, while also being able to *sense* mechanical deformation electrically — something ordinary steel cannot do.

### 1.3 Why Silicon? — The Key Driving Factors

The selection of silicon for MEMS applications is justified by the following properties:

| Property | Value (Single Crystal Si) | Significance in MEMS |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (E) | **130 – 190 GPa** | Comparable to steel (~200 GPa) |
| Density ($\rho$) | **2.33 g/cm³** | Lighter than aluminium (2.70 g/cm³) |
| Yield Strength | **~7 GPa** | Higher than stainless steel |
| Thermal Conductivity | **150 W/m·K** | Excellent heat dissipation |
| Melting Point | **1414 °C** | High thermal stability |
| Bandgap | **1.12 eV** | Ideal semiconductor for ICs |
| Native Oxide (SiO₂) | High quality insulator | Built-in insulation layer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Atomic number of Si: **Z = 14**
> - Atomic mass of Si: **28.0855 g/mol**
> - Lattice constant of Si (diamond cubic): **a = 5.431 Å = 0.5431 nm**
> - Density of Si: **2.33 g/cm³**
> - Bandgap energy of Si at 300 K: **E_g = 1.12 eV**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crystal Structure of Silicon

Silicon crystallizes in the **Diamond Cubic (DC) crystal structure**. This structure can be visualized as two inter-penetrating Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) lattices, displaced along the body diagonal by **a/4** (where `a` is the lattice constant).

**Step-by-step logic of the diamond structure:**

- **Step 1:** Begin with an FCC unit cell (8 corner atoms + 6 face-centered atoms = 14 atoms per conventional cell).
- **Step 2:** Add 4 additional atoms positioned at **(1/4, 1/4, 1/4)**, **(3/4, 3/4, 1/4)**, **(3/4, 1/4, 3/4)**, and **(1/4, 3/4, 3/4)**.
- **Step 3:** Each atom in this structure is **covalently bonded to 4 nearest neighbours** in a perfect tetrahedral arrangement (sp³ hybridization).
- **Step 4:** Total atoms per unit cell = **8 atoms** (FCC: 8×1/8 + 6×1/2 = 4 atoms; Tetrahedral sites: 4 atoms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of Diamond Cubic = 0.34** — This is much lower than FCC (0.74) and explains the relatively low density of silicon despite its strong covalent bonding.

### 2.2 Miller Indices for Crystallographic Planes

Miller indices `(h k l)` are the **reciprocals of the intercepts** made by a plane on the crystallographic axes, reduced to the smallest integers.

**Logic for calculating Miller indices:**

- **Step 1:** Identify the intercepts of the plane on the **x**, **y**, and **z** axes (in units of lattice constant `a`).
- **Step 2:** Take the **reciprocals** of these intercepts.
- **Step 3:** Multiply by the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** to clear fractions and obtain the smallest integer set.
- **Step 4:** Enclose in parentheses `(h k l)`. Negative values are written with an overbar (e.g., $\bar{1}$).

**Common Silicon Planes in MEMS:**

| Miller Index | Plane Name | Inter-Atomic Spacing | Etch Rate in KOH | Use in MEMS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (100) | Primary wafer surface | $d = a / \sqrt{2}$ | Slow | Standard MEMS wafer |
| (110) | Diagonal plane | $d = a / \sqrt{4}$ | Medium | Vertical sidewalls |
| (111) | Close-packed | $d = a / \sqrt{3}$ | Very Slow | Etch-stop plane |

### 2.3 Doping of Silicon

Doping is the controlled introduction of impurity atoms (Group III or Group V) into the silicon lattice to modulate its electrical properties.

- **n-type doping:** Add Group V elements (P, As, Sb) — provides free electrons as majority carriers.
- **p-type doping:** Add Group III elements (B, Ga, In) — provides free holes as majority carriers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration of Si at 300 K:** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$
> This is the equilibrium density of electron-hole pairs in pure (undoped) silicon.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ | Mass-action law (carrier product) | cm⁻⁶ |
| 2 | $N_D = $ doping concentration | Donors in n-type | cm⁻³ |
| 3 | $N_A = $ doping concentration | Acceptors in p-type | cm⁻³ |
| 4 | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)}$ | Resistivity of doped Si | Ω·cm |
| 5 | $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ | Conductivity | S/cm |
| 6 | $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C | Electron charge | C |
| 7 | $\mu_n \approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ | Electron mobility in Si | cm²/V·s |
| 8 | $\mu_p \approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ | Hole mobility in Si | cm²/V·s |
| 9 | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$ | Inter-planar spacing | nm or Å |
| 10 | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{3}}{16} \approx 0.34$ | Atomic packing factor (diamond) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\Delta R / R = \pi_l \cdot \sigma$ | Piezoresistive effect | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Stress} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Mechanical stress | Pa or N/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The piezoresistive effect (formula 11) is the operating principle behind **MEMS pressure sensors** and **accelerometers**. When silicon is mechanically strained, its electrical resistance changes — this change is detected and converted into a measurable voltage signal. This is why silicon is uniquely suited for *integrated* MEMS devices: the same crystal acts as both the structural and the sensing element.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **MEMS Pressure Sensors** (e.g., in automotive TPMS, biomedical catheters)
- **MEMS Accelerometers** (smartphones, airbag deployment systems)
- **Inkjet Printer Nozzles** (thermal/bubble jet heads etched in Si)
- **Gyroscopes** (angular rate sensors in drones, gaming consoles)
- **RF MEMS Switches** (high-frequency communication)
- **Lab-on-a-Chip** (microfluidic channels for medical diagnostics)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Inter-Planar Spacing for a Cubic Lattice

The general formula for the inter-planar spacing $d_{hkl}$ between adjacent `(h k l)` planes in a **cubic** crystal system is:

$$
d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}
$$

**Derivation Steps:**

- **Step 1:** Consider a cubic unit cell with lattice constant $a$. The plane `(h k l)` makes intercepts $a/h$, $a/k$, $a/l$ on the three axes.
- **Step 2:** Construct a normal vector from the origin perpendicular to this plane. The direction cosines of this normal are proportional to $h$, $k$, $l$.
- **Step 3:** The perpendicular distance from the origin to the plane `(h k l)` is computed using the plane equation:

$$
\frac{x}{a/h} + \frac{y}{a/k} + \frac{z}{a/l} = 1
$$

- **Step 4:** The perpendicular distance `d` from the origin to a plane with equation $hx + ky + lz = a$ is given by the standard 3-D distance formula:

$$
d = \frac{\vert hx_0 + ky_0 + lz_0 - a \vert}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}
$$

- **Step 5:** Substituting $(x_0, y_0, z_0) = (0, 0, 0)$ (origin) gives:

$$
d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}
$$

**Numerical Verification for Si (a = 0.5431 nm):**

$$
d_{100} = \frac{0.5431}{\sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2}} = 0.5431 \text{ nm}
$$

$$
d_{110} = \frac{0.5431}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2}} = \frac{0.5431}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.3840 \text{ nm}
$$

$$
d_{111} = \frac{0.5431}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2}} = \frac{0.5431}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.3135 \text{ nm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $d_{100} > d_{110} > d_{111}$. The (111) plane has the highest atomic density and the **smallest inter-planar spacing**, which is why it is the slowest etching plane in anisotropic etchants like KOH.

### 3.2 Derivation: Atomic Packing Factor of Diamond Cubic

The APF is defined as the ratio of the volume occupied by atoms to the total volume of the unit cell.

**Step 1 — Number of atoms per unit cell:**

$$
N = \underbrace{8 \times \frac{1}{8}}_{\text{corners}} + \underbrace{6 \times \frac{1}{2}}_{\text{faces}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{tetrahedral sites}} = 1 + 3 + 4 = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of one silicon atom** (treating Si as a hard sphere with radius $r$):

$$
V_{\text{atom}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3
$$

**Step 3 — Relationship between $r$ and $a$ in the diamond structure:**

In the diamond structure, the atoms touch along the **1/4 body diagonal** direction. The length of this 1/4 body diagonal equals $2r$:

$$
2r = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot a}{4}
$$

Solving for $r$:

$$
r = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot a}{8}
$$

**Step 4 — Total volume of atoms in unit cell:**

$$
V_{\text{atoms}} = 8 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{32}{3} \pi \left( \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot a}{8} \right)^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{32}{3} \pi \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{3} \cdot a^3}{512} = \frac{32 \cdot 3\sqrt{3} \cdot \pi \cdot a^3}{3 \cdot 512} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \pi a^3}{16}
$$

**Step 5 — Volume of unit cell:**

$$
V_{\text{cell}} = a^3
$$

**Step 6 — APF:**

$$
\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{16} \approx 0.3401
$$

This confirms the diamond cubic APF is approximately **34%**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Carrier Concentration in Doped Silicon

For an **n-type** silicon doped with donor concentration $N_D$ (assuming $N_D \gg n_i$):

**Step 1 — Charge neutrality condition:**

$$
n_0 = N_D^+ + p_0
$$

**Step 2 — At room temperature, all donors are ionized** ($N_D^+ \approx N_D$):

$$
n_0 \approx N_D
$$

**Step 3 — Apply mass-action law:**

$$
p_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{n_0} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $N_D = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$:

$$
n_0 = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

$$
p_0 = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}{10^{16}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{20}}{10^{16}} = 2.25 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the minority carrier (hole) concentration is **12 orders of magnitude smaller** than the majority carrier (electron) concentration. This extreme ratio is the foundation of all silicon-based diodes, transistors, and piezoresistive MEMS sensors.

### 3.4 Derivation: Piezoresistive Sensitivity

The **gauge factor (G)** of a piezoresistor is defined as:

$$
G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the applied longitudinal strain.

For a uniformly doped resistor with length $L$, cross-section $A$, and resistivity $\rho$:

$$
R = \frac{\rho L}{A}
$$

**Step 1 — Logarithmic differentiation:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Poisson's ratio $\nu$** for the cross-sectional contraction:

$$
\frac{\Delta A}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L} = -2\nu \varepsilon
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon
$$

**Step 4 — For silicon, the dominant term is $\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$** (piezoresistive effect dominates over dimensional changes):

$$
\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \cdot \sigma
$$

where $\pi_l$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient and $\sigma$ is the stress.

**Step 5 — Final gauge factor for silicon:**

$$
G \approx 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l \cdot E
$$

For p-type Si (lightly doped), $G$ can be as high as **~100 to 200**, which is **50–100 times** that of metal foil strain gauges ($G \approx 2$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diamond Cubic Unit Cell of Silicon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diamond Cubic Unit Cell of Silicon]:::topNode --> B[8 Corner Atoms]
    A --> C[6 Face Centered Atoms]
    A --> D[4 Tetrahedral Interior Atoms]
    A --> E[Bonding Geometry]
    B --> B1[Each corner shared by 8 cells]
    C --> C1[Each face shared by 2 cells]
    D --> D1[Each belongs fully to 1 cell]
    E --> E1[sp3 Covalent Hybridization]
    E --> E2[Bond Angle 109.5 deg]
    E --> E3[Bond Length 0.235 nm]
    E --> E4[Coordination Number 4]
    A --> F[Key Parameters]
    F --> F1[Lattice Constant a = 5.431 A]
    F --> F2[Atoms per cell = 8]
    F --> F3[APF = 0.34]
    F --> F4[Density 2.33 g per cm3]
    classDef topNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Wafer Manufacturing and Crystallographic Orientation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Purified Polysilicon Source]:::stage1 --> B[Czochralski Crystal Pulling]
    B --> C[Single Crystal Ingot Growth]
    C --> D[Ingot Slicing into Wafers]
    D --> E[Wafer Lapping and Polishing]
    E --> F[Wafer Cleaning]
    F --> G[Wafer Inspection]
    G --> H1[100 Wafer for MEMS]
    G --> H2[110 Wafer for Vertical Structures]
    G --> H3[111 Wafer for Etch Stop Layers]
    classDef stage1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Silicon Doping Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pure Intrinsic Silicon]:::neutral --> B[Doping Decision]
    B -->|Add Group V| C[Phosphorus or Arsenic]
    B -->|Add Group III| D[Boron or Gallium]
    C --> E[Donor Atoms Replace Si Sites]
    D --> F[Acceptor Atoms Replace Si Sites]
    E --> G[Extra Valence Electron Becomes Free]
    G --> H[n Type Silicon Majority Electrons]
    F --> I[Hole Created in Covalent Bond]
    I --> J[p Type Silicon Majority Holes]
    H --> K[Used in MEMS Piezoresistors Strain Gauges]
    J --> L[Used in MEMS Piezoresistors Pressure Sensors]
    classDef neutral fill:#37474f,stroke:#263238,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a MEMS Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Input Stage]
        A1[Applied External Pressure]
    end
    subgraph MEM[MEMS Diaphragm]
        B1[Silicon Diaphragm 100 Plane]
        B2[Boron Doped Piezoresistors on Diaphragm]
    end
    subgraph ELE[Electrical Transduction]
        C1[Wheatstone Bridge Configuration]
        C2[Differential Voltage Output]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Stage]
        D1[Amplifier and Signal Conditioning]
        D2[Calibrated Pressure Reading]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The above block diagram maps the *functional interactions* of a silicon-based piezoresistive pressure sensor. In a real device, an external pressure deflects the silicon diaphragm, which strains the embedded piezoresistors, changing their resistance in a balanced Wheatstone bridge and producing a measurable voltage output.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any three mechanical properties of silicon that make it suitable for MEMS applications.**

**Model Answer:**
The three key mechanical properties of silicon that make it suitable for MEMS are:
1. **High Young's Modulus (E ≈ 130–190 GPa):** Comparable to steel, allowing silicon micro-structures to bear significant mechanical loads without permanent deformation.
2. **High Yield Strength (≈ 7 GPa):** Greater than that of stainless steel, ensuring high reliability and a long fatigue life under cyclic loading in MEMS devices.
3. **Low Density (2.33 g/cm³):** Significantly lower than most metals, making silicon ideal for low-mass, high-acceleration applications such as MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes.
4. *(Bonus)* Low mechanical hysteresis and high elasticity, ensuring repeatable mechanical response.

*(3 marks – 1 mark per property with supporting value)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic silicon with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Intrinsic Silicon | Extrinsic Silicon |
|---|---|---|
| Purity | Extremely pure (undoped) | Deliberately doped with impurities |
| Carrier concentration | $n = p = n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³ | Majority carrier >> minority carrier |
| Conductivity | Very low at 300 K | Tunable (higher by orders of magnitude) |
| Example | Pure Si crystal from Czochralski process | Si doped with Phosphorus (n-type) or Boron (p-type) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to state the **type of dopant** for extrinsic silicon. Always specify whether it is n-type (Group V — P, As, Sb) or p-type (Group III — B, Ga, In) for full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the diamond cubic crystal structure of silicon with a neat diagram. Calculate the atomic packing factor (APF) and the density of silicon. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of Miller indices in MEMS fabrication. Calculate the inter-planar spacing for (100), (110), and (111) planes in silicon. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diamond Cubic Structure and APF:**

The diamond cubic structure of silicon consists of two inter-penetrating FCC sublattices displaced by a/4 along the body diagonal. Each silicon atom is covalently bonded to **4 nearest neighbours** in a tetrahedral arrangement with a bond angle of **109.5°** (sp³ hybridization).

**Number of atoms per unit cell:**

$$
N = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} + 6 \times \frac{1}{2} + 4 = 8 \text{ atoms}
$$

**Atomic radius relation** (atoms touch along 1/4 of body diagonal):

$$
2r = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot a}{4} \Rightarrow r = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot a}{8}
$$

**APF calculation:**

$$
\text{APF} = \frac{8 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3}{a^3} = \frac{8 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{8}\right)^3}{a^3} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{16} \approx 0.34
$$

**Density calculation:**

$$
\rho = \frac{N \cdot M}{N_A \cdot a^3} = \frac{8 \times 28.0855}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times (5.431 \times 10^{-8})^3}
$$

$$
\rho = \frac{224.68}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-22}} = \frac{224.68}{96.49} \approx 2.33 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**(b) Miller Indices and Inter-Planar Spacing:**

Miller indices `(h k l)` are a notation system to identify crystallographic planes by the **reciprocals of their intercepts** on the three crystallographic axes. They are essential in MEMS for:

- **Wafer orientation selection** — Standard MEMS wafers are (100) oriented.
- **Anisotropic etching design** — The (111) plane acts as an etch stop in KOH.
- **Predicting sidewall angles** — Etched cavities in (100) Si produce 54.74° sidewalls (angle between (100) and (111) planes).

**Inter-planar spacing calculation** (a = 0.5431 nm):

$$
d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}
$$

For (100):

$$
d_{100} = \frac{0.5431}{\sqrt{1}} = 0.5431 \text{ nm}
$$

For (110):

$$
d_{110} = \frac{0.5431}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.3840 \text{ nm}
$$

For (111):

$$
d_{111} = \frac{0.5431}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.3135 \text{ nm}
$$

**Inferred conclusion:** Smaller $d_{hkl}$ ⟹ higher atomic density ⟹ slower etching rate in KOH. Hence (111) is the **natural etch-stop** plane.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – Internal Choice Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** What is doping? Explain n-type and p-type doping in silicon with energy band diagrams. (7 marks)

**(b)** A silicon sample is doped with phosphorus at $N_D = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻³. Calculate the electron and hole concentrations and the resistivity at 300 K. Given $\mu_n = 1350$ cm²/V·s and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Doping of Silicon:**

**Doping** is the process of intentionally introducing impurity atoms (dopants) into the silicon crystal lattice to modify its electrical conductivity.

- **n-type doping:** Add Group V elements (P, As, Sb). Each donor atom has **5 valence electrons** — 4 form covalent bonds with neighbouring Si atoms, and the 5th is loosely bound. At 300 K, this extra electron is thermally excited into the conduction band, leaving behind a positively ionized donor ($D^+$).
- **p-type doping:** Add Group III elements (B, Ga, In). Each acceptor atom has **3 valence electrons** — 3 covalent bonds are completed, but the 4th bond is missing one electron, creating a **hole**. The hole is thermally excited into the valence band, leaving behind a negatively ionized acceptor ($A^-$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy band interpretation:** In n-type Si, the donor level $E_D$ lies just below the conduction band edge $E_C$ (≈ 0.045 eV below for P). In p-type Si, the acceptor level $E_A$ lies just above the valence band edge $E_V$ (≈ 0.045 eV above for B).

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Electron concentration (majority):**

$$
n_0 \approx N_D = 5 \times 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Hole concentration (minority) using mass-action law:**

$$
p_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{n_0} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}{5 \times 10^{15}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{20}}{5 \times 10^{15}} = 4.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Resistivity (p-type correction ignored since $n_0 \gg p_0$):**

$$
\rho = \frac{1}{q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)} \approx \frac{1}{q \cdot n_0 \cdot \mu_n}
$$

$$
\rho = \frac{1}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (5 \times 10^{15}) \times 1350}
$$

$$
\rho = \frac{1}{1.081 \times 10^{0}} = \frac{1}{1.081} \approx 0.925 \text{ Ω·cm}
$$

**Final Result:** $\rho \approx 0.93$ Ω·cm

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C explicitly** — students often skip this and lose a mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $\mu_n$ and $\mu_p$** — for n-type use electron mobility, for p-type use hole mobility.
> 3. **Wrong units in final resistivity** — must state Ω·cm (not Ω/cm or Ω-m).
> 4. **Neglecting minority carrier term in conductivity** — usually valid since $n_0 \gg p_0$, but the justification must be stated.
> 5. **Not drawing the energy band diagram** in doping questions — for 7-mark questions, a labelled band diagram is **mandatory**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Revision Points for "Materials for MEMS: Silicon":**

- **Silicon is a Group IV semiconductor** with diamond cubic structure, $a = 5.431$ Å, and 8 atoms per unit cell.
- **APF of Si = 0.34** (low — explained by directional covalent bonding).
- **Density of Si = 2.33 g/cm³**; **Young's Modulus ≈ 130–190 GPa**; **Yield Strength ≈ 7 GPa**.
- **Standard MEMS wafer orientation = (100)**; **Etch-stop plane = (111)** in KOH anisotropic etching.
- **Miller indices** = reciprocals of intercepts; **$d_{hkl} = a / \sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$** for cubic systems.
- **Doping types:** n-type (Group V: P, As, Sb) and p-type (Group III: B, Ga, In).
- **Mass-action law:** $n \cdot p = n_i^2$, where $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³ at 300 K.
- **Carrier mobilities:** $\mu_n \approx 1350$ cm²/V·s, $\mu_p \approx 480$ cm²/V·s.
- **Resistivity formula:** $\rho = 1 / [q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)]$.
- **Piezoresistive gauge factor of Si can exceed 100** — 50–100× higher than metallic strain gauges.
- **SiO₂ is a high-quality native insulator** that grows thermally and serves as a masking/insulation layer.
- **Diamond structure** can be visualized as **2 interpenetrating FCC lattices displaced by a/4 along the body diagonal**.
- **Bond angle in Si = 109.5°** (perfect tetrahedral), **bond length ≈ 0.235 nm**.
- **Atomic coordination number of Si = 4** (each atom bonded to 4 nearest neighbours).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymers and Metals Used in MEMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymers and Metals Used in MEMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Materials in MEMS Context

In Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS), **structural and functional materials** form the foundational building blocks that determine the mechanical, electrical, thermal, and chemical behavior of micro-scale devices. Among the broad material spectrum, **polymers** and **metals** occupy two of the most critical categories, alongside semiconductors and ceramics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEPET522, Module 1)**
> "Polymers and Metals Used in MEMS" refers to the study of material properties, deposition techniques, and application-specific selection criteria of polymeric and metallic substances that are employed as structural, sacrificial, conductive, or biocompatible layers in the fabrication and packaging of MEMS devices.

**Polymers in MEMS** are large-molecular-weight organic compounds composed of repeating monomer units. In MEMS, they are primarily used as:
- Structural materials (e.g., SU-8, polyimide)
- Sacrificial layers (e.g., photoresist, PMMA)
- Packaging and encapsulating materials (e.g., parylene, PDMS)

**Metals in MEMS** are crystalline inorganic substances exhibiting metallic bonding, free electron conduction, and high mechanical strength. They are predominantly used as:
- Electrical interconnects and electrodes (Au, Al, Cu, Pt)
- Structural layers (Ni, W, permalloy)
- Adhesion and barrier layers (Ti, Cr)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The LEGO Analogy for MEMS Materials**
> Imagine building a tiny robotic insect. You need three types of blocks:
> 1. **Polymers = The Soft, Moldable Clay** — They can be shaped easily, bent, stretched, and are cheap. Like clay, they can be cast into complex shapes (microfluidic channels, flexible membranes).
> 2. **Metals = The Steel Frame of a Skyscraper** — They are rigid, strong, and conduct electricity. Like the steel beams in a building, metals form the load-bearing skeleton and electrical wiring of a MEMS device.
> 3. **Silicon (semiconductor) = The Brain** — It processes signals and senses the environment.

A typical MEMS accelerometer, for example, uses a **silicon proof mass** (brain + skeleton), **gold electrodes** (electrical wiring), **SU-8 polymer** (structural spacer and packaging), and **aluminum bonding pads** (external connection terminals).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Material Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Material Metrics in MEMS Design**
> - **Young's Modulus (E)**: Measured in **GPa**, indicates stiffness.
> - **Residual Stress (σ_r)**: Measured in **MPa**, can cause buckling/delamination.
> - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)**: Measured in **ppm/°C**, critical for thermal matching.
> - **Electrical Resistivity (ρ)**: Measured in **µΩ·cm**, governs conductor selection.
> - **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g)**: The temperature above which a polymer transitions from rigid to rubbery state.

### 1.4 Course Outcome Alignment

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO1**: "Understand the fundamental materials, properties, and scaling laws relevant to MEMS fabrication."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Polymers Used in MEMS — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 SU-8 (Negative Photoresist)
- **Composition**: An epoxy-based, near-UV (i-line, 365 nm) photoresist.
- **Why Used**: SU-8 is the **workhorse polymer** for high-aspect-ratio (HAR) MEMS structures due to its ability to form vertical sidewalls with aspect ratios exceeding **20:1**.
- **Mechanism**: Upon UV exposure, SU-8 cross-links (cationic polymerization) and becomes insoluble in the developer. The unexposed regions are washed away.
- **Properties**:
  - Young's Modulus: **2–4 GPa** (cured)
  - Glass Transition Temperature: **>200 °C**
  - Chemical resistance: Excellent to most acids and solvents
  - Optical transparency in the visible range
- **Applications**: Microfluidic channels, optical waveguides, micro-gear fabrication, bio-MEMS scaffolds.

#### 2.1.2 PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)
- **Composition**: A silicone-based elastomer with repeating –Si(CH₃)₂–O– units.
- **Why Used**: It is the **gold standard for soft lithography** and rapid prototyping of biological/medical MEMS.
- **Mechanism**: A two-part system (pre-polymer + curing agent) mixed typically in a 10:1 ratio and thermally cured at ~65 °C.
- **Properties**:
  - Young's Modulus: **~750 kPa** (highly elastic)
  - Elongation at break: **>100%**
  - Biocompatible and gas-permeable
  - Hydrophobic (can be plasma-treated for hydrophilicity)
- **Applications**: Lab-on-a-chip, organ-on-chip, flexible electronics, microvalves.

#### 2.1.3 PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)
- **Composition**: A transparent thermoplastic acrylic polymer.
- **Why Used**: Excellent optical clarity and easy processing via hot embossing and injection molding.
- **Properties**:
  - Young's Modulus: **3–3.5 GPa**
  - Glass Transition Temperature: **~105 °C**
  - Transmission: **>92%** in visible spectrum
  - Soluble in acetone, IPA (easy sacrificial release)
- **Applications**: Optical MEMS, sacrificial layers, microfluidic chips via laser ablation and embossing.

#### 2.1.4 Polyimide (PI)
- **Composition**: A polymer of imide monomers (aromatic heterocyclic).
- **Why Used**: High thermal stability, mechanical toughness, and chemical inertness.
- **Properties**:
  - Young's Modulus: **2.5–8.5 GPa**
  - Glass Transition Temperature: **>400 °C** (depending on type)
  - Excellent dielectric strength
  - Low outgassing
- **Applications**: Flexible substrates for electronics, dielectric layers, stress-buffering in packaging.

#### 2.1.5 Parylene C
- **Composition**: A poly(p-xylylene) polymer deposited via chemical vapor deposition (CVD).
- **Why Used**: Provides **conformal, pinhole-free coatings** with uniform thickness even on high-aspect-ratio structures.
- **Properties**:
  - Young's Modulus: **~2.8 GPa**
  - USP Class VI biocompatibility certified
  - Dielectric strength: **~220 V/µm**
- **Applications**: Encapsulation of implantable bio-MEMS, moisture barriers, stiction-reduction coatings.

### 2.2 Metals Used in MEMS — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Gold (Au)
- **Why Used**: The most chemically inert metal; essential where corrosion resistance and biocompatibility are required.
- **Properties**:
  - Resistivity: **2.2 µΩ·cm** (one of the lowest)
  - Young's Modulus: **78 GPa**
  - Melting Point: **1064 °C**
  - Excellent for wire bonding and electrical contacts
- **Limitation**: Poor adhesion to oxides — always requires a **Ti or Cr adhesion layer** (~5–20 nm).
- **Applications**: Bio-MEMS electrodes, RF MEMS switches, plasmonic sensors.

#### 2.2.2 Aluminum (Al)
- **Why Used**: Historically the primary interconnect metal in semiconductor industry, easily evaporated or sputtered.
- **Properties**:
  - Resistivity: **2.65 µΩ·cm**
  - Young's Modulus: **70 GPa**
  - Low density: **2.7 g/cm³**
  - Native oxide (Al₂O₃) is self-passivating
- **Applications**: Bonding pads, acoustic transducers (piezo-MEMS), RF MEMS.

#### 2.2.3 Copper (Cu)
- **Why Used**: Lowest resistivity among practical MEMS metals, second only to silver.
- **Properties**:
  - Resistivity: **1.7 µΩ·cm** (lowest practical choice)
  - Young's Modulus: **110–130 GPa**
  - High electromigration resistance (vs. Al)
- **Limitation**: Diffuses rapidly into silicon — **requires a Ta or TaN barrier layer**.
- **Applications**: High-current inductors, micro-coils, RF MEMS.

#### 2.2.4 Titanium (Ti)
- **Why Used**: An **adhesion promoter** between metals and oxide/silicon substrates.
- **Properties**:
  - Resistivity: **42 µΩ·cm** (high — never used as conductor)
  - Young's Modulus: **110 GPa**
  - Reactive — forms strong Ti–O bonds with substrates
- **Applications**: Adhesion layer, biocompatible coatings, getter materials.

#### 2.2.5 Platinum (Pt)
- **Why Used**: Superior catalytic, electrochemical, and biocompatible properties.
- **Properties**:
  - Resistivity: **10.6 µΩ·cm**
  - Young's Modulus: **168 GPa**
  - High density: **21.45 g/cm³**
  - Excellent corrosion resistance
- **Applications**: Neural stimulation electrodes, electrochemical biosensors, gas sensors.

#### 2.2.6 Nickel (Ni) and Permalloy (Ni-Fe)
- **Why Used**: Magnetic properties for magnetically-actuated MEMS.
- **Properties**:
  - Permalloy (80% Ni, 20% Fe): High magnetic permeability, low coercivity
  - Young's Modulus: **~200 GPa**
- **Applications**: Magnetic microactuators, MEMS hard drives, magnetic sensors.

#### 2.2.7 Tungsten (W)
- **Why Used**: Highest melting point of all metals; used in high-temperature and high-stress applications.
- **Properties**:
  - Melting Point: **3422 °C** (highest)
  - Young's Modulus: **411 GPa** (near diamond-like stiffness)
  - Resistivity: **5.6 µΩ·cm**
  - CTE: **4.5 ppm/°C** (low, matches silicon)
- **Applications**: X-ray masks, high-temperature sensors, inertial mass.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for Polymer & Metal Analysis in MEMS**

| **Formula / Relation** | **Mathematical Form** | **Description** | **Units** |
|------------------------|----------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Young's Modulus definition | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Ratio of stress to strain in elastic regime | GPa |
| Stress (axial) | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Force per unit cross-sectional area | MPa |
| Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Ratio of elongation to original length | Dimensionless |
| Residual stress (biaxial) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{E}{(1-\nu)} \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal mismatch stress between film & substrate | MPa |
| CTE mismatch stress | $\sigma_{CTE} = E_f (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \Delta T$ | Stress due to CTE difference | MPa |
| Beam stiffness (cantilever) | $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | Spring constant of a cantilever beam | N/m |
| Beam stiffness (fixed-fixed) | $k = \dfrac{16 E w t^3}{L^3}$ | Spring constant of a fixed-fixed beam | N/m |
| Resonant frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}}$ | Natural frequency of MEMS resonator | Hz |
| Electrical resistance | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | Resistance of a metal trace | Ω |
| Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance between MEMS electrodes | F |
| Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ | Sharpness of resonance | Dimensionless |
| Stiction force | $F_{st} = \dfrac{12 \pi \gamma A \cos\theta}{D^2}$ | Capillary/adhesion force during release | N |
| Skin depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r}}$ | EM penetration depth in metal | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: The residual stress formula is critical in **Surface Micromachining**, where mismatched CTE between deposited films and the silicon substrate causes **buckling, curling, or cracking** of released structures.

### 2.4 Comparative Material Properties — Quick Reference

> [!TIP]
> **Density & Stiffness Trade-off**
> When selecting a material for inertial sensors (accelerometers, gyroscopes), high **density** + high **Young's Modulus** is desirable. Tungsten is preferred over aluminum for proof masses due to its higher density (19.3 vs. 2.7 g/cm³), giving greater mass in the same volume.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Residual Stress in a Deposited Film

A thin metal or polymer film is deposited onto a thick silicon substrate at deposition temperature T_d. When cooled to operating temperature T₀, thermal mismatch generates residual stress.

**Given:**
- Film thickness: t_f
- Substrate thickness: t_s (with t_s >> t_f, assumed rigid)
- Young's modulus of film: E_f
- Poisson's ratio of film: ν_f
- CTE of film: α_f
- CTE of substrate: α_s
- Temperature drop: ΔT = T_d − T₀

**Step 1:** Express the free thermal strain that would develop in the film if it were unconstrained:
$$
\varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T
$$
*Explanation:* The film wants to contract by (α_f · ΔT) but the substrate contracts by (α_s · ΔT). The relative difference creates strain.

**Step 2:** Since the substrate is much thicker and stiffer, the constraint is biaxial. The biaxial modulus M_f is:
$$
M_f = \dfrac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}
$$
*Explanation:* In biaxial stress state, the effective modulus is enhanced by the (1 − ν) factor because lateral contraction is suppressed.

**Step 3:** The residual biaxial stress is the biaxial modulus times the constrained strain:
$$
\sigma_r = M_f \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = \dfrac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \cdot (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T
$$
*Explanation:* Positive σ_r indicates tensile stress (film tries to contract but is held back by substrate).

**Step 4 (Numerical Example):** A 1 µm gold film deposited on silicon at 200 °C and cooled to 25 °C.
- E_Au = 78 GPa
- ν_Au = 0.44
- α_Au = 14.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- α_Si = 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- ΔT = 175 °C

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_f &= \dfrac{78}{1 - 0.44} = 139.29 \text{ GPa} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{th} &= (14.2 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 175 = 2.03 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
\sigma_r &= 139.29 \times 10^9 \times 2.03 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
\sigma_r &= 2.83 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 283 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A residual tensile stress of 283 MPa is high and can cause film cracking. This is why **adhesion layers (Ti) and stress-relief designs** are critical in MEMS layouts.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stiffness and Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever Beam

A cantilever beam made of SU-8 polymer is used as a resonator.

**Given:**
- Length L, width w, thickness t
- Material: SU-8 with E = 4 GPa, density ρ = 1200 kg/m³

**Step 1:** Spring constant of a rectangular cantilever:
$$
k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}
$$
*Explanation:* Derives from Euler-Bernoulli beam theory where deflection δ = 4FL³/(Ewt³); thus k = F/δ.

**Step 2:** Effective mass of a cantilever (lumped mass model):
$$
m_{eff} = 0.24 \cdot \rho \cdot w \cdot t \cdot L
$$
*Explanation:* Only ~24% of the cantilever's mass contributes to the fundamental mode; the rest cancels out in modal analysis.

**Step 3:** Fundamental resonant frequency:
$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}}
$$
*Explanation:* Standard mass-spring oscillator formula.

**Step 4 (Numerical Example):** For L = 200 µm, w = 50 µm, t = 10 µm:
$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \dfrac{4 \times 10^9 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (10 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^3} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times 10^9 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-15}}{4 \times 8 \times 10^{-12}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-10}}{3.2 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.25 \text{ N/m} \\[4pt]
m_{eff} &= 0.24 \times 1200 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 0.24 \times 1200 \times 10^{-14} = 2.88 \times 10^{-11} \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
f_0 &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{6.25}{2.88 \times 10^{-11}}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{2.17 \times 10^{11}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \times 4.66 \times 10^5 = 74.2 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world meaning:** This 74 kHz cantilever is suitable for use as a **biochemical mass sensor** in the kHz range, where biomolecule binding on its surface shifts the resonant frequency by detectable amounts.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrical Resistance of a Metal Trace in RF MEMS

A 100 µm wide, 5 mm long, 1 µm thick gold interconnect carries signal current in an RF MEMS switch.

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area:
$$
A = w \cdot t = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Resistance of trace:
$$
R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A} = \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-10}} = 1.1 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3:** At 1 GHz, the skin depth in gold is:
$$
\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu_0}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-8}}{2\pi \times 10^9 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}} \approx 2.4 \text{ µm}
$$
Since the thickness (1 µm) < skin depth, the current flows through the entire cross-section — no skin effect loss.

### 3.4 Analytical Material Selection Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Selection Decision Matrix** (Score 1–5; 5 = Excellent fit)

| **Property** | **Au** | **Al** | **Cu** | **Ti** | **Pt** | **SU-8** | **PDMS** | **Polyimide** |
|--------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|----------|----------|---------------|
| Electrical Conductivity | 5 | 4 | 5 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Biocompatibility | 5 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 5 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Adhesion to Substrate | 1 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| Optical Transparency | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Chemical Inertness | 5 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
| Elasticity / Flexibility | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 3 |
| High Aspect Ratio Patterning | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Cost (lower = better) | 1 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 1 | 3 | 3 | 3 |

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Matrix:** For a **bio-MEMS neural electrode**, prioritize Au/Pt (high biocompatibility, high conductivity). For a **lab-on-chip**, prioritize SU-8/PDMS (transparency, flexibility, easy fabrication).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Material Selection Workflow in MEMS Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define MEMS Application Requirements] --> B{Primary Function?}
    B -->|Electrical Interconnect| C[Select Conductive Metal]
    B -->|Structural Layer| D[Select Structural Material]
    B -->|Sacrificial Layer| E[Select Dissolvable Polymer]
    B -->|Bio-interface| F[Select Biocompatible Material]
    B -->|Optical Component| G[Select Transparent Material]

    C --> C1{Corrosion Resistance Required?}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[Gold Au or Platinum Pt]
    C1 -->|No| C3[Aluminum Al or Copper Cu]

    D --> D1{Needs High Aspect Ratio?}
    D1 -->|Yes| D2[SU-8 Photoresist]
    D1 -->|No| D3{High Temperature?}
    D3 -->|Yes| D4[Polyimide or Tungsten W]
    D3 -->|No| D5[PMMA or PDMS]

    E --> E1[Sacrificial Polymer: Photoresist or PMMA]

    F --> F1[Gold Au or Platinum Pt or PDMS]

    G --> G1[PMMA or PDMS or SU-8]

    C2 --> H[Apply Adhesion Layer: Ti or Cr]
    C3 --> H
    D2 --> I[Pattern via Photolithography]
    D4 --> I
    D5 --> I
    E1 --> J[Release via Wet or Dry Etching]
    F1 --> K[Validate Biocompatibility ISO 10993]
    G1 --> L[Optical Characterization]

    H --> M[Final Material Stack]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[KTU Design Review Checkpoint]
```

### 4.2 Layered Cross-Section of a Typical MEMS Device

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stack["Typical MEMS Layered Stack (Bottom to Top)"]
        direction TB
        L1["Silicon Substrate (Si, 500 µm) — Mechanical Base"] 
        L2["Thermal Oxide Layer (SiO2, 1 µm) — Electrical Isolation"]
        L3["Bottom Electrode (Ti/Au, 20 nm / 200 nm) — Adhesion + Conductor"]
        L4["Sacrificial Layer (Photoresist, 2 µm) — Spacer"]
        L5["Structural Polymer (SU-8, 10 µm) — Moving Membrane"]
        L6["Top Electrode (Ti/Al, 20 nm / 300 nm) — Sensing/Actuation"]
        L7["Encapsulation (Parylene C, 2 µm) — Protection"]

        L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7
    end
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Polymer-Based MEMS Fabrication (Soft Lithography with PDMS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Design SU-8 Master Mold on Silicon Wafer] --> B[Step 2: Spin-Coat SU-8 and Soft Bake]
    B --> C[Step 3: UV Exposure through Photomask]
    C --> D[Step 4: Post Exposure Bake and Develop]
    D --> E[Step 5: Hard Bake to Cure Master Mold]
    E --> F[Step 6: Pour PDMS Pre-polymer + Curing Agent 10:1]
    F --> G[Step 7: Degas in Vacuum Chamber]
    G --> H[Step 8: Cure at 65 C for 2 Hours]
    H --> I[Step 9: Peel PDMS Replica from Master]
    I --> J[Step 10: Plasma Bond to Glass Substrate]
    J --> K[Final: PDMS Microfluidic Device Ready]
```

### 4.4 Material Classification Hierarchy in MEMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[MEMS Materials] --> A[Structural]
    Root --> B[Functional]
    Root --> C[Sacrificial]
    Root --> D[Packaging]

    A --> A1[Polymers: SU-8, PMMA, Polyimide, PDMS]
    A --> A2[Metals: Ni, W, Ti, Permalloy]
    A --> A3[Ceramics: SiC, Si3N4]
    A --> A4[Semiconductors: Si, SiGe, GaAs]

    B --> B1[Conductors: Au, Al, Cu, Pt]
    B --> B2[Piezoelectrics: PZT, AlN, ZnO]
    B --> B3[Magnetic: Permalloy, CoNi]

    C --> C1[Polymers: Photoresist, PMMA]
    C --> C2[Oxides: SiO2]
    C --> C3[Metals: Cu, Al — etched selectively]

    D --> D1[Polymers: Parylene C, Epoxy, PDMS]
    D --> D2[Metals: Au, Ti — hermetic lids]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three polymers used in MEMS fabrication and state one key application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three polymers most commonly used in MEMS are:

1. **SU-8 (Negative Photoresist)** — Used for **high-aspect-ratio** structural elements such as microfluidic channels, micro-gears, and optical waveguides due to its ability to form vertical sidewalls with aspect ratios exceeding 20:1.

2. **PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)** — Used as the substrate for **lab-on-a-chip** devices and bio-MEMS. Its biocompatibility, optical transparency, and elasticity (Young's modulus ~750 kPa) make it ideal for organ-on-chip and flexible electronics.

3. **Polyimide** — Used as a **flexible substrate** in flexible electronics and as a dielectric/passivation layer in semiconductor packaging due to its high glass transition temperature (>400 °C) and excellent chemical stability.

*(1 mark per polymer + application = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a thin adhesion layer of titanium (Ti) typically deposited before gold (Au) in MEMS metallization schemes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gold exhibits **poor adhesion** to silicon, silicon dioxide, and most polymer substrates because of its chemical inertness and inability to form strong chemical bonds with these surfaces. Without an adhesion promoter, gold films would **peel, delaminate, or fail under thermal cycling and mechanical stress**.

Titanium is used as the adhesion layer because:
1. It forms **strong Ti–O covalent bonds** with oxide and silicon surfaces (high chemical reactivity).
2. It has a **Young's modulus (~110 GPa) and CTE (8.6 ppm/°C)** reasonably matched to both gold and silicon, reducing interfacial stress.
3. Only a thin layer (**5–20 nm**) is required, preserving the low overall resistivity of the metal stack.

*(1 mark for identifying the issue, 2 marks for the Ti-specific reasons = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – 2024 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **properties, fabrication routes, and applications** of **SU-8 and PDMS** as polymer materials in MEMS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **electrical, mechanical, and chemical properties** of **Gold, Aluminum, and Copper** as interconnect metals in MEMS. Justify when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — SU-8 and PDMS (7 marks)**

**SU-8:**
- **Composition and Chemistry:** SU-8 is an **epoxy-based negative photoresist** consisting of EPON SU-8 resin dissolved in an organic solvent (gamma-butyrolactone), with a triaryl sulfonium salt as the photoinitiator. Upon exposure to **near-UV (i-line, 365 nm)**, the photoinitiator generates a strong acid that catalyzes **cationic cross-linking** of the epoxy groups during the post-exposure bake.
- **Fabrication Route:** Spin-coating → Soft bake (95 °C) → UV exposure → Post-exposure bake (95 °C) → Development in SU-8 developer → Hard bake (200 °C) for final cure.
- **Key Properties:**
  - Young's Modulus: **2–4 GPa**
  - Glass Transition Temperature: **>200 °C**
  - Aspect ratio capability: **>20:1**
  - Chemical resistance to most acids/bases
  - Optical transparency in visible range
- **Applications:** Microfluidic channels, micro-gears, optical MEMS (waveguides, gratings), bio-MEMS scaffolds, packaging.

**PDMS:**
- **Composition:** PDMS is a **silicone elastomer** with repeating –Si(CH₃)₂–O– units. It is supplied as a two-part kit: a pre-polymer (vinyl-terminated base) and a curing agent (hydrosilane cross-linker with Pt catalyst).
- **Fabrication Route:** Soft lithography — mix base and curing agent (typically 10:1) → Degas → Pour over an SU-8 master mold → Cure (65 °C, 2 hr) → Peel off → Plasma bond to glass.
- **Key Properties:**
  - Young's Modulus: **~750 kPa** (highly elastic)
  - Elongation at break: **>100%**
  - Biocompatible (USP Class VI) and gas-permeable
  - Hydrophobic (becomes hydrophilic after O₂ plasma treatment)
- **Applications:** Lab-on-a-chip, organ-on-chip, flexible/stretchable electronics, microvalves, cell culture substrates.

**Part (b) — Au vs. Al vs. Cu (7 marks)**

| **Property** | **Au** | **Al** | **Cu** |
|--------------|--------|--------|--------|
| Resistivity (µΩ·cm) | 2.2 | 2.65 | 1.7 |
| CTE (ppm/°C) | 14.2 | 23.1 | 16.5 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 78 | 70 | 110 |
| Chemical Inertness | Excellent | Moderate (forms oxide) | Poor (oxidizes) |
| Adhesion to Si/SiO₂ | Poor (needs Ti) | Good | Poor (needs barrier) |
| Biocompatibility | Excellent | Poor | Poor |
| Cost | Very High | Low | Moderate |
| Electromigration | High | Moderate | Low (best) |

**Justification:**
- **Gold (Au)** is preferred for **biomedical electrodes, RF MEMS switches, and corrosion-resistant contacts** where chemical inertness and biocompatibility are non-negotiable, despite high cost.
- **Aluminum (Al)** is preferred for **bonding pads, acoustic/piezo-MEMS, and cost-sensitive consumer MEMS** due to its low cost, ease of deposition, and adequate conductivity.
- **Copper (Cu)** is preferred for **high-frequency inductors, micro-coils, and high-current interconnects** where the lowest possible resistivity is required; it must always be encapsulated by a Ta/TaN barrier to prevent Cu diffusion into Si.

---

**Question B. [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of metals in MEMS** with specific reference to **Tungsten (W), Platinum (Pt), and Permalloy (Ni-Fe)**. Discuss their unique properties and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **residual stress** in thin-film MEMS structures? Derive the expression for **thermal mismatch-induced residual stress** in a metal film deposited on a silicon substrate. A 0.5 µm thick Aluminum film is deposited on a Si substrate at 250 °C and cooled to 25 °C. Calculate the residual stress. Given: E_Al = 70 GPa, ν_Al = 0.35, α_Al = 23.1 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, α_Si = 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Roles of W, Pt, Permalloy (7 marks)**

1. **Tungsten (W):**
   - Highest melting point of any metal: **3422 °C**
   - Young's Modulus: **411 GPa** (very stiff)
   - CTE: **4.5 ppm/°C** — closely matches silicon (2.6 ppm/°C), making it ideal for **thermally stable MEMS**.
   - Applications: **X-ray masks, high-temperature pressure sensors, inertial proof masses, and micro-mirror hinges** where stiffness and dimensional stability are critical.

2. **Platinum (Pt):**
   - Superior **electrochemical and catalytic properties**; high chemical inertness.
   - Biocompatible, suitable for **chronic implants**.
   - Applications: **Neural stimulation electrodes, glucose sensors, gas sensors, and electrochemical biosensors**. Pt black (nanoporous Pt) is used to enhance surface area in sensing electrodes.

3. **Permalloy (Ni-Fe, 80:20):**
   - A **magnetic alloy** with high magnetic permeability, low coercivity, and near-zero magnetostriction.
   - Used as the **functional magnetic layer** in magnetically-actuated MEMS.
   - Applications: **Magnetic microactuators, integrated inductors, magnetic hard disk drive heads, and compass sensors**.

**Part (b) — Residual Stress Derivation and Calculation (7 marks)**

**Definition:** Residual stress is the internal stress that remains in a thin film after deposition and cooling, in the absence of any external load. It arises from intrinsic deposition effects, lattice mismatch, and **thermal expansion mismatch** between the film and the substrate.

**Derivation:** When a film is deposited at temperature T_d and cooled to T₀, the film attempts to contract by α_f·ΔT while the substrate contracts by α_s·ΔT. If the substrate is much thicker (t_s >> t_f), it constrains the film.

The biaxial modulus of the film is:
$$
M_f = \dfrac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}
$$

The constrained thermal strain is:
$$
\varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \Delta T
$$

The biaxial residual stress is:
$$
\sigma_r = M_f \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = \dfrac{E_f (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \Delta T}{1 - \nu_f}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- E_Al = 70 GPa
- ν_Al = 0.35
- α_Al = 23.1 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- α_Si = 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- ΔT = 250 − 25 = 225 °C

Step 1: Biaxial modulus
$$
M_f = \dfrac{70 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.35} = 107.7 \text{ GPa}
$$

Step 2: Thermal mismatch strain
$$
\varepsilon_{th} = (23.1 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 225 = 4.61 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Step 3: Residual stress
$$
\sigma_r = 107.7 \times 10^9 \times 4.61 \times 10^{-3} = 4.96 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 496 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the biaxial modulus (1−ν) factor** — Students often write σ = E·α·ΔT, losing **1 mark**. Always clarify that thin films are in a **biaxial stress state**.
> 2. **Sign confusion** — A positive σ_r is **tensile**; a negative value would mean compressive. Always state this in the answer.
> 3. **Unit errors** — E in GPa must be converted to Pa (×10⁹) before multiplying, or the answer will be off by a factor of 10⁹.
> 4. **Numerical part skipped** — In the 7-mark question, failing to substitute numerical values and present the **final numerical answer in MPa or GPa** results in loss of the final 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Polymers in MEMS** are classified into **structural** (SU-8, polyimide, PMMA), **sacrificial** (photoresist, PMMA), and **encapsulating** (parylene C, PDMS) categories.
- [ ] **SU-8** is the most important **negative photoresist** for high-aspect-ratio structures; it cross-links via **cationic polymerization** under UV exposure.
- [ ] **PDMS** is the workhorse of **soft lithography** and bio-MEMS; mixed in a **10:1 base-to-curing-agent ratio**; Young's modulus ~**750 kPa**.
- [ ] **Parylene C** is deposited via **CVD** and provides **conformal, pinhole-free** coatings for implantable devices.
- [ ] **Gold (Au)** is the most inert and biocompatible metal but has **poor adhesion** — always paired with a **Ti adhesion layer** (~5–20 nm).
- [ ] **Copper (Cu)** has the lowest practical resistivity (**1.7 µΩ·cm**) but requires a **Ta or TaN barrier** to prevent diffusion into Si.
- [ ] **Aluminum (Al)** is cost-effective, with a self-passivating Al₂O₃ layer, widely used in bonding pads and piezo-MEMS.
- [ ] **Tungsten (W)** has the highest melting point (3422 °C) and stiffness (411 GPa); preferred for high-temperature and high-precision inertial MEMS.
- [ ] **Platinum (Pt)** is the gold standard for **biomedical electrodes and electrochemical sensors** due to its catalytic activity and biocompatibility.
- [ ] **Permalloy (Ni-Fe 80:20)** is the standard **soft magnetic material** for magnetically actuated MEMS.
- [ ] The **residual stress formula** $\sigma_r = \dfrac{E_f(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T}{1-\nu_f}$ is **HIGH-YIELD** and almost always tested.
- [ ] Always remember to convert **GPa to Pa** in numerical stress calculations.
- [ ] MEMS design always requires **CTE matching** between the structural layer and the substrate to avoid buckling/delamination.
- [ ] For a **Kerala/KTU-style answer**, always pair a polymer/metal with: (i) one **key property**, (ii) one **fabrication method**, and (iii) one **application**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Trick — "SAP TAP"**
> **S**U-8, **A**u, **P**t → **T**i (adhesion), **A**l (cheap), **P**ermalloy (magnetic) — covers 80% of KTU questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
