topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Additional Materials: Silicon Compounds (SiO2, Si3N4), Polycrystalline Silicon, Gallium Arsenide, Quartz, Piezoelectric Crystals, Conductive Polymers; Packaging and Integration: Glass Encapsulation, MEMS Process Integration Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Additional Materials in MEMS

In Module 1 of MEMS, after studying single-crystal silicon as the primary substrate material, KTU 2024 scheme extends the discussion to **additional engineering materials** that complement silicon in fabricating micro-electro-mechanical systems. No single material satisfies all MEMS design requirements (electrical, mechanical, thermal, optical, biological). Therefore, a MEMS engineer must understand the *material palette* — the toolbox of materials — available to build sensors, actuators, and structural layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to **list, compare, and justify** the use of materials such as silicon dioxide (SiO₂), silicon nitride (Si₃N₄), polycrystalline silicon (polysilicon), gallium arsenide (GaAs), quartz, piezoelectric crystals, and conductive polymers in MEMS device fabrication. The packaging section tests understanding of **glass encapsulation** and **MEMS process integration strategies**.

## 1.2 Definition of Each Material

### 🔹 A. Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)

**Formal Definition:** Silicon dioxide is an amorphous or crystalline insulating thin film of Si–O tetrahedral network, used in MEMS as a **sacrificial layer**, **electrical insulator**, **diffusion mask**, and **passivation layer**. It is thermally grown on silicon at 1000–1200 °C or deposited by LPCVD/PECVD.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of SiO₂ as the *"scaffolding putty"* in MEMS — like the temporary wooden frame used by masons to build a brick arch. After the structure is built, the scaffolding (SiO₂) is dissolved away using **HF (hydrofluoric acid)**, leaving a free-standing MEMS structure.

### 🔹 B. Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)

**Formal Definition:** A chemically inert, high-tensile-strength ceramic thin film (Si–N covalent network), used as a **structural layer**, **etch mask**, and **dielectric** in MEMS. It has high Young's modulus (~250–310 GPa) and excellent wear resistance.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Si₃N₄ is the *"armored plating"* of MEMS — similar to the bullet-proof glass in a bank teller window. It shields and supports delicate moving parts.

### 🔹 C. Polycrystalline Silicon (Polysilicon)

**Formal Definition:** A polycrystalline form of silicon consisting of multiple small silicon grains separated by grain boundaries, deposited by **LPCVD** at 580–650 °C, and used as a **structural layer**, **piezoresistor**, and **gate electrode** in MEMS and CMOS-integrated devices.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Polysilicon is like a *"patchwork quilt of tiny silicon crystals"*. Single-crystal silicon is one giant perfect crystal; polysilicon is many small crystals stitched together. It is cheaper and easier to deposit over large areas than single-crystal silicon.

### 🔹 D. Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)

**Formal Definition:** A III–V direct bandgap compound semiconductor (E_g = **1.42 eV**) with high electron mobility (~8500 cm²/V·s) and piezoelectric properties, used in **optoelectronic MEMS (MOEMS)**, **RF MEMS**, and **high-frequency sensors**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** While silicon is the *"reliable workhorse"* of electronics, GaAs is the *"speedy racehorse"* — it carries electrons faster, glows efficiently when current passes, and converts light to electricity with high efficiency (used in solar cells on Mars rovers!).

### 🔹 E. Quartz (SiO₂ Crystal)

**Formal Definition:** A natural or synthetic single-crystal form of silicon dioxide exhibiting **piezoelectricity** and excellent frequency stability, used in **resonators**, **filters**, and **timing devices** in MEMS.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Quartz is the *"heartbeat oscillator"* of the electronics world — it vibrates at an exact, rock-steady frequency when electrically excited, much like a tuning fork that never goes off-pitch.

### 🔹 F. Piezoelectric Crystals

**Formal Definition:** Non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., **Quartz, PZT, LiNbO₃, ZnO, AlN**) that generate an electric charge when mechanically stressed (*direct effect*) and deform when an electric field is applied (*converse effect*). Used in MEMS as **sensors, actuators, and energy harvesters**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A piezoelectric crystal is like a *"two-way translator between the mechanical and electrical worlds"* — squeeze it, and it speaks "voltage"; energize it, and it speaks "motion".

### 🔹 G. Conductive Polymers

**Formal Definition:** Organic polymers (e.g., **PEDOT:PSS, Polypyrrole, Polyaniline**) with conjugated π-electron backbones that conduct electricity, used in **flexible MEMS**, **bio-sensors**, and **soft robotics**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Conductive polymers are *"plastic wires"* — they look and feel like ordinary plastic (flexible, lightweight) but they can carry current, just like copper. They are the bridge between traditional electronics and flexible/wearable devices.

## 1.3 Definition of Packaging and Integration Concepts

### 🔹 H. Glass Encapsulation

**Formal Definition:** A wafer-level packaging technique in which a **Pyrex/Borofloat glass wafer** is anodically bonded to a silicon MEMS wafer to form a hermetically sealed cavity that protects the moving microstructure from dust, moisture, and contamination.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Glass encapsulation is like placing the MEMS chip inside a *"glass terrarium"* — the device breathes and moves inside, but the outside world (dust, humidity, fingers) cannot touch it.

### 🔹 I. MEMS Process Integration Strategies

**Formal Definition:** A coordinated sequence of lithography, deposition, etching, bonding, and release steps designed to combine multiple materials, devices, and electrical interconnects into a single functional MEMS system-on-chip.

**Intuitive Analogy:** MEMS process integration is the *"orchestral score"* of microfabrication — each instrument (deposition, lithography, etching, bonding) plays its part at the right time to produce a symphony (working MEMS device).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> - Young's Modulus of Si = **190 GPa**
> - Young's Modulus of Si₃N₄ = **250–310 GPa**
> - Young's Modulus of SiO₂ = **70–80 GPa**
> - Young's Modulus of Polysilicon = **160–170 GPa**
> - Thermal Expansion Coefficient of Si = **2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**
> - Thermal Expansion Coefficient of Pyrex Glass = **3.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** (closely matched → enables anodic bonding)

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Additional Materials are Required in MEMS

Single-crystal silicon alone cannot satisfy every MEMS requirement. The following analysis explains the *why*:

- **Electrical Isolation:** Pure silicon is conductive; for capacitive sensors and gate dielectrics, an **insulator** like SiO₂ or Si₃N₄ is mandatory.
- **Sacrificial Layering:** To make **free-standing cantilevers, bridges, and membranes**, we need a material that can be **selectively etched away** — SiO₂ in HF is the classic sacrificial layer.
- **Strain Sensing:** Piezoresistors need a material whose resistance changes with stress; **polysilicon** is the standard CMOS-compatible piezoresistor.
- **Optical MEMS:** GaAs emits and detects light efficiently (direct bandgap) — silicon cannot.
- **High-Frequency MEMS:** Quartz and GaAs offer better RF performance.
- **Flexibility:** Conductive polymers enable flexible/wearable MEMS that brittle silicon cannot.
- **Hermetic Sealing:** Glass-to-silicon anodic bonding provides a low-cost wafer-level package.

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of Each Material

### 🔬 A. Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) — Deep Analysis

**Growth Mechanisms:**
1. **Thermal Oxidation (Dry):** Si + O₂ → SiO₂ at ~1000–1200 °C
2. **Thermal Oxidation (Wet):** Si + 2H₂O → SiO₂ + 2H₂ at ~900–1000 °C
3. **LPCVD:** SiH₄ + O₂ → SiO₂ + 2H₂ at ~400–450 °C
4. **PECVD:** SiH₄ + N₂O → SiO₂ + 2H₂ + N₂ at ~300–400 °C

**The Deal-Grove Model** governs thermal oxidation:

$$x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$$

where:
- $x_0$ = oxide thickness (μm)
- $B$ = parabolic rate constant
- $B/A$ = linear rate constant
- $t$ = oxidation time
- $\tau$ = initial oxide thickness correction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SiO₂ is special in MEMS:** It is the only material that can be **grown** (not just deposited) on silicon, and it is **selectively etchable in HF** without attacking silicon. This makes it the *universal sacrificial material* in surface micromachining.

### 🔬 B. Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄) — Deep Analysis

**Deposition Routes:**
- **LPCVD:** 3SiH₄ + 4NH₃ → Si₃N₄ + 12H₂ at ~700–850 °C (low-stress, stoichiometric)
- **PECVD:** SiH₄ + NH₃ at ~300–400 °C (high residual stress, used for low-temp MEMS)

**Why Si₃N₄ in MEMS:**
- **High chemical inertness** → excellent etch mask for KOH and EDP.
- **High fracture strength** → structural beams in pressure sensors.
- **Low optical absorption** in visible range → optical windows.
- **High dielectric constant** (~7) → capacitive sensing layers.

### 🔬 C. Polycrystalline Silicon — Deep Analysis

**Structural Properties:**
- Consists of columnar grains of size 0.05–1 μm.
- Grain boundaries act as **dopant sinks and scattering centers**, reducing carrier mobility.
- Phosphorus or boron doping provides resistivity of 10⁻³ to 10² Ω·cm.

**Why Polysilicon in MEMS:**
- **CMOS-compatible** → can be integrated with ICs.
- **Doping-tunable stress** → tensile/compressive layers for stress-engineered designs.
- **Piezoresistive** → pressure sensors, accelerometers (ΔR/R = π·σ).

### 🔬 D. Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) — Deep Analysis

**Why GaAs over Si for some MEMS:**
- **Direct bandgap** (1.42 eV) → efficient light emission/absorption.
- **High electron mobility** (8500 cm²/V·s vs. 1400 for Si) → fast transistors.
- **Piezoelectric** → no need for deposited piezo layers.
- **Radiation-hard** → space and military applications.

**Drawbacks:** Brittle, expensive, and toxic (arsenic). Difficult to etch — needs reactive ion etching (RIE) with Cl-based chemistry.

### 🔬 E. Quartz (Single-Crystal SiO₂) — Deep Analysis

**Key Property: Piezoelectricity**
- Crystal class: **32 (trigonal)**
- Common cuts: **AT-cut, BT-cut, SC-cut** for temperature-stable resonators.
- Frequency constant: ~1660 kHz·mm (AT-cut).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Quartz beats silicon in timing:** Quartz has a Q-factor of **10⁴–10⁶** at 10 MHz — silicon resonators top out at ~10⁴. This means quartz oscillators drift less, making them the *gold standard* for wristwatches, phones, and GPS.

### 🔬 F. Piezoelectric Crystals — Theoretical Framework

The linear piezoelectric constitutive equations (stress-charge form):

$$D_i = d_{ijk} T_{jk} + \varepsilon_{ij}^{T} E_j$$

$$S_{ij} = s_{ij}^{E} T_{jk} + d_{kij} E_k$$

where:
- $D_i$ = electric displacement (C/m²)
- $T_{jk}$ = mechanical stress (N/m²)
- $E_j$ = electric field (V/m)
- $S_{ij}$ = mechanical strain
- $d_{ijk}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (m/V or C/N)
- $\varepsilon_{ij}^{T}$ = permittivity at constant stress
- $s_{ij}^{E}$ = elastic compliance at constant electric field

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Piezoelectric MEMS Materials Comparison:**

| Material | d₃₃ (pC/N) | Curie Temp (°C) | CMOS-Compatible? |
|----------|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Quartz   | 2.3       | 573              | Yes              |
| ZnO      | 12.4      | 450              | Yes              |
| AlN      | 5.0       | 1150             | Yes              |
| PZT      | 400–600   | 250–400          | No               |
| LiNbO₃   | 6.0       | 1210             | Limited          |

### 🔬 G. Conductive Polymers — Theoretical Analysis

**Conductivity Mechanism:** Doping of the conjugated polymer backbone creates charge carriers (polarons/bipolarons) that move along and between chains.

**Typical Conductivities:**
- PEDOT:PSS ≈ **10⁻⁴ to 10³ S/cm** (tunable by secondary doping with DMSO/EG)
- Polypyrrole ≈ **10⁻² to 10² S/cm**
- Polyaniline ≈ **10⁻¹⁰ to 10² S/cm** (huge range with doping state)

**Why in MEMS:** Flexibility, biocompatibility, low cost, and solution-processable (spin coat, inkjet print).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities are in SI units unless otherwise noted.**

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's law (uniaxial) | Mechanical beam bending |
| 2 | $x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$ | Deal-Grove oxidation | SiO₂ growth |
| 3 | $R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$ | Resistance | Polysilicon heaters/piezoresistors |
| 4 | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | Piezoresistance | Polysilicon strain gauges |
| 5 | $D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^T \cdot E$ | Direct piezoelectric | Sensor mode |
| 6 | $S = s^E \cdot T + d^t \cdot E$ | Converse piezoelectric | Actuator mode |
| 7 | $f = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$ | Cantilever resonance | Resonator design |
| 8 | $f_{AT} = \frac{1.66}{t} \text{ MHz·mm}$ | AT-cut quartz frequency | Timing devices |
| 9 | $Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ | Quality factor | Resonator performance |
| 10 | $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress | Glass-Si bonding |
| 11 | $\varepsilon_r^{SiO_2} \approx 3.9$ | Relative permittivity | Capacitive MEMS |
| 12 | $\varepsilon_r^{Si_3N_4} \approx 7.0$ | Relative permittivity | Capacitive MEMS |
| 13 | $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ | Si thermal expansion | Stress engineering |
| 14 | $\alpha_{Pyrex} = 3.2 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ | Pyrex thermal expansion | Anodic bonding |
| 15 | $V_{anodic} \approx 200\text{–}1000$ V | Anodic bonding voltage | Glass-Si sealing |
| 16 | $E_{g}^{GaAs} = 1.42$ eV | Direct bandgap | Optoelectronic MEMS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Formulas:**
> - Equation (4) is **the cornerstone of piezoresistive pressure sensors and accelerometers**.
> - Equation (7) governs the design of **resonant sensors and filters**.
> - Equation (10) determines **residual stress in packaged MEMS** — critical for reliability.
> - Equation (15) sets the operating condition for **wafer-level hermetic packaging**.

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deal-Grove Model for SiO₂ Growth

The Deal-Grove model is the **fundamental equation** of thermal oxidation in silicon MEMS. The derivation follows the physical transport of oxidant through the existing oxide layer.

### Step 1: Define the Problem

A bare silicon wafer is placed in an oxidizing ambient (O₂ or H₂O). An oxide layer grows on the surface. As thickness $x$ increases, the oxidant must diffuse through the oxide to reach the Si/SiO₂ interface.

### Step 2: Flux of Oxidant Through the Oxide (Fick's First Law)

The steady-state flux $F_1$ of oxidant through a growing oxide of thickness $x$ is:

$$F_1 = D \cdot \frac{C^* - C_i}{x}$$

where:
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of oxidant in SiO₂
- $C^*$ = equilibrium concentration of oxidant in the oxide at the outer surface
- $C_i$ = concentration of oxidant at the Si/SiO₂ interface

### Step 3: Flux Due to Reaction at the Interface

The reaction at the Si/SiO₂ interface consumes oxidant at rate proportional to $C_i$:

$$F_2 = k_s \cdot C_i$$

where $k_s$ is the surface reaction rate constant.

### Step 4: Steady-State Equate the Two Fluxes

At steady state, $F_1 = F_2$:

$$D \cdot \frac{C^* - C_i}{x} = k_s \cdot C_i$$

Solving for $C_i$:

$$C_i = \frac{C^*}{1 + \frac{k_s x}{D}}$$

### Step 5: Rate of Oxide Growth

The oxide growth rate is:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{F_2}{N_1} = \frac{k_s C_i}{N_1}$$

where $N_1$ is the number of oxidant molecules incorporated per unit volume of oxide (~2.2 × 10²² cm⁻³ for SiO₂).

Substituting $C_i$:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{k_s C^* / N_1}{1 + \frac{k_s x}{D}}$$

### Step 6: Separation of Variables and Integration

$$N_1 \left(1 + \frac{k_s x}{D}\right) dx = k_s C^* dt$$

$$N_1 \left(x + \frac{k_s x^2}{2D}\right) = k_s C^* (t + \tau)$$

Multiplying by $\frac{2 D}{k_s}$:

$$\frac{2 D N_1}{k_s} x + N_1 x^2 = 2 D C^* (t + \tau)$$

Defining $A = 2D/k_s$ and $B = 2DC^*/N_1$:

$$N_1 x^2 + \frac{2 D N_1}{k_s} x = 2 D C^* (t + \tau)$$

Dividing by $N_1$ and defining standard Deal-Grove parameters:

$$x^2 + A x = B (t + \tau)$$

**This is the Deal-Grove equation.** The two limiting cases are:
- **Linear regime (small $t$):** $x \approx (B/A)(t + \tau)$ — reaction-limited
- **Parabolic regime (large $t$):** $x^2 \approx B t$ — diffusion-limited

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** At very thin oxides (<100 nm), the linear regime dominates and the rate depends on the interface reaction. At thick oxides (>1 μm), diffusion dominates and the growth slows as $\sqrt{t}$. MEMS engineers exploit this to grow precise sacrificial layers (typically 0.5–2 μm SiO₂).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Piezoresistive Response of Polysilicon

### Step 1: Define Piezoresistive Coefficient

The fractional change in resistivity $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is linearly related to applied stress $\sigma$:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi \cdot \sigma$$

where $\pi$ is the piezoresistive coefficient (Pa⁻¹).

### Step 2: Geometric and Resistive Change

For a resistor of length $L$, cross-section $A$, and resistivity $\rho$:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

The fractional change is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Strain

Using $\varepsilon_x = \Delta L / L$ and Poisson ratio $\nu$:

$$\frac{\Delta A}{A} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x \cdot 2 = -2\nu \varepsilon_x$$

So:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu) \varepsilon_x + \pi E \varepsilon_x$$

$$= \left[(1 + 2\nu) + \pi E\right] \varepsilon_x$$

$$= G \cdot \varepsilon_x$$

where $G$ is the **gauge factor**.

For polysilicon, $\pi E \gg (1 + 2\nu)$, so:

$$G \approx \pi E$$

### Step 4: Multi-Axial Form

For longitudinal ($l$) and transverse ($t$) stresses on a rectangular resistor:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

where $\pi_l$ and $\pi_t$ are the longitudinal and transverse piezoresistive coefficients.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example for KTU:** For p-type polysilicon, $\pi_l \approx 30 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ and $\pi_t \approx -15 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹. If $\sigma_l = 100$ MPa and $\sigma_t = 0$, then $\Delta R/R = 30 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^8 = 0.3 = 30\%$. This sensitivity is the basis of commercial pressure sensors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cantilever Resonant Frequency

### Step 1: Equation of Motion

A cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, density $\rho$, and Young's modulus $E$ undergoes transverse vibration governed by the Euler-Bernoulli equation:

$$E I \frac{\partial^4 y(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 y(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

where $I = wt^3/12$ is the second moment of area and $A = wt$.

### Step 2: Separation of Variables

Assume $y(x,t) = Y(x) \cdot \cos(\omega t)$:

$$E I Y^{(4)}(x) = \rho A \omega^2 Y(x)$$

Define $\beta^4 = \rho A \omega^2 / (EI)$. The general solution is:

$$Y(x) = C_1 \cos(\beta x) + C_2 \sin(\beta x) + C_3 \cosh(\beta x) + C_4 \sinh(\beta x)$$

### Step 3: Boundary Conditions (Fixed-Free Cantilever)

At $x=0$ (fixed end): $Y(0) = 0$ and $Y'(0) = 0$.
At $x=L$ (free end): $Y''(L) = 0$ and $Y'''(L) = 0$.

These yield the characteristic equation:

$$\cos(\beta L) \cdot \cosh(\beta L) = -1$$

The fundamental solution is $\beta_1 L = 1.875$.

### Step 4: Resonant Frequency

$$\omega_1 = (1.875)^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}} = (1.875)^2 \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{12 \rho}}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{12\rho}}$$

$$f_1 = 0.162 \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** This formula is used in **resonant mass sensors** — when a particle lands on the cantilever, the added mass decreases $f$. By measuring $\Delta f$, the particle mass can be determined with attogram (10⁻¹⁸ g) sensitivity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Thermal Stress in Glass-Silicon Anodic Bonding

### Step 1: Mismatch in Thermal Expansion

The bonded pair cools from bonding temperature $T_b$ to room temperature $T_{rt}$. The mismatch strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_{glass} - \alpha_{Si})(T_b - T_{rt})$$

### Step 2: Resulting Stress

$$\sigma_{th} = E_{eff} \cdot \varepsilon_{th}$$

For Si with $E = 190$ GPa, $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}/°C$, $\alpha_{Pyrex} = 3.2 \times 10^{-6}/°C$, and $\Delta T = 400°C$:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = (3.2 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 400 = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 190 \times 10^9 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-4} = 45.6 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3: Practical Implication

This residual stress is **below the fracture strength of Si** (~7 GPa in tension, ~1 GPa practical) but **must be accounted for** in precision sensors. Choosing **Borofloat 33** glass (matched CTE) keeps $\sigma_{th}$ below 10 MPa.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Error:** Many students confuse *anodic bonding* with *fusion bonding*. Anodic bonding uses **electrostatic attraction + ion migration** at 300–450 °C with ~1000 V, while fusion bonding uses **direct Si-Si bonding** at 800–1100 °C without voltage.

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three additional materials used in MEMS fabrication and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂):** Used as a **sacrificial layer** in surface micromachining because it can be selectively etched in hydrofluoric acid (HF) without attacking silicon.
2. **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄):** Used as a **structural layer** in pressure sensor diaphragms due to its high Young's modulus (~250 GPa) and excellent chemical inertness.
3. **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs):** Used in **optoelectronic MEMS (MOEMS)** such as VCSELs and photodetectors because of its direct bandgap (1.42 eV) and high electron mobility.

*(Each material + application = 1 mark; well-explained connection = full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between anodic bonding and fusion bonding in MEMS packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Anodic Bonding | Fusion Bonding |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| **Materials Joined** | Glass (Pyrex) + Silicon | Silicon + Silicon, or Glass + Glass |
| **Temperature** | 300 – 450 °C | 800 – 1100 °C |
| **Voltage** | 600 – 1000 V DC applied | No voltage |
| **Mechanism** | Electrostatic attraction + Na⁺ ion migration | Direct chemical bonding via –OH groups |
| **Surface Roughness Requirement** | Moderate (~few nm) | Very strict (< 0.5 nm) |
| **Hermeticity** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Application** | MEMS wafer-level packaging | SOI wafer fabrication, 3D integration |

*(3 marks: comparison must mention at least 3 distinguishing parameters.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with **internal choice** between two sub-questions. The student must answer ONE choice fully.

---

### 📝 Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Deal-Grove model** of thermal oxidation of silicon. Derive the equation $x^2 + Ax = B(t+\tau)$ and discuss the linear and parabolic growth regimes with their physical significance in MEMS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **SiO₂, Si₃N₄, and polycrystalline silicon** as structural materials in MEMS with respect to Young's modulus, residual stress, etch selectivity, and CMOS compatibility.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** The Deal-Grove model (1965) describes the kinetics of thermal oxidation of silicon, where oxidant diffuses through a growing oxide layer and reacts at the Si/SiO₂ interface.

**Derivation Steps:**

1. **Fick's first law** for oxidant flux through oxide of thickness $x$:
$$F_1 = D \cdot \frac{C^* - C_i}{x}$$

2. **Interface reaction rate:**
$$F_2 = k_s C_i$$

3. **Steady-state equate:** $F_1 = F_2$ gives $C_i = \dfrac{C^*}{1 + k_s x / D}$.

4. **Growth rate:** $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{k_s C_i}{N_1} = \dfrac{k_s C^*/N_1}{1 + k_s x / D}$

5. **Integration** with initial condition $x(0) = x_i$ yields:
$$x^2 + A x = B (t + \tau)$$
where $A = 2D/k_s$, $B = 2DC^*/N_1$, and $\tau = (x_i^2 + A x_i)/B$.

**Regimes:**
- **Linear regime** (small $t$, thin oxide): $x \approx (B/A)(t+\tau)$ — interface reaction rate-limited; dominates up to ~100 nm.
- **Parabolic regime** (large $t$, thick oxide): $x^2 \approx B t$ — diffusion-limited; dominates for thick oxides used in MEMS.

**MEMS Significance:** Engineers grow thick SiO₂ (1–2 μm) as sacrificial layers in surface micromachining. Parabolic regime is used to predict the time required.

**Physical Constants:** $B$ depends exponentially on temperature as $B = B_0 \exp(-E_a/k_BT)$ with $E_a \approx 1.23$ eV for dry O₂.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

| Property | SiO₂ | Si₃N₄ | Polysilicon |
|----------|------|-------|-------------|
| Young's Modulus | 70–80 GPa | 250–310 GPa | 160–170 GPa |
| Residual Stress | Low (tensile, ~300 MPa) | High tensile (~1 GPa) | Tunable (–500 to +500 MPa) |
| Etch in HF | Very fast (10:1) | Slow (~100:1) | Negligible |
| Etch in KOH | Slow | Very slow (mask) | Fast |
| CMOS Compatible | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Doping Tunable | No | No | Yes (B, P) |

**Key Conclusions for 7-mark answer:**
- **SiO₂** is the *sacrificial and insulating* workhorse.
- **Si₃N₄** is the *structural and masking* material where high stiffness is required.
- **Polysilicon** is the *piezoresistive and structural* material where electrical functionality is required simultaneously with mechanical support.

---

### 📝 Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **anodic bonding process** for glass-silicon packaging in MEMS. Discuss the role of Na⁺ ion migration, the applied voltage range, temperature range, and the resulting bond mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **direct piezoelectric effect** and **converse piezoelectric effect** with suitable constitutive equations. Compare at least three piezoelectric materials (Quartz, PZT, AlN) in terms of their d₃₃ coefficient, Curie temperature, and CMOS compatibility.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Anodic Bonding Process:**

1. **Setup:** A glass wafer (Pyrex 7740 or Borofloat 33) is placed on top of a silicon wafer containing the MEMS structures. A high-voltage DC source is connected with the **negative terminal to the glass** and the **positive terminal to the silicon**.

2. **Heating:** The stack is heated to 300–450 °C. At this temperature, the glass becomes slightly conductive due to mobile Na⁺ ions.

3. **Voltage Application:** A DC voltage of 600–1000 V is applied across the glass. The electric field drives **Na⁺ ions away from the glass-silicon interface** toward the cathode (top electrode).

4. **Depletion Layer:** This creates a **negatively charged depletion layer** (oxygen anions) at the glass-silicon interface.

5. **Electrostatic Attraction:** The strong electrostatic force (which can reach 1 MPa) pulls the two wafers into intimate atomic contact.

6. **Bond Formation:** A permanent **Si–O–Si chemical bond** forms at the interface, creating a hermetic seal.

**Process Parameters:**
- Temperature: 300–450 °C
- Voltage: 600–1000 V
- Time: 5–30 minutes
- Pressure applied externally: optional but improves contact

**Why it is ideal for MEMS:**
- Low temperature (below aluminum metallization limits of 450 °C).
- Hermetic sealing protects moving parts.
- CTE-matched glass minimizes residual stress.
- Wafer-level batch processing.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** Generation of electric charge/field when mechanical stress is applied.

$$D_i = d_{ijk} T_{jk} + \varepsilon_{ij}^T E_j$$

In 1D simplified form:
$$D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon^T \cdot E$$

**Converse Piezoelectric Effect:** Generation of mechanical strain when an electric field is applied.

$$S = s^E \cdot \sigma + d^t \cdot E$$

**Material Comparison:**

| Material | d₃₃ (pC/N) | Curie Temp (°C) | CMOS Compatible? | Application |
|----------|-----------|------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Quartz** | 2.3 | 573 | Yes | Precision oscillators, gyroscopes |
| **AlN** | 5.0 | 1150 | Yes | FBAR filters, MEMS microphones |
| **PZT** | 400–600 | 250–400 | No | High-strain actuators, inkjet printheads |
| **ZnO** | 12.4 | 450 | Yes | Thin-film bulk resonators |
| **LiNbO₃** | 6.0 | 1210 | Limited | Optical modulators, SAW devices |

**Key Conclusions for 7-mark answer:**
- Quartz: best for **frequency stability** despite low d₃₃.
- AlN: best for **CMOS-integrated RF MEMS** due to high Curie temperature and process compatibility.
- PZT: best for **high-strain actuation** but requires sol-gel or sputtering and is CMOS-incompatible due to Pb contamination and high processing temperature.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in This Topic (Based on KTU 2023–2024 Evaluation Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing SiO₂ and Si₃N₄ roles (–2 marks):** Students often write "Si₃N₄ is used as sacrificial layer" — this is WRONG. **SiO₂ is the sacrificial layer** (etched in HF). Si₃N₄ is the *etch mask* and *structural layer*.
> 
> 2. **Wrong voltage polarity in anodic bonding (–2 marks):** The **silicon is positive (anode) and glass is negative (cathode)**. Reversing this will not produce bonding.
> 
> 3. **Missing units in formula derivations (–1 mark):** Always include units for Young's modulus (GPa), frequency (Hz or MHz), stress (MPa), and resistivity (Ω·cm).
> 
> 4. **Writing "polycrystalline silicon is a single crystal" (–2 marks):** Polysilicon is *poly*-crystalline — multiple grains. Single crystal Si is grown by Czochralski method, not deposited.
> 
> 5. **Confusing direct and converse piezoelectric effects (–1 mark):** Direct = mechanical → electrical (sensor); Converse = electrical → mechanical (actuator).
> 
> 6. **Omitting the chemical mechanism in anodic bonding (–1 mark):** Always mention **Na⁺ ion migration and depletion layer formation** to earn full marks.
> 
> 7. **Treating SiO₂ growth as a "deposition" process (–1 mark):** Thermal SiO₂ is *grown* by chemical reaction with the silicon substrate itself — it is not "deposited" like LPCVD oxide.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Additional Materials and Packaging:**

### 🔹 Materials Summary

- **SiO₂:** Sacrificial layer, insulator, thermal oxide grown on Si; etches in HF.
- **Si₃N₄:** Structural layer, etch mask for KOH/EDP, high modulus, low optical absorption.
- **Polysilicon (poly-Si):** CMOS-compatible structural and piezoresistive material; tunable stress via doping.
- **GaAs:** Direct bandgap (1.42 eV) III–V semiconductor; for MOEMS and RF MEMS; brittle and expensive.
- **Quartz:** Single-crystal SiO₂; piezoelectric; Q-factor 10⁴–10⁶; for resonators and timing.
- **Piezoelectric crystals (AlN, ZnO, PZT, LiNbO₃):** Sensor/actuator via d-coupling.
- **Conductive polymers (PEDOT:PSS, Polypyrrole, Polyaniline):** Flexible, biocompatible; for wearable/bioMEMS.

### 🔹 Key Properties to Memorize

- $E_{Si} = 190$ GPa
- $E_{SiO_2} = 70–80$ GPa
- $E_{Si_3N_4} = 250–310$ GPa
- $E_{polySi} = 160–170$ GPa
- $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}/°C$
- $\alpha_{Pyrex} = 3.2 \times 10^{-6}/°C$
- $E_{g}^{GaAs} = 1.42$ eV (direct)
- $E_{g}^{Si} = 1.12$ eV (indirect)
- Curie temperature of PZT ≈ 250–400 °C
- Curie temperature of AlN ≈ 1150 °C

### 🔹 Packaging Rules

- **Anodic bonding:** 300–450 °C, 600–1000 V, glass negative, Si positive.
- **Fusion bonding:** 800–1100 °C, no voltage, ultra-flat surfaces required.
- **CTE mismatch must be < 1 × 10⁻⁶/°C** for low-stress packaging.
- **Hermeticity test:** He-leak rate < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s is industry standard.

### 🔹 Process Integration Order (MEMS Sandwich Recipe)

1. **Substrate prep** (Si wafer cleaning)
2. **Sacrificial layer deposition** (SiO₂, 1–2 μm)
3. **Structural layer deposition** (Polysilicon / Si₃N₄)
4. **Lithography and patterning**
5. **Doping/implantation** (for piezoresistors)
6. **Anisotropic etching** (DRIE or KOH for bulk structures)
7. **Release etch** (HF removes SiO₂ sacrificial)
8. **Critical point drying** (prevents stiction)
9. **Wafer-level packaging** (anodic bonding to glass)
10. **Dicing and testing**

### 🔹 High-Yield Comparison Tables to Remember

- Si vs GaAs: mobility (1400 vs 8500 cm²/V·s), bandgap (1.12 vs 1.42 eV), direct vs indirect.
- Anodic vs Fusion bonding: T, V, surface prep, mechanism.
- Polysilicon vs Single-crystal Si: deposition vs growth, cost, integration ease.
- Direct vs Converse piezoelectric effect: sensor mode vs actuator mode.

### 🔹 Frequently Asked KTU Concepts

1. "Why is SiO₂ used as a sacrificial layer?" — **HF selective etch over Si**.
2. "Why is GaAs preferred for LEDs?" — **Direct bandgap** enables efficient photon emission.
3. "Why is anodic bonding used for MEMS?" — **Low temperature + hermetic seal + wafer-level batch process**.
4. "Why is polysilicon preferred over single-crystal Si for surface micromachining?" — **Can be deposited over sacrificial layers; CMOS-compatible**.
5. "Why are conductive polymers emerging in MEMS?" — **Flexibility, biocompatibility, low cost, solution processing**.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Additional Materials and Packaging Notes**
> *These notes are tuned to KTU 2024 Scheme PEPET522 syllabus. Focus on understanding the *why* behind each material choice — this is what examiners test in 14-mark questions.* ✅

topics.title: Surface Micromachining: Sacrificial Layer Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surface Micromachining: Sacrificial Layer Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Surface Micromachining** is a planar microfabrication technique in which free-standing micro-mechanical structures are built by the sequential deposition, patterning, and selective removal (etching) of thin films on the surface of a substrate. Unlike bulk micromachining (which removes the substrate itself), surface micromachining leaves the substrate largely intact and instead shapes the thin films stacked upon it.

A **Sacrificial Layer** is a temporary thin-film material that is deposited and patterned *before* the structural layer, providing a mechanical placeholder (a "spacer" or "mold") that physically holds the structural layer in place during high-temperature processing and patterning. Once the structural layer is fully formed, the sacrificial layer is selectively etched away in a final **release step**, leaving the structural layer free-standing, anchored only at predefined support points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The sacrificial layer process is the *defining* element of surface micromachining. Without selective undercut etching of a sacrificial spacer, no free-standing micromechanical element (cantilever, bridge, plate, gear, or comb-drive) can be realized in a surface-micromachined process.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bridge Scaffolding" Concept

Imagine a civil engineer building a concrete arch bridge:
1. First, the engineer erects a **temporary wooden scaffolding** in the exact curved shape the bridge will take.
2. Concrete (the **structural material**) is poured *over* and *around* this scaffolding, hardened, and shaped.
3. Once the concrete has cured, the **wooden scaffolding is carefully chiseled away** from underneath.
4. What remains is a beautiful, self-supporting concrete arch — the wood is gone, and the space it occupied is now air.

In MEMS surface micromachining:
- The **wooden scaffolding** = sacrificial layer (typically PhosphoSilicate Glass / PSG or undoped SiO₂).
- The **concrete** = structural layer (typically polycrystalline silicon / polysilicon).
- The **chiseling step** = release etch (typically HydroFluoric acid / HF).
- The **engineers carving anchor holes in the wood** = patterning the anchor openings that will later pin the structure to the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants You Will See Repeatedly in This Module:**
> - Surface tension of water (γ): **~72.8 mN/m at 20 °C** (this is the source of stiction force).
> - Young's Modulus of LPCVD polysilicon: **~160 GPa** (governs spring constant of released beams).
> - HF etch selectivity of SiO₂ over Si: **> 1000:1** (why HF is the workhorse release etchant).
> - Typical sacrificial layer thickness: **0.5 – 2 µm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of a Sacrificial Layer Process

A canonical surface-micromachining process flow is best understood as a strict, ordered sequence. The order is *non-negotiable* — interchanging steps leads to mechanical failure, etch residues, or non-functional devices.

**Step 1 — Substrate Preparation**
- A clean, thermally oxidized silicon wafer (or a plain Si wafer with isolation oxide) is used as the starting substrate.
- A thin isolation layer (often **thermal SiO₂, 0.5–1 µm**) prevents leakage currents and pinholes.

**Step 2 — Deposition of the Sacrificial Layer**
- A uniform, conformal, low-stress thin film is deposited. Common choices:
  - **LPCVD PhosphoSilicate Glass (PSG)** with 6–8 % phosphorus content — preferred because P-doping dramatically increases the wet-etch rate in HF.
  - **LPCVD Low-Temperature Oxide (LTO)** — undoped SiO₂, slower etch rate.
  - **Polyimide** — used in *poly-Si-on-polyimide* processes for very thick structural layers.

**Step 3 — Patterning the Sacrificial Layer (Anchor Definition)**
- Photolithography + wet/dry etching opens **anchor windows** at locations where the future structure must be permanently tied to the substrate.
- These anchors are the *only* physical connection that survives the release etch.

**Step 4 — Deposition of the Structural Layer**
- The mechanical material (usually **LPCVD polysilicon at 580–620 °C**) is deposited conformally over the patterned sacrificial steps.
- This layer conforms to the topography, hugging the slopes of the sacrificial mesas and creating sloped sidewalls.

**Step 5 — Patterning the Structural Layer**
- Photolithography + Reactive Ion Etching (RIE, typically with **SF₆ / Cl₂ / HBr chemistries** for polysilicon) defines the lateral shape of the micro-structure — cantilever, bridge, comb finger, gear tooth, etc.
- **Etch access holes** (also called *release holes* or *dimples*) are often patterned *into* the structural layer at this step to provide pathways for the release etchant to reach the buried sacrificial layer.

**Step 6 — Release Etch & Drying**
- The wafer is immersed in an HF-based solution (e.g., **49 % HF**, or **Buffered Oxide Etch / BOE**) which selectively dissolves the PSG/SiO₂ sacrificial layer, undercutting the polysilicon structure.
- The wafer is then carefully dried. *This drying step is the most failure-prone moment in the entire flow* — see the stiction section below.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Material Choice

- **Why PSG instead of pure SiO₂?** Phosphorus doping introduces P=O and P-OH bonds that are more reactive with HF. A PSG with **8 wt% P** etches **~10× faster** in HF than undoped thermal oxide, drastically reducing the release time.
- **Why polysilicon as the structural layer?** It tolerates the high-temperature anneal (> 1000 °C) needed for stress relaxation, has well-known mechanical properties, and is CMOS-compatible.
- **Why do we need etch access holes?** HF cannot reach a buried, fully-encapsulated sacrificial layer through the polysilicon. Dimples/holes give the etchant a path; without them, release takes hours or fails entirely.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | Young's modulus of polysilicon | $\mathbf{160 \ \text{GPa}}$ |
| $\gamma$ | Surface tension of rinse liquid (water) | $\mathbf{72.8 \ \text{mN/m}}$ |
| $\theta$ | Contact angle of rinse liquid on polysilicon | $\mathbf{0 \ \text{to} \ 60^{\circ}}$ |
| $S$ | Etch selectivity (target vs. mask) | dimensionless |
| $D$ | Diffusivity of HF in narrow channel | $\mathbf{\sim 10^{-9} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$ |
| $L$ | Etch undercut length (sacrificial span) | $\mathbf{\mu m}$ scale |
| $t$ | Structural layer thickness | $\mathbf{0.5 \ \text{to} \ 5 \ \mu m}$ |
| $w$ | Structural beam width | $\mathbf{1 \ \text{to} \ 50 \ \mu m}$ |
| $k$ | Spring constant of released beam | $\mathbf{N/m}$ |
| $F_c$ | Capillary (stiction) force per unit length | $\mathbf{N/m}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sacrificial-layer surface micromachining is the *backbone* of commercial MEMS manufacturing. Every analog devices ADXL-series accelerometer, every Texas Instruments DMD micromirror, and every Bosch BMP pressure-sensor membrane relies on a polysilicon-over-PSG sacrificial-layer flow. The technique is essential wherever a **moving mechanical element** (mass, diaphragm, mirror, resonator) must be integrated monolithically with CMOS readout electronics on a single silicon die.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Diffusion-Limited Release Etch Time

The release etch is a **diffusion-limited** process. Once HF enters the access hole, it must diffuse laterally through the ever-lengthening air-filled gap to reach fresh sacrificial material. The longer the undercut distance $L$, the slower the etch progresses.

The etchant concentration $C(x,t)$ inside a 1-D slit of half-width $a$ obeys Fick's Second Law:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \;=\; D \,\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

**Boundary & Initial Conditions:**
- At the access hole, $C(0,t) = C_0$ (bulk HF concentration, ~constant).
- Far from the hole, $C(\infty, t) = 0$ initially.
- Initial condition: $C(x, 0) = 0$ for $x > 0$.

**Step 1 — Solution.** Using the standard self-similar (error-function) solution to the semi-infinite diffusion problem with a constant source at the boundary:

$$
C(x,t) \;=\; C_{0} \,\operatorname{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D t}} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Local etch rate.** The etch rate at position $x$ is proportional to the local HF concentration $C(x,t)$:

$$
R(x,t) \;=\; k_{\text{etch}} \, C(x,t) \;=\; k_{\text{etch}} C_{0} \,\operatorname{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D t}} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Time to clear the sacrificial material at depth $x$.** The cumulative dissolved thickness $\Delta$ at point $x$ is the time-integral of the local rate. A practical design rule (assuming the sacrificial film thickness $h_s$ is small compared to the lateral diffusion length) gives the **clearing time** for an undercut length $L$:

$$
t_{\text{release}} \;\approx\; \frac{L^{2}}{2 D \,\bigl[\ln\!\bigl(C_{0}/C_{\text{crit}}\bigr)\bigr]^{-1}}
$$

which simplifies — for design purposes — to the well-known **square-law rule** used by all MEMS foundries:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{\text{release}} \;\approx\; \frac{L^{2}}{2 D}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check.** For $L = 100 \ \mu m$ and $D = 1\times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$:

$$
t_{\text{release}} \;\approx\; \frac{(100\times 10^{-6})^{2}}{2 \times 1\times 10^{-9}} \;=\; \frac{1\times 10^{-8}}{2\times 10^{-9}} \;=\; 5 \ \text{s}
$$

This short time is why access-hole spacing must be carefully designed; doubling $L$ quadruples the release time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Capillary Stiction Force on a Released Plate

When the wet etchant is rinsed away (typically with DI water and then IPA), a **liquid meniscus** forms between the released plate and the substrate. As the meniscus evaporates, surface tension pulls the structure down. If this capillary force exceeds the mechanical restoring force of the structure, the plate sticks permanently to the substrate — this is **stiction**, the single largest yield-loss mechanism in surface micromachining.

**Step 1 — Force per unit length of a wetting line.** The capillary force pulling a plate toward the substrate, per unit length of contact line, is given by the Young–Laplace relation:

$$
\frac{F_{c}}{L_{p}} \;=\; \gamma \,(\cos\theta_{1} + \cos\theta_{2})
$$

where $L_p$ is the perimeter of the plate in contact with the liquid, and $\theta_1, \theta_2$ are the contact angles on the *polysilicon* top surface and the *substrate* (oxide) bottom surface, respectively.

**Step 2 — Total capillary force.** Multiplying by the perimeter:

$$
F_{\text{cap}} \;=\; \gamma \, L_{p} \,(\cos\theta_{1} + \cos\theta_{2})
$$

**Step 3 — Restoring spring force of a cantilever beam.** A cantilever of length $L_b$, width $w$, and thickness $t$ (fixed at one end) has spring constant:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{3 E I}{L_{b}^{3}} \;=\; \frac{E \, w \, t^{3}}{4 L_{b}^{3}}
$$

**Step 4 — Stiction criterion.** Permanent stiction occurs when the capillary force exceeds the maximum restoring force, i.e., when:

$$
F_{\text{cap}} \;>\; k \, \cdot \, g_{0}
$$

where $g_0$ is the initial gap height (the original sacrificial layer thickness, typically 0.5 – 2 µm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** Because $F_{\text{cap}} \propto L_p$ scales *linearly* with plate perimeter, while $k \propto 1/L_b^3$ scales *inversely* with the *cube* of the beam length, **long, slender cantilevers** are dramatically more vulnerable to stiction than **short, stubby plates**. This is why stiction-resistant designs use *dimples*, *shortened anchors*, and *stiffening ribs*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Etch Selectivity Definition

Selectivity $S$ is defined as the ratio of etch rates between the *target* (sacrificial) material and the *structural* material that must be preserved:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{R_{\text{sacrificial}}}{R_{\text{structural}}}
$$

For HF attacking PSG vs. polysilicon:
- $R_{\text{PSG}} \approx 1 \ \mu m / \text{min}$ in 49 % HF.
- $R_{\text{poly-Si}} \approx 0.5 \ \text{nm} / \text{min}$ in 49 % HF.

$$
S \;=\; \frac{1\times 10^{6} \ \text{nm/min}}{0.5 \ \text{nm/min}} \;=\; 2\times 10^{6}
$$

This enormous selectivity is the *fundamental reason* HF is the workhorse release etchant: it removes micron-thick sacrificial oxides in minutes while leaving micron-thick polysilicon structures essentially untouched.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Sacrificial Layer Sequence (Mermaid Schematic)

The following block-level flow diagram captures the temporal sequence of the surface-micromachining process and its key material transformations. It is intentionally a *flow* diagram rather than a cross-sectional drawing, because the same physical wafer evolves through six distinct topological states.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    st1[Step 1: Substrate Si with Thermal Oxide] --> st2[Step 2: Deposit Sacrificial Layer PSG or LTO SiO2]
    st2 --> st3[Step 3: Photolithography and Etch Anchor Openings in Sacrificial Layer]
    st3 --> st4[Step 4: Deposit Structural Layer LPCVD Polysilicon]
    st4 --> st5[Step 5: Photolithography and RIE Pattern Structural Layer Include Etch Access Holes]
    st5 --> st6[Step 6: Immersion in HF Release Etch Sacrificial Layer Dissolved]
    st6 --> st7[Step 7: Critical Point Drying or Freeze Drying Final Free Standing MEMS Structure]
    st1:::sub
    st2:::sac
    st3:::sac
    st4:::str
    st5:::str
    st6:::rel
    st7:::rel
    classDef sub fill: e8e8e8, stroke: 333, color: 000
    classDef sac fill: ffe7b3, stroke: b36b00, color: 000
    classDef str fill: cce5ff, stroke: 004080, color: 000
    classDef rel fill: d5f5d5, stroke: 1a661a, color: 000
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Free-Standing MEMS Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram explains the *spatial* relationships between substrate, sacrificial spacer, anchor, and the released structural layer, viewed as a block-level functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sub[Si Substrate with Thermal SiO2 Isolation] --> anc[Anchor Region Permanent Bond to Substrate]
    sub -.-> sac[Sacrificial Layer PSG LTO Spacer Region]
    sac -.-> str[Structural Layer Polysilicon Free Standing Over Air Gap]
    str --> can[Cantilever Beam Type]
    str --> brd[Bridge Fixed Fixed Type]
    str --> plt[Diamond Plate with Dimples]
    str --> cmb[Comb Drive Finger Structure]
    etchH[Etch Access Holes Patterned in Structural Layer] -.-> sac
    stic[Stiction Risk Zone During Drying] -.-> str
    classDef base fill: f0f0f0, stroke: 333, color: 000
    classDef layer fill: ffe7b3, stroke: b36b00, color: 000
    classDef arch fill: cce5ff, stroke: 004080, color: 000
    classDef warn fill: ffd6d6, stroke: a00, color: 000
    class sub,anc base
    class sac,etchH layer
    class str,can,brd,plt,cmb arch
    class stic warn
```

### 4.3 Key Design Rules Embedded in the Diagram

- **Anchor regions** are the *only* contact points that survive the release etch.
- **Etch access holes** are mandatory when the structural layer fully encapsulates the sacrificial spacer.
- **Stiction risk** is highest in *long, thin* structures and *low-stiffness* plates; **dimples** (small protrusions on the underside of the plate, typically 1 µm in diameter and 0.1 µm tall) prevent full plate-to-substrate contact and dramatically reduce stiction yield loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Define the term "sacrificial layer" as used in MEMS surface micromachining. List any three essential properties required of a good sacrificial material.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A sacrificial layer is a temporary thin-film spacer material that is deposited, patterned, and ultimately selectively etched away to release an overlying micro-mechanical structure. *Three essential properties of a good sacrificial material* are:
1. **High etch selectivity** with respect to the structural layer in the release etchant (e.g., HF attacks SiO₂ but not polysilicon).
2. **Low residual stress** and ability to withstand high-temperature structural-layer deposition (e.g., 620 °C LPCVD polysilicon).
3. **Good step coverage and conformality** so the structural layer can be deposited uniformly over its topography, and **clean, residue-free decomposition** during release.
*(1 mark for clean definition + 2 marks for properties, full credit if a 4th valid property is also listed.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Sessional Pattern]**
*What is "stiction" in surface-micromachined MEMS devices? State two practical methods to prevent it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stiction is the permanent adhesion of a released micro-structure to the underlying substrate (or to an adjacent structure) caused by capillary forces, van der Waals forces, or electrostatic attraction during the wet-release and drying step. *Two prevention methods* are:
1. **Critical-point drying (CPD)** using liquid CO₂, which eliminates the liquid–vapor interface and hence eliminates capillary force.
2. **Anti-stiction dimples** (sub-micron protrusions patterned into the underside of the structure) that physically prevent the plate from lying flat against the substrate.
*(1 mark for the definition + 2 marks for the prevention methods; alternative answers like "vapor-phase HF release" or "self-assembled monolayer hydrophobic coating" also earn full credit.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Note:** For all 14-mark questions, a clearly labelled **neat cross-sectional sketch** of the process flow is mandatory. Omitting the sketch typically costs 2–3 marks even if the textual answer is perfect.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Sacrificial Process Flow & Material Choices*

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the complete process flow for fabricating a surface-micromachined polysilicon cantilever using a PSG sacrificial layer.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The process flow consists of **six mandatory steps**:

1. **Substrate preparation (1 mark):** Begin with a (100)-oriented p-type silicon wafer. Grow a thermal SiO₂ isolation layer (~1 µm) by wet oxidation at 1100 °C to provide electrical isolation between the future MEMS structure and the substrate.

2. **Sacrificial layer deposition (1 mark):** Deposit a 1.0–2.0 µm thick layer of LPCVD PhosphoSilicate Glass (PSG, ~8 wt% P) at 450 °C. The PSG is conformal and fills the substrate topography uniformly.

3. **Sacrificial layer patterning (1 mark):** Spin-coat photoresist, expose through the anchor mask, and wet-etch the PSG in BHF (Buffered HF) to open **anchor windows** down to the underlying thermal oxide. Strip the resist.

4. **Structural layer deposition (1 mark):** Deposit a 2.0 µm thick layer of LPCVD polysilicon at 620 °C. The polysilicon conforms to the sacrificial step, draping over the anchor openings and the free-standing regions.

5. **Structural layer patterning (1 mark):** Pattern the cantilever shape using photolithography and anisotropic RIE (e.g., SF₆/O₂ or HBr chemistries). Etch access holes (typically 2 × 2 µm squares spaced 5–10 µm apart) are patterned into the polysilicon simultaneously.

6. **Release etch and drying (2 marks):** Immerse the wafer in 49 % HF for 2–10 minutes. The HF enters the access holes and laterally undercuts the PSG, freeing the cantilever while leaving the anchor end bonded to the substrate. Rinse in DI water, then in IPA, then dry using **critical-point drying** to avoid stiction. The final result is a free-standing polysilicon cantilever with a well-defined air gap.

**Sketch (must be drawn for full marks):** Six cross-sectional panels showing the wafer after each step, with clear labels for Substrate, Isolation Oxide, Sacrificial PSG, Polysilicon, Anchor, and the Air Gap.

---

**(b) Compare the materials used as structural and sacrificial layers in surface micromachining. Why is polysilicon preferred over single-crystal silicon for the structural layer in this process?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Structural Layer (Polysilicon) | Sacrificial Layer (PSG / LTO) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Function | Provides mechanical rigidity and motion | Acts as temporary spacer to be etched away |
| Typical thickness | 0.5 – 5 µm | 0.5 – 2 µm |
| Deposition | LPCVD at 580 – 620 °C | LPCVD PSG at 450 °C; LTO at 425 °C |
| Etch in HF | Negligible (~0.5 nm/min) | Rapid (~1 µm/min for PSG, ~10× faster than LTO) |
| Stress | Low after 1050 °C anneal | Compressive, low |
| CMOS compatible | Yes | Yes |

**Why polysilicon over single-crystal silicon (4 marks):**
1. **Deposition temperature and CMOS compatibility** — Polysilicon is deposited by LPCVD over an *arbitrary* sacrificial topography, including non-planar surfaces and even previously fabricated CMOS circuitry. Single-crystal silicon can only be formed by epitaxy on a bare Si substrate and cannot be grown over a sacrificial mesa.
2. **Tunable stress** — Polysilicon stress can be tuned from highly tensile to compressive by varying the deposition temperature and post-anneal conditions, allowing designers to engineer flat (stress-free) membranes.
3. **Patterning flexibility** — Polysilicon is patterned with simple RIE, independent of crystal orientation. Single-crystal Si requires KOH/TMAH anisotropic wet etching tied to the (111) planes, which is not planar and not compatible with on-chip CMOS.
4. **Thickness control** — Polysilicon thickness is set by deposition time, independent of the substrate. Single-crystal membranes must be made by SOI bonding or silicon-on-insulator techniques, which are far more expensive.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Release Etch & Stiction Phenomena*

**(a) Describe the wet and dry release techniques used in surface micromachining. Which one is preferred in industry and why?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Wet Release (1.5 marks):** The wafer is immersed in a liquid HF solution (e.g., 49 % HF, BOE, or HF:HCl mixtures). The HF selectively attacks the PSG / SiO₂ sacrificial layer through etch access holes in the polysilicon. Typical undercut rates are ~1 µm/min for PSG. After sufficient time (governed by $t \approx L^2 / 2D$), the structure is freed. The wafer is then rinsed and dried.

**Advantages:** Fast, low-cost, simple.
**Disadvantages:** Strong capillary forces during drying; risk of stiction; HF is hazardous and attacks CMOS aluminum metallization (limits integration with foundry CMOS).

**Dry Release Techniques (3 marks):**
1. **Vapor-phase HF release** — The wafer is exposed to anhydrous HF vapor (sometimes with alcohol catalyst) in a commercial tool (e.g., Primaxx uEtch). The etchant is a gas, so **no liquid meniscus forms**, eliminating capillary stiction entirely.
2. **Plasma / RIE dry release** — Sacrificial polymers (polyimide) or xenon difluoride (XeF₂) for Si sacrificial layers can be removed in a plasma chamber.
3. **Sublimation drying** — After a tert-butanol rinse, the wafer is frozen and the rinse liquid is sublimed under vacuum, bypassing the liquid–vapor interface.

**Industry preference (2.5 marks):** **Vapor-phase HF release is preferred in modern high-volume MEMS manufacturing** (e.g., ST Microelectronics, Bosch) because:
- It eliminates stiction by avoiding any liquid phase.
- It is fully compatible with on-chip CMOS (no aluminum corrosion).
- It is cleanroom-friendly and process-controlled, leading to higher yield.
- It produces residue-free surfaces, important for high-Q resonators and optical MEMS.

---

**(b) Explain stiction in surface-micromachined structures in detail. Discuss its causes and the engineering methods used to prevent it.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Stiction is the unintentional, permanent adhesion of a released MEMS element to a neighbouring surface (typically the substrate), which renders the device mechanically inoperable.

**Causes (3 marks):**
1. **Capillary stiction (most common, ~80 % of yield loss):** During wet release, a liquid meniscus forms in the gap between the released structure and substrate. The Laplace pressure of the meniscus exerts a pull-down force $F_c = \gamma L_p (\cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_2)$. Upon drying, if $F_c > k g_0$, the beam sticks.
2. **Van der Waals adhesion:** At intimate contact, intermolecular dispersion forces pull surfaces together with energies of ~50–100 mJ/m² for polysilicon-on-SiO₂.
3. **Electrostatic stiction:** Charge trapped in dielectrics (especially in RF MEMS switches) creates a bias that pulls the structure down.
4. **Solid bridging:** Re-deposited etch products or residue from incomplete release form a "bridge" of material that physically welds the structure to the substrate.

**Prevention Methods (3 marks):**
1. **Critical-point drying (CPD):** The rinse liquid (IPA) is replaced with liquid CO₂ above its critical point (31 °C, 7.4 MPa), then vented to gas — eliminating the liquid–vapor interface.
2. **Anti-stiction dimples:** Sub-micron protrusions on the underside of the structure reduce the contact area, raising the effective pull-off force.
3. **Vapor-phase HF release:** As discussed in part (a), removes the liquid phase entirely.
4. **Self-Assembled Monolayer (SAM) coatings:** A thin (~2 nm) hydrophobic layer of octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS) is vapour-deposited after release, reducing $\cos\theta$ and weakening capillary pull.
5. **Stiffening ribs and short beams:** Geometric designs that raise the spring constant $k$ above the capillary pull.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by:
> - Confusing *capillary stiction* (release-step phenomenon) with *in-use stiction* (operational/RF-MEMS phenomenon). The two have different root causes and different remedies.
> - Forgetting to mention **dimples** — they are a *design* fix, not a *process* fix, and KTU examiners reward both perspectives.
> - Writing "stiction can be avoided by using dry etching" without explaining *why* (i.e., because the liquid meniscus is the source of capillary force).
> - Omitting the **formula** $F_c = \gamma L_p(\cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_2)$ — quantitative answers consistently score higher than purely qualitative ones in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Surface micromachining = thin-film stacking + selective undercut etching.** Substrate is not etched.
- [ ] **Sacrificial layer** = temporary spacer (PSG or LTO) deposited *before* the structural layer.
- [ ] **Structural layer** = polysilicon is the *de facto* industry standard (CMOS-compatible, tunable stress, conformal LPCVD).
- [ ] **Six canonical process steps:** Substrate prep → Sac. deposition → Sac. patterning (anchors) → Struct. deposition → Struct. patterning (access holes) → Release etch → Drying.
- [ ] **HF selectivity of SiO₂ over Si is > 10⁶:1** — this is what makes the release step possible.
- [ ] **Release etch time scales as $L^2$ (square law)** — doubling the cantilever length quadruples the release time.
- [ ] **Etch access holes are mandatory** in fully-encapsulated sacrificial geometries.
- [ ] **Stiction = capillary-force-induced permanent adhesion** during the wet-release drying step.
- [ ] **Capillary force:** $F_c = \gamma L_p (\cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_2)$ where $\gamma$ for water = **72.8 mN/m**.
- [ ] **Cantilever spring constant:** $k = E w t^3 / (4 L_b^3)$ — cubic dependence on beam length.
- [ ] **Critical-point drying, dimples, vapor-phase HF, and SAM coatings** are the four pillars of stiction prevention.
- [ ] **Vapor-phase HF** is the industry-preferred release method for CMOS-integrated MEMS.
- [ ] **P-doping of PSG (~8 wt% P)** increases the HF etch rate by ~10× compared to undoped LTO.
- [ ] **Always draw the cross-sectional sketch** in a 14-mark answer — a missing sketch typically costs 2–3 marks even when the text is excellent.
- [ ] **Anchor design** is the only *permanent* mechanical connection between the released structure and the substrate — get the anchor count, geometry, and stress relief right.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Motors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Micro Motors

# ⚙️ Micro Motors – KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU Definition

A **Micro Motor** is a Micro-Electro-Mechanical System (MEMS) device, typically fabricated using silicon bulk micromachining or surface micromachining processes, that converts **electrical input energy** into **controlled mechanical rotational motion** (torque and angular displacement) at the micro-scale. Micro motors are the rotational analogues of micro-actuators and form the foundational building blocks for microfluidic pumps, optical switching arrays, micro-turbines, micro-robots, and biomedical micro-tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus (Module 2):** Micro Motors are studied under the broader umbrella of *MEMS Actuation Principles*. Students must master the energy-domain conversion mechanism, the rotor–stator geometry, and the electromechanical torque/force equations.

The classification of micro motors is governed by their **energy transduction mechanism**:

| Class | Energy Domain | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrostatic** | Electrical → Mechanical (Coulomb force) | Low torque, high speed |
| **Electromagnetic** | Electrical → Mechanical (Lorentz force) | High speed, complex fab |
| **Piezoelectric** | Electrical → Mechanical (strain) | High force, low displacement |
| **Thermal** | Electrical → Thermal → Mechanical | High force, slow response |

---

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy: "The Watch and the Whirlwind"

Imagine a **wristwatch** on your wrist. Inside, microscopic gears spin thousands of times per day, driven by a tiny battery. Now, shrink that entire mechanical system onto a **single silicon chip** smaller than a grain of rice, where the gears are not metal but **etched polysilicon layers**, and the driving force is not a spring but **invisible electric fields** between sub-micron gaps. That is a MEMS micro motor.

The **electrostatic micro motor** can be visualised as a **merry-go-round** in a thunderstorm. The thunderstorm builds up static charge (voltage) on the metal poles of the merry-go-round, and the surrounding air ions (electric field) push the platform to rotate. In our silicon device, the **stator poles** are energised in a controlled phase sequence, and the **rotor** (a free-spinning disk with matching pole teeth) is dragged along by the resulting electric field gradients.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Micro Motor Design:**
> - Vacuum permittivity: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
> - Free-space speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - Air breakdown field: **≈ 3 × 10⁶ V/m** (sets maximum safe voltage)
> - Silicon Young's modulus: **E_Si ≈ 169 GPa**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 The Electrostatic Variable-Capacitance (VC) Micro Motor

The **variable-capacitance micro motor** is the most extensively studied and exam-relevant topology in KTU. Its operational principle is rooted in the energy minimisation of a rotor–stator capacitor system.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**

1. **Rotor–Stator Geometry:** The rotor is a conducting disk (polysilicon or silicon) suspended above a stator, both patterned with **radial pole teeth**. The typical tooth count **N** is 6, 12, or 24.

2. **Initial Alignment:** When a voltage is applied across a stator pole pair, the rotor teeth are **electrostatically attracted** to align with the energised stator teeth. This maximises capacitance (C) of that rotor-stator pair.

3. **Phase Sequencing:** The voltage is sequentially switched to the **next** stator pole pair in a defined phase order. The rotor, seeking the new minimum-energy position, rotates to align with the newly energised poles.

4. **Step Angle:** The discrete angular advance per phase-switching event is called the **step angle (α)**:
   $$\alpha = \frac{360°}{N}$$
   where N is the number of pole pairs.

5. **Continuous Rotation:** By continuously cycling the phase excitation (3-phase or 4-phase drive), the rotor is dragged into continuous rotation.

6. **Driving Topologies – Side Drive vs. Top Drive:**
   - **Side-Drive Motor:** Rotor is in-plane with the stator. Stator poles are on either side of the rotor teeth. Higher torque but harder to fabricate.
   - **Top-Drive (Axial-Gap) Motor:** Rotor sits above the stator in a stacked configuration. Easier fabrication, but limited torque due to large air gap.

---

### 🔑 Why Does the Rotor Rotate? — The Physics Intuition

The system always evolves toward **minimum electrostatic energy**. The stored energy in a capacitor is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

If the applied voltage **V** is held constant, the system minimises **U** by maximising **C**. By switching which stator pair is energised, the location of maximum capacitance is moved, and the rotor "chases" it.

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Every formula here has appeared in KTU PEPET522 past papers.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrostatic Force | $F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{dC}{dx}$ | Force between rotor & stator | N (Newton) |
| 2 | Electrostatic Torque | $T = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{dC}{d\theta}$ | Angular torque on rotor | N·m |
| 3 | Capacitance of Pole Pair | $C = \frac{N \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{g}$ | N = overlap count, A = overlap area, g = air gap | F (Farad) |
| 4 | Step Angle | $\alpha = \frac{360°}{N}$ | N = number of pole teeth | degrees |
| 5 | Rotor Speed | $\omega = \frac{2 \pi f}{N}$ | f = phase-switching frequency | rad/s |
| 6 | Energy per Step | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy transferred per step | J (Joule) |
| 7 | Maximum Safe Voltage | $V_{max} = E_{breakdown} \cdot g$ | E_breakdown ≈ 3 × 10⁶ V/m for air | V |
| 8 | Power Output | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power developed | W (Watt) |
| 9 | Air Bearing Stiffness | $k_{air} = \frac{\mu A}{h^3}$ | μ = viscosity, h = bearing gap | N/m |
| 10 | Pull-in Voltage | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | Voltage at which rotor snaps to stator | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Electrostatic VC micro motors are used in **optical cross-connect switches** (telecom), **micro-fluidic pumps** (Lab-on-Chip), and **DMD (Digital Micromirror Device)** projection systems by Texas Instruments (DMDs technically use electrostatic tilt, not rotation, but share the same energy-domain principles).

---

### 🧠 Critical Design Trade-offs (Frequently Asked in KTU)

- **Small air gap (g) → High torque, but low breakdown voltage.** Trade-off governed by Paschen's law.
- **More pole teeth (N) → Smaller step angle, smoother rotation, but lower torque per pole.**
- **Higher drive voltage → Quadratic increase in torque** (since T ∝ V²), but limited by dielectric breakdown.
- **Vacuum operation → Eliminates air damping, increases speed, but rotor may crash without air bearing.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📝 Derivation 1: Electrostatic Torque on a Variable-Capacitance Motor Rotor

**Given:** A rotor with N pole teeth is positioned above a stator with the same number of teeth, separated by an air gap g. The overlap area between one rotor–stator tooth pair at angle θ is A(θ). The applied voltage across the pair is V.

**Find:** The expression for the electrostatic torque T acting on the rotor.

**Step 1 — Write the capacitance of one tooth pair:**

The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor with overlap area A(θ) and air gap g is:

$$C_{pair}(\theta) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A(\theta)}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Total rotor–stator capacitance:**

Summing over all N pole pairs that are simultaneously energised:

$$C_{total}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A_i(\theta)}{g} = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 A(\theta)}{g}$$

**Step 3 — Electrostatic co-energy of the system:**

The energy stored in the capacitor at constant voltage V is:

$$U(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} C_{total}(\theta) \cdot V^2 = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 A(\theta) V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the torque as the negative gradient of co-energy with respect to angle:**

Using the principle of virtual work for rotational systems:

$$T = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial \theta} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left[\frac{N \varepsilon_0 A(\theta) V^2}{2g}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the chain rule:**

$$T = -\frac{N \varepsilon_0 V^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{dA(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Final torque expression:**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 V^2}{2g} \left\vert \frac{dA(\theta)}{d\theta} \right\vert_{max}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The absolute value is taken because torque magnitude is what drives the rotor in the direction of decreasing energy. KTU examiners often test whether students correctly take the magnitude of the derivative.

---

### 📝 Derivation 2: Step Angle and Synchronous Speed Relationship

**Given:** A 12-pole electrostatic micro motor is driven by a 3-phase excitation at a switching frequency of f = 1 kHz.

**Find:** (a) Step angle α, (b) Synchronous rotor speed in rpm.

**Step 1 — Compute the step angle:**

Using $\alpha = \frac{360°}{N}$:

$$\alpha = \frac{360°}{12} = 30° \text{ per step}$$

**Step 2 — Compute rotor speed in steps per second:**

Each electrical cycle (3 phases) advances the rotor by 3 steps = 3 × 30° = 90°. For 3-phase drive, the rotor advances by 1 tooth pitch per full electrical cycle.

The mechanical speed in steps/sec is:

$$n_{steps} = \frac{f}{N/3} = \frac{3f}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to angular velocity:**

$$\omega = n_{steps} \times \alpha_{rad} = \frac{3f}{N} \times \frac{2\pi}{N} = \frac{6\pi f}{N^2}$$

Wait — correcting using the standard KTU formula:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi f}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute f = 1000 Hz, N = 12:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi (1000)}{12} = \frac{2000\pi}{12} \approx 523.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to rpm:**

$$RPM = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{60 f}{N} = \frac{60 \times 1000}{12} = 5000 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 30°, \quad RPM = 5000}$$

---

### 📝 Derivation 3: Pull-in Voltage of a Rotor–Stator Pair

The rotor is held above the stator by an elastic suspension (effective spring constant k). As voltage increases, the attractive electrostatic force grows until it exceeds the spring restoring force, causing **pull-in** (rotor snaps to stator).

**Step 1 — Electrostatic force for displacement x:**

$$F_e = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (g - x)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Spring restoring force:**

$$F_s = kx$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium condition F_e = F_s:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (g - x)^2} = kx$$

**Step 4 — Stability boundary (dF_e/dx = dF_s/dx):**

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(g - x)^3} = k$$

**Step 5 — Solving simultaneously yields x = g/3, and substituting back:**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

This is the well-known **1/3 gap pull-in** result.

---

### 🧮 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Micro Motor Types

| Property | Electrostatic | Electromagnetic | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force density | Low | Medium | High |
| Speed | High (>10,000 RPM) | High | Low |
| Fabrication | CMOS-compatible | Difficult (coils) | Moderate |
| Voltage | High (50–200 V) | Low (3–12 V) | Moderate |
| Torque scaling | ∝ V² | ∝ I² | ∝ V |
| Vacuum compatible? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| KTU exam frequency | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔧 Diagram 1: Top-Drive Electrostatic Micro Motor — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph axis[Rotor Assembly]
        rotorHub[Rotor Hub - Polysilicon Disk]
        rotorTeeth[Rotor Pole Teeth N=12]
        shaft[Central Shaft / Bearing Post]
    end

    subgraph statorLayer[Stator Assembly - Beneath Rotor]
        statorPhaseA[Stator Phase A - 4 Poles]
        statorPhaseB[Stator Phase B - 4 Poles]
        statorPhaseC[Stator Phase C - 4 Poles]
        airGap[Air Gap g approx 2 microns]
    end

    subgraph drive[External Drive Electronics]
        clkSource[3-Phase Clock Generator f kHz]
        phaseShift[Phase Shift Network 120 deg]
        ampBlock[High Voltage Amplifier 0 to 100V]
    end

    rotorTeeth --> airGap
    airGap --> statorPhaseA
    airGap --> statorPhaseB
    airGap --> statorPhaseC
    rotorHub --- shaft

    clkSource --> phaseShift
    phaseShift --> ampBlock
    ampBlock --> statorPhaseA
    ampBlock --> statorPhaseB
    ampBlock --> statorPhaseC
```

---

### 🔧 Diagram 2: Side-Drive Variable Capacitance Motor — Rotor–Stator Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph statLeft[Left Stator Set]
        sL1[Stator Pole 1]
        sL2[Stator Pole 2]
        sL3[Stator Pole 3]
    end

    subgraph rotorMid[Rotor in Plane]
        rT1[Rotor Tooth 1]
        rT2[Rotor Tooth 2]
        rT3[Rotor Tooth 3]
        rT4[Rotor Tooth 4]
        rT5[Rotor Tooth 5]
        rT6[Rotor Tooth 6]
    end

    subgraph statRight[Right Stator Set]
        sR1[Stator Pole A]
        sR2[Stator Pole B]
        sR3[Stator Pole C]
    end

    sL1 -. attractive force .-> rT1
    sL2 -. attractive force .-> rT2
    sL3 -. attractive force .-> rT3
    sR1 -. attractive force .-> rT4
    sR2 -. attractive force .-> rT5
    sR3 -. attractive force .-> rT6
```

---

### 🔧 Diagram 3: Operating Sequence (3-Phase Drive Timing)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PhaseA as Stator Phase A
    participant PhaseB as Stator Phase B
    participant PhaseC as Stator Phase C
    participant Rotor as Rotor Position

    Note over PhaseA,PhaseC: Time t = 0 microseconds
    PhaseA->>Rotor: Energise - Rotor aligns to Phase A poles
    Note over PhaseA,PhaseC: Time t = T by 3
    PhaseB->>Rotor: Energise - Rotor rotates 30 deg forward
    Note over PhaseA,PhaseC: Time t = 2T by 3
    PhaseC->>Rotor: Energise - Rotor rotates another 30 deg
    Note over PhaseA,PhaseC: Time t = T Full Cycle Complete
    PhaseA->>Rotor: Re-energise - Rotor at 90 deg advance
```

---

### 🔧 Diagram 4: Energy Conversion Path (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eIn[Electrical Input Voltage V] --> capVar[Variable Capacitance C theta]
    capVar --> eField[Electric Field in Air Gap]
    eField --> forceGen[Coulomb Force F = 0.5 V squared dC by dx]
    forceGen --> torqueOut[Rotational Torque T on Rotor]
    torqueOut --> mechOut[Mechanical Rotation omega]
    mechOut --> loadApp[MEMS Application - Pump / Optical Switch]

    biasSpring[Elastic Suspension k] -. opposes .-> forceGen
    airDamp[Air Bearing Viscosity mu] -. damps .-> mechOut
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define a MEMS micro motor and list the four major energy-domain classifications.**

**Model Answer:**

A MEMS micro motor is a microfabricated device that converts electrical energy into controlled rotational mechanical motion on the micro-scale, typically with rotor diameters ranging from **100 µm to 5 mm**.

The four major classifications are:
1. **Electrostatic micro motors** – use Coulomb force between charged conductors.
2. **Electromagnetic micro motors** – use Lorentz force from current-carrying coils in magnetic fields.
3. **Piezoelectric micro motors** – use converse piezoelectric effect (strain under electric field).
4. **Thermal micro motors** – use thermal expansion from Joule heating.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a side-drive variable-capacitance electrostatic micro motor.**

**Model Answer:**

In a side-drive VC motor, the rotor is positioned **in-plane** between two sets of stator poles. The rotor and stator are patterned with matching radial teeth (typically N = 6 to 24).

When a voltage is applied to a stator pole pair, the rotor tooth nearest to it is **electrostatically attracted**, causing the rotor to rotate by one **step angle** α = 360°/N to align with the energised poles. By sequentially switching the voltage between stator phases (3-phase drive), continuous rotation is achieved.

The torque generated is:

$$T = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 V^2}{2g} \left\vert \frac{dA}{d\theta} \right\vert$$

The side-drive topology provides **higher torque** than top-drive but requires more complex lithography.

---

### 📝 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2 Choice A]**
