### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the electrostatic torque developed in a variable-capacitance micro motor with N pole teeth, air gap g, and applied voltage V. Clearly state the assumption of energy minimisation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12-pole electrostatic micro motor operates at 100 V with an air gap of 2 µm. The rotor diameter is 500 µm and the rotor thickness is 5 µm. If the phase-switching frequency is 2 kHz, calculate: (i) Step angle, (ii) Rotor speed in RPM, (iii) Maximum safe operating voltage (use E_breakdown = 3 × 10⁶ V/m).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation of Electrostatic Torque:**

**Step 1:** Consider a rotor–stator pair forming a parallel-plate capacitor with overlap area A(θ) and air gap g. The instantaneous capacitance is:

$$C_{pair}(\theta) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A(\theta)}{g}$$

**Step 2:** For N pole teeth energised simultaneously:

$$C_{total}(\theta) = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 A(\theta)}{g}$$

**Step 3:** The stored electrostatic co-energy at constant V is:

$$U(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} C_{total}(\theta) V^2 = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 A(\theta) V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 4:** **Assumption:** The rotor always moves to minimise the total electrostatic energy of the system (principle of minimum energy).

**Step 5:** The torque is the negative angular gradient of co-energy:

$$T = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial \theta} = -\frac{N \varepsilon_0 V^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{dA(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 6:** Taking magnitude:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 V^2}{2g} \left\vert \frac{dA}{d\theta} \right\vert_{max}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Step angle:**

$$\alpha = \frac{360°}{N} = \frac{360°}{12} = 30°$$

**(ii) Rotor speed in RPM:**

$$RPM = \frac{60 f}{N} = \frac{60 \times 2000}{12} = 10{,}000 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iii) Maximum safe voltage:**

$$V_{max} = E_{breakdown} \times g = (3 \times 10^6) \times (2 \times 10^{-6}) = 6 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> Students frequently make the error of using the formula $RPM = \frac{60 f}{3N}$ for 3-phase motors, which is incorrect. The correct formula is $RPM = \frac{60 f}{N}$ where N is the total number of pole teeth. Also, students often forget to convert the air gap from micrometres to metres before substituting into the breakdown formula — this is a 2-mark deduction trap.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2 Choice B]**

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the working principles, fabrication complexity, and typical applications of electrostatic versus electromagnetic micro motors. Which is more CMOS-compatible and why?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the phase-sequencing operation of a 3-phase, 6-pole electrostatic micro motor. What is the role of the air bearing in such a motor?

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparative Analysis:**

| Aspect | Electrostatic | Electromagnetic |
|---|---|---|
| **Force mechanism** | Coulomb attraction between charged poles | Lorentz force on current in B-field |
| **Torque scaling** | T ∝ V² | T ∝ I² |
| **Operating voltage** | High (50–200 V) | Low (3–12 V) |
| **Current consumption** | Very low (capacitive) | High (inductive) |
| **Fabrication** | CMOS-compatible polysilicon process | Requires coils, magnets — non-CMOS |
| **Power output** | µW range | mW range |
| **Applications** | Optical switches, micro-pumps, DMDs | Hard disk drives, micro-robots |

**CMOS Compatibility:** The **electrostatic** micro motor is far more CMOS-compatible because:
- It uses only **conducting layers** (polysilicon, metal) and **dielectric spacers** (SiO₂).
- It requires **no external magnetic materials** (no permanent magnets, no coils).
- It can be monolithically integrated with on-chip drive electronics using standard IC processes.
- Electromagnetic motors require 3D coils and assembly of permanent magnets, which is incompatible with planar CMOS flow.

**(b) Phase Sequencing and Air Bearing Role:**

**3-Phase, 6-Pole Operation:**

The 6-pole stator has 3 phase windings (A, B, C), each controlling 2 diametrically opposite pole pairs. The rotor also has 6 teeth.

**Phase sequence:**

1. **t = 0:** Phase A energised → Rotor aligns to Phase A poles (position 0°).
2. **t = T/3:** Phase B energised → Rotor rotates 60° (1 step) to align with Phase B.
3. **t = 2T/3:** Phase C energised → Rotor rotates another 60° to align with Phase C.
4. **t = T:** Phase A re-energised → Rotor completes one electrical cycle at 180° advance.

**Block diagram description:**

A **3-phase clock generator** feeds a **phase-shift network** producing 120°-spaced signals, which are amplified to high voltage and routed to the stator pole pairs. The rotor, suspended on an air bearing, follows the moving field.

**Role of the Air Bearing:**

The rotor shaft is supported by a **thin film of air** trapped in micro-machined bearing gaps. The air bearing:
- Provides **near-frictionless support**, enabling high rotational speeds (>10,000 RPM).
- Prevents **direct rotor–stator contact** that would cause wear and stalling.
- Generates a **viscous damping force** that stabilises rotation and prevents lateral wobble.
- The bearing stiffness is given by $k_{air} = \frac{\mu A}{h^3}$, where h is the bearing gap height.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> A common mistake is to draw the 3-phase timing diagram with phases at **0°, 60°, 120°** instead of the correct **0°, 120°, 240°** spacing. This is a 2-mark deduction. Also, students often confuse "number of poles" (3 phase × 2 poles per phase = 6 total) with "number of teeth per phase" — clarify this distinction in your answer.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **micro motor** is a MEMS device converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy.
- ✅ **Electrostatic VC motors** are the most exam-relevant and CMOS-compatible class.
- ✅ **Operating principle:** Rotor chases minimum-energy position by aligning teeth with energised stator poles.
- ✅ **Step angle:** $\alpha = \frac{360°}{N}$, where N = number of pole teeth.
- ✅ **Rotor speed (RPM):** $RPM = \frac{60 f}{N}$, where f = phase-switching frequency.
- ✅ **Torque:** $T = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 V^2}{2g} \vert \frac{dA}{d\theta} \vert$ — scales **quadratically with voltage**.
- ✅ **Pull-in voltage:** $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ — rotor snaps at **1/3 of the air gap**.
- ✅ **Maximum safe voltage:** $V_{max} = E_{breakdown} \times g \approx 3 \times 10^6 \times g$ for air.
- ✅ **Side-drive** motors = higher torque, complex fabrication. **Top-drive** = easier fab, lower torque.
- ✅ **Air bearings** provide frictionless rotor support; bearing stiffness $\propto 1/h^3$.
- ✅ Always state the **principle of minimum electrostatic energy** when explaining rotor motion.
- ✅ **Pitfall:** Never confuse the number of poles (3-phase × 2 = 6) with the rotor tooth count — they can be different.
- ✅ **Pitfall:** Convert all dimensions to SI units (metres, not micrometres) before substituting into formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Micromachining: Micro Needles, Micro Nozzles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bulk Micromachining: Micro Needles, Micro Nozzles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Bulk Micromachining — Foundational Definition

**Bulk Micromachining** is a subtractive microfabrication technique in which three-dimensional micro-structures are carved directly *into* the body (bulk) of a single-crystal substrate — most commonly a (100)-oriented silicon wafer — by selectively removing material using **wet chemical etching** (KOH, TMAH, EDP, CsOH) or **dry etching** (DRIE / Deep Reactive Ion Etching). Selective removal is achieved by patterning a chemically inert mask (typically SiO₂ or Si₃N₄) on the wafer surface, exposing only the desired regions to the etchant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk micromachining is distinct from **surface micromachining** (which builds structures on top of the substrate using deposited thin-film layers). In bulk micromachining, the *substrate itself* becomes the structural material.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Wood-Block Sculptor"

Imagine a jeweler who starts with a solid cube of marble and carves a statue out of it by chipping away unwanted portions. The marble block is the silicon wafer, the protective lacquer painted on the statue's outline is the etch mask, and the hammer-and-chisel is the chemical etchant. Whatever is *not* protected gets dissolved away, and the protected region survives as the final micro-structure. The "shape" of the surviving structure depends critically on *which crystal planes* of the silicon are exposed to the etchant — a concept absolutely central to fabricating micro needles and micro nozzles.

---

## 1.2 Micro Needles — Definition

A **Micro Needle** is a MEMS device with at least one lateral dimension smaller than **1 mm** (typically **50 µm – 500 µm** in length and **10 µm – 200 µm** in base width) and a tip radius often less than **10 µm**, designed to penetrate biological tissue, deliver drugs, sample body fluids, or interface with cells with minimal pain and trauma.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of MEMS Micro Needles:**
> - **Solid Micro Needles** — used as a piercing element to create micro-holes in tissue; coatings or drugs are then applied.
> - **Hollow Micro Needles** — contain an internal micro-channel (lumen) for active fluid infusion or extraction.
> - **In-plane Micro Needles** — the needle axis lies parallel to the wafer surface (suitable for DRIE).
> - **Out-of-plane Micro Needles** — the needle projects vertically out of the wafer (suitable for anisotropic wet etching).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Mosquito's Proboscis"

A micro needle functions exactly like a mosquito's proboscis: it is sharp, slender, biocompatible, and causes minimal sensation upon penetration. Traditional hypodermic needles are millimeters wide and deliver pain; micro needles, being thinner than a human hair in many designs, glide between pain receptors. The fabrication challenge is reproducing this geometry in silicon with batch-process efficiency.

---

## 1.3 Micro Nozzles — Definition

A **Micro Nozzle** is a precisely shaped micro-fluidic channel with a varying cross-section (converging, throat, and diverging sections) used to accelerate, focus, or direct a fluid/gas flow at the micro-scale. Micro nozzles are the heart of **inkjet printheads**, **micro-propulsion thrusters**, **aerosol generators**, and **micro-reactors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two principal geometries of micro nozzles:**
> 1. **Convergent Nozzle** — flow accelerates to a high velocity at the exit (subsonic flow regime). Used in inkjet droplets ejection.
> 2. **Convergent-Divergent (C-D / Laval) Nozzle** — includes a *throat* followed by a *divergent* section to achieve supersonic exhaust velocities. Used in micro-rocket thrusters and supersonic micro-jets.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Hose Nozzle vs The Rocket Engine"

When you partially block the end of a garden hose with your thumb, water shoots out faster — that is a *convergent* micro nozzle. A rocket engine, however, uses a *convergent-divergent* (Laval) nozzle: gases accelerate in the converging section, reach sonic velocity (Mach 1) at the *throat*, and then further accelerate to *supersonic* speeds in the diverging section. MEMS micro nozzles replicate both these principles at chip scale.

---

## 1.4 Why Bulk Micromachining for Needles & Nozzles?

| Criterion | Bulk Micromachining | Surface Micromachining |
|---|---|---|
| Structural material | Single-crystal Si (mechanically robust) | Poly-Si / oxide thin films (fragile) |
| Achievable depth | 10 µm – 500 µm | 1 µm – 5 µm typical |
| Sharpness of tip | Excellent (atomically-defined {111} planes) | Limited by lithography |
| Fluidic pressure tolerance | High | Low |
| Best suited for | Micro needles, micro nozzles, micro channels | Accelerometers, gyroscopes, RF switches |

> [!NOTE]
> The mechanical robustness of bulk-micromachined silicon and its ability to form **atomically sharp pyramidal tips** (a direct consequence of {111} plane etch-stop behaviour) makes it the *only* viable route for fabricating tissue-penetrating micro needles and high-pressure micro nozzles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Crystallographic Foundation of Silicon Bulk Micromachining

Single-crystal silicon has a **diamond cubic lattice**. The three planes most relevant to bulk micromachining are:

- **(100) plane** — wafer surface; etching produces **pyramidal cavities** bounded by four {111} sidewalls.
- **(110) plane** — produces **vertical sidewalls**; ideal for in-plane structures with 90° walls.
- **(111) plane** — extremely slow etching; acts as a natural **etch-stop**.

### The 54.74° Sidewall Angle — Why It Exists

When a (100) wafer is anisotropically etched through a square mask opening aligned with the ⟨110⟩ direction, the four {111} planes emerge as the sidewalls. The angle θ between the (100) surface and any {111} plane is a fixed geometric constant of the silicon lattice:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = 54.7356^{\circ} \approx 54.74^{\circ}$$

This means **every wet-etched pyramidal pit on a (100) wafer has sidewalls inclined at exactly 54.74°** to the wafer surface — and *this* is the angle that gives micro needles their naturally sharp profile.

---

## 2.2 Anisotropic Etching — Etch Rate Hierarchy

The selectivity of an anisotropic etchant is quantified by the **selectivity ratio (SR)**:

$$SR = \frac{R_{(100)}}{R_{(111)}}$$

| Etchant | Temperature | R(100) (µm/hr) | R(111) (µm/hr) | Selectivity Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **KOH (30 wt%)** | 80 °C | 1.4 | 0.005 | ≈ 280 |
| **TMAH (25 wt%)** | 80 °C | 1.0 | 0.015 | ≈ 67 |
| **EDP (S-type)** | 110 °C | 0.8 | 0.012 | ≈ 67 |
| **CsOH (50 wt%)** | 80 °C | 1.6 | 0.04 | ≈ 40 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A selectivity ratio above **100** means the {111} planes act as an *effective etch stop*. KOH, the most common etchant in academic and industrial labs, satisfies this criterion — and is therefore the workhorse chemical for fabricating micro needles.

---

## 2.3 Mask Design Rules for Pyramidal Micro Needles

For a (100) wafer, the **footprint of a square mask opening** must be aligned with the ⟨110⟩ flat. The resulting pit has a base width that is the mask width, but the **pit depth h** depends on the mask half-width a:

$$h = \frac{a}{\tan(54.74^{\circ})} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3} \cdot 2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = a\sqrt{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** If the mask is misaligned by even **1°–2°** from the ⟨110⟩ flat, the pit walls become **asymmetric**, the apex of the pyramid shifts off-center, and the resulting tip becomes blunt or curved. KTU examiners often test this alignment-tolerance concept.

### The Undercutting Phenomenon

When the mask opening is not perfectly aligned, or when an arbitrarily-shaped mask is used, **convex corners** (e.g., at a square mask) get *undercut* by fast-etching planes, producing a **beveled** or **rounded** etch front. Compensation structures (e.g., (100)-oriented bars or triangle-cantilever compensators) are typically added at the mask corners to slow down the undercut.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sidewall angle of pyramidal pit | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = 54.74^{\circ}$ | degrees | Fixed for (100) Si |
| 2 | Pit depth vs mask half-width | $h = a\sqrt{2}$ | µm | Valid for square mask aligned to ⟨110⟩ |
| 3 | Etch depth vs time | $d = R_{(100)} \cdot t$ | µm | Linear until {111} stop |
| 4 | Selectivity ratio | $SR = R_{(100)} / R_{(111)}$ | dimensionless | Higher = sharper etch stop |
| 5 | Convex corner undercut rate | $R_{\text{conv}} \approx R_{(100)} \cdot \cos\alpha$ | µm/min | α = mask-edge angle |
| 6 | Required mask undercut compensation length | $L_{c} = R_{\text{conv}} \cdot t_{\text{total}}$ | µm | Adds sacrificial tabs |
| 7 | Mass flow rate through a nozzle (compressible) | $\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, V$ | kg/s | ρ = density, A = throat area |
| 8 | Continuity for incompressible nozzle | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | m³/s | Mass conservation |
| 9 | Mach number at Laval throat | $M = 1$ | — | Sonic choking condition |
| 10 | Exit Mach number (isentropic) | $\dfrac{A}{A^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{M}\!\left[\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\!\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)\right]^{\!\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | — | γ = ratio of specific heats |
| 11 | Thrust of micro rocket | $F = \dot{m} V_{e} + (P_{e}-P_{a}) A_{e}$ | N | Cold-gas approximation |
| 12 | Capillary pressure (Laplace) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta_{c}}{r}$ | Pa | γ_LV = surface tension, r = radius |
| 13 | Hagen-Poiseuille flow | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Micro-channel flow rate |
| 14 | Reynolds number in micro-channel | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D_h}{\mu}$ | — | Typically Re < 100 in MEMS |
| 15 | Hydraulic diameter | $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P_{\text{wet}}}$ | m | For non-circular channels |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical micro needles** are used in **painless insulin delivery**, **DNA vaccination patches**, and **intracellular single-cell probing**.
- **Micro nozzles** drive **thermal-bubble inkjet printheads** (HP, Canon), **piezoelectric inkjet printheads** (Epson), and **cold-gas micro-thrusters** for cube-satellite attitude control.
- The 54.74° sidewall geometry governs the **droplet ejection cone angle** in inkjet printing and the **tissue-penetration trajectory angle** in microneedle arrays.
- Convergent-divergent micro nozzles are central to **MIT's micro-rock reactor** programs and to **ESA-funded MEMS propulsion** research for nano-satellites.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the 54.74° Pyramidal Sidewall Angle

We will derive the sidewall angle from first principles using the Miller indices of the relevant planes.

### Step 1: Identify the planes and their normal vectors

The (100) plane has a normal vector:

$$\vec{n}_{(100)} = (1, 0, 0)$$

The {111} family includes four equivalent planes; the one tilted toward +X, +Y, +Z has the normal:

$$\vec{n}_{(111)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1, 1, 1)$$

### Step 2: Compute the angle between the two plane normals

The angle between two plane normals equals the angle between the planes themselves. Using the dot product:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{\vec{n}_{(100)} \cdot \vec{n}_{(111)}}{\vert \vec{n}_{(100)} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{n}_{(111)} \vert}$$

Substituting the components:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{(1)(1) + (0)(1) + (0)(1)}{\sqrt{1^{2}+0^{2}+0^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2}}}$$

$$\cos\phi = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

### Step 3: Convert to the angle between the planes

The angle φ between the *normals* is:

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356^{\circ}$$

Because the (100) plane is the *horizontal* wafer surface and the (111) plane is the *sloped* sidewall, the **sidewall inclination angle θ = φ = 54.74°**.

### Step 4: Express the depth-width relationship

For a square mask of half-width *a* aligned to ⟨110⟩, the (111) sidewalls descend at angle θ. The pit depth h and the mask half-width a are related by:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{a}{h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac{a}{\tan(54.74^{\circ})}$$

Evaluating $\tan(54.74^{\circ}) = \sqrt{2}$:

$$h = \frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad a = h\sqrt{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two equivalent forms are interchangeable: $h = a/\sqrt{2}$ when *a* is the half-width, and $h = a\sqrt{2}$ when *a* is the full-width. KTU questions often use the full-width form. Always state the convention.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Pit Depth & Etch Time

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A (100) silicon wafer is patterned with a square mask opening of side **200 µm**, perfectly aligned to the ⟨110⟩ direction. The wafer is etched in **30 wt% KOH at 80 °C** (R(100) = 1.4 µm/hr). Calculate:
1. The depth of the pyramidal pit.
2. The time required to reach the {111} etch-stop.
3. The volume of silicon removed.

### Solution:

**Part 1 — Pit depth:**

The full mask side is 200 µm, so the half-width is a = 100 µm. Applying the formula:

$$h = a \cdot \sqrt{2} = 100 \times \sqrt{2}$$

$$h = 100 \times 1.4142$$

$$h = 141.42 \;\mu\text{m}$$

**Part 2 — Etch time:**

$$t = \frac{h}{R_{(100)}} = \frac{141.42 \;\mu\text{m}}{1.4 \;\mu\text{m/hr}}$$

$$t = 101.01 \;\text{hr} \approx 101 \;\text{hours}$$

**Part 3 — Volume of silicon removed:**

A square pyramidal pit has volume = (1/3) × base area × height. The base of the pit (at the {111} plane intersection) is a square of side $2a = 200$ µm.

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \times (200 \times 200) \times 141.42 \;\mu\text{m}^{3}$$

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \times 40000 \times 141.42 \;\mu\text{m}^{3}$$

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \times 5{,}656{,}800 \;\mu\text{m}^{3}$$

$$V = 1{,}885{,}600 \;\mu\text{m}^{3} \approx 1.886 \times 10^{6} \;\mu\text{m}^{3}$$

Converting to mm³ (1 mm³ = 10⁹ µm³):

$$V = 1.886 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{mm}^{3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that the etch time is *very* long (~4 days). In practice, this is why KTU problems typically give you a *desired depth* and ask for the mask width — because the *time* is often the limiting engineering constraint.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Micro Nozzle Throat Sizing

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A micro convergent-divergent (Laval) nozzle in a MEMS micro-thruster has a throat diameter of **300 µm**. The propellant is gaseous nitrogen ($\gamma = 1.4$). Find:
1. The throat area A*.
2. The exit Mach number if the area ratio A/A* = 3.0.
3. The exit velocity if stagnation temperature T₀ = 300 K.

### Solution:

**Part 1 — Throat area:**

$$A^{*} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{*2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (300 \times 10^{-6})^{2}$$

$$A^{*} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 9 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$A^{*} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{m}^{2} \approx 70.7 \;\mu\text{m}^{2} \cdot 1000 \approx 70{,}686 \;\mu\text{m}^{2}$$

**Part 2 — Exit Mach number from area-Mach relation:**

$$\frac{A}{A^{*}} = \frac{1}{M}\!\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\!\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)\right]^{\!\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

With γ = 1.4 and A/A* = 3.0, this transcendental equation gives M_e ≈ **2.64** (supersonic).

**Part 3 — Exit velocity:**

The exit temperature using the isentropic relation:

$$T_e = \frac{T_0}{1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_e^2}$$

$$T_e = \frac{300}{1 + 0.2 \times (2.64)^2} = \frac{300}{1 + 0.2 \times 6.97} = \frac{300}{1 + 1.394} = \frac{300}{2.394}$$

$$T_e = 125.3 \;\text{K}$$

The speed of sound at T_e:

$$a_e = \sqrt{\gamma R T_e}$$

where R for N₂ = 296.8 J/(kg·K):

$$a_e = \sqrt{1.4 \times 296.8 \times 125.3}$$

$$a_e = \sqrt{52{,}067}$$

$$a_e = 228.2 \;\text{m/s}$$

The exit velocity:

$$V_e = M_e \cdot a_e = 2.64 \times 228.2$$

$$V_e = 602.4 \;\text{m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This exit velocity (≈ 600 m/s) is the *exhaust velocity* of the cold-gas micro-thruster, and is the key parameter determining thrust via $F = \dot{m} V_e$. The high-aspect-ratio silicon micro-nozzle channels that achieve this are *only* fabricable through precision bulk micromachining of (110) or (100) wafers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Etch Chemistry Selection for Needles & Nozzles

| Application | Recommended Etchant | Justification | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sharp solid micro needle (out-of-plane)** | **KOH 30 wt%, 80 °C** | Highest {100}/{111} selectivity (~280) gives atomically sharp tip | CMOS-incompatible (K⁺ contamination) |
| **Hollow micro needle** | **TMAH 25 wt%, 80 °C** | CMOS-compatible; allows on-chip integration of electronics | Lower selectivity (~67) → less sharp tip |
| **Deep vertical micro-channel (in-plane needle / nozzle)** | **DRIE (Bosch process)** | Vertical sidewalls achievable; aspect ratios > 20:1 | Expensive; scalloping on sidewalls |
| **Micro nozzle with smooth throat** | **DRIE + post-etch thermal oxidation** | Oxidation smooths scallops; provides sub-µm throat precision | Adds process steps |
| **Conical micro nozzle** | **KOH + {111} etch-stop on (100)** | Conical profile matches C-D nozzle geometry | Tip blunting from undercut |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Micro Needle Fabrication (Out-of-Plane, Wet Etched)

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete process flow for fabricating an out-of-plane **solid silicon micro needle** by anisotropic wet etching.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: 100 Silicon Wafer] --> B[Thermal Oxidation: Grow 1 micrometer SiO2 mask layer]
    B --> C[Photolithography Step 1: Spin coat positive photoresist]
    C --> D[Soft Bake: 90 deg C for 10 minutes]
    D --> E[Mask Alignment: Align square mask to 110 flat of wafer]
    E --> F[UV Exposure: Dose 100 mJ per cm squared]
    F --> G[Development: Dip in MF319 for 60 seconds]
    G --> H[Hard Bake: 120 deg C for 20 minutes]
    H --> I[BOE Etch: Pattern SiO2 mask to expose silicon]
    I --> J[Photoresist Strip: Acetone plus IPA rinse]
    J --> K[Anisotropic Wet Etch: KOH 30 percent at 80 deg C for 4 hours]
    K --> L[111 Plane Etch Stop: Four sidewalls form pyramidal pit]
    L --> M[Sharp Tip Emerges: 54.74 deg sidewall angle achieved]
    M --> N[Mask Removal: BOE dip to strip SiO2]
    N --> O[Final: Out of plane micro needle array ready]
    O --> P[End: Inspect under SEM, measure tip radius]
```

### Cross-Sectional Architecture of the Final Micro Needle (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Wafer Top Surface 100] --> A2[SiO2 Mask Square Opening]
    A2 --> A3[Pyramidal Cavity 111 Sidewalls]
    A3 --> A4[Apex: Atomically Sharp Tip]
    A4 --> A5[Sidewall Angle: 54.74 deg from horizontal]
    A5 --> A6[Height of pyramid: h = a times sqrt 2]
    A6 --> A7[Base width: 2a equals mask opening]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hollow Micro Needle Fabrication

A **hollow** micro needle requires a micro-channel lumen running through the needle shaft. The typical process combines front-side KOH etching (to form the tip) with back-side DRIE (to form the fluidic channel).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Start: Double Sided Polished 100 Wafer] --> B2[Front Side: Thermal Oxidation 1 micrometer]
    B2 --> B3[Front Side: Photolithography for TIP mask]
    B3 --> B4[Front Side: BOE etch of SiO2 to define tip opening]
    B4 --> B5[Back Side: Photolithography for LUMEN mask aligned to front]
    B5 --> B6[Back Side: DRIE Bosch process to form micro channel]
    B6 --> B7[Front Side: KOH 30 percent etch forms pyramidal tip]
    B7 --> B8[Thinning: KOH reaches back side micro channel and opens lumen]
    B8 --> B9[Final: Hollow micro needle with internal fluidic path]
    B9 --> B10[Anodically bond to glass substrate for fluidic connector]
    B10 --> B11[End: Ready for biofluidic testing]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Convergent-Divergent Micro Nozzle

A Laval (C-D) micro nozzle requires precise throat control. Bulk micromachining of (110) silicon gives vertical sidewalls ideal for cylindrical throat geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Start: 110 Silicon Wafer vertical sidewall plane] --> C2[Thermal Oxidation 1.5 micrometer mask]
    C2 --> C3[Photolithography Step A: Define NOZZLE inlet converging section]
    C3 --> C4[Photolithography Step B: Define THROAT constriction]
    C4 --> C5[Photolithography Step C: Define NOZZLE outlet diverging section]
    C5 --> C6[DRIE Bosch: Etch channels 200 micrometer deep]
    C6 --> C7[Thermal Oxidation 0.5 micrometer: Smooth DRIE scallops]
    C7 --> C8[Anodic Bonding: Seal to Pyrex glass cover]
    C8 --> C9[Inlet port: Drill through glass for propellant feed]
    C9 --> C10[Final: C-D micro nozzle assembly with sonic throat]
    C10 --> C11[End: Connect to micro thruster test rig]
```

### Functional Block Diagram of a Micro Thruster with Laval Nozzle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Propellant Reservoir N2 Gas] --> D2[Micro Pressure Regulator]
    D2 --> D3[Micro Filter 5 micrometer]
    D3 --> D4[Inlet Manifold of Micro Nozzle]
    D4 --> D5[Converging Section: Area A1]
    D5 --> D6[Throat: A star equals sonic condition M equals 1]
    D6 --> D7[Diverging Section: A2 greater than A star for supersonic]
    D7 --> D8[Exit Plane: V exit around 600 m per second]
    D8 --> D9[Thrust Force: F equals m dot times V exit]
    D9 --> D10[Cube Satellite Attitude Control Output]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — Mask Orientation Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Mask Opening Shape on 100 Wafer] --> E2{Square Aligned to 110 Direction}
    E2 -- Yes --> E3[Symmetric 4 sided pyramid: 54.74 deg sidewalls]
    E2 -- No / Misaligned --> E4[Asymmetric pit: Blunted and tilted tip]
    E1 --> E5{Rectangular Mask}
    E5 --> F1[Long aspect ratio: Ridge like blade needle]
    E5 --> F2[Short aspect ratio: Elongated trough]
    E1 --> E6{Circular Mask}
    E6 --> G1[Undercut dominant: Convex corners beveled]
    E6 --> G2[Compensation tabs required to preserve central tip]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: Define bulk micromachining. List three advantages of bulk micromachining over surface micromachining for the fabrication of micro needles.**

**Model Answer:**

Bulk micromachining is a subtractive MEMS fabrication process in which three-dimensional microstructures are formed by selectively etching *into* the bulk of a single-crystal silicon wafer using anisotropic wet etchants (KOH, TMAH, EDP) or dry etchants (DRIE). The etched regions are defined by a lithographically patterned etch mask (typically SiO₂ or Si₃N₄).

**Three advantages for micro needle fabrication:**

1. **Atomically sharp tip formation** — the natural etch-stop behaviour of {111} planes in (100) Si produces tips with curvature radii < 50 nm, far sharper than lithographically defined surface-micromachined structures.

2. **High mechanical robustness** — the needle is carved from a single piece of single-crystal silicon, providing the structural strength needed to penetrate biological tissue without fracture.

3. **Deep, high-aspect-ratio geometries** — etch depths of 100 µm – 500 µm are routinely achieved, which is the typical length required for transdermal microneedle arrays.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: Why is the (111) crystal plane of silicon called a natural etch-stop in KOH etching? Quantify its significance using a numerical example.**

**Model Answer:**

The (111) plane is called a natural etch-stop because the etch rate of KOH on {111} planes is approximately **400 times slower** than on {100} planes (typical values: R(100) = 1.4 µm/hr vs R(111) = 0.003 µm/hr at 30 wt% KOH, 80 °C). This enormous selectivity arises from the high atomic packing density of the {111} plane (8 atoms/nm²) — every surface atom has three back-bonds, leaving only one dangling bond, which the OH⁻ ions must break before removing the atom. Etching effectively *stalls* at {111} surfaces, producing atomically flat sidewalls.

**Numerical Example:**

For a (100) wafer etched through a 200 µm × 200 µm square mask, R(100) = 1.4 µm/hr. The pit reaches {111} etch-stop in time:

$$t = \frac{h}{R_{(100)}} = \frac{141.42 \;\mu\text{m}}{1.4 \;\mu\text{m/hr}} \approx 101 \;\text{hours}$$

After this point, further etching produces negligible depth change (R(111) ≈ 0.003 µm/hr → in 1 hour, only 3 nm is removed), confirming the etch-stop behaviour.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Internal Choice, Part B Model]**

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the fabrication of a solid out-of-plane silicon micro needle array using anisotropic wet etching. Include the role of mask orientation, the origin of the 54.74° sidewall angle, and the steps of photolithography. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A (100) silicon wafer is patterned with a square mask opening of side 250 µm, perfectly aligned to the ⟨110⟩ flat. Calculate the pit depth, the etch time in KOH (30 wt% at 80 °C, R(100) = 1.4 µm/hr), and the volume of silicon removed. Discuss why this etch time is a manufacturing bottleneck. **[(7 marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Wafer Selection:** A p-type (100)-oriented single-crystal silicon wafer of thickness ~525 µm is chosen because (100) wafers are industry-standard, low-cost, and have well-characterized anisotropic etching behaviour.

**Step 2 — Mask Growth:** A 1 µm thick SiO₂ layer is thermally grown at 1100 °C in a wet oxidation furnace. This SiO₂ acts as the etch mask, since KOH etches SiO₂ at only ~1 nm/min — about 1000× slower than Si (100).

**Step 3 — Photolithography:**
- HMDS priming for photoresist adhesion.
- Spin-coat positive photoresist (e.g., AZ 4620) at 3000 rpm.
- Soft-bake at 90 °C for 20 min.
- Align the photomask on a **mask aligner** such that the square opening edges are precisely parallel to the **primary flat** of the wafer (the primary flat lies along the ⟨110⟩ direction by industrial convention).
- UV expose at ~100 mJ/cm².
- Develop in MF-319 for 60 s.
- Hard-bake at 120 °C for 20 min to harden the resist.

**Step 4 — Mask Pattern Transfer:** The wafer is dipped in **Buffered Oxide Etch (BOE)** for ~10 minutes to remove the exposed SiO₂, revealing bare silicon in the square opening.

**Step 5 — Resist Strip:** The photoresist is stripped in acetone, leaving a patterned SiO₂ mask on the Si surface.

**Step 6 — Anisotropic Etching:** The wafer is immersed in 30 wt% KOH at 80 °C with constant stirring. The exposed Si (100) etches at 1.4 µm/hr in the ⟨100⟩ direction. As the etch progresses, four {111} sidewalls are exposed (one on each side of the square opening) — these have an etch rate 400× slower, and effectively act as a self-limiting etch stop.

**Step 7 — Etch-Stop and Tip Formation:** The four {111} planes converge to a single apex point, producing a **square pyramidal pit with a sharp tip** at the centre. The tip is atomically sharp because the {111} planes meet at a mathematically perfect apex.

**Step 8 — Mask Removal:** The SiO₂ mask is stripped in BOE, leaving an array of pyramidal pits (or pyramidal spikes if the mask is on the wafer's *underside* — the inverse geometry).

**Origin of 54.74°:** Geometrically derived in Section 3.1 of these notes — the angle between (100) and {111} planes in the diamond cubic lattice is:

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.74^{\circ}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:** Square mask side L = 250 µm, so half-width a = 125 µm. Etchant: 30 wt% KOH at 80 °C, R(100) = 1.4 µm/hr.

**Step 1 — Pit depth:**

$$h = a \cdot \sqrt{2} = 125 \times 1.4142 = 176.78 \;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Etch time:**

$$t = \frac{h}{R_{(100)}} = \frac{176.78}{1.4} = 126.27 \;\text{hours} \approx 5.26 \;\text{days}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of silicon removed:**

Base of pit = full mask opening = 250 × 250 µm². Pit is a square pyramid.

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \times (250)^{2} \times 176.78 \;\mu\text{m}^{3}$$

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \times 62500 \times 176.78$$

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \times 11{,}048{,}750$$

$$V = 3{,}682{,}917 \;\mu\text{m}^{3} \approx 3.683 \times 10^{6} \;\mu\text{m}^{3}$$

Converting: $V \approx 3.683 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{mm}^{3}$.

**Step 4 — Manufacturing Bottleneck Discussion:**

The etch time of 126 hours is *prohibitive* in a high-volume production environment. Several mitigation strategies exist:

1. **Higher temperature KOH etching** — raising temperature from 80 °C to 100 °C increases R(100) to ~3.4 µm/hr, halving the time, but accelerates mask erosion.
2. **TMAH etching** — comparable rates, but CMOS-compatible for monolithic integration.
3. **DRIE (Bosch process)** — gives much higher etch rates (5–10 µm/min, or 300–600 µm/hr), but at the cost of sidewall verticality and scallop roughness.
4. **Batch processing** — etching 25 wafers in a single KOH bath in parallel improves throughput per fab-day.
5. **Thinner starting wafers** — using 300 µm SOI wafers limits the required etch depth, since the buried oxide acts as an *engineered* etch stop.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Internal Choice Alternative]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the operating principle of a convergent-divergent (Laval) micro nozzle. Using the area-Mach relation, derive the exit Mach number for a nozzle with A/A* = 2.5, given γ = 1.4. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** Describe the bulk micromachining process flow for fabricating a micro nozzle array in (110) silicon. Discuss why DRIE (Bosch process) is preferred over KOH for this application. **[(7 marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Operating Principle of a Laval Nozzle:**

A Convergent-Divergent (C-D) nozzle, also called a **Laval nozzle** (after Swedish engineer Gustaf de Laval, 1889), accelerates a compressible fluid from a low velocity at the inlet to supersonic speeds at the exit. The nozzle has three distinct sections:

1. **Converging Section** — the cross-sectional area decreases from the inlet to the throat. The flow velocity increases and the static pressure decreases (conservation of mass + Bernoulli's principle generalized for compressible flow).

2. **Throat** — the minimum cross-section. For supersonic operation, the Mach number reaches **exactly M = 1** at the throat. This is the **choked flow** condition, and the mass flow rate is maximized for the given upstream stagnation conditions.

3. **Diverging Section** — the cross-section *increases* beyond the throat. Counter-intuitively, for supersonic flow, this further *increases* the velocity and *decreases* the static pressure (in subsonic diverging flow, the opposite occurs). The exit Mach number M_e depends solely on the area ratio A_e/A* and the specific heat ratio γ.

**Derivation of Exit Mach Number for A/A* = 2.5, γ = 1.4:**

The isentropic area-Mach relation is:

$$\frac{A}{A^{*}} = \frac{1}{M}\!\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\!\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)\right]^{\!\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

Substituting γ = 1.4:

$$\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2} = 1.2, \quad \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2} = 0.2, \quad \tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)} = \tfrac{1.2}{0.4} = 3$$

So the equation becomes:

$$\frac{A}{A^{*}} = \frac{1}{M}\!\left[\frac{1}{1.2}\left(1 + 0.2 M^{2}\right)\right]^{3}$$

For supersonic flow, the equation has two solutions for each A/A*: a subsonic and a supersonic one. We seek the supersonic solution.

Setting A/A* = 2.5 and solving iteratively:

- **Try M = 2.0:** Compute $\frac{1}{2.0}\left[\frac{1+0.8}{1.2}\right]^3 = 0.5 \times (1.5)^3 = 0.5 \times 3.375 = 1.6875$ (too low)
- **Try M = 2.5:** Compute $\frac{1}{2.5}\left[\frac{1+1.25}{1.2}\right]^3 = 0.4 \times (1.875)^3 = 0.4 \times 6.5918 = 2.6367$ (slightly high)
- **Try M = 2.45:** Compute $\frac{1}{2.45}\left[\frac{1+1.2005}{1.2}\right]^3 = 0.408 \times (1.8337)^3 = 0.408 \times 6.166 = 2.515$ (very close)
- **Try M = 2.44:** Compute $\frac{1}{2.44}\left[\frac{1+1.1912}{1.2}\right]^3 = 0.4098 \times (1.826)^3 = 0.4098 \times 6.090 = 2.496$ (slightly low)

Interpolating linearly between M = 2.44 and M = 2.45:

$$M_e \approx 2.44 + \frac{2.5 - 2.496}{2.515 - 2.496} \times 0.01 \approx 2.442$$

**Exit Mach number M_e ≈ 2.44** (supersonic).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Fabrication of a Micro Nozzle Array in (110) Silicon:**

**Step 1 — Wafer Selection:** A double-sided polished (110)-oriented silicon wafer is used. (110) wafers are chosen because the (110) plane contains the ⟨111⟩ direction *in-plane*, so {111} planes appear as *vertical* sidewalls during anisotropic etching — ideal for vertical nozzle walls.

**Step 2 — Mask Growth:** 1.5 µm of thermal SiO₂ is grown at 1100 °C.

**Step 3 — Photolithography — Inlet/Inlet-Cover:** The first lithography step patterns the **inlet plenum** and **inlet converging channel** using a thick photoresist (AZ 4620, 10 µm).

**Step 4 — BOE Etch:** Patterned SiO₂ is etched in BOE to expose silicon in the nozzle inlet region.

**Step 5 — DRIE — Converging Section:** The wafer is loaded into a DRIE (Bosch process) chamber. Etch parameters: SF₆ (etch) and C₄F₈ (passivation) alternating, 200 W coil power, 30 mTorr. The converging section is etched to a depth of ~250 µm.

**Step 6 — Second Lithography — Throat:** A second lithography step patterns a narrow constriction (the throat, e.g., 50 µm wide) using a thick resist.

**Step 7 — DRIE — Throat Etch:** The throat is etched to its full depth (~250 µm). DRIE gives vertical sidewalls here, critical for sonic (M = 1) flow at the throat.

**Step 8 — Third Lithography — Diverging Section:** A third lithography step patterns the diverging (expansion) section with a width that grows from 50 µm (at the throat) to 250 µm (at the exit).

**Step 9 — DRIE — Diverging Section Etch:** Etched to 250 µm depth.

**Step 10 — Sacrificial Oxide:** 0.5 µm of thermal SiO₂ is grown and stripped (sacrificial) to smooth DRIE scallops and passivate sidewalls.

**Step 11 — Anodic Bonding:** A Pyrex 7740 glass wafer with pre-drilled inlet/outlet ports is anodically bonded (400 V, 400 °C) to seal the silicon micro channels.

**Step 12 — Dicing & Testing:** The wafer is diced into individual nozzle chips and characterized for flow rate vs. pressure differential.

**Why DRIE is Preferred over KOH for Micro Nozzles:**

| Parameter | DRIE (Bosch) | KOH Wet Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Sidewall profile | **Vertical (90°)** | 54.74° sloped (unsuitable for cylindrical throat) |
| Aspect ratio | **> 20:1** | ~ 1:1 limited by {111} planes |
| Throat dimensional control | Sub-µm lithographic | Limited by undercut |
| Surface roughness | ~ 0.5 µm scallops (smoothable by oxidation) | Atomically smooth |
| Mask compatibility | Photoresist, SiO₂, metal | SiO₂ only |
| Process repeatability | Excellent | Sensitive to convection, bubble formation |
| CMOS compatibility | Yes | No (K⁺ contamination) |

The **vertical sidewalls** and **high aspect ratio** of DRIE are absolutely essential for fabricating the throat and diverging section of a Laval nozzle, where the cross-sectional area must vary smoothly and predictably in the direction of flow.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bulk Micromachining Questions:**
>
> 1. **The "factor of 2" trap in pit depth:** Students often confuse whether *a* in $h = a\sqrt{2}$ is the *half-width* or the *full-width* of the mask opening. KTU examiners *deliberately* swap this in different sub-parts. Always clarify: "a = half-width" or "a = full-width" before computing.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** µm to mm, hours to seconds, m/s to km/s — at least 1–2 marks are routinely lost on unit errors. Always carry units through every algebraic step.
>
> 3. **The {111} etch-stop misnomer:** Etching does NOT *stop completely* on {111}; it slows by a factor of ~400. KOH still etches {111} at ~3 nm/hr. So calling it a "perfect" etch-stop is a half-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Mask alignment to ⟨110⟩:** The 54.74° pyramidal pit geometry is *only* valid when the mask edges are aligned to ⟨110⟩. Misalignment yields asymmetric pits. KTU exams always test whether you mention this.
>
> 5. **Stating KOH "etches SiO₂" or "doesn't etch SiO₂":** The truth is *partial* — KOH etches SiO₂ at ~1 nm/min, which is non-negligible over 100+ hour etches. A *precise* answer says "KOH etches SiO₂ ~1000× slower than Si, providing a usable mask for typical etch times."

---

## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

### Bulk Micromachining — Micro Needles & Micro Nozzles

- ⭐ **Bulk micromachining** = subtractive etching *into* the silicon wafer body.
- ⭐ **Anisotropic etchants**: KOH (most common), TMAH (CMOS-compatible), EDP, CsOH.
- ⭐ **Isotropic etchants**: HNA (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH), XeF₂ (dry).
- ⭐ **The Magic Number: 54.74°** = sidewall angle of {111} sidewalls in a (100) etch pit. Derived from $\cos^{-1}(1/\sqrt{3})$.
- ⭐ **Selectivity Ratio (SR)** = R(100)/R(111); for KOH ≈ 280, for TMAH ≈ 67.
- ⭐ **Pit Depth Formula**: $h = a\sqrt{2}$ (a = half-width) OR $h = a/\sqrt{2}$ depending on convention.
- ⭐ **Mask alignment to ⟨110⟩ primary flat** is critical for symmetric pyramidal pits.
- ⭐ **Solid micro needles**: formed by KOH pyramidal pit OR by DRIE in-plane blade.
- ⭐ **Hollow micro needles**: combine front-side KOH tip + back-side DRIE lumen.
- ⭐ **Micro Nozzle Types**: Convergent (inkjet) vs. Convergent-Divergent (Laval, supersonic).
- ⭐ **Sonic Condition at Throat**: Mach number M = 1 at A = A* (choked flow).
- ⭐ **Area-Mach Relation** is a transcendental equation solved iteratively or via charts.
- ⭐ **DRIE (Bosch process)** is preferred for vertical micro nozzle walls; uses alternating SF₆/C₄F₈ plasma cycles.
- ⭐ **Mass Flow Rate**: $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ at the throat.
- ⭐ **Laval Nozzle Thrust**: $F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$ (cold-gas approximation).
- ⭐ **Micro-channel flow regime**: typically laminar (Re < 100); described by **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**.
- ⭐ **Anodic bonding of Si to Pyrex glass** seals the fluidic channel and provides optical/electrical access.
- ⭐ **Undercutting at convex mask corners** requires **compensation structures** (e.g., (100)-oriented bars) to preserve tip sharpness.
- ⭐ **Applications**: painless drug delivery, blood sampling, inkjet printheads, micro-thrusters for cube satellites, micro-reactors, aerosol generators.
- ⭐ **Critical alignment tolerance**: ±1° to ±2° from ⟨110⟩ for sharp tips; broader tolerance acceptable for nozzles.
- ⭐ **Engineering trade-off**: KOH gives atomically smooth, sharp features but slow; DRIE gives fast, deep, vertical features but rough (scallop ~ 0.5 µm).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Etching Techniques: Dry Etching, Plasma Etching, Wet Etching; High Aspect-Ratio Processes: LIGA Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Etching Techniques: Dry Etching, Plasma Etching, Wet Etching; High Aspect-Ratio Processes: LIGA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Etching in MEMS Fabrication?

**Etching** is the selective removal of material (silicon, silicon dioxide, metals, polymers) from a substrate using chemical, electrochemical, or physical means, governed by a pre-patterned mask. It is one of the two fundamental patterning steps in MEMS and IC fabrication (the other being deposition/lithography).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *Etching is a top–down material removal process in which unprotected regions of a thin film or substrate are dissolved (chemically), reacted with energetic species (physico-chemically), or sputtered away (physically) to transfer a two-dimensional lithographic pattern into a third vertical dimension, thereby defining the structural geometry of MEMS devices.*

In the KTU 2024 framework, etching techniques are broadly classified as shown in Figure 1.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a sculptor carving a miniature temple from a block of granite using a chisel and a stone-cutter's acid paste.

- **Wet etching** is like the **acid paste** — it dissolves the stone chemically wherever the protective wax mask has been scratched off. Cheap, fast, but the acid seeps sideways under the wax (called *undercut*).
- **Dry (plasma) etching** is like the **directed chisel** — ionized gas molecules (the "plasma chisel") strike the surface vertically. The strike is directional, so walls stay straight and vertical.
- **LIGA** is like building the temple from **giant sugar-cube molds cast in metal** — you first build a tall sacrificial template, fill it with metal, and then dissolve the template away. This is how you get skyscraper-tall, pencil-thin walls.

## 1.2 Classification of Etching Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Syllabus Mapping**
> The following sub-topics are explicitly listed:
> 1. **Wet Etching** (chemical)
> 2. **Dry Etching**
>    - **Plasma Etching** (chemical dominant)
>    - **Reactive Ion Etching — RIE** (physical + chemical)
>    - **Ion Beam Etching — IBE** (purely physical)
>    - **Deep Reactive Ion Etching — DRIE** (Bosch process)
> 3. **High Aspect-Ratio (HAR) MEMS Processes**
>    - **LIGA** (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung)
>    - **HAR Si DRIE**, **UV-LIGA**, **SU-8 lithography**

### 1.2.1 Wet Chemical Etching

**Definition:** Material removal by immersion of the substrate in a liquid-phase etchant (e.g., KOH, TMAH, HF, HNO₃).

**Key Sub-Types:**

| Sub-Type | Mechanism | Profile Shape | Direction |
|----------|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Isotropic wet etching** | Pure chemical dissolution, no crystallographic preference | Round, undercut | Equal in all directions |
| **Anisotropic wet etching** | Etch rate depends on crystal plane orientation | V-grooves, pyramidal pits, vertical sidewalls | Plane-dependent |
| **Electrochemical etching** | Bias-assisted dissolution (Porous Si, ECE) | Pore formation | Bias-controlled |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Magic Numbers" of Silicon Anisotropic Etching**
> For KOH (24 wt%, 80 °C) on silicon:
> - Etch rate (100) ≈ **0.99 µm/min**
> - Etch rate (111) ≈ **0.005 µm/min**
> - **Selectivity (100):(111) ≈ 200:1**
> This enormous anisotropy is what allows fabrication of V-grooves, pyramidal cavities, and suspended diaphragms for pressure sensors.

### 1.2.2 Dry Etching & Plasma Etching

**Definition:** Material removal by gaseous reactive species and/or energetic ion bombardment in a low-pressure environment.

| Technique | Dominant Mechanism | Pressure (Torr) | Anisotropy |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Plasma Etching (PE)** | Chemical (radicals) | 0.1 – 10 | Isotropic to mildly anisotropic |
| **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** | Chemical + Ion bombardment | 0.01 – 0.1 | Anisotropic |
| **Sputter/Ion Beam Etching (IBE)** | Pure physical momentum transfer | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻³ | Highly anisotropic, non-selective |
| **Deep RIE (DRIE – Bosch)** | Alternating etch + passivation | 0.01 – 0.05 | Highly anisotropic, vertical walls > 500 µm deep |
| **Cryogenic Etching** | Chemical at –110 °C | 0.01 – 0.05 | Anisotropic, smooth sidewalls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plasma Etching vs. Reactive Ion Etching — the KTU board favourite distinction**
> - **Plasma Etching** operates at higher pressure; the sample sits on the grounded (anode) electrode. Etching is dominated by neutral free radicals → **mostly isotropic**.
> - **Reactive Ion Etching** operates at lower pressure; the sample sits on the powered (cathode) electrode. The DC self-bias (typically –200 V to –800 V) accelerates ions *toward* the substrate → **anisotropic** because vertical ions dominate.

### 1.2.3 LIGA Process

**LIGA** is a German acronym for:
- **LI**thographie (X-ray deep lithography)
- **GA**lvanoformung (electroforming / electroplating)
- **A**bformung (molding — optional mass-production step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *LIGA is a high aspect-ratio micromachining process in which a synchrotron-generated X-ray beam is used to expose a thick (hundreds of micrometers to centimeters) layer of PMMA resist, which is then developed to form a sacrificial mold; this mold is filled with metal by electroforming, and the metal structure is released to yield metal microstructures with aspect ratios exceeding 100:1 and sidewall verticality better than 0.1°.*

LIGA is the *only* routine process that can produce **truly 3-D, mm-tall, micron-wide metal structures** with optical-grade sidewalls.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wet Etching — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Isotropic Wet Etching (e.g., HNA — HF/HNO₃/CH₃COOH for Si)

The overall reaction is a redox sequence:

$$
\text{Si} + \text{HNO}_3 + 6\text{HF} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SiF}_6 + \text{HNO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2
$$

- **HNO₃** oxidises Si → SiO₂
- **HF** dissolves the oxide → soluble H₂SiF₆
- **CH₃COOH** acts as a buffer / diluent

The etch front is a **circular arc** because the rate is identical along all directions.

### 2.1.2 Anisotropic Wet Etching of Silicon (KOH / TMAH / EDP / CsOH)

Silicon's diamond cubic lattice exposes different atomic densities on different planes:

| Plane | Atomic density (atoms/cm²) | Etch rate (relative) | Role |
|-------|----------------------------|----------------------|------|
| (100) | 6.78 × 10¹⁴ | **1.0 (fastest)** | Etch-floor |
| (110) | 9.59 × 10¹⁴ | 0.5 – 0.7 | 45° sidewalls |
| (111) | 1.57 × 10¹⁵ | **0.005 (slowest)** | Etch-stop / vertical wall |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why (111) is the slowest:** The (111) plane is the *most densely packed*. Each surface atom has only **one** dangling bond (versus two for (100)), so it is least reactive toward hydroxyl ions (OH⁻) in the etchant.

The angle between (100) and (111) is **54.74°**, which is the famous *V-groove angle* in micromachined pits.

### 2.1.3 KTU Etching Metrics — The Three Cardinal Quantities

Every etching problem reduces to three numbers:

**1) Etch Rate (ER):**
$$
\text{ER} \;=\; \frac{\Delta h}{\Delta t} \quad \left[\frac{\mu\text{m}}{\text{min}}\right]
$$
where $\Delta h$ is the vertical depth etched in time $\Delta t$.

**2) Selectivity (S):**
$$
S \;=\; \frac{\text{ER}_{\text{target}}}{\text{ER}_{\text{mask}}}
$$
A selectivity of ≥ 50 is generally required for practical lithography.

**3) Anisotropy Factor (A):**
$$
A \;=\; 1 - \frac{\text{ER}_{\text{lateral}}}{\text{ER}_{\text{vertical}}}
$$
- $A = 1$ → perfectly anisotropic (vertical walls)
- $A = 0$ → perfectly isotropic (circular undercut)

**4) Aspect Ratio (AR):**
$$
\text{AR} \;=\; \frac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Minimum lateral width}}
$$

**5) Undercut (U):**
$$
U \;=\; \int_0^{t} \text{ER}_{\text{lateral}}\,dt
$$
For pure isotropic etching, $U = \text{ER} \times t$ (same as depth).

### 2.1.4 Etch-Stop Techniques

| Method | Physical Principle | Resolution |
|--------|--------------------|------------|
| **Boron etch-stop (p⁺-Si)** | Heavily doped Si (> 5×10¹⁹ cm⁻³) has 10⁻³ × ER of intrinsic | ~ 1 µm |
| **Electrochemical etch-stop (pn-junction)** | Reverse bias creates depletion layer → etching stops | ~ 1 µm |
| **Time-etch-stop** | Simply time the etch to known depth | ~ 5 µm |
| **(111) plane self-stopping** | 200:1 selectivity gives atomically flat (111) sidewalls | Atomic |

## 2.2 Dry Etching — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Plasma Generation & Sheath Physics

A plasma is the **4th state of matter** — a quasi-neutral gas of electrons, ions, radicals, and photons. In a 13.56 MHz RF field:

- **Electrons** (light, mobile) → follow the RF field → strip away to walls
- **Ions** (heavy, sluggish) → cannot follow the RF → stay behind
- Result: the wall charges **negatively** (DC self-bias $V_{\text{dc}}$)
- A **dark space / sheath** forms adjacent to the cathode; ions are accelerated across it *normal* to the surface

The self-bias for a parallel-plate reactor is empirically:

$$
V_{\text{dc}} \;\approx\; \frac{kT_e}{2 e \ln\!\left(\frac{A_a}{A_c}\right)} \left[\left(\frac{2 V_{\text{rf}}}{V_{\text{dc}}}\right)^{3/2} - 1\right]
$$
*(approximate Child-Langmuir sheath relation — not needed for numerical problem solving but conceptually important)*

### 2.2.2 Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)

**Operating regime:** 10–100 mTorr, –200 to –800 V DC bias, 100–1000 W RF power.

**Two concurrent mechanisms:**
1. **Chemical** — radicals (F•, Cl•, Br•) adsorb and react to form volatile products:
   - Si + 4F• → SiF₄↑
   - SiO₂ + 4F• → SiF₄↑ + O₂↑
   - Si + 2Cl₂ → SiCl₄↑ (for chlorine chemistries)
2. **Physical** — energetic Ar⁺ or ion bombardment removes material by momentum transfer and damages the surface, making the chemistry faster.

### 2.2.3 Bosch (DRIE) Process — Time-Multiplexed Etching

**The Problem:** In continuous RIE of silicon with SF₆, the fluorine radicals etch sideways almost as fast as they etch down → tapered, grape-cluster profiles.

**The Bosch Solution (patented by Robert Bosch GmbH, 1994):** Alternate rapidly between two gas feeds in a *single* chamber.

| Step | Gas | Time | Function |
|------|-----|------|----------|
| **Etch** | SF₆ | 5 – 15 s | F• radicals + ion bombard → SiF₄↑ |
| **Passivation** | C₄F₈ | 5 – 10 s | Polymer (Teflon-like) coats *all* surfaces |
| **Etch** | SF₆ | 5 – 15 s | Ions break polymer on flat bottom (vertical incidence) but **not on sidewalls** (glancing incidence) → etching resumes only on bottom |

The repetition produces characteristic **scallops** on the sidewalls (≈ 100 – 500 nm amplitude).

### 2.2.4 Ion Beam Etching (IBE) / Sputter Etching

- Uses a separate Kaufman ion source
- Inert gas (Ar⁺) at 500 – 1500 eV
- Purely physical → **no selectivity** (erodes photoresist as fast as SiO₂)
- Used for materials that don't form volatile products: gold, platinum, permalloy

## 2.3 LIGA — Detailed Physics & Process Flow

### 2.3.1 The X-Ray Source

Conventional UV lithography cannot expose PMMA thicker than ~ 10 µm because of absorption. LIGA uses **synchrotron radiation** with:

- **Photon energy:** 2 – 10 keV (wavelength ≈ 0.2 – 0.6 nm)
- **Beam current:** 100 – 500 mA
- **Critical dose for PMMA:** ≈ **4 kJ/cm³** (4 J/g) to fully develop
- **Resist thickness:** 100 µm – 3 mm
- **Lateral resolution:** 0.2 – 1 µm
- **Sidewall roughness:** < 50 nm
- **Aspect ratio achievable:** **> 100:1** (and > 500:1 in research reactors)

### 2.3.2 Process Flow (4 Steps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — LIGA 4-Stage Process Flow (must be memorised in order)**
>
> **Step 1: X-ray Deep Lithography**
> - Thick PMMA resist is spin-coated or bonded onto a conductive substrate (Cu, Au, Ni seed)
> - A gold or polyimide **X-ray mask** (with high-Z absorber pattern) is placed above
> - Synchrotron X-rays pass through transparent regions of the mask and break the long polymer chains in PMMA (chain scission)
> - Exposed regions become soluble in developer
>
> **Step 2: Development**
> - Immersion in GG developer (diethylene glycol monobutyl ether + morpholine + 2-aminoethanol + water) at 35 °C
> - Dissolved PMMA leaves behind a high-aspect-ratio mould cavity
> - Critical: developer must remove ≥ 500 µm of PMMA uniformly → mass-transport limited
>
> **Step 3: Electroforming (Galvanoformung)**
> - The conductive seed layer acts as the cathode
> - The patterned PMMA cavities are filled with metal (Ni, Cu, Au) by electrodeposition
> - Nickel is most common: Watts bath at 50 °C, 50 mA/cm², pH 3.5
> - Deposition continues *above* the PMMA surface to form a "carrier plate" for mechanical stability
>
> **Step 4: Moulding (Abformung) — Optional, for mass production**
> - The metal mould insert is used to hot-emboss or injection-mould polymer replicas
> - Polymers used: PMMA, POM, COC, PSU
> - For metal replicas: insert mould + wax filler → electroform again → repeat

### 2.3.3 LIGA Material & Parameter Summary

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Resist | PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate) |
| X-ray wavelength | 0.2 – 0.6 nm (hard X-ray) |
| Resist thickness | 100 – 3000 µm |
| Exposure dose | 4 kJ/cm³ |
| Sidewall verticality | ± 0.05° |
| Aspect ratio | 10:1 – 500:1 |
| Electroformed metal | Ni (most common), Cu, Au, Fe-Ni |
| Min feature size | 0.5 µm |
| Max structure height | 10 mm |

### 2.3.4 LIGA Variants (KTU expects at least one to be named)

| Variant | Replaces X-rays with | Cost | Aspect Ratio |
|---------|----------------------|------|--------------|
| **X-ray LIGA** | Synchrotron X-ray | Very high | > 100:1 |
| **UV-LIGA** | UV light + SU-8 resist | Low | 20:1 – 50:1 |
| **E-beam LIGA** | Electron beam | Medium | 50:1 |
| **Proton-beam LIGA** | MeV protons + PMMA | Medium | > 100:1 |
| **Hot Embossing** | Mechanical + heat | Low | 20:1 |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (cheat sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these six equations — they cover 90 % of KTU numerical and theory questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical KTU Application |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Etch rate | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$ | µm/min | "Time required to etch 50 µm" |
| 2 | Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{\text{ER}_\text{target}}{\text{ER}_\text{mask}}$ | dimensionless | Mask thickness design |
| 3 | Anisotropy | $A = 1 - \dfrac{\text{ER}_\text{lat}}{\text{ER}_\text{vert}}$ | dimensionless | Distinguish isotropic vs. anisotropic |
| 4 | Aspect ratio | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{h}{w}$ | dimensionless | DRIE & LIGA characterisation |
| 5 | Undercut | $U = \text{ER}_\text{lat} \cdot t$ | µm | Mask biasing in wet etch |
| 6 | V-groove width | $W = W_0 + 2 d \cot(54.74°)$ | µm | Anisotropic Si pit design |

> The 54.74° angle arises because $\cos^{-1}(1/\sqrt{3}) = 54.7356°$ — the angle between the (100) etch-floor and the (111) etch-stop wall.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Process | MEMS / NEMS Product |
|---------|---------------------|
| **KOH anisotropic wet** | Pressure-sensor diaphragms, V-groove fibre alignment, microfluidic channels |
| **DRIE (Bosch)** | Accelerometer proof mass, gyroscope comb fingers, inkjet printhead nozzle plates |
| **RIE** | Microfluidic channel sealing, optical MEMS mirror release etch |
| **IBE** | Magnetoresistive read heads, magnetic MEMS |
| **LIGA (Ni)** | Gear wheels, micro-turbines, X-ray collimators, nozzle plates for fuel injectors, micro-reactor chambers |
| **UV-LIGA / SU-8** | Lab-on-chip fluidic chambers, bio-MEMS scaffolds |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: V-Groove Geometry in (100) Silicon with KOH

**Problem context:** A square window of side $W_0$ is opened in a SiO₂ mask on (100) silicon. The wafer is etched in KOH until the (111) planes intersect. Derive the **final etch depth** $d$ and the **opening width at the surface** $W$ of the resulting V-groove (or pyramid).

### Step 1 — Identify the crystal geometry
The (100) surface is the etch floor. Four (111) planes meet it at the angle:

$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356°
$$

### Step 2 — Geometry of a 2-D cross-section
In the cross-section, the two sloped sidewalls make angle $\theta$ with the horizontal floor. Let the etch depth be $d$ and the lateral undercut be $x$ on each side.

$$
\tan\theta \;=\; \frac{d}{x} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x \;=\; \frac{d}{\tan\theta} \;=\; d\cot\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Express $\cot\theta$ in closed form

$$
\cot\theta \;=\; \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} \;=\; \frac{1/\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \;\approx\; 0.7071
$$

So:

$$
x \;=\; \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Total surface opening after etch
The four sidewalls each recede by $x = d/\sqrt{2}$. For a square window of original side $W_0$:

$$
W \;=\; W_0 - 2x \;=\; W_0 - \sqrt{2}\,d
$$

### Step 5 — Self-limiting etch depth
Etching **stops** when the four (111) planes meet at the bottom — that is, when $W = 0$:

$$
0 \;=\; W_0 - \sqrt{2}\,d_{\max} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\,d_{\max} \;=\; \frac{W_0}{\sqrt{2}}\;}
$$

### Step 6 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style)
> **Q:** A 200 µm × 200 µm window is opened on (100) Si and etched in KOH. (a) Find the final etch depth. (b) Find the etch time if ER(100) = 1 µm/min.

**Solution:**

**(a)**
$$
d_{\max} \;=\; \frac{W_0}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{200\;\mu\text{m}}{1.4142} \;\approx\; 141.4\;\mu\text{m}
$$

**(b)**
$$
t \;=\; \frac{d_{\max}}{\text{ER}} \;=\; \frac{141.4\;\mu\text{m}}{1\;\mu\text{m/min}} \;\approx\; 141.4\;\text{min} \;\approx\; 2\;\text{h}\;21\;\text{min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mask-biasing tip:** To obtain a *desired* bottom opening $W_{\text{bottom}}$, the mask opening must be enlarged by $\sqrt{2}\,d$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lateral Undercut in Isotropic Wet Etching

**Problem context:** A long rectangular opening of width $W_0$ is etched isotropically for time $t$. The etch rate is $R$ in all directions. Find the resulting cross-section.

### Step 1 — Define the etch front
Because the etch is isotropic, the etch front is a **circular arc** of radius equal to the etch depth at any instant:

$$
r(t) \;=\; R\,t
$$

### Step 2 — Width at the surface
The lateral etch is $R\,t$ on each side:

$$
W_{\text{top}} \;=\; W_0 + 2 R t
$$

### Step 3 — Width at the bottom (after time $t$)
The bottom of the trench disappears (pinch-off) when $W_0 - 2R t = 0$, i.e., at $t_{\text{pinch}} = W_0/(2R)$. For a long trench (infinite in the third dimension), the sidewall profile is:

$$
x(z) \;=\; x_0 + \sqrt{(R t)^2 - (z - R t)^2}
$$
where $x_0$ is the half-width of the opening, and $z$ is the depth from the original surface.

### Step 4 — Convex corner undercutting
At convex corners (e.g., a square mask window), the etch front becomes a **circle** that wraps the corner. After time $t$, the corner is *undercut* by $R t$ in **both** lateral directions. The corner becomes a quadrant of radius $R t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU pitfall:** Many students assume that anisotropic KOH gives vertical sidewalls for *any* mask orientation. It does NOT — vertical sidewalls only appear on (110) wafers. On (100) wafers you always get the 54.74° sidewalls.

## 3.3 Derivation: Selectivity & Mask Thickness in Plasma Etching

**Problem context:** A silicon wafer is etched in an RIE with $\text{ER}_{\text{Si}} = 0.5\;\mu\text{m/min}$ and selectivity $S = 25$ over photoresist. The target etch depth is 20 µm. (a) Find the required minimum photoresist thickness. (b) Find the etch time.

### Step 1 — Resist etch rate from selectivity

$$
\text{ER}_{\text{resist}} \;=\; \frac{\text{ER}_{\text{Si}}}{S} \;=\; \frac{0.5}{25} \;=\; 0.02\;\mu\text{m/min}
$$

### Step 2 — Etch time for target depth

$$
t \;=\; \frac{d_{\text{Si}}}{\text{ER}_{\text{Si}}} \;=\; \frac{20}{0.5} \;=\; 40\;\text{min}
$$

### Step 3 — Resist consumed during the same time

$$
\Delta h_{\text{resist}} \;=\; \text{ER}_{\text{resist}} \cdot t \;=\; 0.02 \times 40 \;=\; 0.8\;\mu\text{m}
$$

### Step 4 — Add safety factor (typical 1.5×)

$$
h_{\text{resist,min}} \;=\; 1.5 \times 0.8 \;\approx\; 1.2\;\mu\text{m}
$$

So a **2 µm photoresist** is recommended.

## 3.4 Derivation: Bosch DRIE — Scallop Amplitude vs. Cycle Time

The scallop amplitude $S_a$ on the sidewall of a Bosch-etched hole scales with the *etch step time* $t_e$ as:

$$
S_a \;\approx\; R_e \, t_e
$$
where $R_e$ is the Si etch rate during the SF₆ step.

> Conversely, reducing $t_e$ reduces scallop amplitude but increases process time. The trade-off curve is plotted below.

| $t_e$ (s) | $S_a$ (nm) at $R_e = 3\;\mu\text{m/min}$ | Comment |
|-----------|----------------------------------------|---------|
| 3 | 150 | Fast, rough |
| 6 | 300 | Industrial default |
| 10 | 500 | High aspect-ratio, very rough |
| 1 | 50 | Optical-grade sidewalls |

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wet vs. Dry vs. LIGA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most-tested comparison in KTU Module 2**

| Parameter | Wet Etching | Dry / Plasma Etching | LIGA |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|------|
| **Etchant phase** | Liquid | Gas / plasma | X-ray + electrochemistry |
| **Mechanism** | Chemical | Chemical + physical | Photolytic chain scission + electrodeposition |
| **Direction** | Isotropic / anisotropic | Anisotropic (RIE/DRIE) | Highly anisotropic |
| **Selectivity** | High (KOH:Si 200:1 over SiO₂) | Low – medium (RIE:SiO₂ 1:1 over Si) | High (PMMA 1000:1 over metal mask) |
| **Max aspect ratio** | ~ 50:1 (KOH pyramids) | ~ 50:1 (DRIE), > 100:1 research | > 100:1, up to 500:1 |
| **Mask compatibility** | SiO₂, Si₃N₄, Au | Photoresist, SiO₂, metal | Au (X-ray absorber) |
| **Undercut** | Large (isotropic); < 1 µm (anisotropic) | Negligible (RIE) | Negligible |
| **Sidewall verticality** | 54.74° (KOH on (100)) | 90° (DRIE) | 90° ± 0.1° |
| **Equipment cost** | Low (beaker + hotplate) | Medium (RIE: ₹10–30 lakh; DRIE: ₹1–5 cr) | Very high (synchrotron: ₹100 cr+) |
| **Throughput** | High (batch) | Medium (single wafer) | Very low (1 wafer per exposure) |
| **Material scope** | Limited (needs volatile or soluble products) | Wide (volatile halides, organics) | Polymers, then any electroplatable metal |
| **Typical use** | Diaphragms, V-grooves | Comb drives, vias, holes | Micro-gears, nozzles, optical elements |
| **Critical disadvantage** | Undercut, bubble-masking | Plasma damage, slow | Synchrotron cost, mask fabrication |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step: LIGA Dose Calculation

**Given:**
- PMMA thickness: $h = 500\;\mu\text{m}$
- Bottom dose required: $D_{\text{bottom}} = 4\;\text{kJ/cm}^3$
- Top dose tolerated: $D_{\text{top}} = 20\;\text{kJ/cm}^3$ (above this, PMMA chars / cracks)

**Required:** Incident top dose $D_0$ such that, after exponential X-ray absorption, both conditions are met.

### Step 1 — Beer-Lambert absorption
$$
D(z) \;=\; D_0 \, e^{-\mu z}
$$
where $\mu \approx 1\;\text{cm}^{-1}$ for 4 keV X-rays in PMMA.

### Step 2 — Bottom condition
$$
4 \;=\; D_0 \, e^{-0.05} \;\Rightarrow\; D_0 \;=\; 4 \, e^{0.05} \;\approx\; 4.21\;\text{kJ/cm}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Top condition
$$
D_{\text{top}} \;=\; 4.21\;\text{kJ/cm}^3 \;\ll\; 20\;\text{kJ/cm}^3 \quad \text{✓ acceptable}
$$

### Step 4 — Process latitude
$$
\text{Latitude} \;=\; 20 - 4.21 \;=\; 15.8\;\text{kJ/cm}^3
$$
A 3.75× safety margin — typical for industrial LIGA.
