# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> Each Part-A question in KTU's End Semester Examination (ESE) is allotted **3 marks** and expects a precise 4–6 line answer. The questions below are *style-matched* to KTU's 2024 pattern.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Distinguish between wet etching and dry etching. List two advantages of dry etching over wet etching for MEMS fabrication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wet Etching | Dry Etching |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Etchant phase | Liquid (e.g., KOH, HF) | Plasma / reactive gas (e.g., SF₆) |
| Direction | Isotropic or crystallographic anisotropic | Generally anisotropic |
| Undercut | Significant in isotropic mode | Negligible |
| Selectivity | High (KOH on Si : SiO₂ ≈ 200:1) | Low to medium |
| Equipment | Beaker + hotplate | RF plasma reactor, vacuum |

**Two advantages of dry etching:**
1. **Anisotropic profiles** — sub-micron critical-dimension control is possible because ion bombardment is directionally normal to the surface. Critical for high-density MEMS like comb drives.
2. **Better process reproducibility and uniformity** — vacuum-based gas chemistry is less sensitive to operator handling and bubble formation than liquid-bath chemistry.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**What is the LIGA process? Why is synchrotron X-ray radiation used in LIGA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**LIGA** is a German acronym for **Li**thographie (X-ray deep lithography), **Ga**lvanoformung (electroforming), and **A**bformung (moulding). It is a high aspect-ratio micromachining process for fabricating metallic microstructures with aspect ratios exceeding 100:1.

**Why synchrotron X-rays are used:**
1. **Very short wavelength (0.2 – 0.6 nm)** — penetrates 100 µm – 3 mm of PMMA with minimal diffraction, yielding sub-micron lateral features.
2. **High brilliance & collimation** — the parallel beam gives vertical sidewalls (± 0.05°).
3. **High intensity** — delivers the critical dose of ~ 4 kJ/cm³ in a few minutes, which is unattainable from laboratory X-ray tubes.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Internal-Choice Pattern)

> Under the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 2 carries a 14-mark question. The university provides an *internal choice*: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both choices below are sub-divided into 7-mark parts.

---

## ⭐ QUESTION A (14 MARKS)

### **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat schematics, explain the **Bosch Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** process. How does it achieve aspect ratios greater than 50:1? Mention the role of the two gases used and explain the term **scalloping**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 100 µm × 100 µm square mask window is opened on a (100) silicon wafer and etched in 24 wt% KOH at 80 °C, with $\text{ER}_{(100)} = 1\;\mu\text{m/min}$. Assuming no etch-stop mechanism, find:
   1. The final etch depth.
   2. The etch time.
   3. The lateral undercut if the etch were purely isotropic with $R = 0.8\;\mu\text{m/min}$.

---

### **Solution to Q.A (a) — Bosch DRIE**

**Definition:** Bosch DRIE is a **time-multiplexed** deep reactive ion etching process that alternates between an **etch step** (using SF₆) and a **passivation step** (using C₄F₈) in the same plasma chamber.

**Mechanism — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — SF₆ Etch Cycle (≈ 5 – 15 s):**
- SF₆ gas is admitted; RF power creates fluorine radicals (F•) and SFₓ⁺ ions.
- F• reacts spontaneously with Si to form volatile SiF₄, which is pumped out.
- Etch direction is isotropic initially, but the vertical component dominates because ions are accelerated by the DC self-bias *normal* to the wafer.

**Step 2 — C₄F₈ Passivation Cycle (≈ 5 – 10 s):**
- SF₆ is purged; C₄F₈ (octafluorocyclopentene) is admitted.
- The plasma dissociates C₄F₈ into CF₂ radicals that polymerise on **all** exposed surfaces (top, sidewall, bottom) to form a Teflon-like (–CF₂–CF₂–) film ≈ 10 – 50 nm thick.
- This polymer *protects* the sidewalls from further fluorine attack.

**Step 3 — Resumption of Etch Cycle:**
- The chamber is switched back to SF₆.
- Directional ions accelerated by the DC self-bias strike the **bottom** of the trench (normal incidence), breaking through the passivation film and resuming Si etching.
- The passivation film on the **sidewalls** is *not* removed because ions arrive at a glancing angle and lack the momentum to sputter it off.
- Net result: Si is removed only from the bottom — vertical, high-aspect-ratio sidewalls.

**Why aspect ratio > 50:1 is achievable:**
- Continuous alternation (typically 100 – 300 cycles) deepens the trench without lateral attack.
- Polymer re-formation at every cycle *repairs* any incipient sidewall damage.
- The limiting factor is the *aspect-ratio dependent etch rate* — once AR exceeds ~ 30, ion and radical transport into the bottom becomes diffusion-limited and etch rate drops. This is called the **RIE lag** or **ARDE** (aspect-ratio-dependent etching).

**Scalloping:**
- Because the cycle is *time-multiplexed*, the sidewall consists of a stack of microscopic arcs, one per etch cycle. This wavy sidewall is called **scalloping**.
- Scallop amplitude $S_a \approx R_e \cdot t_e$ (etch rate × etch time per cycle).
- Reducing $t_e$ from 10 s to 1 s reduces $S_a$ from ~ 500 nm to ~ 50 nm, at the cost of longer process time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A(a):**
> - Many students *omit the passivation step* and describe Bosch as "just SF₆ plasma". This loses 3 marks instantly. **Always state both gases and both phases.**
> - Confusing Bosch DRIE with conventional RIE is another common deduction (2 marks).
> - Do not draw a single cross-section. Draw **two cross-sections** — one after etch step, one after passivation — and label ions/radicals.

---

### **Solution to Q.A (b) — Numerical KOH Etch**

**Given:**
- Mask window: $W_0 = 100\;\mu\text{m}$
- $\text{ER}_{(100)} = 1\;\mu\text{m/min}$
- Substrate: (100) Si in 24 wt% KOH at 80 °C
- $\theta_{(100)\to(111)} = 54.74°$

**(1) Final etch depth (anisotropic, V-groove termination):**

$$
d_{\max} \;=\; \frac{W_0}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{100}{1.4142} \;\approx\; 70.71\;\mu\text{m}
$$

**(2) Etch time:**

$$
t_{\text{etch}} \;=\; \frac{d_{\max}}{\text{ER}_{(100)}} \;=\; \frac{70.71}{1} \;\approx\; 70.71\;\text{min} \;\approx\; 1\;\text{h}\;10.7\;\text{min}
$$

**(3) Lateral undercut if process were purely isotropic at $R = 0.8\;\mu\text{m/min}$:**

The corresponding time to reach the same vertical depth would be:

$$
t_{\text{iso}} \;=\; \frac{d_{\max}}{R} \;=\; \frac{70.71}{0.8} \;\approx\; 88.4\;\text{min}
$$

Undercut on each side:

$$
U \;=\; R \cdot t_{\text{iso}} \;=\; 0.8 \times 88.4 \;\approx\; 70.71\;\mu\text{m}
$$

(Note: for *pure* isotropic etching, undercut = depth = 70.71 µm — the mask would have to be enlarged by **2 × 70.71 = 141.4 µm** on every side just to compensate. The V-groove is therefore vastly superior in mask economy.)

---

## ⭐ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — Alternative Choice

### **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **LIGA process** in detail with a process-flow diagram. Explain the role of synchrotron radiation. List **two** LIGA variants and state one advantage of each over conventional X-ray LIGA.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **wet etching, dry (plasma) etching, and LIGA** under the following heads: principle, anisotropy, selectivity, maximum aspect ratio, sidewall quality, and one typical application each.

---

### **Solution to Q.B (a) — LIGA Process**

**LIGA — full form and definition:**
> LIGA = **Li**thographie + **Ga**lvanoformung + **A**bformung. A high aspect-ratio micromachining process combining deep X-ray lithography and electroforming to produce metallic micro-parts with aspect ratios > 100:1.

**Step-by-step process flow:**

**Stage 1 — Lithographie (X-ray deep lithography):**
1. Start with a polished, electrically **conductive** substrate (e.g., silicon coated with a Cu, Au or Ni seed layer ≈ 100 nm).
2. Bond or cast a thick layer of **PMMA** (polymethyl methacrylate) resist, thickness 100 – 3000 µm.
3. Place an **X-ray mask** above the PMMA. The mask consists of:
   - A thin (≈ 2 µm) polyimide or silicon membrane
   - A patterned **high-Z absorber** (typically 10 – 20 µm of gold) defining the geometry.
4. Expose to synchrotron X-rays (2 – 10 keV, λ ≈ 0.2 – 0.6 nm, dose ≈ 4 kJ/cm³).
5. The X-rays **break the long PMMA polymer chains** (chain scission) in the exposed regions, lowering their molecular weight dramatically.

**Stage 2 — Development:**
1. Immerse the exposed PMMA in a **GG developer** (a mixture of diethylene glycol monobutyl ether, morpholine, 2-aminoethanol and water) at ≈ 35 °C.
2. The low-molecular-weight exposed PMMA dissolves selectively, leaving behind a high-fidelity mould cavity with **vertical sidewalls** and **aspect ratios up to 100:1**.
3. Critical step: developer diffusion into deep cavities is mass-transport limited — this determines the maximum aspect ratio.

**Stage 3 — Galvanoformung (Electroforming):**
1. The seed layer beneath the PMMA is wired as the **cathode** of an electroplating cell.
2. The patterned mould cavities are filled with metal — most commonly **nickel** (Watts bath, 50 °C, pH 3.5, 50 mA/cm²). Copper, gold, and Fe-Ni alloys are also used.
3. Plating continues *above* the PMMA surface to form a solid **carrier plate** that holds the delicate microstructures.
4. The PMMA is then dissolved (e.g., in acetone or by plasma ashing) — releasing the free-standing metal micro-parts.

**Stage 4 — Abformung (Moulding) — Optional, for mass production:**
1. The metal mould insert is detached and used as a die for **hot embossing** or **injection moulding** of polymer replicas (PMMA, COC, PSU).
2. For metal replicas: the insert is filled with wax, the wax is overplated, then melted out — leaving a "second-generation" metal mould. The cycle can be repeated to multiply the master.

**Role of synchrotron radiation:**
- **High photon flux** — delivers the critical dose in minutes, not hours.
- **Collimation** — beam divergence < 1 mrad → sidewall verticality better than 0.1°.
- **Short wavelength** — penetrates mm-thick PMMA with negligible diffraction, preserving sub-µm features.

**LIGA variants — any two:**

| Variant | Replaces | Advantage |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| **UV-LIGA (SU-8)** | Synchrotron X-ray with near-UV (365 – 405 nm) | **Low cost** — no synchrotron; uses standard mask aligner. |
| **Proton-beam LIGA** | X-rays with MeV protons | **Higher penetration**; lower mask cost (uses PMMA directly as mask material). |
| **E-beam LIGA** | X-rays with focused electron beam | **No mask required** — direct write; flexible prototyping. |
| **Hot Embossing** | Lithography + electroforming with mechanical imprinting | **Very high throughput** — wafers per hour, not per shift. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(a):**
> - Stating "LIGA uses X-rays" *without* explaining the role of synchrotron costs 1 mark.
> - Skipping the **moulding (Abformung)** step loses 1.5 marks — the examiner expects all three German words to appear in your answer.
> - Writing the steps in the wrong order (e.g., electroforming *before* development) loses 1 mark.

---

### **Solution to Q.B (b) — Comparative Table**

| Parameter | Wet Etching | Dry / Plasma Etching | LIGA |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|------|
| **Principle** | Chemical dissolution in liquid etchant (e.g., KOH on Si) | Chemical + ion-bombardment reactions in plasma (e.g., SF₆ + RIE) | X-ray lithography of thick PMMA + electroforming of metal |
| **Anisotropy** | Anisotropic only with single-crystal Si (54.74° walls) | Anisotropic (RIE, DRIE vertical) | Highly anisotropic (± 0.1° verticality) |
| **Selectivity** | Very high (KOH : SiO₂ ≈ 200:1) | Low to medium (RIE : resist ≈ 5:1) | Very high (PMMA : Au mask > 1000:1) |
| **Max aspect ratio** | ~ 50:1 (pyramids on (100)) | ~ 100:1 (Bosch DRIE) | > 100:1, up to 500:1 (research) |
| **Sidewall quality** | Smooth, crystallographic, but sloped | Scalloped (DRIE ≈ 100 – 500 nm) or smooth (cryogenic) | Optical-grade; roughness < 50 nm |
| **Typical application** | Pressure-sensor diaphragms, V-grooves, microfluidic channels | Comb-finger accelerometers, inkjet nozzle plates, gyroscopes | Micro-gears, micro-turbines, fuel-injector nozzles, X-ray collimators |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> - Writing a *narrative* comparison instead of a *table* is acceptable but is harder to score full marks on. **Always prefer a table** for comparison questions.
> - Confusing **selectivity** with **anisotropy** is a common 1-mark deduction. Selectivity is *material* discrimination; anisotropy is *directional* discrimination.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls to Avoid in Etching Questions (deducted 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Units inconsistency** — always pair µm/min with minutes, µm with µm. Writing "minutes" in µm/min calculations is a 0.5-mark error.
> 2. **Mixing isotropic and anisotropic formulas** — the formula $W = W_0 + 2R t$ applies only to *isotropic* wet etching. For anisotropic KOH on (100), use $W = W_0 - \sqrt{2} d$ (the width *decreases*).
> 3. **Forgetting the (111) 54.74° angle** — this is the single most-tested constant in KTU Module 2. Memorise $\cos^{-1}(1/\sqrt{3}) = 54.74°$.
> 4. **Stating "LIGA is lithography"** — it is a *three-step* process. The lithography is only the first step.
> 5. **Confusing Bosch DRIE with cryogenic DRIE** — Bosch uses *time-multiplexed* SF₆/C₄F₈; cryogenic uses *continuous* SF₆ at –110 °C.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-revision checklist — print this page before the exam.**

- [ ] **Wet etching** = liquid-phase chemical dissolution; can be isotropic (HNA, HF) or anisotropic (KOH, TMAH on Si).
- [ ] **Anisotropy factor** $A = 1 - \text{ER}_{\text{lat}}/\text{ER}_{\text{vert}}$; $A = 1$ is ideal.
- [ ] **Selectivity** $S = \text{ER}_{\text{target}} / \text{ER}_{\text{mask}}$; ≥ 50 is desired.
- [ ] **V-groove angle** on (100) Si is **54.74°**; final depth $d_{\max} = W_0 / \sqrt{2}$.
- [ ] **KOH (100):(111) selectivity ≈ 200:1** — this is the basis of Si bulk micromachining.
- [ ] **Dry etching** = plasma-based, vacuum, uses RF at 13.56 MHz; lower pressure than wet.
- [ ] **Plasma etching** is mostly isotropic (radical-driven, high pressure).
- [ ] **RIE** is anisotropic (low pressure, sample on cathode, ion bombardment normal to surface).
- [ ] **DRIE / Bosch** = time-multiplexed SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation → vertical walls, AR > 50:1.
- [ ] **Scalloping** in DRIE = sidewall waviness ≈ $R_e \times t_e$ per cycle.
- [ ] **LIGA** = Lithographie + Galvanoformung + Abformung (three German words).
- [ ] **Synchrotron X-rays** are essential in LIGA because of mm-scale PMMA penetration + collimation.
- [ ] **LIGA aspect ratio** > 100:1 routinely, > 500:1 in research.
- [ ] **LIGA product** is metal (most often Ni) — unlike silicon MEMS.
- [ ] **UV-LIGA with SU-8** is the low-cost alternative; aspect ratio 20:1 – 50:1.
- [ ] **MEMS products by process:** KOH → pressure sensors; DRIE → accelerometers/gyros; LIGA → micro-gears/nozzles.
- [ ] **RIE-lag / ARDE** = aspect-ratio-dependent etching; etch rate drops at high AR due to transport limits.
- [ ] **Mask materials:** SiO₂ or Si₃N₄ for wet etch; photoresist for RIE; Au for LIGA X-ray absorber.

---

**End of Module 2 — Etching Techniques & LIGA Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEPET522)**

topics.title: Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** is a highly anisotropic, plasma-based dry etching technique used in MEMS and semiconductor fabrication to create **deep, high-aspect-ratio microstructures** in silicon substrates. It is formally defined by the **International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRS)** as a process capable of achieving etch depths greater than **10 µm** with **aspect ratios exceeding 10:1**, while maintaining near-vertical sidewall profiles (typically **88°–90°**).

DRIE is essentially a specialized subset of **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** that combines the chemical reactivity of plasma-generated species with the **directional physical bombardment** of accelerated ions to achieve vertical etching of silicon far beyond the capabilities of conventional RIE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** DRIE is an advanced dry etching process used to fabricate high aspect ratio structures in silicon. The two principal industrial variants are the **Bosch Process** (time-multiplexed etching) and the **Cryogenic DRIE Process**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **carving a deep, narrow well** into a hard rock face using only handheld tools. A chisel (chemical etching) would carve sideways too much, leaving you with a wide crater. A jackhammer (pure physical sputtering) would work vertically but destroy any delicate pattern.

**DRIE is the equivalent of a smart, pulsing jackhammer that switches between two modes thousands of times per second:**
- **Mode 1 (Etch):** Chemical plasma attacks silicon downward
- **Mode 2 (Passivation):** A protective polymer film coats ALL surfaces, including the sidewalls

By cycling between these two modes, DRIE achieves the impossible — **deep vertical digging without sideways erosion**. The sidewalls get continuously re-protected, while ions in Mode 1 punch through the bottom film, ensuring only vertical progress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of pouring hot wax (passivation) into a deep rectangular mold, letting it coat all sides, then dipping the mold bottom into acid (etch). The acid eats through the wax only at the bottom (where ions hit vertically), and the process repeats. The walls remain protected — this is exactly the **Bosch Process** philosophy.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following bolded constants and parameters are universally used in DRIE characterization:

- **Etch Rate (E_R):** measured in **µm/min**, typically **1–10 µm/min** for silicon
- **Selectivity (S):** ratio of silicon etch rate to mask etch rate, ideally **> 50:1**
- **Aspect Ratio (AR):** depth-to-width ratio, achievable up to **100:1** in research, **30:1** in production
- **Sidewall Angle (θ):** typically **88°–90°** (vertical)
- **Sidewall Roughness (R_a):** typically **< 50 nm** for Bosch process
- **Standard Process Pressure:** **10–100 mTorr**
- **Standard RF Power:** **600–3000 W** (13.56 MHz)
- **Bosch Cycle Time:** **5–15 seconds** per cycle
- **Operating Temperature (Bosch):** **−10 °C to +80 °C**
- **Operating Temperature (Cryogenic):** **−110 °C to −130 °C** (using liquid nitrogen cooling)

### 1.4 Why DRIE Was Developed — The Need

Conventional anisotropic wet etching (KOH, TMAH) and standard RIE face the following limitations:

| Limitation | Conventional Method | DRIE Solution |
|------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Crystallographic dependence | KOH etches at 54.74° to surface | Independent of crystal orientation |
| Maximum depth | KOH: ~300 µm; RIE: ~5 µm | Bosch: >500 µm; Cryogenic: ~50 µm |
| Mask selectivity | KOH: 400:1 (SiO₂); RIE: 5:1 | Bosch: 75–150:1 (photoresist) |
| Sidewall profile | Fixed at 54.74° or rounded | Vertical (90° ± 1°) |
| Compatibility with CMOS | Poor (K⁺ contamination) | Excellent (vacuum-based) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Industrial Variants of DRIE

DRIE is implemented using two fundamentally different physical mechanisms:

#### 2.1.1 Bosch Process (Time-Multiplexed Etching — TME)

Developed by **Robert Bosch GmbH** in the early 1990s and patented in 1994, the Bosch process alternates rapidly between two plasma chemistries in a time-sequenced manner inside a single reaction chamber.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Passivation Step (typically 5–10 s):**
   - Inlet gas: **C₄F₈** (octafluorocyclopentene) at ~100 sccm
   - Plasma dissociation: C₄F₈ → CF₂* radicals + F atoms
   - CF₂* radicals polymerize on ALL exposed surfaces (sidewalls + bottom)
   - Forms a **Teflon-like fluorocarbon film** of thickness ~10–100 nm
   - No etching occurs during this step

2. **Etching Step (typically 5–15 s):**
   - Inlet gas: **SF₆** (sulfur hexafluoride) at ~200 sccm
   - Plasma dissociation: SF₆ → S + 6F* (free fluorine radicals)
   - **Ionic bombardment** by SF₅⁺ ions accelerated by **self-bias voltage** (~−100 V)
   - Ions physically sputter the fluorocarbon film off the **horizontal bottom surface** (because they travel vertically)
   - Fluorine radicals chemically react with exposed silicon: **Si + 4F* → SiF₄ (gas)**
   - Sidewalls remain protected by the polymer film (ions can't reach horizontally)

3. **Cycle Repetition:**
   - The two steps alternate **10–100 times per second** effectively
   - Each cycle removes **~50–200 nm** of silicon
   - Process continues for **5–60 minutes** depending on required depth

4. **Why It Achieves Verticality:**
   - The **directional ion flux** is perpendicular to the wafer
   - Sidewalls receive negligible ion flux, hence no sputtering of passivation
   - Bottom receives maximum ion flux, hence continuous etching
   - This creates the characteristic **scalloping** sidewall texture (a hallmark of Bosch DRIE)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Scalloping Phenomenon:** The Bosch process produces a wavy sidewall pattern with peak-to-valley heights of **50–500 nm**. This is called **"scallops"** and is the signature fingerprint of time-multiplexed etching. For applications requiring ultra-smooth sidewalls (e.g., optical MEMS), post-processing smoothing is required.

#### 2.1.2 Cryogenic DRIE Process

Developed at **NTT (Japan)** and **IBM**, the cryogenic process uses a single continuous plasma chemistry at extremely low temperatures.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Wafer Cooling:** Substrate cooled to **−110 °C to −130 °C** using liquid N₂
2. **Single Gas Chemistry:** **SF₆ + O₂** mixture (no time-multiplexing)
3. **Mechanism:**
   - SF₆ provides F* radicals for chemical etching
   - **O₂ enhances sidewall passivation** by forming **SiO_xF_y** layer (oxyfluoride) on sidewalls
   - At cryogenic temperatures, the SiO_xF_y layer is stable only on sidewalls (vertical) because the bottom surface is heated locally by ion bombardment above the desorption temperature
4. **Result:** Smoother sidewalls than Bosch (no scalloping), but limited depth (~50 µm typically)

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes all key formulas, equations, and metrics essential for solving DRIE numerical problems in KTU examinations.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Typical Range |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | **Etch Rate (E_R)** | $E_R = \dfrac{d}{t_{etch}}$ | µm/min | 1–10 |
| 2 | **Selectivity (S)** | $S = \dfrac{E_{R,Si}}{E_{R,Mask}}$ | dimensionless | 50–200 |
| 3 | **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $AR = \dfrac{d}{w}$ | dimensionless | 5–100 |
| 4 | **Mask Erosion Time (t_m)** | $t_m = \dfrac{T_{mask}}{E_{R,Mask}}$ | min | 30–200 |
| 5 | **Scallop Height (H_s)** | $H_s \approx \dfrac{E_{R,Si} \cdot t_{cycle}}{2}$ | nm | 50–500 |
| 6 | **Scallop Pitch (P_s)** | $P_s = E_{R,Si} \cdot t_{cycle}$ | nm | 100–1000 |
| 7 | **Number of Cycles (N)** | $N = \dfrac{t_{total}}{t_{cycle}}$ | dimensionless | 100–1000 |
| 8 | **Total Etch Time (t_total)** | $t_{total} = \dfrac{d}{E_R} + t_{overetch}$ | min | 5–60 |
| 9 | **Etch Depth Uniformity** | $U\% = \dfrac{d_{max} - d_{min}}{2 \cdot d_{avg}} \times 100$ | % | ±2% to ±5% |
| 10 | **Si + F reaction** | $Si + 4F^{\ast} \rightarrow SiF_4 \uparrow$ | chemical | — |
| 11 | **Polymer formation** | $C_4F_8 \xrightarrow{plasma} 2(C_2F_4) \xrightarrow{adsoRb} -(CF_2)_n-$ | chemical | — |
| 12 | **Vertical Etch Rate** | $E_{R,\perp} = k_{chem} \cdot [F^{\ast}] \cdot Y_{ion}$ | µm/min | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Symbols:** In the formulas above, **d** = etch depth, **w** = feature width, **t_etch** = total etch time, **T_mask** = initial mask thickness, **t_cycle** = one full Bosch cycle (passivation + etch), **Y_ion** = ion-enhanced sputter yield.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of DRIE

DRIE is the **workhorse process** enabling several billion-dollar industries:

- **Inertial Sensors (MEMS):** Comb-drive accelerometers, gyroscopes, resonators — fabricated in **STMicroelectronics, Bosch Sensortec, InvenSense** products
- **Tunable Capacitors & RF MEMS:** Air-gap variable capacitors for mobile phone front-end modules
- **Microfluidics:** Through-silicon vias (TSVs), microchannels for lab-on-chip, drug delivery
- **Photonics:** Silicon photonics waveguides, optical benches
- **Medical Devices:** Neural probes, micro-needles, stents (e.g., **Abbott Vascular, Medtronic**)
- **Energy:** Micro-fuel cells, micro-reactors, solar cell texturing
- **Aerospace:** MEMS pressure sensors for turbine engines

> [!NOTE]
> **Famous Application Example:** The **Apple iPhone** uses DRIE-fabricated MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes produced by **InvenSense (TDK)** and **Bosch Sensortec**. The Bosch DRIE process enables the ~10 µm-thick, suspended proof mass that detects motion in 3 axes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Etch Time for a Given Depth

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the total etch time required to etch a silicon wafer to a depth of **d = 100 µm** using the Bosch process with an etch rate of **E_R = 2 µm/min**, including a 20% overetch to compensate for non-uniformity.

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental etch time equation**

The etch rate is defined as the depth of silicon removed per unit time:

$$E_R = \frac{d}{t_{etch}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the nominal etch time**

Rearranging for t_etch (the time to reach nominal depth d):

$$t_{etch} = \frac{d}{E_R}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values**

$$t_{etch} = \frac{100 \ \mu m}{2 \ \mu m/min}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the result**

$$t_{etch} = 50 \ minutes$$

**Step 5 — Add the overetch time**

The overetch is 20% of nominal etch time, to ensure that the deepest point across the wafer reaches the target depth even in low-etch-rate regions:

$$t_{over} = 0.20 \times 50 = 10 \ minutes$$

**Step 6 — Compute total process time**

$$t_{total} = t_{etch} + t_{over} = 50 + 10 = 60 \ minutes$$

**Final Result:** The total process time is **t_total = 60 minutes (1 hour)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The overetch is critical because the DRIE plasma has non-uniform density across the wafer. The edges typically etch faster than the center. Without overetch, the center regions would under-etch and the devices would be unusable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Calculation of Mask Selectivity

**Problem Setup:** A **photoresist mask** of initial thickness **T_mask = 2 µm** is used to etch silicon to a depth of **50 µm**. After the process, the remaining photoresist thickness is measured as **0.5 µm**. Calculate the selectivity.

**Step 1 — Define selectivity**

Selectivity S is the ratio of the silicon etch rate to the mask etch rate:

$$S = \frac{E_{R,Si}}{E_{R,Mask}}$$

**Step 2 — Express etch rates in terms of measured quantities**

For silicon:

$$E_{R,Si} = \frac{d_{Si}}{t_{etch}} = \frac{50 \ \mu m}{t_{etch}}$$

For the photoresist mask:

$$E_{R,Mask} = \frac{T_{initial} - T_{final}}{t_{etch}} = \frac{2.0 - 0.5}{t_{etch}} = \frac{1.5 \ \mu m}{t_{etch}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio (notice t_etch cancels out)**

$$S = \frac{50 \ / \ t_{etch}}{1.5 \ / \ t_{etch}}$$

$$S = \frac{50}{1.5}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the result**

$$S = 33.33$$

**Final Result:** The selectivity is **S ≈ 33:1**, which is acceptable for shallow DRIE but suboptimal for deep etches.

**Practical Implication:** A selectivity of 33:1 means for every 33 µm of silicon etched, only 1 µm of photoresist is consumed. For deep DRIE (e.g., 200 µm), the required photoresist thickness would be:

$$T_{mask,min} = \frac{200 \ \mu m}{33} \approx 6.1 \ \mu m$$

This is why **thick photoresists (AZ 9260, AZ 4562, or SU-8)** or **hard masks (SiO₂, Al)** are used for deep DRIE.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bosch Cycle Analysis — Scallop Dimensions

**Problem Setup:** A Bosch process has an etch time per cycle of **t_etch = 8 s** and a silicon etch rate of **E_R = 2 µm/min**. Calculate the scallop height and the number of cycles required to etch **100 µm** of silicon.

**Step 1 — Calculate the depth etched per cycle**

Convert the etch rate to µm/s:

$$E_{R,s} = \frac{2 \ \mu m/min}{60 \ s/min} = 0.0333 \ \mu m/s$$

Depth per cycle (assuming passivation time is negligible or already included):

$$d_{cycle} = E_{R,s} \times t_{etch} = 0.0333 \ \mu m/s \times 8 \ s$$

$$d_{cycle} = 0.2667 \ \mu m = 266.7 \ nm$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the scallop pitch (full period)**

The scallop pitch is the full vertical distance covered in one cycle:

$$P_s = d_{cycle} = 266.7 \ nm$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the scallop height (peak-to-valley)**

The scallop height is approximately half the cycle depth (since the passivation film thickness is the "valley"):

$$H_s \approx \frac{P_s}{2} = \frac{266.7 \ nm}{2}$$

$$H_s \approx 133.3 \ nm$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the total number of cycles**

$$N = \frac{d_{total}}{d_{cycle}} = \frac{100 \ \mu m}{0.2667 \ \mu m}$$

$$N \approx 375 \ cycles$$

**Final Result:**
- **Scallop pitch:** P_s ≈ 267 nm
- **Scallop height:** H_s ≈ 133 nm
- **Total cycles required:** N ≈ 375 cycles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Trade-off:** A smaller scallop height (smoother sidewall) requires **shorter cycle times**, which means **more cycles per unit depth** and **longer total process time**. This is the fundamental DRIE design trade-off. For optical MEMS, scallops of <50 nm are preferred, requiring cycle times of ~2 s.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE) — The RIE Lag Effect

**Problem Setup:** Demonstrate the ARDE (Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching) or "RIE lag" effect using a DRIE process. Two trenches of widths **w₁ = 2 µm** and **w₂ = 10 µm** are etched simultaneously. The etch rates are observed to be **E_R1 = 1.5 µm/min** and **E_R2 = 2.5 µm/min** respectively. The aspect ratio follows the empirical relation:

$$E_R = \frac{E_{R,0}}{1 + k \cdot AR}$$

where E_R,0 is the open-area etch rate, k is a process-dependent lag coefficient.

**Step 1 — Identify the empirical model**

The aspect ratio is defined as depth/width. At time t, depth = E_R × t, so AR = (E_R × t) / w. This makes the equation implicit in E_R. For analysis, we use the simpler empirical form for the "lag" effect at fixed time.

**Step 2 — Determine the constant k from the open-area etch rate**

Assuming E_R,0 = 3.0 µm/min (the etch rate in a fully open area, AR = 0):

For w₁ = 2 µm: We need AR at the time when these rates are measured. Let us solve iteratively.

Let the etch time be t. Then d₁ = E_R1 × t = 1.5t and AR₁ = d₁/w₁ = 1.5t/2 = 0.75t.
Similarly, AR₂ = d₂/w₂ = 2.5t/10 = 0.25t.

**Step 3 — Apply the ARDE model to both trenches**

For w₁:

$$1.5 = \frac{3.0}{1 + k(0.75t)}$$

$$1 + k(0.75t) = 2.0$$

$$k(0.75t) = 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad kt = 1.333 \quad \quad (Equation \ A)$$

For w₂:

$$2.5 = \frac{3.0}{1 + k(0.25t)}$$

$$1 + k(0.25t) = 1.2$$

$$k(0.25t) = 0.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad kt = 0.8 \quad \quad (Equation \ B)$$

**Step 4 — Note the inconsistency and explain**

Equations A and B give different values of kt, which indicates the simple single-parameter model is approximate. In real processes, k depends on AR itself. The key engineering takeaway is that **narrower trenches etch slower than wider ones**.

**Step 5 — Compute the etch depth difference after t = 10 minutes**

If t = 10 min, then d₁ = 1.5 × 10 = **15 µm** and d₂ = 2.5 × 10 = **25 µm**. The depth difference is **10 µm**, which is the **RIE lag**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Consequence of ARDE:** When designing MEMS devices with multiple feature sizes on the same wafer, the RIE lag causes some features to under-etch. Designers must use **dummy features** or **specialized ARDE-compensation layout** to equalize depths.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bosch Process Cycle — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the time-multiplexed Bosch process cycle. It illustrates the two alternating plasma chemistries and the corresponding sidewall passivation mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Wafer Loaded into ICP Chamber] --> A1[Evacuate Chamber to 1e-6 Torr]
    A1 --> A2[Heat Wafer to Process Temperature 20-80 C]
    A2 --> B1{Cycle Counter Initialized N = 0}
    B1 --> C1[Step 1 Passivation Phase Duration 5-10 s]
    C1 --> C2[Inject C4F8 Gas at 100 sccm]
    C2 --> C3[Strike Plasma at 600 W ICP Power]
    C3 --> C4[CF2 Radicals Adsorb on All Silicon Surfaces]
    C4 --> C5[Form Teflon like Fluorocarbon Film Thickness 50 nm]
    C5 --> D1[Step 2 Etching Phase Duration 5-15 s]
    D1 --> D2[Switch to SF6 Gas at 200 sccm]
    D2 --> D3[Strike Plasma and Apply -100 V Bias]
    D3 --> D4[SF5 Positive Ions Bombard Vertically Downward]
    D4 --> D5[Bottom Fluorocarbon Film Sputtered Away]
    D5 --> D6[Exposed Silicon Reacts with F Radicals]
    D6 --> D7[Si + 4F Equals SiF4 Gas Evacuates]
    D7 --> E1[Cycle Counter Increment N = N plus 1]
    E1 --> F1{Target Depth Reached}
    F1 --> G1[No] --> C1
    F1 --> G2[Yes] --> H1[Stop Plasma and Vent Chamber]
    H1 --> H2[Unload Processed Wafer]
```

### 4.2 Cryogenic DRIE Process — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the single-chemistry cryogenic DRIE process, which is fundamentally different from Bosch because it does not use time-multiplexing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Wafer Mounted on Electrode] --> P2[Cool Wafer to Minus 120 C using LN2]
    P2 --> P3[Evacuate Chamber to 10 mTorr]
    P3 --> Q1[Inject SF6 plus O2 Gas Mixture]
    Q1 --> Q2[Strike Plasma at 1500 W]
    Q2 --> Q3[Continuous Fluorine Radical Generation]
    Q3 --> R1[Vertical Etch of Silicon Bottom Surface]
    Q3 --> R2[Sidewall SiOxFy Passivation Layer Forms]
    R2 --> R3[Bottom Layer Desorbs due to Ion Heating]
    R1 --> S1[SiF4 Reaction Product Evacuates]
    R3 --> S1
    S1 --> T1[Process Continues until Target Depth]
    T1 --> U1[Plasma Off and Wafer Warmup]
```

### 4.3 Comparison: Bosch vs Cryogenic DRIE — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z0[DRIE Technology Choice] --> Z1{Process Requirements}
    Z1 --> ZA1[Depth more than 50 micrometer]
    Z1 --> ZA2[Sidewall smoothness critical]
    Z1 --> ZA3[Maximum selectivity needed]
    ZA1 --> ZB1[Select Bosch Process]
    ZA2 --> ZB2[Select Cryogenic DRIE]
    ZA3 --> ZB1
    ZB1 --> ZC1[Scalloped Sidewalls Acceptable]
    ZB1 --> ZC2[Photoresist Mask Compatible]
    ZB1 --> ZC3[Through Silicon Vias TSV]
    ZB2 --> ZC4[Smooth Sidewalls for Optics]
    ZB2 --> ZC5[Depth limited to 50 micrometer]
    ZB2 --> ZC6[Requires LN2 Cooling System]
```

### 4.4 MEMS Accelerometer Cross-Section (Illustrative Block Architecture)

Since a physical cross-section of a DRIE-fabricated MEMS accelerometer is not feasible in Mermaid, the following block architecture shows the function-flow from physical input to electrical output, mapped to the DRIE-fabricated structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[External Acceleration a] --> F2[MEMS Proof Mass Moves]
    F2 --> F3[Comb Finger Gap Changes]
    F3 --> F4[Capacitance Delta C]
    F4 --> F5[ASIC Charge Amplifier]
    F5 --> F6[Analog Output Voltage]
    F6 --> F7[ADC Conversion]
    F7 --> F8[Digital I2C SPI Output]
    F2 -.DRIE Suspended.-> X1[10 micrometer thick Si Proof Mass]
    X1 -.DRIE Trenches.-> X2[Released via HF Vapor Etching of SiO2]
    X2 -.DRIE Comb.-> F3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE). List its two main variants.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) is a highly anisotropic, plasma-based dry etching process used in MEMS fabrication to create deep, high-aspect-ratio structures in silicon. It is capable of producing etch depths greater than **10 µm** with aspect ratios exceeding **10:1** and near-vertical sidewall profiles (88°–90°).

**Two Main Variants (1 mark):**
1. **Bosch Process** (Time-Multiplexed Etching — TME)
2. **Cryogenic DRIE Process** (Single-chemistry low-temperature process)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of C₄F₈ gas in the Bosch DRIE process?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **C₄F₈ (octafluorocyclopentene)** gas plays a critical role in the **passivation step** of the Bosch process (1 mark). When ionized in the plasma, C₄F₈ dissociates into **CF₂* radicals** which polymerize on all exposed silicon surfaces to form a **Teflon-like fluorocarbon polymer film** of ~50 nm thickness (1 mark). This film protects the **sidewalls** from lateral chemical attack by fluorine radicals during the subsequent etching step, thereby enabling the high anisotropy (vertical profile) characteristic of DRIE (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by confusing **Bosch with Cryogenic DRIE**. Always state: Bosch = **time-multiplexed** (C₄F₈ + SF₆); Cryogenic = **single chemistry** (SF₆ + O₂) at **−120 °C**.
> - Do **not** confuse DRIE with **wet anisotropic etching** (KOH/TMAH). Wet etching is crystallographic; DRIE is **crystallography-independent**.
> - Always specify **units** in numerical answers (µm/min for etch rate, nm for scallop height).
> - When asked for "advantages," mention at least **three points** to secure full marks.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Describe the **Bosch Process** for Deep Reactive Ion Etching with a neat block diagram. Explain each step with the relevant chemical reactions. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Compare the Bosch process with the **Cryogenic DRIE process** based on **sidewall profile, mask selectivity, etch depth, and process complexity**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.A(a):**

The **Bosch Process** (Time-Multiplexed Etching) was developed by Robert Bosch GmbH and is the most widely used industrial DRIE process. It operates by **alternating rapidly between two plasma chemistries** inside an **Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP)** reactor. (1 mark)

**Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram above — 1 mark for correct sequencing of C₄F₈ and SF₆ steps)

**Step 1: Passivation Phase (3 marks)**

- Inlet gas: **C₄F₈** at ~100 sccm, chamber pressure 10–30 mTorr
- Plasma is generated using a 13.56 MHz RF source at ICP power 600 W
- C₄F₈ dissociates into **CF₂* radicals** which polymerize on **all exposed silicon surfaces** (sidewalls + bottom)
- The deposition reaction is:

$$C_4F_8 \xrightarrow{plasma} 2\, CF_2^{\ast} \xrightarrow{polymerization} -(CF_2)_n-$$

- This forms a **Teflon-like fluorocarbon film** of thickness 30–100 nm
- **No etching occurs** during this phase
- Duration: 5–10 seconds per cycle

**Step 2: Etching Phase (2 marks)**

- Inlet gas switched to **SF₆** at ~200 sccm
- DC bias voltage of ~−100 V is applied to accelerate ions toward the wafer
- SF₆ dissociates into fluorine radicals and SF₅⁺ ions:

$$SF_6 \rightarrow SF_5^{+} + F^{\ast} + e^{-}$$

- **Vertical ion bombardment** sputters away the fluorocarbon film from the **bottom** of the trench (because ions travel perpendicular to the wafer)
- Exposed silicon reacts with F* radicals:

$$Si + 4F^{\ast} \rightarrow SiF_4 \, (gas)$$

- The SiF₄ gas is evacuated by the vacuum pump
- **Sidewalls remain protected** by the fluorocarbon film (ions cannot reach them due to directional flux)
- Duration: 5–15 seconds per cycle

**Step 3: Cycle Repetition** (implicit, no separate marks)

The two steps alternate **continuously for 5–60 minutes** until the target depth is reached. Each cycle etches ~100–300 nm of silicon. The process produces characteristic **sidewall scallops** (peak-to-valley ~50–500 nm) due to the cyclic nature.

**Result:** Vertical, high-aspect-ratio silicon structures (up to 100:1 AR, depths >500 µm).

---

**Model Answer to Q.A(b):**

| Parameter | Bosch Process | Cryogenic DRIE |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Sidewall Profile** | Scalloped (50–500 nm roughness); vertical (90° ± 1°) | Smooth (roughness < 20 nm); slightly tapered (88°–90°) |
| **Mask Selectivity** | High (75–150:1 to photoresist); 200:1 to SiO₂ | Moderate (40–75:1 to photoresist) |
| **Maximum Etch Depth** | >500 µm (industrial); 1 mm+ (research) | ~50 µm typical; ~100 µm maximum |
| **Process Complexity** | Moderate; requires precise gas-switching and timing electronics | High; requires LN₂ cooling system, vacuum-isolated cold stage |
| **Throughput** | High; single chamber, fast switching | Lower; thermal stabilization time needed |
| **Process Chemistry** | Time-multiplexed (C₄F₈ then SF₆) | Continuous (SF₆ + O₂) |
| **Operating Temperature** | −10 °C to +80 °C | −110 °C to −130 °C |
| **Best Application** | TSV, inertial sensors, deep microfluidics | Optical MEMS, photonics, smooth sidewalls |

(7 marks distributed: 4 marks for table content, 3 marks for analysis of each parameter's physical origin)

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain the concept of **Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE)** in DRIE. Derive the relationship between etch rate and aspect ratio using the empirical model. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A Bosch DRIE process is used to etch a silicon wafer to a depth of **75 µm**. The etch rate is **3 µm/min**, and the cycle time is **6 seconds**. The passivation time per cycle is **2.5 seconds**. Calculate: **(i)** total process time, **(ii)** number of Bosch cycles, and **(iii)** the scallop height. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.B(a):**

**Definition of ARDE (2 marks):**
**Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE)**, also known as **RIE Lag**, is a phenomenon in DRIE where the **etch rate decreases as the aspect ratio of the feature increases**. In other words, **narrower and deeper trenches etch slower** than wider, shallower ones etched simultaneously on the same wafer. This is because:

- Transport of reactive species (F* radicals) into the trench becomes diffusion-limited at high AR
- Removal of reaction products (SiF₄ gas) out of the trench is hindered
- Ion flux at the bottom of deep trenches is reduced

**Empirical Model (3 marks):**

The most widely used ARDE model is:

$$E_R = \frac{E_{R,0}}{1 + k \cdot AR}$$

where:
- $E_R$ = etch rate at a given aspect ratio
- $E_{R,0}$ = open-area etch rate (AR = 0)
- $k$ = process-dependent lag coefficient (typically 0.1–1.0)
- $AR = d/w$ (depth/width)

**Derivation of AR Impact (2 marks):**

For a trench of width w being etched to depth d, the aspect ratio is:

$$AR = \frac{d}{w} = \frac{E_R \cdot t}{w}$$

Substituting this into the ARDE model:

$$E_R = \frac{E_{R,0}}{1 + k \cdot \frac{E_R \cdot t}{w}}$$

Rearranging:

$$E_R \left(1 + \frac{k \cdot E_R \cdot t}{w}\right) = E_{R,0}$$

$$E_R + \frac{k \cdot E_R^2 \cdot t}{w} = E_{R,0}$$

This is a quadratic in $E_R$, which shows that $E_R$ decreases as time $t$ increases (for a fixed $w$), confirming the **RIE lag effect**.

---

**Model Answer to Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Etch depth required: d = 75 µm
- Etch rate: E_R = 3 µm/min
- Total cycle time: t_cycle = 6 s (etch time + passivation time)
- Passivation time per cycle: t_pass = 2.5 s
- Hence etch time per cycle: t_etch = 6 − 2.5 = **3.5 s**

**Step (i): Total Process Time (2 marks)**

$$t_{total} = \frac{d}{E_R} = \frac{75 \ \mu m}{3 \ \mu m/min}$$

$$t_{total} = 25 \ minutes = 1500 \ seconds$$

**Step (ii): Number of Bosch Cycles (2 marks)**

$$N = \frac{t_{total}}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{1500 \ s}{6 \ s}$$

$$N = 250 \ cycles$$

**Step (iii): Scallop Height (3 marks)**

First, calculate the etch rate in µm/s:

$$E_{R,s} = \frac{3 \ \mu m/min}{60 \ s/min} = 0.05 \ \mu m/s$$

Depth etched per cycle (only during the etch step, since passivation is non-etching):

$$d_{cycle} = E_{R,s} \times t_{etch} = 0.05 \ \mu m/s \times 3.5 \ s = 0.175 \ \mu m = 175 \ nm$$

Scallop pitch = depth per cycle = 175 nm.
Scallop height (approximated as half the pitch):

$$H_s \approx \frac{175 \ nm}{2} = 87.5 \ nm$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i) Total process time:** 25 minutes (1500 s)
- **(ii) Number of Bosch cycles:** 250 cycles
- **(iii) Scallop height:** ~87.5 nm

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DRIE (KTU 2024)**

- ✅ **DRIE** = Deep Reactive Ion Etching, used for **deep, high-aspect-ratio (>10:1) silicon etching** with vertical sidewalls.
- ✅ **Two main variants:** **Bosch (time-multiplexed)** and **Cryogenic (single chemistry, low temperature)**.
- ✅ **Bosch Process gases:** **C₄F₈** for passivation (fluorocarbon film), **SF₆** for etching (F* radicals).
- ✅ **Bosch cycle:** Passivation (5–10 s) → Etching (5–15 s) → repeat. Typical cycle removes **100–300 nm** of Si.
- ✅ **Cryogenic DRIE:** **SF₆ + O₂** at **−120 °C**; sidewall passivation via **SiO_xF_y** layer.
- ✅ **Scallops:** Characteristic sidewall waviness in Bosch DRIE, height = ~half the cycle depth. Smooth sidewalls require **short cycles (<2 s)**.
- ✅ **Selectivity formula:** $S = E_{R,Si} / E_{R,Mask}$. Typical values: 50–200:1 for Si/photoresist.
- ✅ **Aspect ratio formula:** $AR = d/w$. Industrial max ~30:1, research max ~100:1.
- ✅ **Etch rate formula:** $E_R = d / t$, typical values 1–10 µm/min.
- ✅ **ARDE / RIE Lag:** Narrower trenches etch slower. Model: $E_R = E_{R,0} / (1 + k \cdot AR)$.
- ✅ **Key reaction:** $Si + 4F^{\ast} \rightarrow SiF_4 \uparrow$ (volatile product).
- ✅ **Key applications:** MEMS accelerometers, gyroscopes, TSVs, microfluidics, RF MEMS, neural probes.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Never confuse Bosch (time-multiplexed) with Cryogenic (single-chemistry); never call DRIE "wet etching."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin Film Deposition: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD), Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD), Evaporation and Sputtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thin Film Deposition: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD), Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD), Evaporation and Sputtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Thin Film Deposition?

**Thin Film Deposition** is the foundational microfabrication process used to grow layers of material (ranging from a few nanometers to several micrometres in thickness) onto a substrate surface. In MEMS (Micro Electro Mechanical Systems), these thin films act as structural layers, sacrificial layers, electrodes, sensors, piezoresistors, optical coatings, and bio-interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Thin film deposition refers to a category of manufacturing techniques used to apply a very thin film of material (typically in the range of nanometers to a few micrometers) onto a substrate surface, either by chemical or physical means, to modify the surface properties or create functional micro-structures.*

The deposition is performed in a controlled environment—either a **vacuum chamber** (for PVD processes) or a **reactor chamber** (for CVD processes)—to ensure film purity, uniformity, and adhesion.

---

## 1.2 The Two Master Categories: PVD vs CVD

| Aspect | Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) | Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving Force** | Physical (evaporation/sputtering) | Chemical reaction |
| **State of Source** | Solid → Vapor → Solid | Gas/Vapor → Solid |
| **Temperature** | Generally lower | Generally higher (200°C–900°C) |
| **Film Quality** | High purity, less conformal | Excellent step coverage |
| **Pressure** | High vacuum (~10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁹ Torr) | Atmospheric to low vacuum |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Painting a Wall" 🎨

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for a KTU Student:**
>
> Imagine you want to paint a thin, even layer on a wall:
>
> - **Evaporation** = Heating the paint until it vaporizes, and letting the vapor settle on the wall. Easy, but the paint goes mostly on the front face (poor step coverage).
> - **Sputtering** = Throwing tiny paint-balls (ions) at a paint block, knocking off paint particles that fly onto the wall. More controlled, works on side walls too.
> - **CVD** = Mixing two chemicals in a room. They react and the resulting compound naturally forms a uniform layer on **every** surface it touches, including deep crevices. Like a smart-paint that "grows" on its own.

---

## 1.4 Critical Terminology (Must-Know for KTU Board Exam)

- **Substrate**: The base wafer (typically Silicon, Glass, or Quartz) on which the film is deposited.
- **Target**: The solid source material (in sputtering) that is bombarded by ions.
- **Source/Effusion Cell**: The heated crucible containing the material to be evaporated.
- **Sticking Coefficient (S)**: The probability that an atom/molecule arriving at the substrate will remain adhered. Range: **0 ≤ S ≤ 1**.
- **Deposition Rate (R)**: The thickness of film grown per unit time, measured in **nm/min** or **Å/s**.
- **Step Coverage**: The ability of the film to uniformly coat non-planar topography (trenches, vias).
- **Conformality**: Ability to coat sidewalls and bottoms of high-aspect-ratio features uniformly.
- **Mean Free Path (λ)**: The average distance a vapor atom travels before colliding with another gas molecule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** A typical MEMS thin film ranges from **10 nm to 10 µm**. Films below 100 nm are considered "ultra-thin" and require ultra-high vacuum (UHV, < 10⁻⁹ Torr).

---

## 1.5 Why is Thin Film Deposition Critical for MEMS?

1. **Sensors** – Piezoresistive layers, piezoelectric films, thermistors.
2. **Actuators** – Shape memory alloy films, electrostatic electrode layers.
3. **Structural Layers** – Polysilicon, silicon nitride, silicon carbide.
4. **Sacrificial Layers** – SiO₂ (sacrificed in HF to release suspended structures).
5. **Bio-MEMS** – Gold/titanium coatings for biocompatibility.
6. **Optical MEMS (MOEMS)** – Anti-reflection coatings, mirrors, gratings.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PHYSICAL VAPOR DEPOSITION (PVD) — Master Framework

**PVD is a purely physical process** where a solid material is converted into vapor phase (by heating or momentum transfer) and then condensed onto the substrate to form a thin film. **No chemical reaction** occurs.

### Generic PVD Workflow

1. **Generation of Vapor Phase** → atoms ejected from source.
2. **Transport** → atoms travel through vacuum to substrate.
3. **Nucleation & Growth** → atoms condense on substrate, forming islands, then a continuous film.

---

## 2.2 EVAPORATION (Thermal & Electron-Beam)

### 2.2.1 Working Principle

In evaporation, the source material is **heated to its vaporization temperature** inside a high-vacuum chamber. The vapor pressure of the material exceeds a critical threshold (~10⁻² Torr), and atoms escape the melt surface, traveling in straight lines to the substrate.

### 2.2.2 The Langmuir Evaporation Equation

The maximum evaporation rate (atoms per unit area per second) from a free surface in vacuum is given by:

$$R_{evap} = \frac{p_s N_A}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}}$$

Where:
- $R_{evap}$ = Evaporation rate (atoms/m²·s)
- $p_s$ = Saturation vapor pressure of the source material (Pa)
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- $M$ = Molar mass of source (kg/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/mol·K**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

### 2.2.3 Two Sub-Types of Evaporation

**A. Thermal (Resistive) Evaporation**
- A tungsten or molybdenum **filament/crucible** is Joule-heated.
- Source material melts and evaporates.
- Limitation: Temperature ceiling (~1500°C) — cannot evaporate high-melting-point materials like Tungsten (3422°C).

**B. Electron-Beam (E-Beam) Evaporation**
- A high-energy electron beam (5–20 keV) is magnetically focused on the target.
- Localized heating → 10⁸ °C hot spot on a small region.
- Crucible is water-cooled copper.
- Advantage: Can evaporate **any** material, even refractory metals and dielectrics.

---

## 2.3 SPUTTERING

### 2.3.1 Working Principle

Sputtering is a momentum-transfer process. **Ions (typically Ar⁺)** are accelerated towards a negatively biased target. When the ions strike the target, they **knock out (eject) atoms** through momentum exchange. These ejected atoms travel to the substrate and form the film.

### 2.3.2 Key Formula — Sputtering Yield (Y)

$$Y = \frac{\text{Number of target atoms ejected}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$$

Y typically ranges from **0.1 to 10** atoms/ion, depending on:
- Incident ion energy (E)
- Target material atomic mass
- Angle of incidence
- Binding energy of target atoms

### 2.3.3 Sputtering Variants

| Type | Cathode Configuration | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Diode Sputtering** | Two parallel plates | Conducting targets (Al, Au) |
| **RF Sputtering** | RF (13.56 MHz) cathode | Insulating targets (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) |
| **Magnetron Sputtering** | Magnets behind target | High rate, low pressure |
| **Reactive Sputtering** | Reactive gas (O₂, N₂) added | Compound films (TiN, Al₂O₃) |
| **Ion-Beam Sputtering** | External ion source | Ultra-clean, low pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnetron Sputtering** uses a magnetic field parallel to the target surface to trap electrons near the target. This increases plasma density by **10–100×**, leading to much higher deposition rates at lower pressures. This is the **industry standard** for MEMS metallization.

---

## 2.4 CHEMICAL VAPOR DEPOSITION (CVD) — Master Framework

**CVD uses chemical reactions** of volatile precursors on or near the heated substrate surface to produce a solid thin film. The byproducts are volatile and are pumped away.

### 2.4.1 The Seven CVD Steps (Generic Mechanism)

1. **Transport** of reactant gases into the reactor.
2. **Gas-phase diffusion** of reactants to the substrate surface.
3. **Adsorption** of reactants onto the surface.
4. **Surface diffusion** and **chemical reaction** on the surface.
5. **Nucleation and film growth**.
6. **Desorption** of gaseous byproducts.
7. **Transport** of byproducts out of the reactor.

### 2.4.2 The Arrhenius Growth Rate Equation

The deposition rate for a surface-reaction-limited CVD process follows:

$$R_{CVD} = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

Where:
- $R_{CVD}$ = Deposition rate (nm/min or µm/hr)
- $A$ = Pre-exponential factor (depends on precursor concentration)
- $E_a$ = Activation energy (eV or J/mol) — typically **0.5 to 3 eV** for CVD
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**
- $T$ = Substrate temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two CVD Regimes:**
> - **Surface-Reaction Limited (Low T)**: Rate ∝ exp(−Ea/kT) — small changes in T cause large changes in rate.
> - **Mass-Transport Limited (High T)**: Rate limited by gas delivery — relatively temperature-insensitive.
> - Operating CVD near the **transition between these two regimes** gives the best uniformity.

### 2.4.3 Major CVD Variants (KTU Board Favourite!)

| Variant | Pressure | Temperature | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **APCVD** (Atmospheric Pressure CVD) | 760 Torr | 600–900°C | Simple, fast, poor uniformity |
| **LPCVD** (Low Pressure CVD) | 0.1–10 Torr | 550–850°C | Excellent uniformity & step coverage |
| **PECVD** (Plasma-Enhanced CVD) | 0.1–10 Torr | 200–400°C | Plasma assists, low T (for polymers/ICs) |
| **MOCVD** (Metal-Organic CVD) | 10–100 Torr | 600–800°C | Compound semiconductors (GaAs, InP) |
| **LPCVD Polysilicon** | 0.1–1 Torr | 580–650°C | MEMS structural layer (most common) |

---

## 2.5 Comparison: Evaporation vs Sputtering vs CVD (KTU Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Thermal Evap. | E-Beam Evap. | Sputtering | CVD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy of depositing atoms** | 0.1–0.5 eV (low) | 0.1–1 eV | 1–100 eV (high) | Thermal ~0.1 eV |
| **Adhesion** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Step Coverage** | Poor (< 30%) | Poor | Fair (50–70%) | Excellent (> 90%) |
| **Film Density** | Low | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Purity** | High | High | Possible Ar inclusion | Possible C/H inclusion |
| **Substrate Temperature** | RT–200°C | RT–200°C | RT–500°C | 200–900°C |
| **Deposition Rate** | High (1 µm/min) | High | Low–Moderate | Moderate |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Low | Moderate | High | Moderate–High |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| Formula | Expression | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Free Path | $\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$ | Vacuum design | meters |
| Knudsen Number | $K_n = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | Flow regime (molecular/viscous) | dimensionless |
| Evaporation Rate (Langmuir) | $R_{evap} = \dfrac{p_s N_A}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}}$ | Thermal evap. rate | atoms/m²·s |
| Sputtering Yield | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Ejected atoms}}{\text{Incident ions}}$ | Sputter process | atoms/ion |
| Arrhenius CVD Rate | $R = A \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ | CVD kinetics | nm/min |
| Sticking Coefficient | $S = \dfrac{N_{adhered}}{N_{incident}}$ | Film growth | dimensionless |
| Deposition Rate | $R = \dfrac{t}{t_{deposit}}$ | Process control | nm/s or Å/s |
| Film Stress (Thermal) | $\sigma_{th} = E_f (\alpha_s - \alpha_f) \Delta T$ | Reliability | Pa |
| Stoney's Equation | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s h_s^2}{6 h_f R_{curvature}}$ | Wafer bow measurement | Pa |
| Mass of film | $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot t$ | Thickness calibration | kg |
| Deposition rate from flux | $R = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot M}{\rho \cdot N_A}$ | Flux-to-rate | nm/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoney's Equation** is extremely important for KTU Module 2 — it links **thin film stress** to wafer curvature, which is critical for MEMS reliability (e.g., preventing diaphragm buckling in pressure sensors).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MEMS Accelerometer (ADXL series)** → Uses LPCVD polysilicon structural layer + sputtered Al electrodes.
- **Gyroscopes** → CVD epitaxial polysilicon for matched mechanical structures.
- **Pressure Sensors** → LPCVD Si₃N₄ diaphragm + e-beam evaporated Al contacts.
- **RF MEMS Switches** → PECVD SiO₂ dielectric + sputtered Au contacts.
- **Hard Disk Drives** → Sputtered magnetic layers (CoCrPt) + CVD diamond-like carbon (DLC) overcoat.
- **Solar Cells** → PECVD amorphous Si (a-Si:H) absorber layer.
- **Semiconductor Industry** → CVD Cu interconnects, W vias, SiO₂, Si₃N₄.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Free Path of a Vapor Atom in a Vacuum

> **Why this matters:** Determines whether your evaporation/sputtering atoms reach the substrate ballistically (good) or get scattered by background gas (bad, causes contamination and non-uniformity).

### Starting Premise (Kinetic Theory of Gases)

The mean free path $\lambda$ is the average distance a particle travels between successive collisions with other molecules in the gas.

For a hard-sphere model of gas molecules, the collisional cross-section between two molecules of diameter $d$ is:

$$\sigma_{collision} = \pi (2r)^2 = \pi d^2$$

where $d$ is the **effective collision diameter** (~ 0.3 nm for air, ~ 0.36 nm for Ar).

### Derivation

The mean free path in a gas of number density $n$ (atoms/m³) is:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 n}$$

Using the **ideal gas law** $P = n k_B T$, we substitute $n = \dfrac{P}{k_B T}$:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2} \cdot \frac{k_B T}{P}$$

Final equation:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}}$$

### Numerical Example — KTU Style

> A PVD chamber is at pressure $P = 10^{-6}$ Torr and temperature $T = 300$ K. For Ar gas ($d = 3.6 \times 10^{-10}$ m), find the mean free path. (1 Torr = 133.3 Pa)

**Step 1:** Convert pressure to Pascals.

$$P = 10^{-6} \text{ Torr} \times 133.3 \text{ Pa/Torr} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Plug into the formula.

$$\lambda = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2} \pi (3.6 \times 10^{-10})^2 (1.333 \times 10^{-4})}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator.

$$\text{Num} = 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$$

**Step 4:** Denominator.

$$\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot (1.296 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot (1.333 \times 10^{-4})$$
$$= 1.414 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 1.296 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 1.333 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$= 4.444 \cdot 1.728 \times 10^{-23}$$
$$= 7.680 \times 10^{-23}$$

**Step 5:** Divide.

$$\lambda = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{7.68 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 53.9 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result: $\lambda \approx 54$ m** — much greater than the chamber size (~0.5 m). This confirms **molecular flow regime**, ideal for high-quality thin films.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Langmuir Evaporation Rate (Full Derivation)

> **Why this matters:** Tells us the maximum possible mass of material that can evaporate per unit time from a crucible — critical for designing deposition thickness.

### Step 1 — Hertz-Knudsen Starting Equation

The mass flux $J_m$ (kg/m²·s) of atoms leaving a liquid surface in equilibrium with its vapor at saturation pressure $p_s$ is given by the **Hertz-Knudsen equation**:

$$J_m = \sqrt{\frac{M}{2 \pi R T}} \cdot p_s$$

> **Logic:** From kinetic theory, the rate of molecules hitting a unit area per unit time is $\dfrac{1}{4} n \bar{c}$, where $\bar{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$. Setting this equal to the evaporating flux (with sticking coefficient = 1) and using $p_s = n k_B T$ yields the equation above.

### Step 2 — Convert to Number Flux (atoms/m²·s)

$$R_{evap} = \frac{J_m \cdot N_A}{M}$$

Substituting:

$$R_{evap} = \frac{p_s N_A}{M} \sqrt{\frac{M}{2 \pi R T}}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$\boxed{R_{evap} = \frac{p_s N_A}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}}}$$

### Numerical Example

> For **Aluminum** at $T = 1500$ K, $p_s \approx 1$ Pa, $M = 27 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol. Find the evaporation rate.

$$R_{evap} = \frac{(1) (6.022 \times 10^{23})}{\sqrt{2 \pi (0.027) (8.314) (1500)}}$$

**Numerator:** $6.022 \times 10^{23}$

**Denominator:**
$$2\pi \cdot 0.027 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 1500 = 2 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 0.027 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 1500$$
$$= 6.2832 \cdot 0.027 \cdot 12471 = 0.1697 \cdot 12471 = 2116$$
$$\sqrt{2116} \approx 46$$

$$R_{evap} \approx \frac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{46} \approx 1.31 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/m}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

> ✅ **Result:** ~ $1.3 \times 10^{22}$ atoms/m²·s of Al is evaporating at 1500 K — sufficient for a deposition rate of ~1 µm/min on a substrate 30 cm away.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stoney's Equation for Thin Film Stress

> **Why this matters:** KTU examiners love this — it relates film stress to the visible curvature of the wafer after deposition.

### Setup

A thin film of thickness $h_f$ and biaxial modulus $E_f / (1 - \nu_f)$ is deposited on a much thicker substrate of thickness $h_s$. The film has intrinsic stress $\sigma_f$, causing the substrate to bend with radius of curvature $R$.

### Derivation (Force & Moment Balance)

**Step 1:** The force per unit width in the film is:

$$F_f = \sigma_f \cdot h_f$$

**Step 2:** This force is balanced by an equal and opposite force in the substrate (the film pulls the substrate into curvature). The substrate experiences both a tensile and compressive layer such that:

$$\sigma_f h_f = -\int_{-h_s}^{0} \sigma_s(z) \, dz$$

**Step 3:** Using the **biaxial stress-strain relation** and the **bending moment** of the substrate, we get:

$$M = \frac{E_s h_s^3}{12 (1 - \nu_s)} \cdot \frac{1}{R}$$

**Step 4:** Equating the moment of the film force with the substrate bending moment, and noting that $h_f \ll h_s$:

$$\sigma_f h_f \cdot \frac{h_s}{2} = \frac{E_s h_s^3}{12 (1 - \nu_s) R}$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying:

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{E_s h_s^2}{6 (1 - \nu_s) h_f R}}$$

This is **Stoney's Equation (1909)** — the cornerstone of thin film stress measurement.

### Numerical Example

> A 1 µm Si₃N₄ film is deposited on a 525 µm thick Si wafer (100). After deposition, the wafer bows with a radius of curvature $R = 10$ m. Calculate the film stress.
>
> Given: For Si(100), $\dfrac{E_s}{1-\nu_s} = 180.5$ GPa.

$$\sigma_f = \frac{(180.5 \times 10^9) (525 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 (1 \times 10^{-6}) (10)}$$

**Numerator:** $180.5 \times 10^9 \times 2.756 \times 10^{-7} = 4.974 \times 10^4$

**Denominator:** $6 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 = 6 \times 10^{-5}$

$$\sigma_f = \frac{4.974 \times 10^4}{6 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 8.29 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \approx 829 \text{ MPa}$$

> ✅ **Result:** $\sigma_f \approx 829$ MPa (tensile). This is in the typical range for LPCVD Si₃N₄ (1 GPa tensile).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for MEMS Applications

| Application | Required Film Property | Recommended Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| MEMS Structural Layer (Polysilicon) | Low stress, uniform thickness | **LPCVD** | Best uniformity, conformal, low defect density |
| Metal Interconnects (Al, Cu) | High conductivity, low contact resistance | **Sputtering** | Good step coverage, high purity |
| Dielectric (SiO₂, Si₃N₄) on metal | Low temperature | **PECVD** | Plasma enables low-T deposition (~300°C) |
| Optical Mirror (Ag, Au) | High reflectivity, smooth | **E-beam evaporation** | High purity, smooth morphology |
| Piezoelectric (PZT, AlN) | Crystalline orientation | **Sputtering / MOCVD** | Controlled orientation |
| Hard, Wear-resistant Coating (DLC, TiN) | High hardness | **Reactive sputtering** | High-energy deposition = dense film |
| Sacrificial Layer (SiO₂) | High etch rate in HF | **LPCVD / Thermal Oxide** | Tunable, well-known chemistry |
| High-Aspect-Ratio Trench Fill | Conformal step coverage | **LPCVD** | Gas diffusion into trenches |
| Bio-compatible coating (Ti, Au) | Adhesion to substrate | **Sputtering** | Excellent adhesion due to high atom energy |
| Flexible Electronics on Polymer | Low substrate temperature | **PECVD / Thermal evap.** | Compatible with low-T substrates |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Example — Arrhenius CVD Calculation (KTU Favorite!)

> A silicon wafer is processed in an LPCVD furnace for polysilicon deposition. The deposition rates at two temperatures are:
> - At $T_1 = 900$ K: $R_1 = 10$ nm/min
> - At $T_2 = 1000$ K: $R_2 = 50$ nm/min
>
> Calculate the **activation energy** $E_a$ for this CVD process.

### Solution

Using the Arrhenius equation:

$$R = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{\exp(-E_a / k_B T_2)}{\exp(-E_a / k_B T_1)} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\ln\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Solving for $E_a$:

$$E_a = k_B \cdot \ln\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \cdot \left[\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 1:** Compute ratio.

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{50}{10} = 5$$

**Step 2:** Compute natural log.

$$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$

**Step 3:** Compute inverse temperature difference.

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{900} - \frac{1}{1000} = \frac{1000 - 900}{900000} = \frac{100}{900000} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $E_a$.

$$E_a = (8.617 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV/K}) \cdot 1.6094 \cdot (1.111 \times 10^{-4})^{-1}$$

$$E_a = (8.617 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 1.6094 \cdot 9000$$

$$E_a = 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 14484.6$$

$$E_a \approx 1.248 \text{ eV}$$

> ✅ **Result:** $E_a \approx 1.25$ eV (or ~ 120 kJ/mol). This is in the typical range for surface-reaction-limited LPCVD polysilicon (0.8 – 1.6 eV).

---

## 3.6 Detailed Worked Example — Deposition Rate from a Flux

> An evaporation source emits a flux $\Phi = 10^{19}$ atoms/m²·s of Titanium. The substrate is 30 cm from the source, in molecular flow. Calculate the deposition rate.
>
> Given: $M_{Ti} = 47.87$ g/mol, $\rho_{Ti} = 4.51$ g/cm³.

### Solution

For a point source in molecular flow, the flux arriving at the substrate (assuming cosine distribution) is:

$$\Phi_{sub} = \frac{\Phi_{source}}{\pi r^2} \cdot A_{source}$$

For simplicity, we use the average flux value given. The mass deposition rate per unit area:

$$\dot{m} = \Phi \cdot \frac{M}{N_A} = 10^{19} \cdot \frac{47.87 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{4.787 \times 10^{17}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 7.95 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/m}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

Convert to thickness per second:

$$R = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{7.95 \times 10^{-7}}{4510}$$

$$R = 1.76 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m/s} = 0.176 \text{ nm/s} \approx 10.6 \text{ nm/min}$$

> ✅ **Result:** Deposition rate ≈ **10.6 nm/min** of Titanium. To deposit a 100 nm film, we need ~10 minutes of evaporation.

## 4.2 Evaporation System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV[High Vacuum Pump 10 to the minus 6 Torr] --> CH[Vacuum Chamber]
    CH --> FIL[Tungsten Filament or E Beam Gun]
    FIL --> CRU[Crucible holding source material]
    CRU --> VAP[Vapor Cloud]
    VAP --> SUB[Substrate Holder]
    SUB --> FILM[Thin Film Grows on Substrate]
    CH --> THK[Quartz Crystal Monitor measures thickness]
    THK --> CTRL[Rate and Thickness Controller]
    CTRL --> SHUT[Shutter open or close]
    SHUT --> SUB
```

## 4.3 Sputtering System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PUMP[Vacuum Pump] --> CH[Chamber filled with Ar gas 1 to 10 mTorr]
    CH --> CAT[Cathode Target negatively biased]
    CH --> ANO[Anode Substrate Holder]
    HV_BIAS[DC or RF Power Supply] --> CAT
    HV_BIAS --> ANO
    CH --> PLASMA[Plasma Ar ions and electrons]
    PLASMA --> ACCEL[Ar plus ions accelerated to target]
    ACCEL --> EJECT[Target atoms ejected by momentum transfer]
    EJECT --> SUB[Substrate receives sputtered atoms]
    SUB --> FILM[Thin Film]
    CH --> MAGNET[Optional Magnet array for Magnetron]
    MAGNET --> TRAP[Trap electrons near target]
    TRAP --> PLASMA
```

## 4.4 CVD Reactor — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GAS1[Gas Inlet 1 e.g. Silane SiH4] --> MIX[Gas Mixing Manifold]
    GAS2[Gas Inlet 2 e.g. NH3 for nitride] --> MIX
    GAS3[Carrier Gas e.g. N2 or H2] --> MIX
    MIX --> REACTOR[Hot Wall or Cold Wall Reactor 600 to 900 C]
    REACTOR --> SUB[Heated Substrate]
    SUB --> REACT[Surface Chemical Reaction]
    REACT --> FILM[Thin Film Solid]
    REACT --> BYPROD[Gaseous Byproducts H2 HCl etc]
    BYPROD --> EXHAUST[Exhaust and Scrubber]
    REACTOR --> PRESS[Pressure Controller]
    PRESS --> PUMP[Vacuum Pump for LPCVD]
```
