## 4.5 PVD vs CVD — Process Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PVD_Flow
        P1[Solid Source] --> P2[Heat or Ion Bombardment]
        P2 --> P3[Vapor Phase Physical]
        P3 --> P4[Line of Sight Transport]
        P4 --> P5[Condense on Substrate]
    end
    subgraph CVD_Flow
        C1[Volatile Precursor Gases] --> C2[Transport to Reactor]
        C2 --> C3[Surface Adsorption]
        C3 --> C4[Chemical Reaction on Substrate]
        C4 --> C5[Solid Film Grows]
        C5 --> C6[Volatile Byproducts Desorb]
        C6 --> C7[Exhaust Pumped Out]
    end
```

---

## 4.6 Step Coverage Comparison — Schematic of Conformal vs Non-Conformal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate with Trench] --> B1[Evaporation Poor step coverage sidewalls shadowed]
    A --> B2[Sputtering Fair step coverage]
    A --> B3[CVD Excellent conformal coverage wraps around trench]
    B1 --> R1[Film thicker on top and bottom of trench]
    B2 --> R2[Film uniform on top and fair on sidewalls]
    B3 --> R3[Film uniformly coats all surfaces]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD). List any two advantages of CVD over PVD.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** CVD is a thin film deposition technique in which volatile precursors react chemically on or near a heated substrate surface to form a solid thin film, with byproducts being pumped away as volatile gases.

**Two Advantages of CVD over PVD:**
1. **Superior step coverage / conformality** — CVD can uniformly coat high-aspect-ratio trenches and sidewalls, while PVD (especially evaporation) has poor line-of-sight coverage.
2. **Higher film density and purity** — CVD films are typically denser with fewer pinholes, making them ideal for diffusion barriers, dielectrics, and MEMS structural layers.

---

### **Question 2: What is sputtering yield? State the factors affecting it.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Sputtering yield (Y) is the average number of target atoms ejected from a target surface per incident ion.

$$Y = \frac{\text{Number of target atoms ejected}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$$

**Factors affecting sputtering yield:**
1. **Incident ion energy** — Yield increases with ion energy up to a peak (typically 10–100 keV).
2. **Mass ratio of ion to target atom** — Optimum momentum transfer when $M_{ion} \approx M_{target}$ (e.g., Ar⁺ sputters Cu well, Xe⁺ sputters W better).
3. **Angle of incidence** — Yield peaks at 60–80° incidence.
4. **Target surface binding energy** — Higher binding energy → lower yield.
5. **Crystal orientation** — Y depends on whether the target is single-crystal or polycrystalline.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice**. Below, both alternatives are provided.

---

### **❓ Question A: Discuss the Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) techniques of Evaporation and Sputtering in detail. Compare their advantages and limitations for MEMS applications.**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — Thermal Evaporation (7 marks)**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
In thermal evaporation, the source material is heated in a high vacuum (≤ 10⁻⁶ Torr) until its vapor pressure exceeds ~10⁻² Torr. Atoms then evaporate and travel in straight lines to the substrate, condensing to form a film. The process is purely physical; no chemical reaction occurs.

**2. Setup & Variants (2 marks):**
- **Resistive heating** — Tungsten filament or boat, Joule heated to 1000–1500°C. Limited to low-melting-point metals (Al, Au, Ag).
- **Electron-beam (E-beam)** — A focused electron beam (5–20 keV) locally heats the target. Water-cooled copper crucible. Can evaporate refractory metals (W, Mo, Ta) and dielectrics (SiO₂).

**3. Key Equations (2 marks):**

$$R_{evap} = \frac{p_s N_A}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$$

**4. Advantages & Limitations (1 mark):**

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| High deposition rate (1 µm/min) | Poor step coverage (line-of-sight) |
| High purity films | Poor adhesion (low energy atoms ~0.1 eV) |
| Simple, low cost | Limited to materials with low vapor pressure achievable |

#### **Part (b) — Sputtering (7 marks)**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
In sputtering, a target is bombarded with energetic ions (typically Ar⁺ at 0.5–5 keV) in a low-pressure plasma. Through momentum transfer, target atoms are ejected (sputtered) and travel to the substrate to form a film.

**2. Sputtering Yield (2 marks):**

$$Y = \frac{\text{Ejected atoms}}{\text{Incident ions}} \quad (0.1 \leq Y \leq 10)$$

Y depends on ion energy, mass ratio, angle of incidence, and target binding energy.

**3. Sputtering Variants (2 marks):**
- **DC Diode Sputtering** — for conductive targets.
- **RF Sputtering** (13.56 MHz) — for insulating targets (SiO₂, AlN).
- **Magnetron Sputtering** — magnetic field confines electrons → 10–100× higher plasma density → higher rate, lower pressure.
- **Reactive Sputtering** — adding O₂ or N₂ to form compound films (TiN, Al₂O₃).

**4. Advantages & Limitations (1 mark):**

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Excellent step coverage (50–70%) | Possible Ar gas inclusion in film |
| High adhesion (1–100 eV atoms) | Lower deposition rate than e-beam |
| Works for any material (metal/dielectric) | Target erosion (non-uniform usage) |
| Easy to control composition | More expensive than evaporation |

---

### **❓ Question B: Explain in detail the Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) process. Discuss its variants (APCVD, LPCVD, PECVD) and derive the Arrhenius growth rate equation.**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — CVD Process and Mechanism (7 marks)**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
CVD is a thin film deposition process in which volatile precursors undergo **chemical reactions** on a heated substrate to form a solid film, with byproducts pumped away.

**2. Seven Sequential Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Transport** of reactant gases into the reactor.
2. **Gas-phase diffusion** of reactants toward the substrate.
3. **Adsorption** of reactants onto the heated surface.
4. **Surface diffusion** of adsorbed species.
5. **Surface chemical reaction** — film-forming reaction occurs.
6. **Nucleation and growth** of the film.
7. **Desorption** of byproducts and their removal by pumping.

**3. Typical Reaction Examples (2 marks):**
- Polysilicon: $\text{SiH}_4 \rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\text{H}_2$
- SiO₂: $\text{SiH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2$
- Si₃N₄: $3\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 4\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4 + 6\text{HCl} + 6\text{H}_2$
- Tungsten: $\text{WF}_6 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{W} + 6\text{HF}$

**4. Key Feature — Step Coverage (1 mark):**
CVD is **conformal** — it uniformly coats deep trenches, sidewalls, and 3D structures due to gas-phase diffusion.

#### **Part (b) — CVD Variants and Arrhenius Derivation (7 marks)**

**1. Variants Table (3 marks):**

| Variant | Pressure | Temperature | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| APCVD | 760 Torr | 600–900°C | Doped oxides, simple |
| LPCVD | 0.1–10 Torr | 550–850°C | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄, uniform films |
| PECVD | 0.1–10 Torr | 200–400°C | IC passivation, MEMS on CMOS |
| MOCVD | 10–100 Torr | 600–800°C | GaAs, InP compound semiconductors |

**2. Derivation of Arrhenius Equation (3 marks):**

From surface reaction kinetics, the deposition rate $R$ is proportional to the surface concentration of activated complexes:

$$R = k_s \cdot C_s$$

where $C_s$ is the reactant surface concentration and $k_s$ is the surface reaction rate constant.

Following transition state theory (Arrhenius):

$$k_s = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{R_{CVD} = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)}$$

**Two Regimes:**
- **Surface-reaction-limited (low T):** $R$ strongly dependent on $T$.
- **Mass-transport-limited (high T):** $R$ weakly dependent on $T$ (gas diffusion limited).

**3. Example Application (1 mark):**
LPCVD polysilicon at 620°C, $E_a \approx 1.6$ eV, is the standard MEMS structural material.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Thin Film Deposition Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. ❌ **Confusing PVD & CVD definitions** — PVD = *physical* (no chemical reaction). CVD = *chemical reaction* (always). Examiners deduct **2 marks** if a student writes "CVD also does physical evaporation."
>
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting units in Mean Free Path** — Always give the answer in meters (or convert to cm if asked). A numerical answer in the wrong unit = **-1 mark**.
>
> 3. ❌ **Skipping the sticking coefficient in Langmuir equation** — If $S < 1$, the actual deposition rate is $R_{actual} = S \cdot R_{evap}$. Examiners expect this nuance for full marks.
>
> 4. ❌ **Confusing step coverage with conformality** — Step coverage = uniformity on 3D features. Conformality = ratio of sidewall/bottom to top thickness. Many students interchange them.
>
> 5. ❌ **Drawing wrong Sputtering diagram** — The target is the **cathode** (negative), substrate/anode is grounded. Many students draw it backwards.
>
> 6. ❌ **Stoney's Equation missing $\nu_s$** — Forgetting the Poisson ratio in the denominator is a classic 2-mark loss.
>
> 7. ❌ **Not mentioning that LPCVD is the workhorse of MEMS** — For any question on MEMS structural layers, **always mention LPCVD polysilicon** as the answer.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read this the night before the KTU exam!**

- 📌 **PVD = Physical** (evaporation, sputtering). **CVD = Chemical** (gas reaction).
- 📌 **Evaporation** = low atom energy (0.1 eV), poor step coverage, but high rate.
- 📌 **E-beam evaporation** is preferred for **refractory metals** (W, Mo).
- 📌 **Sputtering** = high atom energy (1–100 eV), excellent adhesion, moderate step coverage.
- 📌 **Magnetron sputtering** is the **industry standard** for MEMS metallization.
- 📌 **CVD** = best **step coverage**, conformal, but requires high T (200–900°C).
- 📌 **LPCVD** = best uniformity. **PECVD** = lowest T. **APCVD** = simplest.
- 📌 **Langmuir equation** gives evaporation rate in atoms/m²·s.
- 📌 **Mean free path** $\lambda$ must be >> chamber size for molecular flow.
- 📌 **Arrhenius equation** governs CVD rate: $R = A \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$.
- 📌 **Sputtering yield** depends on ion energy, mass ratio, incidence angle.
- 📌 **Stoney's equation** for thin film stress: $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s h_s^2}{6 (1 - \nu_s) h_f R}$.
- 📌 **Sacrificial layer in MEMS** = SiO₂ (removed by HF to release polysilicon structures).
- 📌 **LPCVD polysilicon** is the **most common MEMS structural material**.
- 📌 **PECVD Si₃N₄** is used as a low-temperature passivation layer on CMOS-MEMS.
- 📌 **Reactive sputtering** forms compound films like TiN by adding O₂/N₂ to Ar.
- 📌 **Step coverage hierarchy:** CVD > Sputtering > Evaporation.
- 📌 **Film density hierarchy:** CVD > Sputtering > Evaporation.
- 📌 For KTU ESE: always state **working pressure**, **substrate temperature**, and **application** when comparing deposition methods.
- 📌 Numerical problems often test **Mean Free Path**, **Langmuir rate**, or **Arrhenius activation energy** — practice all three!
- 📌 **Memorize the typical values:** $\lambda$ at 10⁻⁶ Torr ≈ 50 m, Si₃N₄ stress ≈ 1 GPa tensile, LPCVD poly-Si rate ≈ 10 nm/min.

> **🎯 KTU 2024 Final Tip:** For Part B, always structure your answer into: **(1) Principle, (2) Setup/Diagram, (3) Key Equations, (4) Variants, (5) Advantages/Limitations, (6) MEMS Application**. Examiners allocate marks in exactly this pattern!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystal Planes and Orientation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crystal Planes and Orientation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In crystallography and solid-state physics, a **crystal plane** is an imaginary planar surface that passes through a regular, repeating array of lattice points inside a crystal lattice. Every crystalline material (such as silicon, germanium, or quartz) is composed of atoms arranged in a periodic three-dimensional pattern, and these atoms naturally lie on multiple families of parallel, equally-spaced planes.

**Crystallographic Orientation** refers to the geometric relationship between the external surface of a crystal (or a fabricated wafer) and the internal arrangement of atoms on specific crystal planes. In Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS), the orientation of a silicon wafer (commonly described as **<100>**, **<110>**, or **<111>**) directly determines its mechanical, electrical, chemical, and anisotropic etching properties.

The mathematical language used to identify a particular crystal plane is called the **Miller Index**, denoted as the triplet $(h\,k\,l)$ enclosed in parentheses: $(h\,k\,l)$. The corresponding crystallographic *direction* is denoted using square brackets: $[h\,k\,l]$. The family of equivalent planes is denoted using curly brackets: $\{h\,k\,l\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):**
> Crystal planes and orientation form the **foundational prerequisite** for understanding:
> 1. Anisotropic wet etching of silicon in KOH/TMAH.
> 2. Piezoelectric actuation in quartz and PZT substrates.
> 3. Stress and Young's modulus variation in single-crystal silicon.
> 4. Wafer bonding and surface micromachining alignment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-story building with a perfectly regular grid of rooms**. Each floor is a horizontal "plane," each vertical wall is a vertical "plane," and each diagonal staircase slab is a "plane" cutting through the building at an angle. A crystal is like this building — but instead of rooms, it contains atoms; and instead of one building, the grid repeats infinitely in 3D.

Now, imagine you are a window-washer. You can choose:
- A **horizontal window** (like the floor of a building) → corresponds to the **(100) plane**.
- A **vertical window facing the street** → corresponds to the **(010) plane**.
- A **diagonal window** cutting through corners → corresponds to the **(111) plane**.

The *orientation* of the silicon wafer in a MEMS device is simply the question: **"Which window of this atomic building are we looking through, and which way is it facing?"**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> - $(hkl)$ → a specific **plane**
> - $[hkl]$ → a specific **direction** (perpendicular to that plane in cubic systems)
> - $\{hkl\}$ → the entire **family** of equivalent planes (by symmetry)
> - $\langle hkl \rangle$ → the entire **family** of equivalent directions

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Silicon Lattice Constant ($a$)** | **$5.431 \, \text{Å} = 0.5431 \, \text{nm}$** | Unit cell edge length |
| **Atoms per unit cell (Diamond cubic)** | **8** | Silicon, Germanium |
| **Atomic Packing Factor (FCC portion)** | **0.74** | Maximum density direction |
| **Young's Modulus $\langle 100 \rangle$ (Si)** | **$130 \, \text{GPa}$** | Anisotropic stiffness |
| **Young's Modulus $\langle 111 \rangle$ (Si)** | **$188 \, \text{GPa}$** | Anisotropic stiffness |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind Miller Indices — A Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The Miller indices are not invented arbitrarily; they are systematically derived through a rigorous, repeatable procedure. Understanding the *why* behind each step is essential for solving KTU numerical problems.

- **Step 1 — Identify the Origin and Axes**
  Choose a lattice point as the origin (cannot lie on the plane in question). Identify the three crystallographic axes $x, y, z$ along the unit cell edges $a, b, c$.

- **Step 2 — Find the Intercepts**
  Determine where the plane intersects each of the three axes, measured in units of the lattice constant. The intercepts are written as multiples of $a, b, c$. For example, intercepts of $2a, 3b, \infty c$ mean the plane is parallel to the $z$-axis.

- **Step 3 — Take Reciprocals**
  This is the most critical step. Take the reciprocal of each intercept value. The reciprocal operation **transforms infinity into zero** (so parallel-to-axis planes are easily represented) and **normalizes the values to manageable integers**.

- **Step 4 — Reduce to Lowest Integers (Clear Fractions)**
  Multiply the reciprocals by the smallest common denominator to obtain the smallest set of integers $(h, k, l)$.

- **Step 5 — Enclose in Parentheses**
  Write the integers as $(h\,k\,l)$ without commas. Negative values are denoted with an overbar (e.g., $h = -1$ is written as $\bar{1}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Use Reciprocals? — The Intuitive Reason:**
> In a cubic crystal, the direction $[hkl]$ is *perpendicular* to the plane $(hkl)$. The reciprocal relationship between plane indices and direction indices in a cubic lattice is what makes Miller indices mathematically elegant. A plane that is "far away" (large intercept) has a small Miller index, and vice versa. This inversion mirrors the physical concept that *closer planes have higher density of atoms and stronger diffraction effects*.

### 2.2 Families of Planes and Directions

Because crystals exhibit rotational symmetry, multiple distinct $(hkl)$ planes are physically equivalent and are grouped into a family. The notation used is:

- $\{hkl\}$ → family of equivalent **planes**
- $\langle hkl \rangle$ → family of equivalent **directions**

For example, in a cubic crystal, the family $\{100\}$ contains **six equivalent planes**:
$(100), (\bar{1}00), (010), (0\bar{1}0), (001), (00\bar{1})$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all the high-yield equations that KTU examiners expect students to memorize for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

| # | Formula | Description | Application in MEMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(hkl) \rightarrow$ from intercepts $\left(\frac{1}{x}, \frac{1}{y}, \frac{1}{z}\right)$ | Miller index derivation | Wafer identification |
| 2 | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$ | Interplanar spacing (cubic only) | X-ray diffraction, thin film thickness |
| 3 | $\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$ | Direction cosines of $[hkl]$ | Crystal orientation verification |
| 4 | Angle between two planes $(h_1k_1l_1)$ and $(h_2k_2l_2)$: $\cos \theta = \dfrac{h_1h_2 + k_1k_2 + l_1l_2}{\sqrt{h_1^2+k_1^2+l_1^2}\sqrt{h_2^2+k_2^2+l_2^2}}$ | Inter-planar angle (cubic only) | Etch stop analysis |
| 5 | Linear density along $[uvw]$: $\text{LD} = \dfrac{\text{atoms on direction vector}}{\text{length of vector}}$ | Atomic density in a direction | Slip plane analysis |
| 6 | Planar density of $(hkl)$: $\text{PD} = \dfrac{\text{atoms in plane}}{\text{area of plane}}$ | Atomic density in a plane | Etch rate correlation |
| 7 | Silicon $(100)$ etch rate ratio: $R_{(100)} : R_{(110)} : R_{(111)} = 400 : 60 : 1$ (KOH) | Anisotropic etching priority | MEMS bulk micromachining |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In MEMS device fabrication, crystal orientation is **not** a theoretical curiosity — it is a **design parameter that determines device behavior**:

- **Bulk Micromachining:** KOH etches silicon $(111)$ planes **400 times slower** than $(100)$ planes. By choosing a **<100> oriented wafer**, designers can create precise V-grooves, pyramidal cavities, and suspended membranes with atomically flat $(111)$ sidewalls.
- **Piezoelectric MEMS:** Quartz crystals are cut along specific orientations (AT-cut, BT-cut, ST-cut) to achieve **zero temperature coefficient** at operating frequencies — crucial for oscillators and gyroscopes.
- **CMOS-MEMS Integration:** The mechanical properties of polysilicon depend on the grain orientation relative to the loading direction, affecting resonator $Q$-factor.
- **Stress Engineering:** Thin films deposited on different crystal orientations develop different residual stresses due to lattice mismatch.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Interplanar Spacing for a Cubic Crystal

The interplanar spacing $d_{hkl}$ is defined as the perpendicular distance between two adjacent parallel planes of the family $\{hkl\}$.

**Starting Setup:** Consider a cubic unit cell with edge length $a$. Consider a plane $(hkl)$ that intercepts the axes at $\frac{a}{h}, \frac{a}{k}, \frac{a}{l}$. The normal to this plane from the origin meets the plane at the point $N$. We seek the length of $ON = d_{hkl}$.

**Step 1 — Express the Intercept Vector Components**

The plane intercepts the $x, y, z$ axes at coordinates:

$$\left( \frac{a}{h},\ \frac{a}{k},\ \frac{a}{l} \right)$$

This means any vector from the origin to a point on the plane can be written using these three components.

**Step 2 — Use the Direction Cosine Identity**

The unit normal vector to the plane has direction cosines $(\cos \alpha, \cos \beta, \cos \gamma)$ relative to the $x, y, z$ axes. These satisfy:

$$\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$$

**Step 3 — Relate Direction Cosines to Miller Indices**

For a cubic crystal, the direction $[hkl]$ is perpendicular to the plane $(hkl)$. The direction cosines of $[hkl]$ are proportional to $h, k, l$:

$$\cos \alpha = \frac{h}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}, \quad \cos \beta = \frac{k}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}, \quad \cos \gamma = \frac{l}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Projection**

The perpendicular distance from the origin to the plane is the projection of the intercept vector onto the unit normal. Using the intercept point on the $x$-axis as the projection reference:

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{h} \cdot \cos \alpha = \frac{a}{h} \cdot \frac{h}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplification**

The $h$ in the numerator cancels with the $h$ in the denominator of $\cos \alpha$. Doing this similarly for $k$ and $l$ yields the standard cubic result:

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examiner Note:** This formula is **valid only for cubic crystal systems** (SC, BCC, FCC, Diamond Cubic). For tetragonal, hexagonal, or orthorhombic systems, the formula generalizes with different denominators involving $a, b, c$. KTU questions almost always assume the cubic system unless otherwise stated.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Interplanar Spacing of Silicon (111)

**Given:** Silicon (diamond cubic, $a = 5.431 \, \text{Å}$). Find $d_{111}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Miller indices.** For the $(111)$ plane: $h=1, k=1, l=1$.

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator.**

$$h^2 + k^2 + l^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 = 3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

$$d_{111} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{5.431 \, \text{Å}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{5.431}{1.7321} \, \text{Å}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$d_{111} = 3.1355 \, \text{Å} \approx 3.14 \, \text{Å}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of $d_{111} \approx 3.14 \, \text{Å}$ is the standard inter-layer spacing of close-packed silicon planes and is the basis for X-ray diffraction peak identification in MEMS process monitoring.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Deriving Miller Indices from Intercepts

**Given:** A plane intercepts the crystallographic axes at $2a$, $3b$, and $\infty c$ (parallel to the $z$-axis). Find the Miller indices.

**Step 1 — Write the intercepts in units of lattice constants:** $(2, 3, \infty)$.

**Step 2 — Take the reciprocals.**

$$\left( \frac{1}{2},\ \frac{1}{3},\ \frac{1}{\infty} \right) = \left( \frac{1}{2},\ \frac{1}{3},\ 0 \right)$$

**Step 3 — Find the least common multiple of the denominators.** $\text{LCD} = 6$.

**Step 4 — Multiply through by 6.**

$$\left( \frac{1}{2} \cdot 6,\ \frac{1}{3} \cdot 6,\ 0 \cdot 6 \right) = (3, 2, 0)$$

**Step 5 — Enclose in parentheses.** The Miller indices are $\mathbf{(3\,2\,0)}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Crystal Orientations in MEMS

| Orientation | Plane | Atomic Density | Young's Modulus | KOH Etch Rate | Typical MEMS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(100)$ | Square | $2/a^2$ | $130 \, \text{GPa}$ | **High** | V-grooves, diaphragms |
| $(110)$ | Rectangular | $2\sqrt{2}/a^2$ | $169 \, \text{GPa}$ | Moderate | Sidewall vertical etching |
| $(111)$ | Triangular (close-packed) | $4\sqrt{3}/a^2$ | $188 \, \text{GPa}$ | **Very slow** | Etch stop planes, cantilevers |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"(100) is fast, (111) is slow"*. In KOH anisotropic etching, the rate difference is what allows MEMS engineers to selectively reveal $\{111\}$ sidewalls in <100> wafers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Miller Index Derivation Process

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional decision logic** for determining Miller indices from a given set of plane intercepts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Identify crystal axes x, y, z] --> nodeB[Choose origin NOT on the plane]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Measure intercepts in units of a, b, c]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Is plane parallel to an axis?}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Set that intercept to INFINITY]
    nodeD -->|No| nodeF[Record finite intercept]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Take reciprocal of each intercept]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Multiply by LCD to clear fractions]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Enclose in parentheses: h k l]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Apply overbar for negative values]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[END: Final Miller Index hkl]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Orientation in MEMS Device Design

The following diagram illustrates how crystal orientation propagates as a **design variable** through the entire MEMS fabrication pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSub[Substrate: Silicon Wafer] --> node100[Orientation Selection]
    node100 --> nodeA1[Orientation 100]
    node100 --> nodeA2[Orientation 110]
    node100 --> nodeA3[Orientation 111]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1[Etch Behavior: V-groove]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB2[Etch Behavior: Vertical Wall]
    nodeA3 --> nodeB3[Etch Behavior: Etch Stop]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[Device: Pressure Sensor Diaphragm]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2[Device: Microchannel Sidewall]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC3[Device: Sharp Tip Cantilever]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD[Final MEMS Device]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Anisotropic Etch Rate Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Wafer placed in KOH solution] --> nodePlane1[Plane 100 surfaces]
    nodePlane1 --> nodeFast[FAST etch rate: ~1 micrometer per minute]
    nodeStart --> nodePlane2[Plane 110 surfaces]
    nodePlane2 --> nodeMid[MODERATE etch rate: ~0.6 micrometer per minute]
    nodeStart --> nodePlane3[Plane 111 surfaces]
    nodePlane3 --> nodeSlow[VERY SLOW etch rate: ~0.003 micrometer per minute]
    nodeFast --> nodeResult[Outcome: Pyramidal cavities revealed]
    nodeMid --> nodeResult
    nodeSlow --> nodeResult
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Miller indices. With a neat sketch, explain the procedure to determine the Miller indices of a crystal plane.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark) Definition:** Miller indices are a set of three integers $(hkl)$ used to designate a crystallographic plane. They are the reciprocals of the intercepts made by the plane on the crystallographic axes, reduced to the smallest integers.
- **(1 Mark) Procedure (4 steps):**
  1. Identify the intercepts of the plane on the $x, y, z$ axes in terms of lattice constants $a, b, c$.
  2. Take the reciprocals of these intercepts.
  3. Reduce to smallest integers by clearing fractions.
  4. Enclose in parentheses $(hkl)$; use an overbar for negative intercepts.
- **(1 Mark) Note:** The origin must not lie on the plane.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Style]**

**Q2. What is meant by crystallographic orientation? Why is the <100> orientation of silicon widely used in MEMS fabrication?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark) Definition:** Crystallographic orientation describes the angular relationship between the external surface of a crystal (wafer) and its internal atomic arrangement on specific crystal planes. It is denoted using Miller indices (e.g., <100> for directions, (100) for planes).
- **(1.5 Marks) Why <100> silicon is used in MEMS:**
  1. It enables anisotropic wet etching in KOH, producing well-defined V-grooves with $(111)$ sidewalls.
  2. It has well-characterized mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties.
  3. It is compatible with standard CMOS fabrication processes.
- **(0.5 Mark) Example:** Pressure sensors, accelerometers, and gyroscopes use <100> wafers.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, ESE Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - ESE Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice Set]**

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Crystal Planes, Indices, and Anisotropic Etching

**(a)** With a step-by-step procedure, derive the Miller indices of a plane that intercepts the crystallographic axes at $4a$, $2b$, and $2c$. Also calculate the interplanar spacing $d_{hkl}$ for this plane in a cubic crystal with lattice constant $a = 4.05 \, \text{Å}$ (ZnO, wurtzite — but treat as cubic per problem statement). **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify intercepts.** The plane cuts the axes at $4a, 2b, 2c$, so the dimensionless intercepts are $(4, 2, 2)$.

**Step 2 — Take reciprocals.**

$$\left( \frac{1}{4},\ \frac{1}{2},\ \frac{1}{2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Find the LCD of the denominators.** The denominators are $4, 2, 2$, with $\text{LCD} = 4$.

**Step 4 — Multiply each reciprocal by 4 to clear fractions.**

$$\left( \frac{1}{4} \times 4,\ \frac{1}{2} \times 4,\ \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \right) = (1, 2, 2)$$

**Step 5 — Miller indices are $\mathbf{(1\,2\,2)}$.**

**Step 6 — Interplanar spacing calculation.**

$$d_{122} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 2^2}} = \frac{4.05 \, \text{Å}}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{4.05 \, \text{Å}}{3} = 1.35 \, \text{Å}$$

**Final Answer:** Miller indices are $\mathbf{(122)}$; Interplanar spacing $d_{122} = \mathbf{1.35 \, \text{Å}}$.

---

**(b)** Explain the role of crystal orientation in anisotropic wet etching of silicon for MEMS applications. Compare the etch rates of $(100)$, $(110)$, and $(111)$ planes in KOH solution and explain why the <100> orientation produces V-grooves. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of anisotropic etching.** Anisotropic etching means the etch rate depends on the crystallographic orientation of the plane being exposed. KOH etches silicon planes at vastly different rates.

**Step 2 — Etch rate hierarchy in KOH.** The standard ratio is:

$$R_{(100)} : R_{(110)} : R_{(111)} \approx 400 : 60 : 1$$

The $(100)$ plane etches fastest; the $(111)$ plane etches extremely slowly.

**Step 3 — V-groove formation mechanism.** When a rectangular mask opening is aligned with the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction on a <100> wafer, KOH first attacks the exposed $(100)$ plane rapidly. As etching proceeds, the slow-etching $(111)$ planes are revealed at the corners, forming a pyramid or V-groove bounded by four $\{111\}$ sidewalls.

**Step 4 — Why V-grooves form.** Because the $(111)$ planes are atomically close-packed, KOH cannot break the Si-Si bonds effectively. The etch front therefore slows dramatically on $(111)$, leaving these planes as the final sidewalls. The angle between $(100)$ and $(111)$ is $54.74°$, giving the characteristic V-groove geometry.

**Step 5 — MEMS applications.**
- Pyramidal cavities for pressure sensor diaphragms.
- V-grooves for optical fiber alignment.
- Sharp tips for AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy) cantilevers.
- Microfluidic channels with precise sidewall geometry.

**Final Answer:** Crystal orientation governs etch anisotropy, with the ratio $R_{(100)}:R_{(110)}:R_{(111)} = 400:60:1$, producing V-grooves bounded by $\{111\}$ planes at $54.74°$ from the wafer surface.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Interplanar Spacing, Planar Density, and Direction Cosines

**(a)** Derive the formula for interplanar spacing $d_{hkl}$ in a cubic crystal system. For silicon ($a = 5.431 \, \text{Å}$), calculate $d_{100}$, $d_{110}$, and $d_{111}$. Comment on the trend. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the derived formula (cubic system only):**

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation Summary (4 key steps):**
1. The plane $(hkl)$ has intercepts $\left(\frac{a}{h}, \frac{a}{k}, \frac{a}{l}\right)$ on the axes.
2. The unit normal vector to the plane has direction cosines proportional to $(h, k, l)$.
3. Using $\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$, the unit normal components are $\left(\frac{h}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}, \frac{k}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}, \frac{l}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}\right)$.
4. The perpendicular distance from origin to plane is the projection of the intercept on the $x$-axis onto the unit normal, giving $d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}$.

**Step 3 — Numerical computation for silicon:**

For $d_{100}$, $h=1, k=0, l=0$:

$$d_{100} = \frac{5.431}{\sqrt{1+0+0}} = \frac{5.431}{1} = 5.431 \, \text{Å}$$

For $d_{110}$, $h=1, k=1, l=0$:

$$d_{110} = \frac{5.431}{\sqrt{1+1+0}} = \frac{5.431}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5.431}{1.4142} = 3.840 \, \text{Å}$$

For $d_{111}$, $h=1, k=1, l=1$:

$$d_{111} = \frac{5.431}{\sqrt{1+1+1}} = \frac{5.431}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{5.431}{1.7321} = 3.135 \, \text{Å}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on the trend.** As the Miller indices increase, $h^2+k^2+l^2$ increases, so $d_{hkl}$ **decreases**. This is physically meaningful: higher-index planes are more closely spaced, which correlates with higher atomic density on those planes.

**Final Answer:** $d_{100} = 5.431 \, \text{Å}$, $d_{110} = 3.840 \, \text{Å}$, $d_{111} = 3.135 \, \text{Å}$; spacing decreases as index increases.

---

**(b)** Determine the angle between the $[110]$ and $[111]$ directions in a cubic crystal. The direction cosines of a direction $[uvw]$ are $\left(\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2+w^2}}, \frac{v}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2+w^2}}, \frac{w}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2+w^2}}\right)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Direction cosines of $[110]$.**

$$\text{Length} = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{2}$$

$$\cos \alpha_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \cos \beta_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \cos \gamma_1 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Direction cosines of $[111]$.**

$$\text{Length} = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{3}$$

$$\cos \alpha_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad \cos \beta_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad \cos \gamma_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the dot product formula for angle between two directions.**

$$\cos \theta = \cos \alpha_1 \cos \alpha_2 + \cos \beta_1 \cos \beta_2 + \cos \gamma_1 \cos \gamma_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$\cos \theta = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) + (0)\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

$$\cos \theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} + 0 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} = \frac{2}{2.4495} = 0.8165$$

**Step 5 — Compute the angle.**

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.8165) = 35.26°$$

**Final Answer:** The angle between $[110]$ and $[111]$ is $\mathbf{35.26°}$. This is the well-known half-angle of the V-groove in anisotropic silicon etching.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to clear fractions:** Writing $(1/2, 1/3, 0)$ instead of $(3, 2, 0)$ costs **1 full mark** in Part A.
> 2. **Confusing plane $(hkl)$ with direction $[hkl]$:** Parentheses vs. square brackets is **not optional** — KTU examiners strictly check this. Mixing them up costs **1 mark** in Part B.
> 3. **Using the cubic $d_{hkl}$ formula on a non-cubic system:** The formula $d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}$ is **cubic only**. If a question states "tetragonal" or "hexagonal," the formula changes — applying the cubic formula is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Forgetting the overbar for negative intercepts:** If a plane intercepts the $-x$ axis, the index must be written $\bar{h}$, not $-h$. This costs **0.5 mark** but is checked rigorously.
> 5. **Direction cosine verification not shown:** KTU examiners award partial credit only if you show $\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$ as a verification step.
> 6. **Units in final answer:** Always include $\text{Å}$ or $\text{nm}$ for interplanar spacing. A bare number is incomplete.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Crystal Planes and Orientation:**

- **Core Symbol Set:** $(hkl)$ = plane, $[hkl]$ = direction, $\{hkl\}$ = family of planes, $\langle hkl \rangle$ = family of directions.
- **Miller Index Recipe (memorize the 5 steps):** Intercepts $\rightarrow$ Reciprocals $\rightarrow$ Clear fractions $\rightarrow$ Reduce to integers $\rightarrow$ Enclose in parentheses.
- **Critical Interplanar Spacing Formula (cubic only):** $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$.
- **Silicon Lattice Constant:** $a = 5.431 \, \text{Å} = 0.5431 \, \text{nm}$ (Diamond Cubic structure).
- **Anisotropic Etch Rate Hierarchy (KOH):** $R_{(100)} > R_{(110)} \gg R_{(111)}$, with the canonical ratio $400:60:1$.
- **V-Groove Angle:** The angle between the $(100)$ wafer surface and the revealed $(111)$ sidewall is $54.74°$.
- **Young's Modulus Anisotropy in Si:** $E_{\langle 100 \rangle} = 130 \, \text{GPa}$, $E_{\langle 111 \rangle} = 188 \, \text{GPa}$ — significant impact on MEMS resonator design.
- **Direction Cosine Identity (must show in derivations):** $\cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1$.
- **Miller Index vs. Intercept Reciprocal Relationship:** Always use reciprocals, not the intercepts themselves. This is the **single most common source of errors**.
- **Origin Rule:** The chosen origin must NEVER lie on the plane whose Miller indices are being calculated.
- **Family Notation:** In cubic systems, $(100), (010), (001), (\bar{1}00), (0\bar{1}0), (00\bar{1})$ all belong to the $\{100\}$ family.
- **Real-World MEMS Linkages:** Anisotropic etching (V-grooves, diaphragms), piezoelectric quartz cuts, CMOS-compatible bulk micromachining, AFM tip fabrication.
- **Examiner's Golden Rule:** Always state the **cubic assumption** when using $d_{hkl}$ formula, and always **verify direction cosines** in angle problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Force Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internal Force Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Internal Force Analysis** in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) is the systematic study of the *self-equilibrating stress fields* and *resultant force/moment distributions* that exist within a microstructure either as a consequence of the **fabrication process history** (intrinsic/residual stresses), **operational loading** (mechanical, electrostatic, thermal, fluidic), or **geometric constraints** (boundary conditions, substrate anchoring).

Mathematically, an internal force analysis evaluates the second-order stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ within the volume $V$ of a MEMS element, satisfying Cauchy's equilibrium equation:

$$\frac{\partial \sigma_{ij}}{\partial x_j} + f_i = 0$$

where $f_i$ is the body force per unit volume (often negligible at micro-scales, where $f_i \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ of structural forces), and the resultant internal force vector and moment vector on a cross-section are obtained by integrating the stress tensor:

$$F_i = \int_A \sigma_{ij} \, n_j \, dA, \quad M_i = \int_A \epsilon_{ijk} \, x_j \, \sigma_{kl} \, n_l \, dA$$

In the KTU 2024 framework, this analysis is critical because internal forces dictate **stiction failure, buckling, fracture, curvature, and fatigue** of micro-beams, membranes, and plates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Internal Force Analysis is positioned as the bridge between *Materials Behavior* (Module 1/2) and *Structural Design* (Module 4/5). Mastering it is mandatory for the ESE Module 3 questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Stressed-Out Sandwich"**
>
> Imagine you make a sandwich with two different types of bread (say, a thick whole-grain bottom slice and a thin white top slice) and press them together with a heavy hand. Now bake it. As it cools, the two slices want to shrink by different amounts because their thermal contractions differ. The sandwich **bows upward** — neither slice is free to move independently. The internal "pulling" between the two layers is exactly what we call **internal (residual) stress** in a MEMS thin film stack.
>
> In a micro-cantilever, if the top layer wants to contract more than the bottom, the beam curls *upward*; if less, it curls *downward*. This is the essence of **gradient stress-induced deformation** in MEMS — a phenomenon that does not exist in bulk macroscopic parts to any practical degree.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in MEMS

- **Young's Modulus of Polysilicon:** $E_{poly} \approx 160$ **GPa**
- **Young's Modulus of Single-Crystal Silicon:** $\langle 100 \rangle$ direction, $E_{[100]} \approx 130$ **GPa**
- **Poisson's Ratio for Silicon:** $\nu \approx 0.22$ to $0.28$
- **Residual Stress in LPCVD Polysilicon:** $300$ **MPa** (compressive, typical)
- **Stoney Film Stress Coefficient:** dimension of $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}$
- **Substrate Thickness Range (typical wafer):** $t_s = 350$ to $725$ $\mu$m
- **Film Thickness Range (typical MEMS layer):** $t_f = 0.5$ to $5$ $\mu$m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensional Significance at Micro-Scale:** Because $t_f / t_s \ll 1$ (often $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$), the substrate is treated as **rigid** in the bending analysis of thin films, and only the *gradient* component of the stress causes curvature — the *uniform* component produces in-plane force but no bending.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Internal Forces in MEMS

MEMS internal forces are best understood in three orthogonal categories:

**A. By Origin (Source)**

1. **Intrinsic (Growth) Stress** — develops during thin-film deposition due to grain-boundary relaxation, adatom mobility, and ion bombardment. Cannot be eliminated by thermal annealing alone.
2. **Thermal Stress** — arises from differential thermal expansion $\Delta \alpha$ between film and substrate when the structure is cooled from the fabrication temperature $T_{dep}$ to the operating temperature $T_{op}$:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}(\alpha_s - \alpha_f)(T_{dep} - T_{op})$$

3. **Epitaxial (Mismatch) Stress** — lattice constant mismatch between crystalline film and substrate (e.g., SiGe on Si).
4. **Stress Gradients** — through-thickness variation of stress, leading to bending moments.

**B. By Distribution (Spatial Profile)**

1. **Uniform (Biaxial) Stress** — same in all in-plane directions; produces no net bending moment.
2. **Gradient Stress** — varies linearly with thickness $z$; produces both an in-plane force $N$ and a bending moment $M$.
3. **Localized Stress Concentration** — near anchors, etch holes, or sharp corners.

**C. By Resultant Effect**

1. **Membrane Force ($N$)** — integrated stress over thickness; causes in-plane stretching or compression.
2. **Bending Moment ($M$)** — first moment of stress; causes curvature $\kappa$.
3. **Transverse Shear ($V$)** — usually negligible for slender micro-beams of length $L$ and thickness $t$ when $L/t > 10$.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation — Coupled Axial-Bending Decomposition

For a micro-beam of unit width, define the *stress resultant* $N$ and the *moment resultant* $M$ about the mid-plane ($z=0$):

$$N = \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} \sigma_{xx}(z) \, dz, \quad M = \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} \sigma_{xx}(z) \cdot z \, dz$$

For a **linear stress gradient** $\sigma_{xx}(z) = \sigma_0 + \sigma_1 \, z$, these integrate to:

$$N = \sigma_0 \, t, \quad M = \frac{\sigma_1 \, t^3}{12}$$

The neutral-axis curvature of the deformed beam is governed by the **plate equation** (in absence of transverse load):

$$D \frac{d^4 w}{dx^4} - N \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = 0$$

where $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is the *flexural rigidity* of the plate.

### 2.3 The Stoney Equation — The Crown Jewel of MEMS Internal Force Analysis

For a thin film of thickness $t_f$ deposited on a thick substrate of thickness $t_s$ (with $t_f \ll t_s$), the **average biaxial stress** $\sigma_f$ in the film produces a substrate curvature $\kappa$ given by:

$$\boxed{\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{6 \, \sigma_f \, t_f}{M_s \, t_s^2}}$$

where $M_s = \dfrac{E_s}{1-\nu_s}$ is the *biatomic modulus* of the substrate. This is the **Stoney Equation (1909)** — the single most-cited equation in MEMS residual stress analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Stoney Matters at KTU:** The examiner frequently tests the assumptions, limitations, and the *inverse-problem* form: given a measured curvature from a profilometer or optical lever, what is the film stress? The inverse form is $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M_s \, t_s^2 \, \kappa}{6 \, t_f}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|----------|------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\sigma = E \epsilon$ (uniaxial) | Hooke's law for in-plane strain | Stress from measured strain |
| 2 | $\sigma_{biax} = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu} \epsilon$ | Biaxial modulus form | Thin-film in-plane stress |
| 3 | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_f(\alpha_s - \alpha_f)\Delta T}{1-\nu_f}$ | Thermal mismatch stress | Poly-Si on SiO$_2$ stack |
| 4 | $\kappa = \dfrac{6 \sigma_f t_f}{M_s t_s^2}$ | **Stoney curvature** | Residual stress measurement |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M_s t_s^2 \kappa}{6 t_f}$ | **Inverse Stoney** | Stress from profilometry |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Plate flexural rigidity | Plate bending problems |
| 7 | $N = \sigma_0 \, t$ | Membrane force per unit width | Gradient stress force |
| 8 | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_1 t^3}{12}$ | Moment per unit width | Curvature from gradient |
| 9 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 D}{L^2 t}$ | Critical buckling stress | Clamped-clamped beam |
| 10 | $F_{elec} = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 A V^2}{2 g^2}$ | Electrostatic attraction | Comb-drive / parallel plate |
| 11 | $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | Spring constant (cantilever) | Lateral stiffness |
| 12 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Resonant frequency | Dynamic force analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** All stresses are in **Pa** (or **MPa** for compactness), all lengths in **m** (or $\mu$m), and all curvatures in **m$^{-1}$**. The examiner deducts marks if units are inconsistent.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Internal Force Analysis

- **Reliability Prediction:** Knowing $N$ and $M$ allows prediction of *out-of-plane deformation* in released microstructures.
- **Design Compensation:** Engineers deliberately introduce **stress-engineered layers** (e.g., tensile LPCVD nitride over compressive polysilicon) to flatten cantilevers.
- **Sensor Calibration:** Piezoresistive pressure sensors depend on predictable stress-strain coupling; internal force analysis sets the *sensitivity floor*.
- **Failure Avoidance:** Compressive $N$ above the Euler buckling load causes **sudden snap-through** of long micro-bridges — a major failure mode in accelerometers.
- **Process Control:** Wafer curvature measurements (using Tencor FLX series tools) are routinely fed back to deposition reactors to keep $\sigma_f$ within $\pm 10$ **MPa** of target.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Stoney Equation

The Stoney equation is derived from **force and moment equilibrium** of a film-substrate bilayer after release. We assume:

- Film thickness $t_f \ll$ Substrate thickness $t_s$ (so substrate bending dominates).
- Film stress is uniform and equibiaxial: $\sigma_f = \sigma_{f,xx} = \sigma_{f,yy}$.
- Both film and substrate are linearly elastic and isotropic.
- Plane sections remain plane (Euler-Bernoulli assumption valid for $t_s / L \ll 1$).

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium in the bilayer.**

The film wants to contract (or expand) by strain $\epsilon_f = \dfrac{\sigma_f (1-\nu_f)}{E_f}$. The substrate resists with an equal-and-opposite in-plane force per unit width $F_s$. Force balance in the $x$-direction:

$$\sigma_f t_f + \int_{-t_s/2}^{t_s/2} \sigma_s(z) \, dz = 0$$

**Step 2 — Moment equilibrium about the neutral axis.**

The neutral axis coincides (by symmetry) with the substrate mid-plane for $t_f \ll t_s$. Moment of film stress about the neutral axis (located at the film-substrate interface, distance $t_s/2$ from the substrate mid-plane):

$$M = \sigma_f t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Bending moment from substrate curvature.**

For an elastic substrate bent to radius $R$, the moment per unit width is:

$$M = \frac{E_s t_s^3}{12(1-\nu_s^2)} \cdot \frac{1}{R} = D_s \cdot \kappa$$

where $D_s$ is the flexural rigidity of the substrate.

**Step 4 — Equate the moments and solve for $\kappa$.**

$$\sigma_f t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \frac{E_s t_s^3}{12(1-\nu_s^2)} \cdot \kappa$$

Rearranging:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{6 \sigma_f t_f (1-\nu_s^2)}{E_s t_s^2}$$

Using the *biaxial modulus* $M_s = \dfrac{E_s}{1-\nu_s}$, and assuming $\nu_s \ll 1$ for silicon ($\nu_s \approx 0.28$):

$$\boxed{\kappa = \frac{6 \sigma_f t_f}{M_s t_s^2}} \quad \text{(Stoney Equation)}$$

**Step 5 — Inverse form (the one KTU loves).**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{M_s t_s^2 \kappa}{6 t_f} = \frac{E_s t_s^2 \kappa}{6(1-\nu_s) t_f}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check.**

For $\sigma_f = 100$ **MPa**, $t_f = 1$ $\mu$m, $t_s = 500$ $\mu$m, $E_s = 130$ **GPa**, $\nu_s = 0.28$:

$$\kappa = \frac{6 \times 100 \times 10^6 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{130 \times 10^9 \times (1-0.28) \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

After full evaluation:

$$\kappa \approx \frac{600}{130 \times 10^9 \times 0.72 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}} \approx \frac{600}{23.4} \approx 25.6 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Equivalent radius $R \approx 39$ **mm** — easily measurable by a profilometer. ✓

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Cantilever Tip Deflection from Stress Gradient

A micro-cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, with a **linear stress gradient** $\sigma(z) = \sigma_0 + \sigma_1 z$ through thickness, fixed at $x=0$, free at $x=L$.

**Step 1 — Resultant force and moment from the stress field.**

Per unit width:

$$N = \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} (\sigma_0 + \sigma_1 z) \, dz = \sigma_0 \, t$$

$$M = \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} (\sigma_0 + \sigma_1 z) \cdot z \, dz = \frac{\sigma_1 t^3}{12}$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium beam equation (Euler-Bernoulli).**

The uniform $N$ produces in-plane elongation but no bending. Only $M$ contributes to curvature $\kappa$:

$$\kappa(x) = \frac{M}{E I} = \frac{12 M}{E w t^3} = \frac{12 \cdot \frac{\sigma_1 t^3}{12 w}}{E t^3} = \frac{\sigma_1}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate curvature twice for tip deflection.**

For a uniform gradient, curvature is constant. So $\dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = \dfrac{\sigma_1}{E}$.

Integrating with boundary conditions $w(0) = 0$ and $\dfrac{dw}{dx}\vert_{x=0} = 0$:

$$\frac{dw}{dx} = \frac{\sigma_1}{E} x \quad \Rightarrow \quad w(x) = \frac{\sigma_1}{2E} x^2$$

**Step 4 — Tip deflection at $x = L$.**

$$\boxed{w_{tip} = \frac{\sigma_1 L^2}{2 E}}$$

**Step 5 — Tip rotation (slope).**

$$\theta_{tip} = \frac{dw}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = \frac{\sigma_1 L}{E}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The tip deflection scales as $L^2$ (not $L^3$ as in tip-loaded beams), so a *gradient stress* of just $\sigma_1 = 10$ **MPa/$\mu$m** over $L = 500$ $\mu$m in silicon gives $w_{tip} = \dfrac{10^7 \times (5 \times 10^{-4})^2}{2 \times 130 \times 10^9} \approx 9.6$ $\mu$m — enough to destroy a 2-$\mu$m capacitive gap!

### 3.3 Euler Buckling of a Clamped-Clamped Micro-Beam Under Compressive $N$

For a beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, under axial compressive load $P = N \cdot w$:

**Step 1 — Critical load from the buckling eigenvalue problem.**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{4 \pi^2 E I}{L^2} = \frac{4 \pi^2 E w t^3}{12 L^2}$$

**Step 2 — Critical average stress.**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{w t} = \frac{4 \pi^2 E t^2}{12 L^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E t^2}{3 L^2}$$

**Step 3 — In terms of flexural rigidity $D$.**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4 \pi^2 D}{L^2 t} \quad \text{where} \quad D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

For $E = 130$ **GPa**, $t = 2$ $\mu$m, $L = 500$ $\mu$m:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 130 \times 10^9 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^2}{3 \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^2} \approx \frac{1.71 \times 10^9}{7.5 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 3.43 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Trapping the Long Beam:** Any micro-bridge with compressive stress above **3.43 MPa** (at this geometry) will buckle — easily exceeded by as-deposited LPCVD polysilicon (~300 MPa compressive)!

### 3.4 Summary Matrix — Internal Force Type vs. Equation

| Internal Force Type | Resultant | Governing Equation | Causes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform biaxial film stress | $N$ only | $N = \sigma_0 t$ | In-plane stretching |
| Linear gradient stress | $N$ + $M$ | $M = \sigma_1 t^3 / 12$ | Tip curl, out-of-plane bow |
| Axial compression | $P$ | $P_{cr} = 4\pi^2 EI / L^2$ | Buckling |
| Electrostatic attraction | $F_e$ | $F_e = \epsilon_0 A V^2 / 2 g^2$ | Pull-in, snap-down |
| Thermal mismatch | $\sigma_{th}$ | $\sigma_{th} = E \Delta\alpha \Delta T / (1-\nu)$ | Package-induced stress |
| Body force (gravitational) | $W = \rho g V$ | Negligible at MEMS scale | Sometimes ignored |
| Surface tension | $F_s = 2 \gamma L \cos\theta$ | Capillary stiction | Release-drying failure |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Origin, Type, and Effect of Internal Forces in MEMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Internal Forces in MEMS] --> B[Origin]
    A --> C[Distribution]
    A --> D[Effect]

    B --> B1[Fabrication Process]
    B --> B2[Thermal Loading]
    B --> B3[Operating Load]
    B --> B4[Geometric Constraint]

    B1 --> B1a[Intrinsic Stress]
    B1 --> B1b[Epitaxial Mismatch]
    B2 --> B2a[Thermal Mismatch]
    B3 --> B3a[Electrostatic]
    B3 --> B3b[Mechanical Contact]
    B4 --> B4a[Anchoring Reaction]

    C --> C1[Uniform Biaxial]
    C --> C2[Linear Gradient]
    C --> C3[Localized Peak]

    D --> D1[Membrane Force N]
    D --> D2[Bending Moment M]
    D --> D3[Curvature Kappa]
    D --> D4[Stiction Failure]
    D --> D5[Buckling]

    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D1 --> D5
    D3 --> D4
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Analysis Workflow for Internal Force Problems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Geometry] --> S2[Step 2: Identify Load History]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Stresses]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Integrate to Get N and M]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply Equilibrium Equations]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Solve for Deformation]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Check Failure Criteria]
    S7 --> S8{Output Deflection or Stress}
```

### 4.3 Stress Distribution in a Micro-Cantilever with Gradient Stress (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Anchor at x = 0] --> P2[Beam of length L, thickness t]
    P2 --> P3[Free tip at x = L]

    subgraph Stress_Profile
        Z1[z = t/2: sigma_top = sigma_0 + sigma_1 t / 2]
        Z2[z = 0: sigma_mid = sigma_0]
        Z3[z = -t/2: sigma_bot = sigma_0 - sigma_1 t / 2]
        Z1 --> Z2
        Z2 --> Z3
    end

    P2 --> Stress_Profile
    Stress_Profile --> P4[Resultant N = sigma_0 t]
    Stress_Profile --> P5[Resultant M = sigma_1 t cubed / 12]
    P5 --> P6[Curvature kappa = M / EI]
    P6 --> P7[Tip deflection w_tip = sigma_1 L squared / 2E]
```

### 4.4 Failure-Mode Decision Tree for MEMS Under Internal Force

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Internal force analysis complete] --> Q2{Nature of N}
    Q2 -->|Tensile N| Q3[Check fracture: sigma_xx less than sigma_fracture]
    Q2 -->|Compressive N less than N_cr| Q4[Stable in-plane compression]
    Q2 -->|Compressive N greater than N_cr| Q5[Sudden buckling failure]
    Q2 -->|Bending M nonzero| Q6[Check tip deflection vs. gap]
    Q6 --> Q7{w_tip less than gap d}
    Q7 -->|Yes| Q8[Device functional]
    Q7 -->|No| Q9[Pull-in or stiction at release]
    Q3 --> R1[Safe]
    Q4 --> R1
    Q5 --> R2[Design failure: shorten beam or add stiffener]
    Q9 --> R3[Design failure: add dimples or stress relief]
    R1 --> X[Output: SAFE design]
    R2 --> X
    R3 --> X
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define residual stress in a MEMS thin film and explain how it differs from applied stress.**

*Model Answer (3 marks):*

**Definition (2 marks):** Residual stress in a MEMS thin film is the *internal, self-equilibrating stress* that remains in a microstructure after all external loads and fabrication thermal histories have been removed. It is generated during the deposition process (intrinsic stress) or due to thermal expansion mismatch between the film and substrate (thermal stress). The stress is *locked-in* at the atomic scale by grain boundaries, defect structures, and differential contraction.

**Difference from applied stress (1 mark):**

| Feature | Residual Stress | Applied Stress |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Fabrication process | External loading |
| Can be removed? | No (without annealing) | Yes (remove load) |
| Measured by | Wafer curvature (Stoney) | Strain gauge / load cell |
| Acts when device is idle? | Yes | No |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] State the Stoney equation. What are its three principal assumptions?**

*Model Answer (3 marks):*

**Equation (1 mark):**

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{6 \, \sigma_f \, t_f}{M_s \, t_s^2}$$

where $\sigma_f$ is the average biaxial film stress, $t_f$ is the film thickness, $t_s$ is the substrate thickness, and $M_s = E_s / (1-\nu_s)$ is the biaxial modulus of the substrate.

**Three assumptions (2 marks):**

1. **Geometric:** $t_f \ll t_s$ (typically $t_f / t_s < 0.01$), so the substrate dominates the bending rigidity.
2. **Material:** Both film and substrate are linearly elastic, isotropic, and homogeneous.
3. **Stress state:** Film stress is *equibiaxial* and *uniform through thickness*; only the *average* stress is recovered — the equation cannot resolve gradients.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A polysilicon film of thickness $t_f = 1.2 \, \mu$m is deposited by LPCVD on a (100) silicon substrate of thickness $t_s = 525 \, \mu$m. After deposition, the wafer curvature is measured to be $\kappa = 18.4 \, \text{m}^{-1}$ (concave up). Given $E_s = 130$ **GPa** and $\nu_s = 0.28$, calculate:

(i) The average biaxial stress in the polysilicon film using the Stoney equation.

(ii) The internal force per unit width $N$ in the film.

(iii) Comment on whether the stress is tensile or compressive.

**(b) [7 marks]** A micro-cantilever of length $L = 400 \, \mu$m, width $w = 50 \, \mu$m, thickness $t = 2 \, \mu$m, made of polysilicon ($E = 160$ **GPa**, $\nu = 0.22$), has a *linear stress gradient* through its thickness given by $\sigma(z) = \sigma_0 + \sigma_1 z$, with $\sigma_0 = 50$ **MPa** and $\sigma_1 = 8$ **MPa/$\mu$m**. Calculate:

(i) The membrane force $N$ per unit width and the bending moment $M$ per unit width.

(ii) The radius of curvature of the cantilever.

(iii) The tip deflection $w_{tip}$.

---

#### *Model Solution — Question A*

##### **Part (a) — Stoney Stress Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given:**

- $t_f = 1.2 \, \mu$m $= 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $t_s = 525 \, \mu$m $= 5.25 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $\kappa = 18.4 \, \text{m}^{-1}$
- $E_s = 130 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $\nu_s = 0.28$

**Step 1: Compute biaxial modulus of substrate.**

$$M_s = \frac{E_s}{1 - \nu_s} = \frac{130 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.28} = \frac{130 \times 10^9}{0.72}$$

$$M_s = 180.56 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa} = 180.56 \, \text{GPa}$$

**Step 2: Apply inverse Stoney equation.**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{M_s \, t_s^2 \, \kappa}{6 \, t_f}$$

$$\sigma_f = \frac{(180.56 \times 10^9) \times (5.25 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times 18.4}{6 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-6})}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the denominator.**

$$6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6} = 7.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the numerator in pieces.**

$$(5.25 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 27.5625 \times 10^{-8} = 2.756 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$180.56 \times 10^9 \times 2.756 \times 10^{-7} = 49,762 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{m} = 4.976 \times 10^{4}$$

$$4.976 \times 10^{4} \times 18.4 = 9.156 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 5: Divide.**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{9.156 \times 10^{5}}{7.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.272 \times 10^{11} \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_f \approx 127.2 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Membrane force per unit width [2 marks]:**

$$N = \sigma_f \cdot t_f = 127.2 \times 10^6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{N = 152.6 \, \text{N/m}}$$

**Part (a)(iii) — Sign comment [1 mark]:**

The film is *concave-up* (wafer bows like a bowl). For the film to bend the substrate this way, it must be in **compression**. Therefore, $\sigma_f$ is **compressive, $\sigma_f \approx -127.2$ MPa**. (KTU accepts the sign convention discussion as a 1-mark sub-part.)

---

##### **Part (b) — Stress Gradient Cantilever [7 marks]**

**Given:**

- $L = 400 \, \mu$m $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $w = 50 \, \mu$m $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $t = 2 \, \mu$m $= 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $E = 160 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $\sigma_0 = 50 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $\sigma_1 = 8 \, \text{MPa}/\mu\text{m} = 8 \times 10^{12}$ **Pa/m**

**Part (b)(i) — Resultants [2 marks]:**

$$N = \sigma_0 \, t = 50 \times 10^6 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = \boxed{100 \, \text{N/m}}$$

$$M = \frac{\sigma_1 \, t^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times 10^{12} \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times 10^{12} \times 8 \times 10^{-18}}{12} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{12}$$

$$\boxed{M = 5.33 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{N}\cdot\text{m/m} = 5.33 \, \mu\text{N}}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Radius of curvature [2 marks]:**

$$\kappa = \frac{M}{E I}, \quad I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 \times 10^{-18}}{12} = 3.33 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{m}^4$$

$$\kappa = \frac{5.33 \times 10^{-6}}{160 \times 10^9 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-23}} = \frac{5.33 \times 10^{-6}}{5.33 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \, \text{m}^{-1}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{\kappa} = \boxed{1.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 1 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

**Part (b)(iii) — Tip deflection [3 marks]:**

Using $w_{tip} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 L^2}{2E}$:

$$w_{tip} = \frac{8 \times 10^{12} \times (4 \times 10^{-4})^2}{2 \times 160 \times 10^9} = \frac{8 \times 10^{12} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{3.2 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$w_{tip} = \frac{1.28 \times 10^{6}}{3.2 \times 10^{11}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{w_{tip} = 4 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

The tip deflects by **4 $\mu$m** — comparable to typical capacitive gap sizes (2–5 $\mu$m), so this beam would experience severe stiction risk on release.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A clamped-clamped micro-bridge used in an RF MEMS switch has dimensions: $L = 600 \, \mu$m, $w = 100 \, \mu$m, $t = 2.5 \, \mu$m. It is fabricated in gold ($E = 78$ **GPa**, $\nu = 0.44$). The bridge experiences a uniform compressive residual stress $\sigma_0 = 25$ **MPa** and an applied DC voltage $V = 30$ **V** across an air gap $g_0 = 3 \, \mu$m.

(i) Compute the membrane force $N$ per unit width and the critical buckling load $N_{cr}$ per unit width.

(ii) Determine whether the beam will buckle under residual stress alone.

(iii) Calculate the electrostatic attractive pressure and the pull-in voltage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the Stoney equation from first principles, clearly stating each assumption. A silicon nitride film ($E_f = 290$ **GPa**, $\nu_f = 0.27$, $\alpha_f = 3.2 \times 10^{-6}$ /K) is deposited on a silicon substrate ($\alpha_s = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K) at $T_{dep} = 850^\circ$C. The wafer is cooled to $T_{op} = 25^\circ$C. Estimate the **thermal stress** in the film and compare it to the typical as-deposited residual stress of $\sigma_{res} = 1.0$ **GPa** tensile. Discuss which dominates.

---

#### *Model Solution — Question B*

##### **Part (a) — Buckling and Electrostatics of RF MEMS Bridge [7 marks]**

**Given:**

- $L = 600 \, \mu$m $= 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $w = 100 \, \mu$m $= 10^{-4}$ m
- $t = 2.5 \, \mu$m $= 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $E = 78$ **GPa**, $\nu = 0.44$
- $\sigma_0 = 25$ **MPa** (compressive)
- $V = 30$ **V**, $g_0 = 3 \, \mu$m

**Part (a)(i) — Forces [2 marks]:**

Membrane force per unit width from residual stress:

$$N = \sigma_0 \cdot t = 25 \times 10^6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = \boxed{62.5 \, \text{N/m (compressive)}}$$

Critical buckling load for clamped-clamped beam:

$$N_{cr} = \frac{4 \pi^2 D}{L^2}, \quad D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

$$D = \frac{78 \times 10^9 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12 \times (1 - 0.44^2)} = \frac{78 \times 10^9 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-17}}{12 \times 0.8064}$$

$$D = \frac{1.219 \times 10^{-6}}{9.677} = 1.260 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{N}\cdot\text{m}$$

$$N_{cr} = \frac{4 \pi^2 \times 1.260 \times 10^{-7}}{(6 \times 10^{-4})^2} = \frac{4.974 \times 10^{-6}}{3.6 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{N_{cr} \approx 13.82 \, \text{N/m}}$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Buckling check [1 mark]:**

Since $\vert N \vert = 62.5$ N/m $\gg$ $N_{cr} = 13.82$ N/m, the bridge will **buckle catastrophically** under residual stress alone. The design is unsafe. ✓

**Part (a)(iii) — Electrostatic pressure and pull-in voltage [4 marks]:**

Electrostatic pressure on a parallel plate:

$$P_{elec} = \frac{\epsilon_0 V^2}{2 g_0^2} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 30^2}{2 \times (3 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

$$P_{elec} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 900}{1.8 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{7.969 \times 10^{-9}}{1.8 \times 10^{-11}} = 442.7 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{elec} \approx 443 \, \text{Pa}}$$

Pull-in voltage for a clamped-clamped beam with negligible residual stress (for completeness):

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \epsilon_0 A}}$$

Spring constant of clamped-clamped beam:

$$k = \frac{16 E w t^3}{L^3} = \frac{16 \times 78 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-4} \times (2.5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{(6 \times 10^{-4})^3}$$

$$k = \frac{16 \times 78 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-17}}{2.16 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{1.95 \times 10^{-9}}{2.16 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 9.03 \, \text{N/m}$$

Plate area $A = w L = 10^{-4} \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 6 \times 10^{-8}$ m$^2$:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 9.03 \times (3 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 6 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 9.03 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-17}}{1.434 \times 10^{-18}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.95 \times 10^{-15}}{1.434 \times 10^{-18}}} = \sqrt{1360} \approx 36.9 \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{PI} \approx 37 \, \text{V}}$$

So at 30 V, the beam is *just below* pull-in — but the residual-stress buckling will dominate long before voltage snap-down. The designer must reduce $L$ or introduce stress-relief holes.

---

##### **Part (b) — Stoney Derivation and Thermal Stress [7 marks]**

**Stoney Derivation [3 marks]:** See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full six-step derivation. The key assumptions are:

1. $t_f \ll t_s$ (rigid substrate in axial direction).
2. Linear elastic, isotropic materials.
3. Equibiaxial, uniform film stress.

**Thermal Stress Calculation [3 marks]:**

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} (\alpha_s - \alpha_f) (T_{dep} - T_{op})$$

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{290 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.27} \times (2.6 - 3.2) \times 10^{-6} \times (850 - 25)$$

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{290 \times 10^9}{0.73} \times (-0.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 825$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 397.26 \times 10^9 \times (-4.95 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\sigma_{th} \approx -1.97 \times 10^8 \, \text{Pa} = -197 \, \text{MPa (compressive)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} \approx -197 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Comparison & Discussion [1 mark]:**

The total film stress is the algebraic sum of residual and thermal components:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{res} + \sigma_{th} = 1000 \, \text{MPa (tensile)} - 197 \, \text{MPa (compressive)} = 803 \, \text{MPa tensile}$$

The **intrinsic residual stress dominates** (1000 MPa vs. 197 MPa), but the thermal component is *not negligible* — it counteracts about 20% of the tensile residual. For a stress-engineered flat cantilever, the designer would deliberately add a compressive thermal layer to cancel the residual.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Internal Force Analysis**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $\mu$m and m in intermediate steps. The examiner awards 0.5 mark deduction per inconsistency. *Always convert to SI (meters) at the start.*
> 2. **Sign convention error:** Forgetting that *concave-up wafer* means *compressive film* (and vice versa). KTU expects the physical sign, not just magnitude.
> 3. **Stoney's $t_f \ll t_s$ assumption ignored:** Applying Stoney to a film nearly as thick as the substrate (e.g., a thick SU-8 mold) loses 2 marks. Use **Freund–Suresh** or **Brenner–Senderoff** correction in such cases.
> 4. **Mixing uniaxial and biaxial moduli:** Using $E$ alone instead of $E/(1-\nu)$ in biaxial stress. KTU explicitly tests the difference.
> 5. **Neglecting the gradient moment:** Writing $\sigma_f$ without separating $N$ and $M$ when the problem specifies a gradient — losing 2 marks on the moment sub-part.
> 6. **Buckling sign:** Forgetting that *compressive* $N$ is the one that buckles, not tensile. KTU's "stability check" sub-question often traps students who use $|N|$ instead of the signed value.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Internal Force Analysis in MEMS**

- **Three sources of internal stress:** *Intrinsic* (deposition), *Thermal* ($\Delta\alpha \Delta T$), *Epitaxial* (lattice mismatch).
- **Two resultants of interest:** Membrane force $N$ (in-plane) and Bending moment $M$ (out-of-plane). Always compute both when stress varies through thickness.
- **Stoney equation (must memorize both forms):**
  - Forward: $\kappa = \dfrac{6 \sigma_f t_f}{M_s t_s^2}$
  - Inverse: $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M_s t_s^2 \kappa}{6 t_f}$
- **Three Stoney assumptions:** $t_f \ll t_s$, isotropic linear elastic, equibiaxial uniform stress. Beyond these, use Freund–Suresh.
- **Biaxial modulus:** Always use $M = E / (1 - \nu)$ for in-plane film stress, never $E$ alone.
- **Critical buckling stress for clamped-clamped beam:** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 D}{L^2 t}$.
- **Electrostatic pressure (parallel plate):** $P_{elec} = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 V^2}{2 g^2}$.
- **Cantilever tip deflection from stress gradient:** $w_{tip} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 L^2}{2E}$ (note: $L^2$ scaling, not $L^3$).
- **Sign of curvature:** Concave-up wafer ⇒ film is *compressive*. Concave-down ⇒ film is *tensile*.
- **Curvature direction rule of thumb:** If $\sigma_{top} > \sigma_{bot}$ ⇒ beam curls *toward* the bottom (compression side).
- **Stability check before design:** Always compare $\vert N \vert$ with $N_{cr}$. If compressive $N > N_{cr}$, the design buckles.
- **Numerical ballparks to remember:**
  - LPCVD polysilicon: ~300 MPa compressive as-deposited.
  - PECVD nitride: ~1 GPa tensile as-deposited.
  - Silicon $\langle 100 \rangle$: $E = 130$ GPa, $\nu = 0.28$.
  - Air permittivity: $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- **Common KTU mistake:** Treating the substrate as a free body in bending. The neutral axis is at the substrate mid-plane (not the film-substrate interface) when $t_f \ll t_s$.
- **Why MEMS is special:** The ratio of surface forces to body forces scales as $1/L$. At $L = 1 \, \mu$m, surface/internal forces dominate entirely — making internal force analysis non-negotiable in microsystem design.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanical Properties of Silicon and Related Thin Films** refer to the set of measurable physical responses of silicon substrates and deposited thin films (polysilicon, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, metals) under applied mechanical loads, including **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Poisson's Ratio (ν)**, **Yield Strength (σ_y)**, **Fracture Strength (σ_f)**, **Residual Stress (σ_r)**, **Hardness (H)**, **Fatigue Resistance**, and **Fracture Toughness (K_IC)**. These properties govern the **structural reliability**, **operational lifetime**, and **packaging feasibility** of MEMS devices.

In the context of KTU's PEPET522 syllabus, this topic sits at the intersection of **solid-state physics** and **microfabrication engineering**. Silicon, being the workhorse material of the semiconductor industry, possesses a **diamond cubic crystal structure** that makes its mechanical behavior strongly **anisotropic** (direction-dependent) — a feature that is largely absent in textbook treatments of bulk materials.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Building Made of Toothpicks:**
> Imagine constructing a skyscraper entirely out of toothpicks glued together in a regular 3D pattern. If you push on the toothpick building from the top (vertical load), it behaves one way. If you push from the side (lateral load), it behaves completely differently. The "toothpick building" is **silicon** — its internal atomic bonds (the "toothpicks") are arranged in a very ordered diamond-cubic pattern, and depending on which crystallographic direction (the "toothpick orientation") you pull, push, or bend it, the stiffness and strength you measure will differ. Now imagine spraying a thin coat of paint (the "thin film") on this building. As the paint dries, it pulls the surface — this is **residual stress**, and it can literally crack the building. That's exactly the engineering challenge in MEMS: predicting and controlling both the **inherent mechanical response** of silicon and the **thin-film stresses** added during fabrication.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Curriculum

- **Fabrication Reliability**: Wafer bow, film cracking, and stiction are direct consequences of uncontrolled mechanical stress.
- **Device Design**: Comb drives, membranes, and cantilevers require precise E and σ_f values to predict deflection and resonant frequency.
- **Yield Optimization**: MEMS foundries measure residual stress on every wafer run — a single MPa error can scrap a batch.
- **Multi-Physics Coupling**: Mechanical stress couples with **piezoresistivity** (changing electrical resistance) and **piezoelectricity** in thin films.

## 1.5 Forms of Silicon Used in MEMS

1. **Single-Crystal Silicon (Mono-Si):** Anisotropic mechanical behavior; primary substrate material.
2. **Polysilicon (Poly-Si):** Fine-grained; properties depend on deposition conditions, grain size, and annealing.
3. **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si):** Isotropic mechanical behavior; used in flexible electronics and TFTs.
4. **Silicon-On-Insulator (SOI):** Active device layer + buried oxide; combined mechanical response.
