# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an electrostatic sensor. List the three fundamental parameters that govern its capacitance, and write the governing equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **electrostatic sensor** (also called a **capacitive sensor**) is a MEMS transducer that converts a physical measurand (displacement, pressure, force, humidity, acceleration) into a measurable change in **electrical capacitance** $C$ between two conductive electrodes separated by a dielectric.

The three governing parameters are:
1. **Effective overlap area** between the plates, $A$ (in m²)
2. **Separation gap** between the plates, $d$ (in m)
3. **Relative permittivity** of the intervening dielectric, $\varepsilon_r$ (dimensionless)

The governing equation is the **parallel-plate capacitance** formula:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$ is the permittivity of free space. Any of the three parameters may be varied to achieve transduction.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between variable-gap and variable-area capacitive sensors with one application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Variable-Gap Sensor | Variable-Area Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Parameter varied | Separation $d$ changes | Overlap area $A$ changes |
| Sensitivity | Nonlinear, $\propto 1/d^2$ | Linear, $\propto 1/d$ |
| Range | Small displacements ($\mu$m) | Larger displacements |
| Application | Pressure sensor, microphone | Angular position, liquid level |

- **Variable-gap example:** A MEMS **pressure sensor** where the diaphragm deflects by $\Delta d$ under pressure, changing $C$.
- **Variable-area example:** A **linear position sensor** where a movable grounded plate slides between two fixed plates, altering the overlap $A$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor. Hence, derive the sensitivity of a variable-gap capacitive displacement sensor, and explain why MEMS designs favor small initial gaps.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation of parallel-plate capacitance** (3 of 7 marks)
Using Gauss's law on a pillbox enclosing one plate:

$$D \cdot A = Q, \quad D = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \frac{V}{d}$$

$$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**(ii) Sensitivity derivation** (3 of 7 marks)
For a perturbation $\Delta d$ around nominal $d_0$:

$$C(d) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d} \approx C_0 \left(1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}\right)$$

$$S_d = \frac{\partial C}{\partial d} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = -\frac{C_0}{d_0}$$

**(iii) Justification for small gaps** (1 of 7 marks)
Since $S_d \propto 1/d_0^2$, reducing the initial gap from 5 μm to 1 μm increases the sensitivity by a factor of **25**. This is why MEMS uses sub-micron gaps — to obtain fF-level resolution from nanometer-scale displacements.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A differential capacitive accelerometer uses two capacitors of nominal $C_0 = 2 \ \text{pF}$, gap $d_0 = 2 \ \mu\text{m}$, electrode area $A = 0.25 \ \text{mm}^2$, and air dielectric. When subjected to an acceleration of $5g$ ($g = 9.8 \ \text{m/s}^2$) with a proof mass of $0.5 \ \text{mg}$, the proof mass displaces by $0.08 \ \mu\text{m}$. Find (i) the differential capacitance change, and (ii) the electrostatic pull-in voltage if the spring constant is $k = 1 \ \text{N/m}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Differential capacitance change** (4 of 7 marks)
Using the differential output equation:

$$\Delta C = 2 C_0 \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} = 2 \times 2 \ \text{pF} \times \frac{0.08 \ \mu\text{m}}{2.0 \ \mu\text{m}} = 0.16 \ \text{pF} = 160 \ \text{fF}$$

**(ii) Pull-in voltage** (3 of 7 marks)
Using the pull-in formula:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 1 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.25 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8 \times 10^{-18}}{5.98 \times 10^{-17}}} = \sqrt{1.070 \, \text{V}^2} \approx 1.034 \ \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting the factor of **2** in the differential output (single-ended vs. half-bridge confusion) is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - Using $d_0$ instead of $(d_0/3)$ in pull-in derivation loses 1 mark.
> - Failing to convert units to SI (μm → m, mm² → m²) costs **2 marks** in numerical problems.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of a variable-dielectric capacitive humidity sensor. Derive the expression for the fractional change in capacitance, and discuss how the relative permittivity of water vapor enables humidity transduction.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Working principle** (3 of 7 marks)
A **variable-dielectric humidity sensor** uses a hygroscopic polymer (e.g., polyimide) as the dielectric between two electrodes. As the ambient relative humidity (RH) increases, water molecules ($\varepsilon_r = 80$) are absorbed into the polymer, **increasing its effective relative permittivity** $\varepsilon_r$. The capacitance is therefore a direct function of RH.

**(ii) Derivation** (3 of 7 marks)
The capacitance with mixed dielectric (polymer + absorbed water):

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{eff} A}{d}$$

For small water absorption $\Delta m$ (or change in $\Delta\varepsilon_r$):

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\Delta \varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_{r,0}}$$

**(iii) Role of water's high permittivity** (1 of 7 mark)
Since liquid water has $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ versus dry polymer's $\varepsilon_r \approx 3$, a small fractional change in water content causes a large fractional change in $\varepsilon_{r,eff}$, producing a **measurable and monotonic** capacitance change with RH.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A comb-drive actuator has 50 finger pairs, each of length $L = 100 \ \mu\text{m}$, overlap $x_0 = 5 \ \mu\text{m}$, gap $d = 2 \ \mu\text{m}$, and $\varepsilon_r = 1$. Calculate the total capacitance at zero displacement and the lateral electrostatic force when a voltage of $10 \ \text{V}$ is applied. Comment on the linearity of the force-displacement characteristic.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Capacitance per finger pair** (2 of 7 marks)

$$C_{pair} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times (100 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_{pair} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-15} \ \text{F} \approx 2.21 \ \text{fF}$$

**(ii) Total capacitance** (1 of 7 marks)
With 50 pairs in parallel:

$$C_{total} = 50 \times 2.21 \ \text{fF} = 110.7 \ \text{fF}$$

**(iii) Lateral force** (3 of 7 marks)
For a comb drive, the lateral force is:

$$F_x = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \cdot N \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L \cdot t}{d}$$

Assuming finger thickness $t = 5 \ \mu\text{m}$:

$$F_x = 0.5 \times 100 \times 50 \times \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_x = 0.5 \times 100 \times 50 \times 2.2135 \times 10^{-15} = 5.53 \ \text{nN}$$

**(iv) Linearity comment** (1 of 7 marks)
The comb-drive force is **independent of lateral displacement** $x$ because $C \propto x$ linearly, making $\partial C/\partial x$ constant. This is a major advantage of comb drives over parallel-plate actuators for **linear force-displacement** behavior.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to multiply by the **number of finger pairs N** in the force equation costs 2 marks.
> - Confusing *area* for a comb drive ($A = L \times t$, not $L \times x_0$) loses 1 mark.
> - Failing to specify whether the structure is "lateral" or "vertical" comb drive costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Electrostatic Sensors**

- ✅ **Core equation:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — memorize with units (F).
- ✅ **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$ — exact value must be remembered.
- ✅ **Three transduction variables:** $A$ (area), $d$ (gap), $\varepsilon_r$ (dielectric) — know which is varied for each sensor type.
- ✅ **Gap sensor sensitivity** is **nonlinear** ($\propto 1/d^2$); **area sensor sensitivity** is **linear** ($\propto 1/d$).
- ✅ **Differential (half-bridge)** topology **doubles sensitivity** and cancels common-mode errors — used in every commercial MEMS accelerometer.
- ✅ **Electrostatic force:** $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}$ — **attractive**, quadratic in $V$.
- ✅ **Pull-in voltage:** $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$ — occurs at $d = d_0/3$; **never operate above $V_{PI}$ in closed-loop designs**.
- ✅ **Comb drives** offer **displacement-independent** force (linear characteristic) — preferred for actuators and gyroscopes.
- ✅ **Variable-dielectric** sensors exploit the high $\varepsilon_r$ of water (~80) for humidity detection.
- ✅ **Interface circuits:** C/V converter, charge amplifier, switched-capacitor, sigma-delta modulator.
- ✅ **Advantages:** low power, low noise, CMOS-compatible, temperature-stable, high sensitivity.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** high-impedance output (susceptible to parasitic capacitance and EMI), limited dynamic range, requires sophisticated readout ASIC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermal Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Sensors** are a class of Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) devices designed to detect, measure, and convert thermal energy (temperature, heat flux, or thermal radiation) into a quantifiable electrical or mechanical signal. In the context of MEMS, thermal sensors exploit the temperature-dependent properties of materials—such as electrical resistance, Seebeck coefficient, thermal expansion, and radiative emission—to transduce thermal stimuli into measurable outputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A thermal sensor is a micro-scale transducer that uses microfabrication techniques to integrate temperature-sensitive materials (metals, semiconductors, polymers, or bimorphs) with signal-conditioning electronics on a single chip, enabling localized, high-precision thermal measurements.

The fundamental transduction mechanisms used in MEMS thermal sensors include:
1. **Thermo-resistive** (resistance change with temperature)
2. **Thermoelectric/Seebeck** (voltage generation from temperature gradient)
3. **Thermo-mechanical** (deflection due to thermal expansion mismatch)
4. **Pyroelectric** (charge generation from rate of temperature change)
5. **Radiative/Infrared** (detection of emitted thermal radiation)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of a thermal sensor like a "smart thermometer on a chip." Just as a mercury thermometer expands when heated, a MEMS thermal sensor changes its electrical properties (resistance, voltage, or physical shape) when temperature changes. But unlike a bulky glass thermometer, these sensors are microscopic—often smaller than a grain of sand—and can be embedded into smartphones, cars, medical devices, and industrial machines to "feel" temperature changes with extreme speed and accuracy.

Imagine placing a thin metal wire under a microscope. When you heat it, the wire's resistance increases because the electrons inside the wire vibrate more and collide with the moving current. A thermal sensor essentially watches this resistance change continuously and translates it into a temperature reading. Some thermal sensors work like tiny voltage generators—similar to how a thermocouple produces a small voltage when one junction is hot and the other is cold (this is the **Seebeck effect**).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Temperature Reference:** $T_0 = 273.15$ **K** (0°C)
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ **W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J·K⁻¹**
- **Seebeck Coefficient (Silicon):** $\alpha_{Si} \approx 200$ to **$1000$ µV/K**
- **Seebeck Coefficient (Constantan):** $\alpha \approx -35$ **µV/K**
- **Seebeck Coefficient (Chromel):** $\alpha \approx +25$ **µV/K**
- **Operating Range:** $-55°C$ to $+300°C$ (typical MEMS), extending to **$1000°C$** for specialized sensors
- **Thermal Conductivity of Silicon:** $k_{Si} \approx 150$ **W/(m·K)**
- **Thermal Conductivity of Air:** $k_{air} \approx 0.026$ **W/(m·K)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** MEMS thermal sensors offer 100× to 1000× lower power consumption, faster response times (milliseconds), and smaller form factors compared to conventional macroscopic thermal sensors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of MEMS Thermal Sensors

MEMS thermal sensors are broadly classified into **five categories** based on their operating principle:

#### 2.1.1 Thermocouples (Seebeck Effect-Based Sensors)

**Operational Principle:**
- Two dissimilar metals (Type K, Type J, Type T) are joined at a hot junction while the cold junction is held at a reference temperature.
- The open-circuit voltage generated is proportional to the temperature difference.

**Why It Works (Physics Behind):**
- Charge carriers (electrons) diffuse from the hot end to the cold end at different rates in the two metals, creating a net potential difference.
- The magnitude depends on the Seebeck coefficient difference.

**Working Steps:**
1. Heat is applied to the measuring junction.
2. Mobile charge carriers (electrons/holes) gain kinetic energy.
3. Diffusion of carriers from hot to cold regions creates an EMF.
4. The Seebeck voltage is measured across the open ends.
5. The cold junction is compensated electronically (Cold Junction Compensation - CJC).

#### 2.1.2 Thermistors (Thermally Sensitive Resistors)

**Two Subtypes:**
- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance decreases as temperature rises.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance increases as temperature rises.

**Working Steps:**
1. Temperature changes alter carrier mobility in the semiconductor oxide (e.g., metal oxides like Mn₃O₄, NiO, CoO).
2. For NTC, increased temperature excites more charge carriers, reducing resistance.
3. For PTC (especially in barium titanate), phase transitions cause resistance to spike sharply.
4. The resistance change is measured using a Wheatstone bridge or voltage divider.

#### 2.1.3 Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs)

**Operational Principle:**
- Uses pure metals (Platinum, Nickel, Copper) whose resistance increases linearly with temperature.
- **PT100 / PT1000** are industry standards (100 Ω and 1000 Ω at 0°C).

**Why Platinum?** 
- Highly linear resistance-temperature relationship
- Excellent chemical stability
- Wide operating range (-200°C to +850°C)
- High reproducibility

#### 2.1.4 Semiconductor-Based Temperature Sensors (Bandgap Sensors)

**Working Steps:**
1. Two transistors are operated at different current densities.
2. The difference in base-emitter voltages (ΔV_BE) is proportional to absolute temperature (PTAT - Proportional To Absolute Temperature).
3. This ΔV_BE is used to generate an accurate temperature reading.

#### 2.1.5 Thermomechanical Sensors (Bimetallic / Bimorph Actuators)

**Working Steps:**
1. Two materials with different Coefficients of Thermal Expansion (CTE) are bonded.
2. Temperature change causes differential expansion.
3. The structure bends/deflects, and this deflection is measured capacitively or piezoresistively.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Sensitivity |
|---|-------------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, dT$ | $V$: Voltage (V), $T$: Temperature (K), $\alpha$: Seebeck coefficient (V/K) | 10–80 µV/°C |
| 2 | NTC Thermistor | $R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | $R_T$: Resistance (Ω), $B$: Beta constant (K), $T$: Temperature (K) | -3% to -6% per °C |
| 3 | RTD (Platinum) | $R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2\right]$ | $R_0$: Resistance at 0°C, $\alpha, \beta$: Callendar-Van Dusen coefficients | ~0.385 Ω/°C (PT100) |
| 4 | Linear Approximation (RTD) | $R_T \approx R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3} /°C$ for Pt | Linear regime |
| 5 | Semiconductor Bandgap | $V_{out} = \frac{k_B T}{q} \cdot \ln(N) \cdot M$ | $k_B$: Boltzmann constant, $q$: electron charge, $N$: current ratio, $M$: multiplier | ~10 mV/°C |
| 6 | Bimetallic Deflection | $\delta = \frac{3 L^2 \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T}{t(4 + 6h_1/t + 4(h_1/t)^2 + (E_1/E_2)(t/h_1)^3 + (E_2/E_1)(h_1/t)^3)}$ | $L$: Length, $\Delta\alpha$: CTE difference, $t$: thickness | µm-scale deflection |
| 7 | Stefan-Boltzmann (IR Sensor) | $E = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot T^4$ | $E$: Radiant exitance, $\varepsilon$: emissivity | Wavelength-dependent |
| 8 | Heat Transfer (Conduction) | $Q = k A \frac{\Delta T}{d}$ | $Q$: Heat flow (W), $k$: thermal conductivity (W/m·K) | Fourier's Law |
| 9 | Thermal Time Constant | $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$ | $R_{th}$: Thermal resistance (K/W), $C_{th}$: Thermal capacitance (J/K) | Response speed |
| 10 | Seebeck Coefficient | $\alpha = \frac{dV}{dT}$ | Material property (V/K) | Material-dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** "**T**hermocouple = **V**oltage" (Seebeck), "**T**hermistor = **R**esistance" (exponential), "**R**TD = **R**esistance" (linear), "**B**andgap = **V**oltage" (PTAT), "**B**imetallic = **B**ending" (mechanical deflection).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive:** Engine control units (ECUs), cabin climate control, exhaust gas temperature monitoring.
- **Medical:** Patient thermometry, PCR thermal cyclers, surgical tooltip monitoring.
- **Consumer Electronics:** CPU/GPU thermal management, smartphone battery protection.
- **Industrial:** HVAC systems, process control, semiconductor fabrication thermal mapping.
- **Aerospace:** Hypersonic vehicle skin temperature, satellite thermal regulation.
- **Environmental:** Weather stations, soil moisture-temperature combined sensors.
- **IR Imaging & Defense:** Night vision cameras, missile seeker arrays, motion detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice between sensor types depends on **accuracy, range, response time, and cost**. Thermocouples dominate high-temperature industrial use, while semiconductor bandgap sensors dominate consumer electronics due to their on-chip integration with CMOS circuitry.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: NTC Thermistor Equation (Steinhart-Hart Model)

The Steinhart-Hart equation is the most accurate empirical model for NTC thermistor behavior over a wide temperature range.

**Starting Point:** The conduction in a semiconductor oxide follows an Arrhenius-type behavior:

$$
\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{-E_a}{2 k_B T}\right)
$$

where:
- $\sigma(T)$ is the temperature-dependent electrical conductivity
- $\sigma_0$ is a material-dependent constant
- $E_a$ is the activation energy
- $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant
- $T$ is the absolute temperature

**Step 1:** Since resistance is the inverse of conductivity, $R = \dfrac{L}{\sigma \cdot A}$:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{2 k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the **Beta (B) constant** to simplify:

$$
B = \frac{E_a}{2 k_B}
$$

**Step 3:** Express the resistance at a reference temperature $T_0$:

$$
R(T_0) = R_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{T_0}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio $R(T)/R(T_0)$:

$$
\frac{R(T)}{R(T_0)} = \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the canonical NTC equation:

$$
\boxed{R(T) = R(T_0) \cdot \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]}
$$

**Step 6 (Higher-Order Correction):** The Steinhart-Hart three-parameter model for greater accuracy:

$$
\frac{1}{T} = A + B_1 \ln(R) + C_1 [\ln(R)]^3
$$

where $A$, $B_1$, and $C_1$ are empirical Steinhart-Hart coefficients.

### 3.2 Derivation: Seebeck Voltage in a Thermocouple

**Starting Point:** The total Seebeck voltage is the integral of the Seebeck coefficient difference between two dissimilar metals A and B over the temperature range from the cold junction $T_c$ to the hot junction $T_h$.

**Step 1:** Define the differential EMF generated in a small temperature slice $dT$:

$$
dV = (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, dT
$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from the cold junction to the hot junction:

$$
V = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} [\alpha_A(T) - \alpha_B(T)] \, dT
$$

**Step 3:** For a Type K thermocouple (Chromel-Alumel), the polynomial approximation is:

$$
V(T) = a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 + \cdots + a_n T^n
$$

**Step 4:** A simplified linear approximation (valid for small temperature ranges):

$$
V \approx (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \cdot (T_h - T_c) = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 5:** For a Type K thermocouple, $\Delta\alpha \approx 40$ µV/°C. If $T_h = 250°C$ and $T_c = 25°C$:

$$
V = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times (250 - 25) = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 225 = 9 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 9 \, mV
$$

**Step 6 (Cold Junction Compensation):** Since the voltmeter measures $V_{measured} = V(T_h, T_{ref}) - V(T_c, T_{ref})$, the cold junction at $T_c$ must be temperature-compensated:

$$
V_{true} = V_{measured} + V(T_c, T_{ref})
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: RTD Resistance-Temperature Relationship (Callendar-Van Dusen Equation)

**Step 1:** Start from the linear approximation for small temperature ranges:

$$
R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the temperature coefficient of resistance.

**Step 2:** For platinum RTDs, the resistance exhibits slight non-linearity. The Callendar-Van Dusen equation for $T \geq 0°C$ is:

$$
R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 - 100 \alpha \beta T^3 + \cdots \right]
$$

**Step 3 (For $T < 0°C$):** A 4th-order term is added:

$$
R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma T^3 (T - 100) - \beta \gamma T^4\right]
$$

**Step 4 (Standard Platinum Coefficients - IEC 60751):**
- $\alpha = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} /°C$
- $\beta = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} /°C^2$
- $\gamma = -4.183 \times 10^{-12} /°C^4$

**Step 5 (Numerical Example):** Calculate the resistance of a PT100 RTD at $T = 100°C$:

$$
R_{100} = 100 \left[1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(100) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(100)^2\right]
$$

$$
R_{100} = 100 \left[1 + 0.39083 - 0.005775\right] = 100 \times 1.385055 = 138.5055 \, \Omega
$$

This matches the standard PT100 value, validating the model.

### 3.4 Derivation: Bimetallic Strip Deflection (Timoshenko Bimetal Thermostat Formula)

**Starting Point:** Two strips of materials 1 and 2 with different CTEs ($\alpha_1 \neq \alpha_2$) are bonded along their length.

**Step 1:** Define parameters:
- $h_1, h_2$ = thickness of layers
- $E_1, E_2$ = Young's moduli
- $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ = Coefficients of Thermal Expansion
- $L$ = length of the strip
- $\Delta T$ = temperature change

**Step 2:** The curvature $\kappa$ of the bimetallic strip upon heating:

$$
\kappa = \frac{6(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T (1+m)^2}{h_1 \left[3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn}\right)\right]}
$$

where $m = h_1/h_2$ is the thickness ratio and $n = E_1/E_2$ is the modulus ratio.

**Step 3:** The tip deflection $\delta$ for a cantilever beam of length $L$:

$$
\delta = \frac{\kappa L^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4 (Simplified form for $m = 1$ and $E_1 = E_2$):**

$$
\delta = \frac{3L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{4 h_1}
$$

**Step 5 (Numerical Example):** A bimorph with $L = 1$ mm, $h_1 = 10$ µm, $\Delta\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $\Delta T = 100°C$:

$$
\delta = \frac{3 \times (10^{-3})^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{4 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.75 \times 10^{-8} \, m = 37.5 \, nm
$$

This nanoscale deflection is then detected capacitively or via piezoresistive readouts in MEMS implementations.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Thermocouple | Thermistor | RTD | Semiconductor Bandgap |
|-----------|-------------|------------|-----|----------------------|
| **Range (°C)** | -270 to +1800 | -55 to +150 | -200 to +850 | -55 to +150 |
| **Accuracy** | ±1 to ±3°C | ±0.1 to ±1°C | ±0.01 to ±0.5°C | ±0.5 to ±2°C |
| **Sensitivity** | 10–80 µV/°C | -3% to -6% per °C | ~0.385 Ω/°C | ~10 mV/°C |
| **Linearity** | Low | Exponential (Non-linear) | High (linear) | High (linear) |
| **Self-Heating** | Negligible | Significant | Low | Low |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | High | Medium |
| **Typical Application** | Industrial furnaces | Consumer electronics | Laboratory/calibration | ICs, SoCs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Heating Error Formula:** $\Delta T_{self} = P \cdot R_{th}$, where $P$ is the dissipated power and $R_{th}$ is the thermal resistance. This is a common KTU exam trap!

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Seebeck Effect. Explain its significance in MEMS thermocouples. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Seebeck Effect is the generation of an electromotive force (EMF) across a temperature gradient in a conducting material. When two dissimilar metals (A and B) are joined to form a closed loop and their junctions are maintained at different temperatures ($T_h$ and $T_c$), a net EMF is produced:

$$
V = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, dT
$$

**Significance in MEMS:** (1) Enables self-powered temperature sensing without external excitation. (2) Allows integration of thermopile arrays for IR detection. (3) Used in thermoelectric energy harvesting. (4) Provides direct voltage output proportional to temperature difference, simplifying readout electronics.

---

#### Question 2: Differentiate between NTC and PTC thermistors. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | NTC Thermistor | PTC Thermistor |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| **Behavior** | Resistance **decreases** with ↑T | Resistance **increases** with ↑T |
| **Mechanism** | Increased carrier concentration in semiconductor oxide | Phase transition in barium titanate ceramic |
| **Equation** | $R = R_0 \exp[B(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ | Sharp rise near Curie temperature (~120°C) |
| **Application** | Inrush current limiters, temperature monitoring | Self-regulating heaters, motor protection |
| **Material** | Mn₃O₄, NiO, CoO oxides | Barium titanate (BaTiO₃) doped with La/Sb |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the governing equation for an NTC thermistor. A thermistor has $R_{25} = 10\,k\Omega$ and a Beta value of $B = 3950\,K$. Calculate its resistance at $85°C$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a MEMS thermopile infrared sensor. List its applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of NTC Thermistor Equation:**

Starting from semiconductor Arrhenius behavior:

$$
\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{-E_a}{2k_B T}\right)
$$

Since $R = L/(\sigma A)$:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{T}\right), \quad \text{where} \quad B = \frac{E_a}{2k_B}
$$

Reference resistance at $T_0 = 298.15$ K:

$$
R(T_0) = R_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{T_0}\right)
$$

Dividing both equations:

$$
\frac{R(T)}{R(T_0)} = \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Final Governing Equation:**

$$
\boxed{R(T) = R_{25} \cdot \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $R_{25} = 10\,k\Omega$, $B = 3950\,K$, $T_0 = 298.15\,K$, $T = 85°C = 358.15\,K$

$$
R(358.15) = 10000 \times \exp\left[3950 \left(\frac{1}{358.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)\right]
$$

Compute the bracketed term:

$$
\frac{1}{358.15} = 2.7922 \times 10^{-3} \, K^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} \, K^{-1}
$$

$$
\Delta = 2.7922 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -5.618 \times 10^{-4} \, K^{-1}
$$

$$
B \cdot \Delta = 3950 \times (-5.618 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.2191
$$

$$
R(358.15) = 10000 \times e^{-2.2191} = 10000 \times 0.1085 = 1085 \, \Omega \approx 1.085 \, k\Omega
$$

**Result:** $R(85°C) \approx 1.085\,k\Omega$ (1 Mark for final answer)

**(b) MEMS Thermopile IR Sensor Operation:**

A thermopile consists of multiple thermocouples connected in series, fabricated on a suspended MEMS membrane.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["IR Radiation Source"] --> B["Absorber Membrane"]
    B --> C["Hot Junctions of Thermopile"]
    C --> D["Thermopile Series Connection"]
    D --> E["Cold Junctions on Heat Sink"]
    E --> F["Differential Voltage Output"]
    F --> G["Amplifier Stage"]
    G --> H["ADC and Microprocessor"]
```

**Operational Steps:**
1. IR radiation strikes the absorber membrane (typically a black-body coating like gold-black or carbon nanotubes).
2. The membrane absorbs IR energy and its temperature rises.
3. The hot junctions (on the membrane) experience temperature rise $T_h$.
4. The cold junctions (anchored to the bulk silicon substrate) remain at $T_c \approx T_{ambient}$.
5. Each thermocouple generates Seebeck voltage; $N$ thermocouples in series produce $N \times V_{Seebeck}$.
6. Output voltage is proportional to incident IR power, enabling non-contact temperature measurement.

**Applications:** (1) Non-contact thermometers, (2) Pyrometers, (3) Gas analyzers (NDIR), (4) Occupancy detection, (5) Earth observation satellites, (6) Fire detection systems.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD). Derive the Callendar-Van Dusen equation and calculate the resistance of a PT100 RTD at $200°C$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With diagrams, describe the construction and working of a MEMS-based micro-hotplate gas sensor. How does it differ from a conventional macro-scale gas sensor? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RTD Working Principle & Callendar-Van Dusen Equation:**

**Working Principle:**
- An RTD exploits the predictable, near-linear increase in metal resistance with temperature.
- Platinum is the preferred metal due to its chemical inertness, high linearity, and reproducibility.
- Resistance is measured using a 2-wire, 3-wire, or 4-wire Kelvin connection (4-wire preferred for accuracy).

**Derivation of Callendar-Van Dusen Equation:**

Starting with linear approximation: $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$

To account for the slight non-linearity of platinum, add a quadratic correction:

$$
R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)
$$

For temperatures below 0°C, an additional cubic term emerges due to asymmetry in phonon scattering:

$$
\boxed{R_T = R_0\left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma T^3(T - 100) - \beta \gamma T^4\right] \text{ for } T < 0°C}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_T = R_0\left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2\right] \text{ for } T \geq 0°C}
$$

**Numerical Calculation at 200°C:**

Given: $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ (PT100), $\alpha = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\,°C^{-1}$, $\beta = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\,°C^{-2}$

$$
R_{200} = 100 \left[1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(200) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(200)^2\right]
$$

$$
= 100 \left[1 + 0.78166 - 0.02310\right]
$$

$$
= 100 \times 1.75856 = 175.856 \, \Omega
$$

**Result:** $R(200°C) \approx 175.86\,\Omega$

**(b) MEMS Micro-Hotplate Gas Sensor:**

**Construction:**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Silicon Wafer Substrate"] --> B["Anisotropic Etching to Form Cavity"]
    B --> C["Thin Dielectric Membrane SiO2 or Si3N4"]
    C --> D1["Heater Meander Platinum"]
    C --> D2["Temperature Sensor RTD or Thermistor"]
    C --> D3["Interdigitated Electrodes"]
    D1 --> E["Gas Sensitive Layer SnO2 or ZnO"]
    D3 --> E
    D2 --> F["Bond Pads"]
    D1 --> F
    D3 --> F
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The heater is energized to raise the membrane temperature (typically $200°C$–$400°C$).
2. The gas-sensitive layer (e.g., SnO₂) is heated to its optimal operating temperature.
3. Target gas molecules adsorb on the sensing layer, altering its electrical resistance.
4. The interdigitated electrodes measure the resistance change.
5. The RTD monitors the operating temperature for closed-loop control.

**Differences from Conventional Macro-Scale Sensors:**

| Parameter | Macro-Scale Sensor | MEMS Micro-Hotplate |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Power Consumption** | 1–10 W | 10–100 mW (100× less) |
| **Response Time** | 10–60 s | 1–10 ms (1000× faster) |
| **Size** | cm³ | mm³ |
| **Thermal Mass** | High | Very low |
| **Cost (mass production)** | High | Very low (batch fabrication) |
| **On-chip Integration** | No | Yes (with CMOS) |
| **Portability** | Limited | Excellent |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin in the NTC thermistor equation ($T$ must be in **absolute Kelvin**, not Celsius).
> 2. **Sign of Beta Constant:** The exponential term in the NTC equation **must have the correct sign**; many students write $R = R_0 \exp[-B(1/T - 1/T_0)]$, which is **wrong**.
> 3. **Seebeck Coefficient Sign:** Always remember it is a **difference** $(\alpha_A - \alpha_B)$; never use a single metal's coefficient.
> 4. **Self-Heating Effect:** Failing to mention that measurement current $I$ causes self-heating $P = I^2 R$, leading to errors in precision sensors.
> 5. **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC):** In thermocouple problems, forgetting that the voltmeter reads only the **difference**; the cold-junction voltage must be added back.
> 6. **Linearization of NTC:** Stating "NTC is linear" — this is a fatal mistake; it is **highly non-linear** and requires Steinhart-Hart or Beta-equation compensation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [x] **Seebeck Effect** = Voltage generation from temperature gradient; governed by $V = \int (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) dT$.
- [x] **Peltier Effect** = Reverse of Seebeck; current flow causes heating/cooling at junctions.
- [x] **Thomson Effect** = Heating/cooling in a single conductor carrying current in a temperature gradient.
- [x] **NTC Equation** = $R(T) = R_0 \exp[B(1/T - 1/T_0)]$; **always use Kelvin**.
- [x] **PTC Equation** = Sharp resistance rise near Curie temperature of BaTiO₃.
- [x] **RTD Linear Equation** = $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ for small ranges; **Callendar-Van Dusen** for full range.
- [x] **PT100** = 100 Ω at 0°C; α = 3.9083 × 10⁻³ /°C; β = -5.775 × 10⁻⁷ /°C².
- [x] **Bandgap Sensor** = PTAT (Proportional to Absolute Temperature) using $\Delta V_{BE}$ between two BJTs at different current densities.
- [x] **Bimetallic Strip** = Differential thermal expansion; deflection measured capacitively in MEMS.
- [x] **Thermopile** = Series array of thermocouples for enhanced sensitivity to IR radiation.
- [x] **Thermal Time Constant** = $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$; determines response speed.
- [x] **Self-Heating Error** = $\Delta T_{self} = P \cdot R_{th}$ (use low measurement currents to minimize).
- [x] **Micro-Hotplate** = Suspended membrane with integrated heater, thermometer, and sensing layer.
- [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** = $E = \sigma \varepsilon T^4$; basis for non-contact IR pyrometers.
- [x] **Seebeck Coefficient of Silicon** = 200–1000 µV/K (much higher than metals, ideal for on-chip integration).
- [x] **Advantages of MEMS** = Low power, fast response, batch fabrication, CMOS integration, low cost.
- [x] **Disadvantages of MEMS** = Limited high-temperature range, packaging challenges, lower mechanical robustness.
- [x] **Wheatstone Bridge** = Standard readout for resistive sensors; converts ΔR to ΔV.
- [x] **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** = Essential for accurate thermocouple measurements.
- [x] **Curie Temperature** = Phase transition point where PTC thermistor resistance spikes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU Part B questions, always combine **theory + diagram + numerical example** in your answer. Examiners award bonus marks for clear, well-labeled block diagrams and correctly converted units. Memorize the NTC and RTD equations in both °C and K forms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoresistive Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piezoresistive Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoresistive Effect:** The piezoresistive effect is the phenomenon by which the **electrical resistivity** of a material changes when it is subjected to an applied **mechanical stress or strain**. A device that exploits this effect to transduce mechanical quantities (force, pressure, acceleration) into electrical signals is called a **Piezoresistive Sensor**.

In semiconductor materials, the piezoresistive effect is several orders of magnitude larger than in metals. This is because applied stress modifies the **energy band structure** and **carrier mobility** of the material, which in turn changes its resistivity. The mathematical relationship between fractional change in resistivity and applied stress is given by the **piezoresistive coefficient tensor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The piezoresistive effect in metals was first observed by **Lord Kelvin (Sir William Thomson) in 1856**, but the effect in semiconductors (silicon, germanium) was discovered by **Charles Smith in 1954**, which revolutionized the field of solid-state sensing.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **sponge soaked in salty water (representing a conductive material with mobile charge carriers)**. When you squeeze the sponge, the channels through which the water flows become narrower and longer. This increases the resistance to flow. Similarly, when a piezoresistive material is stretched or compressed:

- The **lattice spacing** of the atoms changes.
- This alters the **mobility of charge carriers** (electrons or holes).
- The result is a measurable change in **electrical resistance**.

In a metal strain gauge, the change in resistance comes mostly from **geometric deformation** (length and cross-sectional area). In a **semiconductor piezoresistor**, the dominant contribution comes from the change in **resistivity itself**, which is why silicon piezoresistors are **50–200 times more sensitive** than metallic strain gauges.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metric — The Gauge Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gauge Factor (G):** Defined as the ratio of **fractional change in resistance** to the **applied strain** in the direction of current flow. It is a **dimensionless number** that quantifies the sensitivity of a piezoresistive element.

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

where $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the mechanical strain along the current direction. The gauge factor for **silicon** typically lies in the range **G = 50 to 200**, whereas for **metallic strain gauges** it is only about **G = 2**.

### 1.4 Standard Units and Physical Constants

- **Piezoresistive Coefficient (π):** Units: **Pa⁻¹** (inverse Pascal) or **m²/N**.
- **Gauge Factor (G):** **Dimensionless**.
- **Young's Modulus of Silicon:** **E = 190 GPa** (along ⟨100⟩ direction).
- **Poisson's Ratio of Silicon:** **ν ≈ 0.28** (along ⟨100⟩).
- **Resistivity of doped silicon (typical):** **ρ = 0.001 to 0.01 Ω·cm** depending on doping.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Piezoresistive Coefficient Tensor

For a **cubic crystal** such as silicon, the piezoresistive effect is described by a **3 × 3 tensor** linking the fractional change in resistivity to the applied stress:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho_{ij}}{\rho} = \sum_{k,l} \pi_{ijkl} \sigma_{kl}$$

In **Voigt notation** (with indices 1,2,3,4,5,6 corresponding to xx,yy,zz,yz,xz,xy), the tensor reduces to a **6 × 6 matrix** with only **three independent components** for silicon:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta\rho_1/\rho \\
\Delta\rho_2/\rho \\
\Delta\rho_3/\rho \\
\Delta\rho_4/\rho \\
\Delta\rho_5/\rho \\
\Delta\rho_6/\rho
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\pi_{11} & \pi_{12} & \pi_{12} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\pi_{12} & \pi_{11} & \pi_{12} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\pi_{12} & \pi_{12} & \pi_{11} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \pi_{44} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \pi_{44} & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \pi_{44}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\sigma_1 \\
\sigma_2 \\
\sigma_3 \\
\sigma_4 \\
\sigma_5 \\
\sigma_6
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.2 Longitudinal and Transverse Coefficients

For practical design, we use two scalar coefficients:

- **Longitudinal coefficient (πₗ):** Stress applied **parallel** to the current direction.
- **Transverse coefficient (πₜ):** Stress applied **perpendicular** to the current direction.

The fractional change in resistivity for a piezoresistor along an arbitrary crystallographic direction is:

$$\frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho} = \pi_\ell \sigma_\ell + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

### 2.3 Typical Piezoresistive Coefficient Values for Silicon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For p-type silicon (most commonly used in MEMS sensors):**
> - $\pi_{11} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$
> - $\pi_{12} \approx -1.1 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$
> - $\pi_{44} \approx 138.1 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ (dominant)
>
> **For n-type silicon:**
> - $\pi_{11} \approx -102.2 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$
> - $\pi_{12} \approx 53.4 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$
> - $\pi_{44} \approx -13.6 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$

### 2.4 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle

A piezoresistive sensor operates through the following causal chain:

1. **Mechanical Input (Force / Pressure):** An external mechanical quantity acts on a sensing element (typically a thin silicon diaphragm).
2. **Stress Generation:** The input creates internal **mechanical stress (σ)** distributed across the diaphragm.
3. **Strain Production:** The stress produces **strain (ε = σ/E)** in the diaphragm material.
4. **Resistivity Change:** The strain alters the energy band structure, changing **carrier mobility** and thus **resistivity (ρ)**.
5. **Resistance Change:** The resistance of the embedded piezoresistor changes according to **ΔR/R = G · ε**.
6. **Signal Conditioning:** The resistance change is converted to a **voltage** using a **Wheatstone bridge**.
7. **Electrical Output:** The bridge output voltage is **linearly proportional** to the applied mechanical input.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge Factor definition | dimensionless |
| $G = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge Factor (general) | dimensionless |
| $G \approx \dfrac{\Delta\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon} = \pi_\ell E$ | Gauge Factor (semiconductor) | dimensionless |
| $\dfrac{\Delta\rho}{\rho} = \pi_\ell \sigma_\ell + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | Resistivity change under stress | dimensionless |
| $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \pi_\ell \sigma_\ell + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | Total fractional resistance change | dimensionless |
| $V_{out} \approx \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Half-bridge output (small signal) | V |
| $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$ | Full Wheatstone bridge | V |
| $\sigma = \dfrac{3P}{8}\left(\dfrac{a^2}{h^2}\right)\left(1 - \dfrac{r^2}{a^2}\right)$ | Radial stress in clamped circular diaphragm (center: r=0) | Pa |
| $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3Pa^2}{8h^2}$ | Maximum stress at center of diaphragm | Pa |
| $\sigma_{edge} = \dfrac{-3Pa^2}{4h^2}$ | Edge stress (tangential, clamped) | Pa |
| $w(r) = \dfrac{3P(1-\nu^2)}{16Eh^3}\left(a^2 - r^2\right)^2$ | Deflection of clamped circular diaphragm | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **In the table:** $P$ = applied pressure, $a$ = diaphragm radius, $h$ = diaphragm thickness, $r$ = radial position, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $V_{in}$ = bridge excitation voltage, $V_{out}$ = bridge output voltage.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Piezoresistive sensors are the **workhorse of low-cost, high-volume MEMS sensing** and are deployed in:

- **Automotive tire-pressure monitoring systems (TPMS)**
- **Medical blood pressure transducers**
- **Industrial process control pressure transmitters**
- **MEMS accelerometers** (airbag deployment sensors, consumer electronics)
- **AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy) cantilever readout**
- **Inertial measurement units (IMUs)** in smartphones and drones
- **Force / tactile sensors** in robotics

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gauge Factor from Fundamental Resistance Equation

**Step 1: Start with the basic resistance formula for a conductor:**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is resistivity, $L$ is length, $A$ is cross-sectional area.

**Step 2: Take the natural logarithm of both sides to convert multiplicative changes to additive ones:**

$$\ln R = \ln\rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 3: Differentiate both sides to get fractional changes:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the mechanical strain terms:**

- Longitudinal strain: $\varepsilon_\ell = dL / L$
- Transverse strain: $\varepsilon_t = dA / A = 2 \cdot (-\nu \cdot \varepsilon_\ell) = -2\nu\varepsilon_\ell$ (negative because Poisson contraction reduces area when length increases)

Substituting:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon_\ell - (-2\nu\varepsilon_\ell) = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon_\ell(1 + 2\nu)$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to isolate the gauge factor:**

$$\frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon_\ell} = \frac{\Delta\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon_\ell} + (1 + 2\nu)$$

Therefore:

$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_\ell}\cdot\frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 6: Apply the piezoresistive relation $\Delta\rho/\rho = \pi_\ell \sigma_\ell$ and $\sigma_\ell = E \cdot \varepsilon_\ell$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G &= 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\pi_\ell E \varepsilon_\ell}{\varepsilon_\ell} \\
G &= 1 + 2\nu + \pi_\ell E
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Interpret the two contributions:**

- **$1 + 2\nu$** is the **geometric contribution** (dominant for metals, value ≈ 1.6 for steel, ≈ 1.9 for constantan).
- **$\pi_\ell E$** is the **piezoresistive contribution** (dominant for semiconductors, value ≈ 100–200 for silicon).

> [!NOTE]
> For silicon, $\nu \approx 0.28$, so $1+2\nu \approx 1.56$, which is **negligible** compared to the piezoresistive term. Thus, for silicon piezoresistors, $G \approx \pi_\ell E$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage

**Step 1: Consider a full Wheatstone bridge with four resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arranged as shown in the diagram, with excitation $V_{in}$ applied across one diagonal and output $V_{out}$ measured across the other.**

**Step 2: Compute the potential at the top node (between $R_1$ and $R_2$):**

$$V_a = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3: Compute the potential at the bottom node (between $R_3$ and $R_4$):**

$$V_b = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 4: The differential output voltage is:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_a - V_b \\
V_{out} &= V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \\
V_{out} &= V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2(R_3+R_4) - R_4(R_1+R_2)}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)} \\
V_{out} &= V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Balanced bridge condition** ($V_{out} = 0$):

$$R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 6: Suppose only $R_1$ changes by a small amount $\Delta R$ (due to applied stress). Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_{in} \cdot \frac{R \cdot R - (R+\Delta R)\cdot R}{(R+\Delta R + R)(R+R)} \\
V_{out} &= V_{in} \cdot \frac{R^2 - R^2 - \Delta R \cdot R}{(2R+\Delta R)(2R)} \\
V_{out} &= V_{in} \cdot \frac{-\Delta R \cdot R}{(2R+\Delta R)(2R)} \\
V_{out} &= -V_{in} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R+\Delta R)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Apply the small-signal approximation $\Delta R \ll R$:**

$$V_{out} \approx -V_{in} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} = -\frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

The magnitude of the output is:

$$|V_{out}| = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

This is the **fundamental transduction equation** of a piezoresistive sensor.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A silicon piezoresistor with gauge factor $G = 120$ is mounted on a cantilever beam. The beam has length $L = 5\ \text{mm}$, thickness $t = 200\ \mu\text{m}$, and Young's modulus $E = 190\ \text{GPa}$. A force $F = 10\ \text{mN}$ is applied at the free end. The piezoresistor is connected in a Wheatstone bridge with $V_{in} = 5\ \text{V}$. Calculate the output voltage.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Bending strain at the surface of the cantilever (at the fixed end):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{6FL}{E b t^2}$$

For a square cross-section with $b = t = 200\ \mu\text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{6 \times (10 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3})}{190 \times 10^9 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})} \\
\varepsilon &= \frac{6 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{190 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}} \\
\varepsilon &= \frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{1.52 \times 10^0} = 1.97 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Fractional change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \times 1.97 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0237 \approx 2.37\%$$

**Step 3: Wheatstone bridge output voltage (single active resistor):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \times 0.0237 = 0.0296\ \text{V} \approx 29.6\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 4: If a full-bridge configuration is used (4 active piezoresistors with two in tension and two in compression), the output is doubled:**

$$V_{out,full} = \frac{V_{in}}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 59.2\ \text{mV}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Piezoresistive Sensor System

The following block diagram maps the signal flow from physical input to electrical output, illustrating how a piezoresistive MEMS sensor operates at the system level.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHYSICAL INPUT - PRESSURE OR FORCE] --> B[MICROMACHINED SILICON DIAPHRAGM OR CANTILEVER]
    B --> C[MECHANICAL STRESS AND STRAIN IN DIAPHRAGM]
    C --> D[EMBEDDED PIEZORESISTOR NETWORK]
    D --> E[CHANGE IN ELECTRICAL RESISTANCE DELTA R]
    E --> F[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE READOUT CIRCUIT]
    F --> G[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT VOLTAGE VOUT]
    G --> H[ANALOG SIGNAL CONDITIONING - AMPLIFIER]
    H --> I[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERSION]
    I --> J[DIGITAL OUTPUT - MICROCONTROLLER OR PROCESSOR]
    G --> K[TEMPERATURE COMPENSATION NETWORK]
    K --> F
```

### 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Configuration with Piezoresistors

The figure below shows a fully-active Wheatstone bridge, which is the standard readout topology for piezoresistive pressure sensors. Resistors R1 and R3 are oriented along the tangential stress direction (placed at the diaphragm edge where tensile stress is maximum), while R2 and R4 are placed at the center where compressive radial stress is maximum (or oriented transversely). This configuration maximizes sensitivity and provides **first-order temperature compensation** because all four resistors experience the same temperature.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VIN - BRIDGE EXCITATION VOLTAGE] --> B[NODE A - TOP]
    B --> C[RESISTOR R1 - TANGENTIAL EDGE PIEZORESISTOR]
    B --> D[RESISTOR R2 - RADIAL CENTER PIEZORESISTOR]
    C --> E[NODE B - VOUT POSITIVE]
    D --> F[NODE C - VOUT NEGATIVE]
    E --> G[RESISTOR R3 - TANGENTIAL EDGE PIEZORESISTOR]
    F --> H[RESISTOR R4 - RADIAL CENTER PIEZORESISTOR]
    G --> I[GROUND - REFERENCE NODE]
    H --> I
    E -.-> J[VOUT - DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER INPUT]
    F -.-> J
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of a Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor

The following schematic depicts the layered structure of a typical bulk-micromachined piezoresistive absolute pressure sensor, showing the silicon substrate with etched cavity, the thin diaphragm, the four piezoresistors, and the electrical interconnects leading to the bond pads.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOPLAYER [TOP SURFACE - METALLIZATION AND CONTACTS]
        A1[BOND PAD 1] --- A2[BOND PAD 2]
        A2 --- A3[BOND PAD 3]
        A3 --- A4[BOND PAD 4]
    end
    subgraph SILICON [SILICON STRUCTURE LAYER]
        B1[PIEZORESISTOR R1 AT EDGE] --- B2[PIEZORESISTOR R2 AT CENTER]
        B2 --- B3[PIEZORESISTOR R3 AT EDGE]
        B3 --- B4[PIEZORESISTOR R4 AT CENTER]
    end
    subgraph DIAPHRAGM [THIN SILICON DIAPHRAGM]
        C1[FLEXIBLE MEMBRANE - THICKNESS 5 TO 50 MICROMETER]
    end
    subgraph CAVITY [BULK ETCHED VACUUM CAVITY]
        D1[SEALED REFERENCE CAVITY FOR ABSOLUTE PRESSURE MEASUREMENT]
    end
    subgraph SUBSTRATE [SILICON SUBSTRATE WAFER]
        E1[SUPPORTING HANDLE WAFER - 400 TO 700 MICROMETER]
    end
    TOPLAYER --> SILICON
    SILICON --> DIAPHRAGM
    DIAPHRAGM --> CAVITY
    CAVITY --> SUBSTRATE
    F1[APPLIED EXTERNAL PRESSURE ACTING ON DIAPHRAGM] -.-> DIAPHRAGM
```

### 4.4 Piezoresistive vs. Piezoelectric — Comparison Flow

The following decision-flow diagram helps students differentiate between the two related but fundamentally different transduction mechanisms, a common point of confusion in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[APPLIED MECHANICAL STRESS] --> B{IS THE MATERIAL A QUARTZ-TYPE OR POLAR CRYSTAL?}
    B -- YES --> C[PIEZOELECTRIC EFFECT]
    B -- NO --> D{IS THE MATERIAL A SEMICONDUCTOR OR METAL WITH CARRIER MOBILITY DEPENDENCE ON STRESS?}
    D -- YES --> E[PIEZORESISTIVE EFFECT]
    D -- NO --> F[NO TRANSDUCTION - PURELY MECHANICAL]
    C --> G[OUTPUT IS CHARGE OR VOLTAGE - DC RESPONSE REQUIRES EXTERNAL CHARGE AMPLIFIER]
    E --> H[OUTPUT IS RESISTANCE CHANGE - READ VIA WHEATSTONE BRIDGE WITH EXCITATION VOLTAGE]
    G --> I[SUITABLE FOR DYNAMIC AND HIGH FREQUENCY MEASUREMENTS]
    H --> J[SUITABLE FOR STATIC AND DC MEASUREMENTS]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The **piezoelectric effect** is reversible (works in both directions — actuator and sensor) and produces a **charge/voltage** directly, but cannot measure static (DC) signals without a charge amplifier that drifts. The **piezoresistive effect** produces a **resistance change** that requires external excitation but can measure **static (DC) signals** reliably, making it the preferred choice for pressure sensors and accelerometers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]:** Define the **piezoresistive effect** and state the **gauge factor** of a piezoresistor. Why is the gauge factor of silicon much larger than that of a metal?

**Model Answer:**

The **piezoresistive effect** is the change in electrical resistivity of a material when subjected to mechanical stress. The **gauge factor (G)** is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the applied strain:

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

The gauge factor has two contributions: a **geometric contribution** $(1+2\nu)$ and a **piezoresistive contribution** $(\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$. In **metals**, the geometric term dominates because stress causes only minor changes in the resistivity (electron scattering changes are small), giving $G \approx 2$. In **silicon (a semiconductor)**, stress dramatically alters the **energy band structure** and **carrier mobility**, causing a large change in resistivity. This makes the piezoresistive term $\pi_\ell E$ dominant, yielding gauge factors of **G = 50 to 200**, which is one to two orders of magnitude larger than metallic strain gauges.

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]:** Explain why a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration is used in piezoresistive sensor readout instead of measuring the piezoresistor directly with an ohmmeter.

**Model Answer:**

A piezoresistor's resistance change under typical stress conditions is extremely small (on the order of a few percent or less), so direct resistance measurement with an ohmmeter would lack the required **sensitivity and resolution**. Additionally, the **baseline resistance** of the piezoresistor is also strongly affected by **temperature**, making it impossible to distinguish stress-induced resistance changes from temperature-induced changes.

A **Wheatstone bridge** provides three critical advantages:

1. **High sensitivity:** The bridge converts the small $\Delta R$ into a **differential voltage** proportional to $\Delta R / R$, which can be amplified.
2. **Temperature compensation:** When all four bridge arms are **identical piezoresistors** on the same die, temperature changes affect all four equally, and the **bridge remains balanced** (common-mode rejection).
3. **Common-mode noise rejection:** Stray noise and supply voltage fluctuations are rejected because the output is measured **differentially** between the two midpoints.

The output voltage for a single active piezoresistor in a bridge with excitation $V_{in}$ is $V_{out} \approx (V_{in}/4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question 3A [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **working principle of a piezoresistive pressure sensor**. Describe the role of the silicon diaphragm, piezoresistor placement, and Wheatstone bridge in signal generation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon piezoresistive pressure sensor has a square diaphragm of side $a = 1\ \text{mm}$ and thickness $h = 25\ \mu\text{m}$. Four identical p-type piezoresistors with $\pi_{44} = 138 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ are placed at the diaphragm edges in a Wheatstone bridge configuration. The bridge excitation is $V_{in} = 5\ \text{V}$, and the maximum stress at the diaphragm edge is given by $\sigma_{max} = 0.308 \cdot P \cdot (a/h)^2$, where $P$ is the applied pressure. Calculate the output voltage when $P = 100\ \text{kPa}$. Assume the piezoresistors are oriented along the ⟨110⟩ direction where the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient is $\pi_\ell = (\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44})/2$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **piezoresistive pressure sensor** consists of a thin **silicon diaphragm** (typically 5–50 µm thick) fabricated by bulk micromachining of a silicon wafer. Four piezoresistors are **ion-implanted or diffused** near the surface of the diaphragm, two placed near the **clamped edges** (where **tangential tensile stress** is maximum) and two near the **diaphragm center** (where **radial compressive stress** is maximum), all oriented along the ⟨110⟩ crystallographic direction for maximum sensitivity in p-type silicon.

When external pressure $P$ is applied, the diaphragm deflects, producing a non-uniform stress distribution. The two edge piezoresistors experience **tensile stress** (resistance increases), while the center piezoresistors experience **compressive stress** (resistance decreases). The four piezoresistors form a **Wheatstone bridge** such that the resistance changes of opposite arms are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, producing a **doubled differential output** compared to a half-bridge.

The bridge output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

for a full bridge with two tension and two compression piezoresistors. This arrangement also provides **first-order temperature compensation** because all four resistors experience the same temperature and are on the same die.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate the maximum stress at the diaphragm edge:**

$$\sigma_{max} = 0.308 \times P \times \left(\frac{a}{h}\right)^2 = 0.308 \times (100 \times 10^3) \times \left(\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-6}}\right)^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= 0.308 \times 10^5 \times (40)^2 \\
\sigma_{max} &= 0.308 \times 10^5 \times 1600 \\
\sigma_{max} &= 4.928 \times 10^7\ \text{Pa} = 49.28\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient for p-type silicon in ⟨110⟩ direction:**

$$\pi_\ell = \frac{\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44}}{2} = \frac{(6.6 + (-1.1) + 138.1) \times 10^{-11}}{2}$$

$$\pi_\ell = \frac{143.6 \times 10^{-11}}{2} = 71.8 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the fractional change in resistivity:**

$$\frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho} = \pi_\ell \times \sigma_{max} = 71.8 \times 10^{-11} \times 4.928 \times 10^7 = 3.538 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4: Approximate the gauge factor for p-type silicon along ⟨110⟩:**

$$G = \pi_\ell \times E = 71.8 \times 10^{-11} \times 190 \times 10^9 = 136.4$$

**Step 5: Calculate the bridge output voltage (full-bridge configuration with 2 active tension and 2 active compression piezoresistors):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{2} \times \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{2} \times \frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho} = \frac{5}{2} \times 3.538 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$V_{out} = 0.0885\ \text{V} \approx 88.5\ \text{mV}$$

> **Answer:** $V_{out} \approx 88.5\ \text{mV}$

---

> **Question 3B [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 – Alternative Choice]:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **gauge factor of a piezoresistive sensor** starting from the basic resistance equation $R = \rho L / A$. Clearly explain the two contributions to the gauge factor and discuss why silicon has a much higher gauge factor than metals.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A piezoresistive accelerometer uses a **seismic mass** of $m = 1\ \text{mg}$ suspended by a silicon cantilever of length $L = 2\ \text{mm}$, width $b = 500\ \mu\text{m}$, and thickness $t = 20\ \mu\text{m}$. A single p-type piezoresistor with gauge factor $G = 120$ is placed at the base of the cantilever (point of maximum strain). The piezoresistor is in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with $V_{in} = 3\ \text{V}$, and the other three arms have fixed resistors of equal value. The Young's modulus of silicon is $E = 190\ \text{GPa}$. Calculate the **bridge output voltage** when the accelerometer experiences an acceleration of $a = 5g$ (where $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$). Assume small-deflection bending theory applies.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The detailed derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key result is:

$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_\ell E$$

where:
- The **geometric term** $(1+2\nu)$ arises from dimensional changes (length and cross-section) when the resistor is strained. For most materials, this contributes $G_{geom} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$.
- The **piezoresistive term** $\pi_\ell E$ arises from the stress-induced change in carrier mobility and band structure. For metals, $\pi_\ell$ is negligibly small ($10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ range, dominated by geometric effects), giving $G \approx 2$. For silicon, the piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_{44}$ is enormous (especially in p-type, $\pi_{44} \approx 138 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$), and with $E = 190\ \text{GPa}$, the term $\pi_\ell E$ alone contributes $G \approx 100$–$200$, dwarfing the geometric term.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate the equivalent force on the seismic mass:**

$$F = m \cdot a = (1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg}) \times (5 \times 9.81)\ \text{m/s}^2 = 4.905 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the maximum strain at the base of the cantilever (assuming a tip-loaded cantilever):**

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6FL}{E b t^2} = \frac{6 \times 4.905 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{190 \times 10^9 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{max} &= \frac{5.886 \times 10^{-7}}{190 \times 10^9 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{-10}} \\
\varepsilon_{max} &= \frac{5.886 \times 10^{-7}}{3.8 \times 10^{-2}} \\
\varepsilon_{max} &= 1.549 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the fractional change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon_{max} = 120 \times 1.549 \times 10^{-5} = 1.859 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the bridge output voltage (single active arm):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{3}{4} \times 1.859 \times 10^{-3} = 1.394 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 1.39\ \text{mV}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the sensor sensitivity (output per unit acceleration) for design verification:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = \frac{1.39 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 9.81} = 28.4\ \mu\text{V}/(\text{m/s}^2) = 0.279\ \text{mV/g}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Piezoresistive Sensor Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the piezoresistive tensor structure (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often write $\Delta R / R = \pi \sigma$ without specifying whether $\pi$ is the longitudinal ($\pi_\ell$) or transverse ($\pi_t$) coefficient. Both terms must be considered when stress is multiaxial: $\Delta R / R = \pi_\ell \sigma_\ell + \pi_t \sigma_t$.
>
> 2. **Using metallic gauge factor formula for silicon (–2 marks):** Do not write $G = 1 + 2\nu$ for silicon. For semiconductors, the dominant term is $\pi_\ell E$. The geometric term is **negligible** for silicon.
>
> 3. **Confusing piezoresistive and piezoelectric effects (–3 marks):** Piezoresistive = change in **resistance** (requires external excitation, measures static signals). Piezoelectric = generation of **charge/voltage** (self-powered, but only AC/dynamic signals without charge amplifier).
>
> 4. **Ignoring Wheatstone bridge configuration (–2 marks):** When a problem says "full bridge with two tension and two compression resistors," use $V_{out} = (V_{in}/2) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$, **not** $V_{in}/4$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in piezoresistive coefficient (–1 mark):** $\pi$ is in **Pa⁻¹**, not dimensionless. The stress $\sigma$ is in **Pa**, and their product is dimensionless.
>
> 6. **Diaphragm stress formula misapplication (–3 marks):** The maximum stress in a clamped circular diaphragm is $\sigma_{max} = 3Pa^2 / (8h^2)$ at the center, and $\sigma_{edge} = -3Pa^2 / (4h^2)$ at the edge. Sign matters — center stress is tensile in radial direction, edge stress is compressive in radial direction.
>
> 7. **Not stating assumptions explicitly (–1 mark):** Always state: "assuming small-signal approximation," "assuming uniform stress distribution," "assuming linear elasticity," etc.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Piezoresistive Sensors (KTU PEPET522 Module 4):**
>
> - **Piezoresistive Effect** = Change in **resistivity** of a material due to applied **mechanical stress** (NOT to be confused with piezoelectric effect, which produces charge).
> - **Gauge Factor (G)** = $(\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ — dimensionless number, key figure of merit.
> - **G = 1 + 2ν + πₗE**: Geometric term is small (~1.5), piezoresistive term is huge for silicon (~100–200).
> - **Three independent piezoresistive coefficients for silicon**: $\pi_{11}, \pi_{12}, \pi_{44}$ (full tensor has 6×6 form with zeros off-diagonal).
> - **π₄₄ is the dominant coefficient for p-type silicon** → use p-type resistors in ⟨110⟩ direction for maximum sensitivity.
> - **π₁₁ is the dominant coefficient for n-type silicon** → use n-type resistors in ⟨100⟩ direction.
> - **Resistance change equation:** $\Delta R / R = \pi_\ell \sigma_\ell + \pi_t \sigma_t + (1+2\nu)\varepsilon$ — three contributions.
> - **Wheatstone Bridge** is the standard readout — provides sensitivity, temperature compensation, and common-mode noise rejection.
> - **Half-bridge output:** $V_{out} \approx (V_{in}/4)(\Delta R / R)$ (one active arm).
> - **Full-bridge output:** $V_{out} \approx (V_{in}/2)(\Delta R / R)$ (two tension + two compression arms).
> - **Diaphragm stress (clamped circular):** $\sigma_{max} = 3Pa^2/(8h^2)$ at center; $\sigma_{edge} = -3Pa^2/(4h^2)$ at edge.
> - **Sensitivity of silicon piezoresistor:** 50–200 times higher than metallic strain gauges.
> - **Static vs Dynamic:** Piezoresistive sensors can measure **static (DC) signals**, unlike piezoelectric sensors.
> - **Temperature sensitivity:** Both baseline resistance and gauge factor depend on temperature → must be compensated using bridge configuration or on-chip temperature sensors.
> - **Common materials:** Monocrystalline silicon, polysilicon, germanium, SiC, and metals (NiCr, constantan) — silicon is preferred for MEMS due to CMOS compatibility and high $\pi_{44}$.
> - **Applications:** Pressure sensors, accelerometers, force/tactile sensors, AFM probes, bio-MEMS, automotive TPMS, and inertial measurement units.
> - **Key silicon parameters to memorize:** $E = 190$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.28$, p-type $\pi_{44} \approx 138 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹, n-type $\pi_{11} \approx -102 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
