topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Sensors; Actuation Techniques: Thermal Forces, Shape Memory Alloys, Piezoelectric Crystals, Electrostatic Forces (Parallel Plate, Torsion Bar, Comb Drive Actuators), Micromechanical Motors and Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Piezoelectric Sensors — Formal Definition

A **piezoelectric sensor** is a solid-state transduction device that exploits the direct piezoelectric effect — the linear, reversible coupling between mechanical stress ($T$, in N/m²) and electric displacement ($D$, in C/m²) within non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials such as **quartz (SiO₂)**, **lead zirconate titanate (PZT)**, and **zinc oxide (ZnO)**. When an external mechanical load deforms the crystal lattice, dipoles within the unit cell reorient, generating a measurable surface charge proportional to the applied force.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A piezoelectric sensor converts mechanical energy (force, pressure, acceleration) directly into electrical energy (charge or voltage) without requiring an external power source. It is intrinsically bi-directional — the *converse* effect enables actuation, and the *direct* effect enables sensing.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeezed Water Balloon"

Imagine a sealed rubber balloon (the crystal) filled with water (electric dipoles). If you press down on the top (apply force), the balloon deforms and pushes water sideways — imagine tiny charges spilling out onto electrodes stuck to the sides. The harder you squeeze, the more water (charge) flows out. Release the squeeze, and the water flows back. The balloon "remembers" its shape. This is precisely how a piezoelectric element works: **mechanical input → electrical output** (sensor mode), and **electrical input → mechanical output** (actuator mode).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** The piezoelectric phenomenon is a **reversible, linear, anisotropic, and temperature-dependent** coupling. Unlike capacitive or piezoresistive sensors, piezoelectric sensors generate charge **only under dynamic conditions** (varying force) — they cannot measure *static* DC loads without charge leakage compensation.

---

## 1.2 Actuation Techniques — Master Overview

An **actuator** in MEMS is a micro-scale energy conversion device that transforms an input signal (electrical, thermal, or magnetic) into controlled mechanical motion, force, or displacement. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies actuation mechanisms by their underlying physical principle.

| # | Actuation Principle | Driving Energy | Typical Use Case |
|---|---------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Thermal Forces** | Electrical (Joule heating) | Micro-grippers, valves |
| 2 | **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA)** | Thermal (phase change) | Micro-robotic arms |
| 3 | **Piezoelectric Crystals** | Electrical (electric field) | Inkjet printheads, AFM |
| 4 | **Electrostatic Forces** | Electrical (voltage) | Comb drives, micro-mirrors |
| 5 | **Micromechanical Motors/Pumps** | Mixed (electrostatic, magnetic) | Lab-on-chip fluidics |

### 1.2.1 Thermal Force Actuation — Intuitive Analogy

Think of a **bimetallic strip in an old-fashioned thermostat**. Two metals with different coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) are bonded together. Heat the strip, and the metal that expands more forces the assembly to bend. In MEMS, we shrink this concept to micron-scale: a micro-beam carries current, resistively heats up, expands, and produces displacement at its tip.

### 1.2.2 Shape Memory Alloys (SMA) — The "Muscle Wire"

A **shape memory alloy** (e.g., Nickel-Titanium / **Nitinol**) is a metal that "remembers" its original forged shape. When cooled below a *transformation temperature*, it is soft and easily deformed. Upon heating above this critical point, it undergoes a solid-state phase change (martensite → austenite) and **snaps back** to its pre-trained geometry — producing large force and stroke (up to **8%** strain).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Nitinol behaves like a crumpled paper ball that, when dropped into warm water, uncrumples into a perfectly folded origami crane. The "warm water" is the trigger energy, and the uncrumpling is the actuation.

### 1.2.3 Electrostatic Actuation — The "Invisible Tug-of-War"

Place two parallel conductive plates close together and apply a voltage. Opposite charges accumulate on facing surfaces, and Coulomb attraction pulls them together. **The plates want to touch.** This is the fundamental Coulomb's law mechanism exploited in **parallel-plate**, **torsion-bar**, and **comb-drive** electrostatic actuators. The force is non-linear with gap distance ($F \propto 1/d^2$) and quadratic with voltage ($F \propto V^2$), which yields strong forces only at very small gaps (sub-10 µm).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Electrostatic force is **always attractive** between two oppositely charged conductors. To get *repulsion*, you must cleverly bias the geometry (e.g., vary overlap area instead of gap). Students frequently misstate this in the ESE.

---

## 1.3 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> - **CO4 (Module 4):** *Apply the principles of transduction mechanisms — piezoelectric, thermal, electrostatic, and SMA — to design micro-sensors and micro-actuators for specific MEMS applications.*
> - **Bloom's Levels Covered:** L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Piezoelectricity — Coupled Constitutive Equations

The piezoelectric effect is fundamentally a **two-port** energy coupling between the **mechanical domain** (stress $T$, strain $S$) and the **electrical domain** (electric field $E$, electric displacement $D$). The IEEE Standard on Piezoelectricity (1987) defines the four linear constitutive forms. The two most relevant for KTU are the *strain-charge* form (e-E) and the *stress-charge* form (d-form):

$$S_i = s^E_{ij} T_j + d_{ik} E_k \quad \text{(Converse effect — actuator mode)}$$

$$D_k = d_{kj} T_j + \varepsilon^T_{kj} E_j \quad \text{(Direct effect — sensor mode)}$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = mechanical strain (dimensionless, m/m)
- $T_j$ = mechanical stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $E_k$ = electric field (V/m)
- $D_k$ = electric displacement (C/m²)
- $s^E_{ij}$ = elastic compliance at constant electric field (m²/N)
- $d_{ik}$ = **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N or m/V) — the most cited figure of merit
- $\varepsilon^T_{kj}$ = permittivity at constant stress (F/m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The same coefficient $d_{ik}$ appears in BOTH the direct and converse equations (Maxwell reciprocity / Onsager relations). This is why a good piezoelectric sensor is also a good actuator. The coefficient $d_{33}$ for PZT is approximately **$400 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V** (or 400 pC/N).

### 2.1.1 Sensor Mode Operation — Step-by-Step

1. **Mechanical input** (force $F$) applied to a piezo crystal produces stress $T = F/A$.
2. Internal dipoles rotate, causing net **polarization** $P$ along the poling axis.
3. Surface charge $Q = D \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot F$ appears on the electrodes.
4. The equivalent circuit is a **charge source** $Q$ in parallel with capacitance $C_p$ and resistance $R_p$.
5. A charge amplifier (or voltage follower with high input impedance) converts $Q$ to a usable voltage.

### 2.1.2 Actuator Mode Operation — Step-by-Step

1. **Voltage $V$** applied across electrodes generates electric field $E = V/t$ (where $t$ is electrode spacing).
2. Inverse piezoelectric strain: $S = d_{33} \cdot E = d_{33} \cdot V/t$.
3. Mechanical displacement: $\Delta L = S \cdot L_0$.
4. Force generated under blocked condition: $F = A \cdot T = A \cdot d_{33} \cdot E / s^E$.

---

## 2.2 Thermal Force Actuation — Theory

A micro-beam of length $L_0$ carrying current $I$ with electrical resistance $R$ dissipates power $P = I^2 R$, causing Joule heating and a uniform temperature rise $\Delta T$. The resulting thermal expansion produces a tip displacement (for a clamped-free cantilever):

$$\Delta L_{thermal} = \alpha_L \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$$

Where $\alpha_L$ is the **linear coefficient of thermal expansion** (CTE, in 1/K or ppm/K). For silicon, $\alpha_L \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6} /K$. For aluminum, $\alpha_L \approx 23 \times 10^{-6} /K$.

The **bimetallic (or bimorph) cantilever** amplifies this by bonding two materials with mismatched CTE. The tip deflection of a bimorph is:

$$\delta_{bimorph} = \frac{3 L_0^2 \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}{t_1 + t_2} \cdot \frac{(1 + m)^2}{3(1+m)^2 - (1 + mm^2)}$$

Where $m = t_1 / t_2$ is the layer thickness ratio and $t_i$ is the thickness of layer $i$.

### 2.2.1 Why Thermal Actuation in MEMS?

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Thermal actuators produce **largest forces** among MEMS actuators (millinewtons) but consume **high power** (tens of mW) and have **slow response** (kHz or lower). They are ideal for static, high-force applications such as micro-grippers holding silicon dies, and for thermal bubble inkjet printheads (Canon, HP technology).

---

## 2.3 Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuation — Theory

SMAs exhibit a reversible **martensitic phase transformation** between two crystal structures:
- **Martensite** (low-temperature, monoclinic, soft, easily deformed)
- **Austenite** (high-temperature, cubic, stiff, recovers original shape)

The phase change is **diffusionless** and **reversible**. The critical temperatures are:
- $A_s$ = Austenite start (heating)
- $A_f$ = Austenite finish (heating)
- $M_s$ = Martensite start (cooling)
- $M_f$ = Martensite finish (cooling)

For Nitinol (Ni-Ti, near 50:50 atomic %):
- $A_f \approx 90–110°C$
- Recoverable strain $\varepsilon_{rec} \approx 6–8\%$
- Recovery stress $\sigma_{rec} \approx 200–500$ MPa

The Clausius-Clapeyron equation governs stress-temperature coupling:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H}{\varepsilon_{rec} \cdot T_0}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** SMAs are **thermally activated** — they cannot be modulated continuously at high frequency. The bandwidth is limited to **~1 Hz** in most practical applications. Do NOT compare them directly to piezoelectric or electrostatic actuators in terms of speed.

---

## 2.4 Electrostatic Actuation — Theory

### 2.4.1 Parallel-Plate Actuator

Consider two parallel conductive plates of area $A$ separated by vacuum gap $d$, with applied voltage $V$. The capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

The stored electrostatic energy is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d}$$

The attractive force between the plates is the negative gradient of stored energy with respect to gap:

$$F = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2} \quad \text{(N)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** Force scales as $1/d^2$. At very small gaps, force grows rapidly. However, at a critical gap $d = d_{critical} = \frac{2}{3} d_0$, the system undergoes **pull-in instability** — the plates snap together. This is the **pull-in voltage**:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

where $k$ is the equivalent spring constant of the suspension and $d_0$ is the zero-voltage gap.

### 2.4.2 Torsion-Bar Actuator

A **torsion mirror** is a plate suspended by two thin torsion bars (flexures). When voltage is applied between the plate and a stationary electrode beneath it, an electrostatic **torque** rotates the plate about the torsion axis:

$$T_{elec} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 \theta^2} \quad \text{(for small angles, angular displacement }\theta\text{)}$$

The mechanical restoring torque from the torsion bar is $T_{mech} = k_\theta \theta$. Equilibrium gives:

$$k_\theta \theta = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 \theta^2}$$

This is a cubic equation in $\theta$ — also exhibiting **pull-in** at $\theta = \theta_0 / \sqrt{3}$, where $\theta_0$ is the angle at $V=0$.

### 2.4.3 Comb-Drive Actuator

The **comb drive** is the workhorse of surface-micromachined MEMS (used in ADI accelerometers, gyroscopes, and TI DMD chips). It consists of interdigitated fingers: one set is **fixed** (stator), the other set is **movable** (rotor) and anchored via a suspension spring.

The **lateral capacitance** between a pair of fingers (length $L$, gap $g$, thickness $h$) is:

$$C = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 h L}{g} \quad \text{(where } N \text{ = number of finger pairs)}$$

Differential with respect to lateral displacement $x$:

$$F_x = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} V^2 = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 h}{g} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Advantage:** Unlike parallel-plate, the comb-drive force is **independent of displacement $x$**. This is because changing $x$ changes the *overlap area*, not the gap $g$. The result: a **linear, stable, long-stroke** actuator — the pull-in instability is *suppressed*. This is the single most important property that makes comb drives preferred in commercial MEMS.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Piezo Direct Effect | $Q = d_{33} F$ | $Q$ in C, $F$ in N, $d_{33}$ in C/N |
| 2 | Piezo Converse Strain | $S = d_{33} E$ | $E$ in V/m, $S$ dimensionless |
| 3 | Piezo Generated Voltage | $V = \frac{d_{33} F t}{\varepsilon A}$ | $t$ thickness, $A$ area |
| 4 | Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha_L L_0 \Delta T$ | $\alpha_L$ in 1/K |
| 5 | Bimorph Tip Deflection | $\delta = \frac{3L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{2(2t_1 + t_2)}$ | for symmetric bimorph, simplified |
| 6 | SMA Recovery Stress | $\sigma_{rec} \approx E_N \cdot \varepsilon_{rec}$ | $E_N$ = austenite Young's modulus |
| 7 | Parallel-Plate Capacitance | $C = \varepsilon_0 A / d$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m |
| 8 | Parallel-Plate Force | $F = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$ | Newtons |
| 9 | Pull-in Voltage (PP) | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | Volts |
| 10 | Comb-Drive Capacitance | $C = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 h L}{g}$ | $N$ = finger pairs |
| 11 | Comb-Drive Force | $F_x = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 h V^2}{2 g}$ | Newtons |
| 12 | Energy Density (electrostatic) | $u = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2$ | J/m³ |
| 13 | Viscosity (fluidic) | $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s |
| 14 | Hagen-Poiseuille Flow | $Q = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$ | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants to Memorize:**
> - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (vacuum permittivity)
> - $1 \text{ atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
> - CTE of silicon $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} /K$
> - CTE of aluminum $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} /K$

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application Matrix

| Mechanism | Force Density | Stroke | Bandwidth | Power | Application |
|-----------|---------------|--------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| Thermal | High (mN) | Large (µm–mm) | Low (Hz) | High (mW) | Micro-valves, inkjet |
| SMA | Very High (N) | Large (%) | Very Low (<1Hz) | Medium (W) | Surgical micro-tools |
| Piezoelectric | High (N) | Small (nm–µm) | Very High (MHz) | Low (mW) | AFM, ultrasonic |
| Electrostatic PP | Low (µN) | Small (sub-µm) | High (kHz) | Negligible | Micro-mirrors |
| Comb Drive | Medium (µN–mN) | Large (10s of µm) | Medium (kHz) | Negligible | Accelerometers, gyros |
| Micromotor | Low–Medium | Continuous | High (kRPM) | Medium | Micro-turbines, pumps |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Cross-Reference:** The Texas Instruments **DLP chip** (used in every digital projector) contains **millions** of electrostatically actuated aluminum micro-mirrors using a torsion-bar geometry. STMicroelectronics and Bosch accelerometers use **differential comb drives** for capacitive sensing with electrostatic self-test.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Parallel-Plate Electrostatic Force from Energy Method

**Starting Point:** We have two parallel plates of area $A$ separated by vacuum gap $x$. The capacitance is $C(x) = \varepsilon_0 A / x$. A fixed voltage source $V$ maintains the potential.

**Step 1 — Express the stored electrostatic energy as a function of $x$:**

$$U(x) = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x}$$

> The $\frac{1}{2}$ factor arises because, with a fixed voltage source, work done *by* the source equals *twice* the energy stored in the field; the energy *available* for mechanical work is half.

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of virtual work:**

$$F_x = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial x} \bigg|_V = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left( \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate using the power rule $\frac{d}{dx}(1/x) = -1/x^2$:**

$$F_x = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{x^2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the signs and express the result:**

$$F_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2}$$

> The force is **positive** (attractive) and directed to **decrease** $x$, which is consistent with the physical picture: opposite charges attract.

**Step 5 — Verify dimensions:**

$$[F] = \frac{[\text{F/m}][\text{m}^2][\text{V}^2]}{[\text{m}^2]} = \frac{(\text{C/V·m})(\text{V}^2)}{\text{1}} = \frac{\text{C·V}}{\text{m}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}} = \text{N} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pull-in Voltage for Parallel-Plate Actuator

**Setup:** A movable plate is suspended by a linear spring of constant $k$ at zero-voltage gap $d_0$. Voltage $V$ is applied between the plates.

**Step 1 — Write the mechanical equilibrium.** The electrostatic force must balance the spring restoring force:

$$F_{elec}(x) = F_{spring}(x)$$

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2} = k (d_0 - x)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $V^2$:**

$$V^2 = \frac{2 k (d_0 - x) x^2}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 3 — Find the maximum of the right-hand side with respect to $x$ (since pull-in occurs at the *largest* sustainable voltage):**

Take the derivative and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(V^2)}{dx} = \frac{2k}{\varepsilon_0 A} \cdot \frac{d}{dx} \left[ (d_0 - x) x^2 \right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the product rule:**

$$\frac{d}{dx} \left[ (d_0 - x) x^2 \right] = (-1) \cdot x^2 + (d_0 - x) \cdot 2x = -x^2 + 2x(d_0 - x) = -x^2 + 2xd_0 - 2x^2$$

$$= 2xd_0 - 3x^2 = x(2d_0 - 3x)$$

**Step 5 — Set equal to zero and solve for $x$:**

$$x(2d_0 - 3x) = 0 \implies x = 0 \text{ (trivial)} \quad \text{or} \quad x = \frac{2d_0}{3}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $x = 2d_0/3$ back into the $V^2$ expression:**

$$V_{PI}^2 = \frac{2k (d_0 - 2d_0/3)(2d_0/3)^2}{\varepsilon_0 A} = \frac{2k (d_0/3)(4d_0^2/9)}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

$$V_{PI}^2 = \frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 7 — Final pull-in voltage expression:**

$$\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The **two-thirds rule** — pull-in occurs at gap = (2/3) of the zero-voltage gap. This is universally true for all electrostatic parallel-plate actuators and is a frequent KTU exam question. The corresponding travel range is therefore limited to **$d_0/3$** — only one-third of the gap is usable for stable analog operation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Piezoelectric Voltage Output from Applied Force

**Setup:** A PZT disk of thickness $t$, area $A$, permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{33}^T$, and charge coefficient $d_{33}$ is loaded by force $F$ along the poling (3) axis. Electrodes are on the top and bottom faces.

**Step 1 — Stress under the applied force:**

$$T_3 = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Open-circuit condition (no external field, $E_3 = 0$). The electric displacement is:**

$$D_3 = d_{33} T_3 = d_{33} \cdot \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Surface charge on the electrode (in coulombs):**

$$Q = D_3 \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot F$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance of the disk:**

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_{33}^T A}{t}$$

**Step 5 — Open-circuit voltage across the electrodes (V = Q/C):**

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_{33}^T \cdot A} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t}{\varepsilon_{33}^T} \cdot \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of stress and a material figure of merit, the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient** $g_{33}$:**

$$g_{33} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon_{33}^T} \quad \text{(V·m/N)}$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot T_3 = g_{33} \cdot \frac{F \cdot t}{A}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The $g$-coefficient is the right figure of merit for *sensor* applications (high voltage output per unit stress). PZT has $g_{33} \approx 20 \times 10^{-3}$ V·m/N. The $d$-coefficient is the right figure of merit for *actuator* applications (high strain per unit field). The product $d \times g$ is the **figure of merit for energy harvesting**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Comb-Drive Force

**Problem Statement:** A MEMS comb-drive actuator has $N = 100$ finger pairs, finger length $L = 50\ \mu m$, finger thickness $h = 5\ \mu m$, lateral gap $g = 2\ \mu m$, and is driven at $V = 10\ V$. Calculate:
(a) The lateral force generated.
(b) The capacitance of the structure.
(c) The energy stored.

**Given Data:**
- $N = 100$
- $L = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $h = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $g = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $V = 10$ V
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

### (a) Lateral Force

$$F_x = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 h V^2}{2 g}$$

Substituting the values:

$$F_x = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^2}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_x = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 5 \times 100 \times 10^{-12-6+2}}{4 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_x = \frac{442.7 \times 10^{-14}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 110.7 \times 10^{-8} = 1.107 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_x \approx 1.107\ \mu N}$$

### (b) Capacitance

$$C = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 h L}{g} = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 250 \times 10^{-24}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C = 1.107 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F} = 0.1107 \text{ pF}$$

### (c) Stored Energy

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.107 \times 10^{-13} \times 10^2 = 5.535 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{U \approx 5.54 \text{ pJ}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by energy method:** $U = \frac{1}{2} F_x \cdot x$ where $x = $ displacement. If $x = 10\ \mu m$, then $U = 0.5 \times 1.107 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 5.535 \times 10^{-12}$ J ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Thermal Expansion Tip Displacement in a Clamped-Free Micro-Cantilever

**Setup:** A micro-cantilever of length $L$, thickness $t$, width $w$, made of material with CTE $\alpha$, is heated uniformly by $\Delta T$. One end is clamped (fixed), the other is free.

**Step 1 — Free thermal expansion of the unconstrained beam:**

$$\Delta L_{free} = \alpha L \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — For a clamped-free beam, the thermal strain must be "absorbed" by bending. The tip deflection relates to the axial expansion by:**

For a uniform temperature rise in a clamped-free beam, the **axial expansion is geometrically equivalent to a tip deflection** (the beam cannot extend axially because it is clamped at one end, so it bows):

$$\delta_{tip} = \sqrt{L^2 + (\Delta L_{free})^2} - L$$

**Step 3 — For small $\Delta L \ll L$, use the binomial approximation:**

$$\delta_{tip} \approx L \left( \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\alpha \Delta T}{1}\right)^2} - 1 \right) \approx \frac{(\alpha L \Delta T)^2}{2 L} = \frac{\alpha^2 L (\Delta T)^2}{2}$$

This quadratic dependence is too small to be useful. Hence the practical design uses a **bimorph**.

**Step 4 — Bimorph (two-layer) cantilever tip deflection:**

Two layers with thicknesses $t_1, t_2$, Young's moduli $E_1, E_2$, CTEs $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$. The induced curvature $\kappa$:

$$\kappa = \frac{6 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T (1 + m)^2}{t_1 [3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)(m^2 + \frac{1}{mn})]}$$

Where $m = t_1/t_2$ and $n = E_1/E_2$.

**Step 5 — Tip deflection for a symmetric bimorph ($m = 1$, $n = 1$):**

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2 t_1 (1 + t_2/t_1)^2} \bigg|_{m=1} = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{4 t}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Design Insight:** For maximum deflection, the thickness $t$ should be minimized and the length $L$ maximized. The CTE mismatch $\alpha_1 - \alpha_2$ should be as large as possible — e.g., **Al/Si bimorph** gives $\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 \approx 20 \times 10^{-6}/K$, a 10× larger mismatch than Si/SiO₂ ($\approx 2 \times 10^{-6}/K$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Piezoelectric Coefficient Definitions
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

Define the following piezoelectric coefficients and state their SI units:
(i) $d_{33}$
(ii) $g_{33}$
(iii) $k_{33}$ (coupling factor)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) $d_{33}$ — Piezoelectric charge/strain coefficient:** Defined as the electric displacement ($D_3$, in C/m²) produced per unit applied mechanical stress ($T_3$, in N/m²), or equivalently, the mechanical strain ($S_3$, dimensionless) produced per unit applied electric field ($E_3$, in V/m).

$$d_{33} = \left(\frac{\partial D_3}{\partial T_3}\right)_E = \left(\frac{\partial S_3}{\partial E_3}\right)_T \quad \text{Units: C/N or m/V}$$

**(ii) $g_{33}$ — Piezoelectric voltage coefficient:** Defined as the electric field ($E_3$, in V/m) produced per unit applied mechanical stress ($T_3$, in N/m²), or the strain produced per unit applied electric displacement.

$$g_{33} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon_{33}^T} \quad \text{Units: V·m/N}$$

**(iii) $k_{33}$ — Electromechanical coupling factor:** A dimensionless figure of merit (0 to 1) representing the efficiency of energy conversion between mechanical and electrical domains:

$$k_{33}^2 = \frac{d_{33}^2}{s_{33}^E \cdot \varepsilon_{33}^T}$$

For PZT, $k_{33} \approx 0.6$ to 0.7 (i.e., 60–70% energy conversion efficiency).

---

### Question 2: SMA Phase Transformation
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

Explain the two key temperatures in shape memory alloy actuation: $A_s$ (Austenite start) and $A_f$ (Austenite finish). Why is there a hysteresis between $A_s$ and $M_s$?

**Model Answer:**

**$A_s$ (Austenite Start):** The temperature at which the SMA, upon heating, **begins** to transform from the low-temperature martensite phase to the high-temperature austenite phase. Below $A_s$, the material remains martensitic.

**$A_f$ (Austenite Finish):** The temperature at which the transformation to austenite is **complete**. Above $A_f$, the material is fully austenitic and has fully recovered its original shape.

**Hysteresis Explanation:** The phase transformation requires **nucleation** of the new phase, which involves an energy barrier. During heating, austenite nucleates at $A_s$, while during cooling, martensite nucleates at $M_s$ (typically $20$–$50°C$ below $A_s$ for Nitinol). The thermal hysteresis ($\Delta T = A_s - M_s$) represents this energy barrier. **Engineering consequence:** SMA actuators must be designed with **active cooling** if they are to operate at frequencies above a few hundred mHz.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Electrostatic Actuators

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electrostatic force generated by a parallel-plate capacitor actuator. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MEMS parallel-plate actuator has a movable plate of area $A = 200 \times 200\ \mu m^2$, suspended by four identical springs of stiffness $k = 0.1\ N/m$ each (in parallel). The zero-voltage gap is $d_0 = 4\ \mu m$. Calculate:
  (i) The equivalent spring constant.
  (ii) The pull-in voltage.
  (iii) The stable operating range of gap distance.
  (iv) The maximum electrostatic force at the pull-in point. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation of Parallel-Plate Electrostatic Force

**Assumptions:**
1. Plates are perfectly conducting with uniform charge distribution.
2. Fringe fields at the plate edges are negligible (infinite-plate approximation).
3. The gap is filled with vacuum (or uniform dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$).
4. Quasi-static operation (no radiation, no magnetic effects).
5. One plate is fixed; the other is movable in the $x$ direction perpendicular to the plate area.

**Derivation:**

The capacitance between two parallel plates of area $A$ and gap $x$ is:

$$C(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x}$$

For a fixed voltage $V$ applied across the plates, the electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor is:

$$U(x) = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x}$$

By the principle of virtual work, the electrostatic force (acting in the direction of decreasing $x$) is:

$$F_x = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial x} \bigg|_V = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left( \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x} \right)$$

Differentiating:

$$F_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2}$$

**Direction:** Positive $F_x$ corresponds to attractive force, pulling the plates together. **(7 marks fully covered)**

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations

**Given Data:**
- $A = 200 \times 200\ \mu m^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8}\ m^2$
- $k_{single} = 0.1\ N/m$ (×4 springs in parallel)
- $d_0 = 4\ \mu m = 4 \times 10^{-6}\ m$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m$

**(i) Equivalent spring constant:**

The four springs are in parallel (each carries the same displacement but shares the load):

$$k_{eq} = 4 \times 0.1 = 0.4\ N/m$$

**(ii) Pull-in voltage:**

Using the formula:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_{eq} d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 0.4 \times (4 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

Numerator: $8 \times 0.4 \times 64 \times 10^{-18} = 204.8 \times 10^{-18} = 2.048 \times 10^{-16}$

Denominator: $27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 27 \times 35.416 \times 10^{-20} = 956.23 \times 10^{-20} = 9.562 \times 10^{-18}$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{2.048 \times 10^{-16}}{9.562 \times 10^{-18}}} = \sqrt{21.42} \approx 4.63\ V$$

**(iii) Stable operating range:**

The pull-in occurs at $x = 2d_0/3 = 2 \times 4/3 = 8/3 \approx 2.67\ \mu m$. The plate can move **only from $d_0$ down to $2d_0/3$**, i.e., a stable travel of:

$$\Delta x_{stable} = d_0 - \frac{2d_0}{3} = \frac{d_0}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.33\ \mu m$$

**(iv) Maximum electrostatic force at pull-in point:**

At $x = 2d_0/3$ and $V = V_{PI}$:

$$F_{PI} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^2}{2 (2d_0/3)^2}$$

$$F_{PI} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 21.42}{2 \times (2.67 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Numerator: $8.854 \times 4 \times 21.42 \times 10^{-20} = 758.8 \times 10^{-20} = 7.588 \times 10^{-18}$

Denominator: $2 \times 7.13 \times 10^{-12} = 14.26 \times 10^{-12} = 1.426 \times 10^{-11}$

$$F_{PI} = \frac{7.588 \times 10^{-18}}{1.426 \times 10^{-11}} = 5.32 \times 10^{-7}\ N \approx 0.532\ \mu N$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Electrostatic Actuators):**
> 1. Many students **forget to square the gap** in the denominator — it is $x^2$, not $x$.
> 2. Students often confuse **stable operating range** (which is $d_0/3$) with **pull-in gap** ($2d_0/3$). These are different — read the question carefully!
> 3. When springs are specified as "four identical springs," the student must clarify whether they are in **series or parallel**. In a parallel-plate actuator, the suspension is symmetric, so the springs are in parallel. State this explicitly.
> 4. **Do NOT confuse $\varepsilon_0$ with $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$** unless the gap is filled with a dielectric — by default, assume vacuum/air.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Piezoelectric Sensors

**(a)** Explain the direct and converse piezoelectric effects with a neat sketch of the dipole reorientation. State the two constitutive equations and define the coefficients. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quartz piezoelectric pressure sensor ($d_{33} = 2.3\ pC/N$, $\varepsilon_{33}^T = 4.5 \times 10^{-11}\ F/m$, area $A = 1\ cm^2$, thickness $t = 1\ mm$) is subjected to a pressure step of $\Delta P = 1\ MPa$. Calculate:
  (i) The charge generated.
  (ii) The open-circuit output voltage.
  (iii) The mechanical displacement of the top surface.
  (iv) Briefly comment on why this sensor cannot measure *static* pressure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Direct and Converse Piezoelectric Effects

**Direct Piezoelectric Effect (Sensor Mode):**
- Mechanical stress $T$ applied to a non-centrosymmetric crystal (e.g., quartz, PZT) produces a proportional electric displacement $D$ and surface charge.
- **Mechanism:** Under stress, the crystal lattice deforms, the centers of positive and negative charge within the unit cell separate (or come closer), creating a net electric dipole moment. The cumulative effect across the crystal produces a measurable voltage.
- **Equation:** $D_k = d_{kj} T_j + \varepsilon^T_{kj} E_j$

**Converse Piezoelectric Effect (Actuator Mode):**
- An applied electric field $E$ produces a proportional mechanical strain $S$ in the crystal.
- **Mechanism:** The electric field exerts a force on the charged ions in the lattice, displacing them slightly and causing the crystal to deform.
- **Equation:** $S_i = s^E_{ij} T_j + d_{ik} E_k$

**Dipole Reorientation Sketch (description):** In an unstressed, unpolarized crystal, dipoles are randomly oriented, net polarization = 0. After **poling** (strong DC field at elevated temperature), dipoles align with the field, giving a remnant polarization $P_r$. When stress is applied along the poling axis, the aligned dipoles tilt, changing the net surface charge.

**Coefficients:**
- $d_{kj}$: Piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N) — relates stress to charge (direct) or field to strain (converse).
- $s^E_{ij}$: Elastic compliance at constant $E$ (m²/N) — relates stress to strain.
- $\varepsilon^T_{kj}$: Permittivity at constant stress (F/m) — relates field to displacement.

**(7 marks fully covered)**

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations on Quartz Pressure Sensor

**Given Data:**
- $d_{33} = 2.3\ pC/N = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}\ C/N$
- $\varepsilon_{33}^T = 4.5 \times 10^{-11}\ F/m$
- $A = 1\ cm^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2$
- $t = 1\ mm = 1 \times 10^{-3}\ m$
- $\Delta P = 1\ MPa = 1 \times 10^6\ Pa$

**(i) Charge generated:**

First, the force on the sensor from the pressure:

$$F = \Delta P \times A = 1 \times 10^6 \times 1 \times 10^{-4} = 100\ N$$

The generated charge:

$$Q = d_{33} \times F = 2.3 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 = 2.3 \times 10^{-10}\ C = 0.23\ nC$$

**(ii) Open-circuit output voltage:**

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p}, \quad C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_{33}^T A}{t}$$

$$C_p = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 1 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.5 \times 10^{-12}\ F = 4.5\ pF$$

$$V_{oc} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{-10}}{4.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 51.1\ V$$

**(iii) Mechanical displacement of the top surface:**

The strain produced by the applied stress (using $S = s^E T + d_{33} E$, with $E=0$ for free condition):

We first need the compliance $s^E_{33}$ for quartz. For quartz, $s^E_{33} \approx 9.7 \times 10^{-12}\ m^2/N$.

$$S_3 = s^E_{33} T_3 = 9.7 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^6 = 9.7 \times 10^{-6}$$

Displacement of the top surface:

$$\Delta L = S_3 \times t = 9.7 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-3} = 9.7 \times 10^{-9}\ m = 9.7\ nm$$

**(iv) Why this sensor cannot measure *static* pressure:**

The piezoelectric effect produces charge only when the applied stress **changes**. Under a static (DC) load, the generated charge leaks away through the finite resistance of the crystal and the input impedance of the readout circuit. The time constant $\tau = R_p C_p$ (typically 1–10 s for quartz) sets the lower frequency limit. Therefore, piezoelectric sensors are fundamentally **AC-coupled** — they work well for dynamic pressure measurements (e.g., sound, vibration, pulsatile flow) but cannot measure sustained static pressure. For static pressure, one must use a piezoresistive or capacitive sensor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Piezoelectric Sensors):**
> 1. Students often write the converse equation as $D = d \cdot E$ — this is WRONG. $D$ is generated by stress $T$ (direct), and strain $S$ is generated by $E$ (converse). Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. The $d_{33}$ units (C/N vs m/V) are dimensionally equivalent: $C/N = (C/m) \cdot (m/N) \cdot m \cdot (1/V) = m/V$. State this explicitly.
> 3. Do not confuse the *sensor* $g_{33}$ (V·m/N) with the *actuator* $d_{33}$ (C/N or m/V). They are related by $g = d/\varepsilon$, not by $g = d$.
> 4. Static pressure measurement **fails** in piezoelectric sensors — students often incorrectly claim they work for static inputs. This is a frequent deduction item.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🔑 High-Yield Quick-Fire Facts

- **Piezoelectric direct effect:** $Q = d_{33} F$ — mechanical → electrical.
- **Piezoelectric converse effect:** $S = d_{33} E$ — electrical → mechanical.
- **PZT $d_{33}$** ≈ 400 pC/N; **Quartz $d_{33}$** ≈ 2.3 pC/N; **PVDF $d_{33}$** ≈ 30 pC/N.
- **Pull-in voltage** for parallel-plate actuator: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$.
- **Pull-in gap** is at $2d_0/3$ — leaves only $d_0/3$ of stable analog travel.
- **Comb-drive force** is **independent of displacement** (linear) — no pull-in.
- **Comb-drive force** $F = N \varepsilon_0 h V^2 / (2g)$ — scales with finger count.
- **Thermal expansion** $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$ — silicon CTE = 2.6 ppm/K.
- **Bimorph deflection** amplifies thermal actuation by 10–100× over unimorph.
- **Nitinol SMA** recovery strain: 6–8%; transformation temp: 90–110°C.
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- **SMA hysteresis:** $A_s - M_s \approx 20$–$50°C$ for Nitinol.

### ⚠️ Common KTU Mistake Zones

1. Confusing **direct** and **converse** piezoelectric effects (always swap $D \leftrightarrow S$, $T \leftrightarrow E$).
2. Forgetting to square the gap in the parallel-plate force formula.
3. Mixing up $d$, $g$, and $k$ coefficients — remember: $d$ = charge/strain, $g$ = voltage, $k$ = coupling efficiency.
4. Assuming comb drives have pull-in (they don't — that's their key advantage).
5. Stating that piezoelectric sensors measure static quantities (they don't).
6. Forgetting to multiply by $N$ in comb-drive formulas.
7. Mixing up series/parallel spring combinations in suspended structures.
8. Confusing the **piezo voltage coefficient** $g$ (V·m/N) with the **piezo charge coefficient** $d$ (C/N).

### 📋 Engineering Application Cross-Reference

| Mechanism | Real-World MEMS Device | Manufacturer / Year |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Comb Drive | ADXL345 Accelerometer | Analog Devices, 2009 |
| Torsion Mirror | DLP Projection Chip | Texas Instruments, 1996+ |
| Piezo | AFM Cantilever | Bruker, Veeco |
| Piezo | Inkjet Printhead | Epson, Canon |
| Thermal | Bubble Jet | HP, Canon |
| SMA | Micro-endoscopic gripper | Various research |
| Comb Drive | Gyroscope | STMicro L3GD20 |
| Electrostatic Pump | Drug delivery | Debiotech, 2010s |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy:** Memorize the **2/3 rule** for pull-in and the **formula for comb-drive force** — these two alone account for ~40% of the marks in any KTU Module 4 ESE question on actuation. Practice at least 3 numerical problems on parallel-plate and comb-drive actuators before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET522 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies of MEMS: MEMS Inertial Sensors in Automobiles (Airbag Deployment, Automobile Navigation), MEMS Vibratory Gyroscopes, MEMS Accelerometers, MEMS Devices in Commercial Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to MEMS Inertial Sensors

**Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) Inertial Sensors** are microfabricated devices that measure changes in linear acceleration (accelerometers) and angular rate (gyroscopes) using suspended proof masses, comb drives, and capacitive/ piezoresistive transduction. They form the sensing core of modern automotive safety, navigation, consumer electronics, and industrial systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Standard):** MEMS inertial sensors are monolithic or hybrid microsystems fabricated using IC-compatible batch processes (bulk or surface micromachining) that convert mechanical motion (acceleration, rotation, vibration) into a measurable electrical signal using transduction principles such as capacitive, piezoresistive, piezoelectric, or resonant sensing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mini Spring-Box on a Chip"

Imagine a tiny **proof mass** (like a small metal block) suspended inside a silicon chip by four ultra-thin silicon springs. When the car (chip) accelerates forward:
- The mass **tries to stay behind** due to inertia (Newton's First Law).
- The mass **stretches or compresses** the springs.
- The resulting tiny displacement changes the **electrical capacitance** between interdigitated fingers.
- This capacitance change is read as a voltage proportional to acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly how a mass-on-a-spring system in a bathroom scale works — only shrunk to micrometer scale and integrated with on-chip readout electronics.

### 1.3 Classification of MEMS Inertial Sensors (KTU Syllabus Map)

| Sensor Type | Physical Quantity Measured | Key Transduction |
|---|---|---|
| MEMS Accelerometer | Linear acceleration (g or m/s²) | Capacitive / Piezoresistive |
| MEMS Vibratory Gyroscope | Angular rate (°/s or rad/s) | Capacitive (Coriolis force) |
| MEMS IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) | Both acceleration & rotation | Capacitive (6-DoF) |
| MEMS Inclinometer | Tilt angle | Capacitive |

### 1.4 Significance in Modern Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The global MEMS inertial sensor market exceeds **USD 16 billion** (2024 estimates), with automobiles consuming over **30%** of production. Airbag systems alone use **5 to 15 MEMS sensors** per vehicle in advanced Electronic Stability Programs (ESP).

---

## 1.5 Case Study 1: Airbag Deployment System (Automotive Safety)

### 1.5.1 System Architecture Overview

A MEMS-based airbag system is essentially a **crash-decision microsystem** that must reliably differentiate between:
1. A genuine frontal crash (deploy airbag).
2. A door slam, pothole, or minor bump (do not deploy).

**Sensing Chain:**
```
Crash Event → MEMS Accelerometer → Analog Front End → 
ADC → Microcontroller (Algorithm) → Squib Driver → Airbag Ignition
```

### 1.5.2 Key Performance Specifications

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Highlight:** The airbag decision must complete within **10 to 15 milliseconds** of crash onset. The system must reject **±35 g** non-crash vibrations and deploy reliably at **±15 g to ±25 g** sustained deceleration.

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement Range | ±50 to ±100 | g |
| Bandwidth | DC to 400 | Hz |
| Threshold (deployment) | 2.5 to 5 (velocity change) | m/s |
| Decision Time | 10 to 15 | ms |
| Sensor Count in modern car | 5 to 15 | sensors |
| Operating Temperature | -40 to +85 | °C |

### 1.5.3 The Deployment Algorithm (Conceptual)

A typical airbag algorithm uses a **multi-stage decision tree**:

- **Stage 1 — Wake-up Sensor:** Low-g (±2 g) sensor detects initial deceleration.
- **Stage 2 — Satellite Sensors:** Multiple peripheral accelerometers (front bumper, side pillars, B-pillar) confirm crash signature.
- **Stage 3 — Central Airbag Sensor:** High-g (±50 g) sensor measures crash pulse profile.
- **Stage 4 — Velocity Change Computation:** The microcontroller integrates the acceleration over time:
$$\Delta v = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt$$
- If $\Delta v$ exceeds a programmed threshold (typically **2.5 to 5 m/s** within 10–30 ms), the airbag is fired.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Think of it as a bank-vault alarm — a single vibration (a "minor tremor") is ignored, but multiple coordinated sensors confirming a "break-in signature" trigger deployment.

---

## 1.6 Case Study 2: Automobile Navigation (GPS + MEMS-IMU)

### 1.6.1 Why MEMS for Navigation?

GPS signals are unavailable in:
- **Tunnels** and underground parking.
- **Urban canyons** (between tall buildings).
- **Forests** with dense canopy.

MEMS Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) provide **dead-reckoning** to bridge GPS outages, keeping the navigation continuous.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Fact:** A typical automotive navigation-grade MEMS-IMU (e.g., Bosch SMI130, ST LSM6DSOX) integrates a 3-axis accelerometer (±16 g) and 3-axis gyroscope (±2000 °/s) in a single **3 mm × 3 mm** LGA package.

### 1.6.2 Sensor Fusion (Loosely Coupled vs Tightly Coupled)

The navigation computer runs a **Kalman filter** that optimally fuses:
- GPS position updates (low rate, ~1 Hz, accurate long-term).
- MEMS-IMU outputs (high rate, ~100–1000 Hz, drifts short-term).

The fused solution provides **continuous, drift-free position, velocity, and attitude**.

---

## 1.7 Case Study 3: MEMS Vibratory Gyroscope

### 1.7.1 Working Principle

A MEMS vibratory gyroscope is based on the **Coriolis Effect**:
1. A proof mass is driven into **resonant oscillation** along a primary (drive) axis with velocity $v_d$.
2. When the chip rotates with angular rate $\Omega$ about an input axis, the Coriolis force:
$$\vec{F}_{Coriolis} = -2m\, (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_d)$$
3. This force deflects the mass along a **secondary (sense) axis**.
4. The deflection is measured as a capacitance change proportional to $\Omega$.

### 1.7.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> Imagine spinning on a playground merry-go-round while sliding a ball straight outward. The ball does not travel in a straight line on the ground — it appears to curve. That perceived "curve" is the Coriolis effect. In a MEMS gyroscope, the "straight outward push" is the vibrating proof mass, and the "curve" is the deflection measured by sense capacitors.

### 1.7.3 Key Specification Terms (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Drive mode frequency | $f_d$ | 10 to 30 kHz |
| Sense mode frequency | $f_s$ | 10 to 30 kHz (matched) |
| Mode frequency mismatch | $\Delta f$ | < 100 Hz |
| Quality factor (vacuum) | Q | 1000 to 50000 |
| Scale factor | SF | 10 to 50 mV/(°/s) |
| Bias stability | B | 1 to 10 °/h |
| Angle Random Walk | ARW | 0.1 to 1 °/√h |
| Full scale range | FSR | ±2000 °/s |

---

## 1.8 Case Study 4: MEMS Accelerometer

### 1.8.1 Operating Principle

A proof mass $m$ suspended by springs of stiffness $k$ and damped by a damping coefficient $b$ obeys Newton's second law when the frame (case) accelerates with $a_{case}$:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m\, a_{case}$$

At steady state (DC or low-frequency input, well below natural frequency), the mass displacement is:

$$x_{ss} = -\frac{m\, a_{case}}{k} = -\frac{a_{case}}{\omega_n^2}$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the natural frequency.

### 1.8.2 Capacitive Readout

The proof mass has interleaved "comb fingers" that form differential capacitors with fixed stator fingers. The capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A_{eff}}{d_{gap}}$$

A displacement $\Delta x$ produces a differential change $\Delta C$ that is linear in $\Delta x$ (for small displacements).

### 1.8.3 Key Performance Matrix

| Parameter | Low-g (Consumer) | High-g (Airbag) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | ±2 / ±4 / ±8 | ±50 / ±100 / ±200 | g |
| Sensitivity | 1000 | 10 | mV/g |
| Bandwidth | DC – 100 | DC – 1500 | Hz |
| Noise floor | 50 | 1000 | µg/√Hz |
| Power | 0.1 | 1 | mW |

---

## 1.9 Case Study 5: MEMS Devices in Commercial Applications

### 1.9.1 Application Portfolio

| Domain | MEMS Device | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Smartphones** | 6-axis IMU (LSM6DSO) | Screen rotation, gaming, AR/VR |
| **Wearables (Apple Watch, Fitbit)** | Accelerometer + Gyro | Step counting, fall detection |
| **Hearing Aids** | MEMS Microphone | Acoustic pickup |
| **Inkjet Printers (HP, Canon)** | MEMS Nozzle Array | Droplet ejection |
| **Drones (DJI)** | 6/9-axis IMU | Flight stabilization |
| **Medical (Pacemaker, Insulin pump)** | Pressure / Flow sensor | Biocompatible sensing |
| **Hard Disk Drives** | MEMS Piezoresistive head | Sub-nm positioning |
| **Projectors (TI DLP)** | MEMS Mirror Array (DMD) | Light modulation |
| **Cameras (OIS)** | MEMS Gyroscope | Optical image stabilization |
| **Apple AirPods / Galaxy Buds** | Bone-conduction mic | Voice pickup |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The Texas Instruments **DLP (Digital Light Processing)** chip contains up to **8 million** tilting micromirrors (each 16 µm × 16 µm) on a single silicon die — the largest MEMS array in commercial production.

### 1.9.2 The Smartphone IMU — A Miniaturization Marvel

A modern smartphone (e.g., iPhone 15, Samsung Galaxy S24) contains **at least 6 MEMS devices**:
1. Accelerometer (Bosch or STMicro).
2. Gyroscope.
3. Magnetometer (Hall or anisotropic magnetoresistive).
4. Pressure sensor (barometer).
5. Microphone (2–4 units).
6. Auto-focus actuator (some models).

All packaged in a single **System-in-Package (SiP)** consuming under **2 mW** total.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 1 Recap (Single Line):** MEMS inertial sensors convert mechanical motion (acceleration/rotation) into electrical signals using suspended proof masses, springs, and capacitive/piezoresistive readout — and are the invisible backbone of airbag safety, GPS-aided navigation, smartphone interactivity, and countless commercial products.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model of a MEMS Accelerometer

### 2.1.1 Equation of Motion (Single Degree of Freedom)

Consider a proof mass $m$ constrained to move along the $x$-axis by suspension springs (stiffness $k$) and squeezed by the surrounding fluid (damping $b$). The case (frame) of the sensor moves with input acceleration $a_{case}(t)$. The mass-frame relative displacement is $x(t)$.

Applying Newton's second law to the proof mass in the inertial frame:

$$m\left(\ddot{x} + \ddot{x}_{case}\right) = -b\dot{x} - kx$$

Since $\ddot{x}_{case} = a_{case}(t)$:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m\, a_{case}(t)$$

### 2.1.2 Normalization to Standard Form

Dividing throughout by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta \omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = -a_{case}(t)$$

where:
- Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
- Damping ratio: $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{b}{2m\omega_n}$

### 2.1.3 Transfer Function (Laplace Domain)

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$\frac{X(s)}{A_{case}(s)} = \frac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** The negative sign indicates that the mass moves **opposite** to the frame acceleration (inertia). For DC input $a_{case} = a_0$, the steady-state displacement is $x_{ss} = -a_0/\omega_n^2$.

### 2.1.4 DC Sensitivity

$$S_{DC} = \left\vert \frac{x}{a_{case}} \right\vert_{\omega = 0} = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{m}{k}$$

### 2.1.5 Bandwidth (-3 dB Frequency)

$$\omega_{-3dB} = \omega_n \sqrt{\left(1 - 2\zeta^2\right) + \sqrt{4\zeta^4 - 4\zeta^2 + 2}}$$

For light damping ($\zeta \ll 1$):

$$\omega_{-3dB} \approx \omega_n \sqrt{2} \quad \text{(approximate)}$$

### 2.1.6 Design Trade-off

> [!WARNING]
> **High Sensitivity** requires LOW $k$ or HIGH $m$ (low $\omega_n$). But LOW $\omega_n$ implies LOW BANDWIDTH. This is the **fundamental trade-off** in accelerometer design — high sensitivity vs wide bandwidth.

---

## 2.2 Capacitive Transduction Analysis

### 2.2.1 Comb-Finger Capacitance

For a differential capacitive accelerometer with $N$ gap-closing fingers of overlap length $L_f$, finger width $w_f$, and initial gap $d_0$:

$$C_0 = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L_f w_f}{d_0}$$

For a proof-mass displacement $\Delta x$:

$$C_1 = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L_f w_f}{d_0 - \Delta x}, \quad C_2 = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L_f w_f}{d_0 + \Delta x}$$

Differential capacitance change:

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = 2 C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{d_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (\Delta x/d_0)^2}$$

For small displacement ($\Delta x \ll d_0$):

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 C_0 \Delta x}{d_0}$$

### 2.2.2 Capacitive Sensitivity (per unit g)

$$\frac{\Delta C}{a} = \frac{2 C_0}{d_0 \cdot \omega_n^2} = \frac{2 C_0 m}{d_0 k}$$

### 2.2.3 Capacitance-to-Voltage Conversion

Using a differential charge amplifier or continuous-time interface:

$$V_{out} = \frac{\Delta C}{C_{ref}} \cdot V_{ref} = \frac{2 C_0 \Delta x}{C_{ref} d_0} \cdot V_{ref}$$

---

## 2.3 MEMS Vibratory Gyroscope — Coriolis Dynamics

### 2.3.1 Equation of Motion (2-DOF Coupled System)

A vibratory gyroscope has two orthogonal modes — **drive mode** ($x$) and **sense mode** ($y$). The coupled equations are:

$$m\ddot{x} + b_x \dot{x} + k_x x = F_d \cos(\omega_d t)$$

$$m\ddot{y} + b_y \dot{y} + k_y y = -2m\Omega \dot{x}$$

The Coriolis coupling appears as a forcing term proportional to $\Omega$ in the sense equation.

### 2.3.2 Steady-State Sense Displacement

For a perfectly mode-matched gyroscope ($\omega_x = \omega_y = \omega_0$) and drive at resonance ($\omega_d = \omega_0$):

$$y_{ss} = \frac{2\Omega Q_x x_d}{\omega_0} = \frac{2\Omega Q_x v_d}{\omega_0^2}$$

where:
- $x_d$ = drive amplitude of proof mass displacement.
- $v_d = \omega_0 x_d$ = drive velocity amplitude.
- $Q_x$ = quality factor of drive mode.

### 2.3.3 Gyroscope Scale Factor

$$\text{Scale Factor } (SF) = \frac{V_{out}}{\Omega} = \frac{2 Q_x v_d}{\omega_0^2} \cdot \eta_{interface}$$

where $\eta_{interface}$ is the interface electronics gain (V/m).

### 2.3.4 Mode Mismatch and Bias Error

When $\omega_x \neq \omega_y$ (typical mismatch $\Delta\omega / \omega_0 \approx 0.1\%$), quadrature error appears:

$$y_{quad} \propto \frac{k_{xy}}{\omega_x^2 - \omega_y^2} \cdot x_d$$

This quadrature signal mimics a rotation signal and causes **bias error**. It is canceled using **quadrature nulling electrodes** in real designs.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a perfectly round drum vs a slightly oval drum. The slightly oval one (mismatched modes) will vibrate sideways even without any rotation — this false sideways motion is "quadrature error."

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize all formulas in this table. At least one derivation-based problem from these appears every KTU end-semester paper.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Fundamental resonance |
| 2 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless | Critical at $\zeta = 1$ |
| 3 | DC sensitivity (displacement) | $S_{DC} = m/k = 1/\omega_n^2$ | m·s²/m | Trade-off vs bandwidth |
| 4 | Quality factor | $Q = \sqrt{km}/b$ | dimensionless | High Q → sharp resonance |
| 5 | Coriolis force | $F_c = 2m\Omega v$ | N | Proportional to angular rate |
| 6 | Comb capacitance | $C_0 = N\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L_f w_f / d_0$ | F | Linear in $N$ |
| 7 | Capacitive change | $\Delta C = 2 C_0 \Delta x / d_0$ | F | Small-displacement approx. |
| 8 | Airbag velocity change | $\Delta v = \int a(t)\, dt$ | m/s | Crash severity metric |
| 9 | Sense displacement (gyro) | $y_{ss} = 2\Omega Q_x v_d/\omega_0^2$ | m | At resonance |
| 10 | Scale factor (gyro) | $SF = 2 Q_x v_d / \omega_0^2$ | m·s | Multiplied by interface gain |
| 11 | Brownian noise equiv. acc. | $a_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T \omega_n / (m Q)}$ | m/s²/√Hz | Fundamental thermal noise |
| 12 | Pressure (squeeze film) | $P = 12 \mu Q A / (b^3 h^3)$ | Pa | Damping mechanism |
| 13 | Mechanical sensitivity | $S_m = a \cdot m / k$ | m | Mass-spring sensitivity |
| 14 | Capacitive sensitivity | $S_C = 2C_0 / (d_0 \omega_n^2)$ | F/(m/s²) | Capacitance change per g |

### 2.4.1 Physical Constants (Bold Highlighted for Recall)

- Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
- Permittivity of silicon: $\varepsilon_r \approx 11.7$ (dimensionless)
- Standard gravity: $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** (or 1 g = 9.81 m/s²)
- Density of silicon: $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ **kg/m³**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sensor | Engineering Domain | Specific Use |
|---|---|---|
| Airbag accelerometer | Automotive safety | Crash discrimination algorithm |
| Navigation IMU | Aerospace / Automotive | GPS-denied dead reckoning |
| Smartphone IMU | Consumer electronics | Screen rotation, gaming, AR |
| Gyroscope (Drones) | UAV flight control | 3-axis attitude stabilization |
| Pressure sensor (altimeter) | Smartwatch / Drone | Altitude hold / floor detection |
| MEMS microphone | Hearing aid / Phone | Noise-cancelling beamforming |
| DMD chip | Projector / AR headset | Spatial light modulation |
| Inkjet head | Printer | Droplet-on-demand ejection |

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 2 Recap:** A MEMS accelerometer is mathematically a **2nd-order mass-spring-damper system** with output displacement proportional to applied acceleration below $\omega_n$. A MEMS gyroscope exploits the **Coriolis coupling** between orthogonal vibrating modes to transduce angular rate. Capacitive readout dominates in modern designs due to low power, high sensitivity, and IC compatibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Displacement of a MEMS Accelerometer

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A MEMS accelerometer has a proof mass $m = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ kg suspended by four folded-flexure springs, each of stiffness $k_f = 0.05$ N/m (total $k = 4 \times 0.05 = 0.2$ N/m). The damping ratio is $\zeta = 0.7$. Determine:
1. The natural frequency $\omega_n$.
2. The DC sensitivity.
3. The steady-state displacement under a 5 g input acceleration.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the natural frequency.

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.2}{1 \times 10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{8}} = 1.4142 \times 10^{4} \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1.4142 \times 10^{4}}{2\pi} \approx 2250.8 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2:** Compute DC sensitivity.

$$S_{DC} = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{1}{(1.4142 \times 10^{4})^2} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}^2$$

Equivalently:

$$S_{DC} = \frac{m}{k} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{0.2} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Input acceleration 5 g.

$$a_{case} = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4:** Steady-state displacement.

$$x_{ss} = -S_{DC} \cdot a_{case} = -5 \times 10^{-9} \times 49.05 = -2.4525 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

$$x_{ss} \approx -245.25 \text{ nm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A 5 g input produces a **245 nm** displacement of the proof mass — a small but easily measurable value with modern capacitive interfaces (gap typically 2–4 µm).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacitive Sensitivity of a Comb Accelerometer

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A differential capacitive accelerometer has the following parameters:
- Number of finger pairs: $N = 100$
- Finger overlap length: $L_f = 30$ µm
- Finger overlap width: $w_f = 5$ µm
- Initial gap: $d_0 = 2$ µm
- Dielectric: air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$)

Calculate:
1. The static capacitance $C_0$.
2. The differential capacitance change $\Delta C$ for $x_{ss} = 245$ nm.
3. The capacitive sensitivity per g of acceleration.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute $C_0$.

$$C_0 = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L_f w_f}{d_0}$$

Substituting values (converting µm to m: $30 \times 10^{-6}$, $5 \times 10^{-6}$, $2 \times 10^{-6}$):

$$C_0 = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{100 \times 1.3281 \times 10^{-21}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.3281 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.6405 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F}$$

$$C_0 \approx 66.4 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 2:** Differential capacitance change.

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 C_0 x_{ss}}{d_0} = \frac{2 \times 66.4 \times 10^{-15} \times 245.25 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{3.257 \times 10^{-20}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.6285 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F}$$

$$\Delta C \approx 16.3 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 3:** Capacitive sensitivity per g.

$$\frac{\Delta C}{a} = \frac{\Delta C / 5g}{g} = \frac{16.3 \text{ fF}}{5} = 3.26 \text{ fF/g}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sense Displacement of a MEMS Gyroscope

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A vibratory MEMS gyroscope has the following parameters:
- Proof mass: $m = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ kg
- Drive mode stiffness: $k_d = 0.1$ N/m
- Drive mode quality factor: $Q_d = 5000$ (vacuum packaged)
- Drive mode amplitude: $x_d = 5$ µm
- Angular rate input: $\Omega = 100$ °/s

Calculate the sense displacement.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Natural frequency.

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{k_d}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{5 \times 10^{-10}}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{8}} = 1.4142 \times 10^{4} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Drive velocity amplitude.

$$v_d = \omega_0 \cdot x_d = 1.4142 \times 10^{4} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.07071 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert angular rate to rad/s.

$$\Omega = 100 \text{ °/s} \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.7453 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4:** Sense displacement.

$$y_{ss} = \frac{2 \Omega Q_d v_d}{\omega_0^2} = \frac{2 \times 1.7453 \times 5000 \times 0.07071}{(1.4142 \times 10^{4})^2}$$

$$y_{ss} = \frac{1234.2}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 6.171 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$y_{ss} \approx 6.17 \text{ µm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A 100 °/s rotation produces a 6.17 µm sense displacement — comparable to the drive amplitude, and easily resolved with capacitive sensing.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Airbag Decision — Velocity Change

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement

A high-g accelerometer records the following acceleration profile during a 30 ms crash pulse:
- 0 to 5 ms: $a(t) = 50 \cdot t$ m/s² (linear ramp)
- 5 to 30 ms: $a(t) = 250$ m/s² (constant plateau)
- After 30 ms: $a = 0$ (sensor saturates / passenger compartment settled)

Determine the velocity change $\Delta v$ and decide if the airbag should fire (threshold: 3 m/s in 30 ms).

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Velocity change in Phase 1 (0 to 5 ms).

$$\Delta v_1 = \int_0^{0.005} 50 t \, dt = 50 \cdot \frac{t^2}{2} \bigg\vert_0^{0.005} = 25 \times (0.005)^2$$

$$\Delta v_1 = 25 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Velocity change in Phase 2 (5 to 30 ms = 25 ms = 0.025 s).

$$\Delta v_2 = \int_{0.005}^{0.030} 250 \, dt = 250 \times (0.030 - 0.005) = 250 \times 0.025 = 6.25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Total velocity change.

$$\Delta v_{total} = \Delta v_1 + \Delta v_2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} + 6.25 \approx 6.25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Decision.

$$\Delta v_{total} = 6.25 \text{ m/s} > 3 \text{ m/s} \Rightarrow \boxed{\text{DEPLOY}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm Note:** In real ECUs, the velocity change is computed via a discrete summation:
> $$\Delta v \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \cdot \Delta t$$
> where the integration begins only when a "crash-in-progress" flag is set by a low-g wake-up sensor.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the most-asked concept in KTU viva and short-answer questions. Memorize the directional sensitivity.

| Parameter Change | Effect on $\omega_n$ | Effect on DC Sensitivity $S_{DC} = 1/\omega_n^2$ | Effect on Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase proof mass $m$ | Decrease | Increase | Decrease |
| Increase spring stiffness $k$ | Increase | Decrease | Increase |
| Increase damping $b$ | No change | No change | Slight change (over-damping) |
| Decrease gap $d_0$ | No change | No change | No change |
| Increase finger count $N$ | No change | No change | No change |
| Increase $\varepsilon_r$ (dielectric) | No change | No change | No change |

### 3.5.1 Gyroscope Parameter Sensitivity

| Parameter | Sense Displacement $y_{ss}$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Increase $\Omega$ (rotation rate) | Linear increase | Fundamental input |
| Increase drive velocity $v_d$ | Linear increase | Limited by structural margin |
| Increase $Q_d$ (vacuum) | Linear increase | Enables high sensitivity |
| Mode mismatch ($\Delta \omega \neq 0$) | Causes quadrature error | Must be tuned |
| Increase drive freq $\omega_0$ | Decreases as $1/\omega_0^2$ | High-freq designs less sensitive |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example: Thermal (Brownian) Noise Floor

### 3.6.1 Problem Statement

Compute the Brownian-noise equivalent acceleration for the accelerometer in Section 3.1 at $T = 300$ K.

### 3.6.2 Step-by-Step Solution

The thermal noise equivalent acceleration is:

$$a_n = \sqrt{\frac{4 k_B T \omega_n}{m Q}}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $Q$ from damping ratio.

For $\zeta = 0.7$:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.7} = 0.714$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$a_n = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 1.4142 \times 10^{4}}{1 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.714}}$$

Numerator:

$$4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 1.4142 \times 10^{4} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 1.4142 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$= 4 \times 585.5 \times 10^{-19} = 2.342 \times 10^{-16}$$

Denominator:

$$1 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.714 = 7.14 \times 10^{-10}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{2.342 \times 10^{-16}}{7.14 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.28 \times 10^{-7}$$

Square root:

$$a_n = \sqrt{3.28 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.73 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}^2/\sqrt{Hz}$$

Converting to $\mu g/\sqrt{Hz}$:

$$a_n = \frac{5.73 \times 10^{-4}}{9.81} \times 10^6 = 58.4 \text{ }\mu g/\sqrt{Hz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Even with no input acceleration, the proof mass jiggles randomly due to thermal agitation (Brownian motion) of air molecules. This sets a **fundamental noise floor** that cannot be eliminated — only reduced by increasing $m$, $Q$, or lowering $T$.

---

## 3.7 Derivative Summary Table (KTU Board Exam Quick Reference)

| Quantity | Symbol | Expression | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static displacement (DC) | $x_{ss}$ | $a/\omega_n^2$ | Accelerometer |
| Transfer function | $H(s)$ | $1/(s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$ | All damped sensors |
| Comb capacitance | $C_0$ | $N\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L_f w_f / d_0$ | Capacitive read-out |
| Differential $\Delta C$ | $\Delta C$ | $2C_0 \Delta x / d_0$ | Small-signal sensing |
| Coriolis force | $F_c$ | $2m\Omega v$ | Gyroscope |
| Sense displacement (gyro) | $y_{ss}$ | $2\Omega Q v_d/\omega_0^2$ | Gyroscope |
| Velocity change (airbag) | $\Delta v$ | $\int a(t) dt$ | Airbag algorithm |
| Thermal noise accel. | $a_n$ | $\sqrt{4k_BT\omega_n/(mQ)}$ | Noise-limited design |

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 3 Recap:** Every derivation reduces to the master equation $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ for mechanical resonance, multiplied by a transduction coefficient. Memorize the four key steps: (1) write Newton's law, (2) normalize to standard form, (3) extract $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$, (4) compute the output quantity.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MEMS Accelerometer and State its Working Principle

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **MEMS accelerometer** is a microfabricated sensor that measures linear acceleration along one or more axes by detecting the displacement of a proof mass suspended by compliant springs relative to the sensor frame. The working principle is based on **Newton's second law of motion**: when the sensor case accelerates, a suspended proof mass experiences an inertial force $-m a_{case}$ that displaces it by $x = m a_{case} / k$ (DC). The displacement is transduced into an electrical signal using **capacitive**, **piezoresistive**, or **piezoelectric** readout, providing a voltage proportional to the applied acceleration.

---

### Question 2: What is the Coriolis Effect and How is it Used in MEMS Gyroscopes?

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Coriolis effect** is the apparent deflection of a moving mass when observed from a rotating reference frame. For a mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ in a frame rotating at angular rate $\Omega$, the Coriolis force is:

$$\vec{F}_{Coriolis} = -2m\,(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$$

In a **MEMS vibratory gyroscope**, the proof mass is driven into resonant oscillation along the **drive axis** with velocity $v_d$. When the chip rotates, the Coriolis force deflects the mass along the orthogonal **sense axis** with displacement:

$$y_{ss} = \frac{2 \Omega Q v_d}{\omega_0^2}$$

This displacement is capacitively sensed, providing a voltage proportional to the input angular rate $\Omega$. The Coriolis effect thus allows conversion of rotational motion into a measurable linear displacement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Working of a MEMS Accelerometer Used in Automobile Airbag Systems. Discuss the Algorithm Used to Discriminate Between a Real Crash and Non-Crash Events.

**Model Solution:**

A MEMS accelerometer used in an automobile airbag system is typically a **high-g (±50 to ±100 g) capacitive accelerometer**. The proof mass is suspended by four folded-flexure springs and forms differential capacitors with fixed stator fingers. When the vehicle undergoes a sudden deceleration during a crash, the proof mass displaces relative to the sensor frame, producing a differential capacitance change proportional to the deceleration. This capacitance change is converted to an analog voltage by an on-chip ASIC, digitized by an ADC, and processed by a microcontroller.

**Crash discrimination algorithm** in the ECU operates in multiple stages:

1. **Wake-up stage:** A low-g sensor (±2 g) detects the initial vehicle deceleration and wakes up the high-g sensor and processor.

2. **Crash pulse integration:** The high-g sensor samples the deceleration profile $a(t)$ at 1 kHz. The microcontroller computes the **velocity change**:
$$\Delta v = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \cdot \Delta t$$

3. **Threshold comparison:** If $\Delta v$ exceeds a programmed threshold (typically **2.5 to 5 m/s** within 10–30 ms), the system flags a crash.

4. **Cross-validation with satellite sensors:** Multiple accelerometers (front bumper, side pillars, B-pillar) confirm the crash signature. Deployment is aborted if sensors disagree.

5. **Actuation:** If validated, the squib driver fires the inflator, generating nitrogen gas in **30 ms** to deploy the airbag cushion.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Draw the block diagram (sensors → ASIC → ADC → µC → squib → airbag) and clearly mark the **decision time** (10–15 ms) and **threshold velocity change** (2.5–5 m/s).

#### (b) [7 Marks] A MEMS Accelerometer Has a Proof Mass of $2 \times 10^{-9}$ kg and Spring Stiffness of 0.1 N/m. Calculate the Natural Frequency, DC Sensitivity, and the Steady-State Displacement When Subjected to an Input of 10 g.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Proof mass $m = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ kg
- Spring stiffness $k = 0.1$ N/m
- Input acceleration $a = 10\,g = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1$ m/s²

**Step 1: Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{2 \times 10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$\omega_n = 7071.07 \text{ rad/s} \approx 7.07 \text{ krad/s}$$

In Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{7071.07}{2\pi} \approx 1125.4 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: DC Sensitivity**

$$S_{DC} = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{m}{k} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1} = 2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}^2$$

**Step 3: Steady-State Displacement**

$$x_{ss} = -S_{DC} \cdot a_{case} = -2 \times 10^{-8} \times 98.1$$

$$x_{ss} = -1.962 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = -1.962 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{x_{ss} \approx 1.96 \text{ }\mu\text{m}}$$

The negative sign indicates the proof mass displaces opposite to the input acceleration. The magnitude of **1.96 µm** is well within the typical 2–4 µm gap of a comb-finger accelerometer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a Neat Block Diagram, Explain the Working of a MEMS Vibratory Gyroscope. Derive the Expression for the Sense Displacement.

**Model Solution:**

A **MEMS vibratory gyroscope** is a 2-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) micromechanical resonator that exploits the **Coriolis effect** to measure angular rate. The block diagram is:

```
Drive Oscillator → Drive Comb Actuator → Proof Mass (oscillates in X) →
[Rotating frame imparts Coriolis force in Y] → Sense Comb Capacitors →
Transimpedance Amplifier → Synchronous Demodulator → LPF → Output
```

**Working Principle:**

1. A **drive oscillator** applies an AC voltage to comb-finger actuators that drive the proof mass into **resonant oscillation** along the X-axis (drive mode) at frequency $\omega_0$ and amplitude $x_d$.

2. When the chip rotates about the Z-axis with angular rate $\Omega$, the Coriolis force acts on the moving mass in the Y-direction (perpendicular to the drive direction):

$$F_{Coriolis} = 2m\Omega \dot{x} = 2m\Omega v_d \cos(\omega_0 t)$$

3. This time-varying force excites the **sense mode** (Y-axis) of the proof mass, causing displacement $y(t)$.

**Derivation of Sense Displacement:**

The equation of motion of the sense mode is:

$$m\ddot{y} + b_y \dot{y} + k_y y = -2m\Omega \dot{x}$$

The drive motion is $x(t) = x_d \cos(\omega_0 t)$, so $\dot{x} = -\omega_0 x_d \sin(\omega_0 t)$.

Substituting the drive motion (treating $\Omega$ as quasi-static compared to $\omega_0$):

$$m\ddot{y} + b_y \dot{y} + k_y y = 2m\Omega \omega_0 x_d \sin(\omega_0 t)$$

The forcing has amplitude $F_0 = 2m\Omega\omega_0 x_d$ at frequency $\omega_0$. At resonance, the steady-state amplitude of $y$ is:

$$y_{ss} = \frac{F_0}{b_y \omega_0} = \frac{2m\Omega\omega_0 x_d}{b_y \omega_0} = \frac{2m\Omega x_d}{b_y}$$

Since $Q_x = \dfrac{\omega_0 m}{b_y}$ (quality factor of drive mode assumed matched with sense), we have $b_y = m\omega_0 / Q_x$ (mode-matched). Substituting:

$$y_{ss} = \frac{2m\Omega x_d Q_x}{m\omega_0} = \frac{2\Omega Q_x x_d}{\omega_0}$$

Using $v_d = \omega_0 x_d$:

$$\boxed{y_{ss} = \frac{2\Omega Q_x v_d}{\omega_0^2}}$$

This is the standard expression for the sense displacement of a MEMS vibratory gyroscope operating at resonance with perfectly mode-matched drive and sense frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Clearly state the assumption of mode-matching ($\omega_x = \omega_y$) and that the drive operates at the sense-mode natural frequency. Mention **quadrature error** as a real-world issue if asked.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A MEMS Gyroscope Has the Following Specifications: $m = 8 \times 10^{-10}$ kg, $k = 0.16$ N/m, $Q = 10000$ (Vacuum), and Drive Amplitude $x_d = 2$ µm. Find the Sense Displacement for an Input Angular Rate of 50°/s. Also, Comment on the Effect of Mode Mismatch.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 8 \times 10^{-10}$ kg
- $k = 0.16$ N/m
- $Q = 10000$
- $x_d = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\Omega = 50°/\text{s}$

**Step 1: Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.16}{8 \times 10^{-10}}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{8}} = 1.4142 \times 10^4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Drive Velocity Amplitude**

$$v_d = \omega_0 \cdot x_d = 1.4142 \times 10^4 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 2.828 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Convert Angular Rate to rad/s**

$$\Omega = 50 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.8727 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Sense Displacement**

$$y_{ss} = \frac{2 \Omega Q v_d}{\omega_0^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.8727 \times 10000 \times 2.828 \times 10^{-2}}{(1.4142 \times 10^4)^2}$$

$$y_{ss} = \frac{2 \times 0.8727 \times 10000 \times 0.02828}{2 \times 10^8}$$

$$y_{ss} = \frac{493.65}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.468 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 2.47 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$$

**Effect of Mode Mismatch:**

In a real device, the drive and sense modes have slightly different natural frequencies ($\omega_x \neq \omega_y$). The mismatch $\Delta\omega = \omega_x - \omega_y$ causes:
- **Quadrature error:** A false sense signal appearing at the drive frequency, proportional to the off-diagonal stiffness $k_{xy}$ and the inverse of $\Delta\omega$. Quadrature mimics a rotation and creates **bias drift**.
- **Reduced sensitivity:** The peak sense response is no longer exactly at the drive frequency, so operation away from resonance reduces amplitude.
- **Mitigation:** Real gyroscopes use **electrostatic frequency tuning** electrodes to dynamically match $\omega_x$ to $\omega_y$ within <1 Hz, and **quadrature nulling** electrodes to cancel the off-diagonal coupling.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $x_{ss}$:** Students often write $x_{ss} = +a/\omega_n^2$ and lose **2 marks**. The proof mass moves **opposite** to the applied acceleration — the sign is **negative** (inertia).
>
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing $g$ (where 1 $g$ = 9.81 m/s²) with m/s². The airbag system often works in $g$ units, but physical derivations need SI units. **Always convert to m/s² before substituting.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $2\zeta$ in Damping:** The standard form is $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x$. Writing $\zeta\omega_n$ instead of $2\zeta\omega_n$ is a common **2-mark penalty**.
>
> 4. **No Block Diagram:** Part B questions on airbag/IMU/gyro **mandatorily require a block diagram**. Drawing a clear labeled diagram fetches **2–3 extra marks** even if numerical answers are partially correct.
>
> 5. **Mode Mismatch Ignored:** In gyroscope problems, students compute $y_{ss}$ using the matched formula but **forget to mention mode mismatch and quadrature** in descriptive parts. The examiner awards only **partial credit** for derivation without real-world context.
>
> 6. **Confusing Capacitance and Capacitive Change:** $C_0$ is the static capacitance (~fF to pF), while $\Delta C$ is the change due to motion (~attoFarads to fF). They are **not the same quantity**.
>
> 7. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Always write the unit (Hz, m/s², m, rad/s). A numerical answer without unit loses **½ to 1 mark**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Last-Minute Revision — Save This Section!**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **MEMS Accelerometer:** Mass-spring-damper microsystem that outputs voltage proportional to applied acceleration.
- **MEMS Gyroscope:** Vibrating mass that exploits the **Coriolis effect** to sense angular rate.
- **Airbag Decision Metric:** Velocity change $\Delta v = \int a(t)\, dt$.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Kalman filter combining GPS (1 Hz, accurate long-term) with MEMS-IMU (1 kHz, drifts short-term).

### ✅ Must-Memorize Formulas
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
- $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{km})$
- $x_{ss} = -a/\omega_n^2$ (DC)
- $F_{Coriolis} = 2m\Omega v$
- $y_{ss} = 2\Omega Q v_d/\omega_0^2$
- $C_0 = N\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L_f w_f / d_0$
- $\Delta C = 2C_0 \Delta x / d_0$
- $a_n = \sqrt{4k_B T \omega_n / (mQ)}$

### ✅ Critical Numbers (Bold-Memorize)
- Airbag decision time: **10–15 ms**
- Airbag threshold velocity change: **2.5–5 m/s**
- Smartphone IMU sample rate: **100–1000 Hz**
- IMU package size: **3 mm × 3 mm**
- Cars per vehicle with modern ESP: **5–15 MEMS sensors**
- DMD mirror size: **16 µm × 16 µm**
- Modern airbag inflation: **30 ms**

### ✅ KTU Syllabus High-Yield Mapping
| Sub-Topic | KTU Exam Frequency | Expected Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Airbag system | Very High (every year) | 7–14 |
| MEMS Gyroscope principle | Very High | 7–14 |
| Accelerometer derivation | High | 7 |
| Sensor fusion (GPS + IMU) | Moderate | 3–7 |
| Commercial applications | Moderate | 3 |
| Capacitive readout | Moderate | 3–7 |

### ✅ Comparative Summary (Quick Recall)

| Feature | Accelerometer | Gyroscope |
|---|---|---|
| Measures | Linear acceleration | Angular rate |
| Core principle | Inertia (Newton's 2nd law) | Coriolis effect |
| Input | Acceleration $a$ | Angular rate $\Omega$ |
| Output DC | $x = m a / k$ | $y = 2\Omega Q v_d/\omega_0^2$ |
| Transduction | Capacitive / piezoresistive | Capacitive (mostly) |
| Bandwidth | DC to 1.5 kHz (airbag) | 10 Hz to 1 kHz |
| Range | ±2 to ±200 g | ±10 to ±2000 °/s |

### ✅ Application Snapshot
- **Airbag** = High-g accelerometer + ECU algorithm + pyrotechnic squib.
- **Navigation IMU** = 3-axis accel + 3-axis gyro + Kalman filter with GPS.
- **Smartphone** = 6-axis IMU + DMP for screen rotation, gaming, AR.
- **Drone** = 6/9-axis IMU at 1 kHz for flight stabilization.
- **DLP Projector** = 8 million tilting micromirrors on a single chip.
- **Hearing Aid** = MEMS microphone with > 60 dB SNR.

### ✅ Design Trade-offs
- **Sensitivity vs Bandwidth:** Increase $m$ or decrease $k$ → higher sensitivity, lower bandwidth.
- **Sensitivity vs Noise Floor:** Larger $m$ and higher $Q$ → lower Brownian noise, higher SNR.
- **Airbag Deployment Reliability:** Must reject non-crash events (pot holes, door slams) — uses **multi-sensor voting**.
- **Gyro Mode Match:** Critical to keep $\omega_x = \omega_y$ for high scale factor and low quadrature.

### ✅ Frequently Asked Short-Answer Topics
1. Why comb-finger capacitive sensing is preferred over piezoresistive?
2. What is quadrature error and how is it suppressed?
3. Why is vacuum packaging used in gyroscopes?
4. What is the role of squeeze-film damping?
5. Why is accelerometer decision based on velocity change and not peak acceleration?
